actions.thought: I am holding AdKc (AKo) in the UTG position (Lojack in 6-max). This is a premium starting hand that sits near the top of my range. With 100BB stack depth, the standard play is to open-raise to build the pot and thin the field, rather than limping which invites multi-way action where AKo realizes less equity. A standard opening size from early position is between 2.2x and 3x the Big Blind. I will open to 2.5BB (250) to balance value generation with pot control while out of position.

UTG (Pro) opened to 250 from early position in a 6-handed game — that range is fairly strong. Q7 suited is marginal: it can flop well sometimes but is often dominated by QT+, KQ and other Qx hands, and has poor playability multiway. With 100bb effective stacks I don’t want to invest 250 into a pot against an early-position raiser out of position and with several players left to act who could reraise or isolate. A 3-bet would be thin and speculative; a call gives poor implied odds vs. stronger ranges and exposes me to reverse implied odds. Fold and conserve stack for a better spot.

Preflop in cutoff facing an UTG raise to 2.5BB from Pro, who likely has a strong range (TT+, AJo+, KQo+ etc.). J8o is offsuit, has poor equity (~30-35% vs tight open), bad playability, and prone to domination. Even in position, calling leads to many tough spots postflop where we are often OOP in 3bet pots or drawing thin. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament with 100BB depth. No need to speculate Hand 1.

2c4c is a very weak hand. Facing a UTG raise (which typically indicates strength), this suited connector is too weak to call even with position. Early in the tournament with deep stacks, I should be disciplined and wait for better spots.

Facing a strong UTG open from the small blind, 87o plays poorly out of position, is easily dominated, and realizes equity badly (plus the BB can squeeze). The 200 to call isn’t worth it against an early-position range, so I’ll preserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 6s. UTG (Pro) has raised to 250. Everyone else folded. The cost to call is 150 into a pot of 400. While I am getting decent pot odds (nearly 3.7 to 1), 46o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a UTG range which is typically tight and strong. Folding is the standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Fold. 7♥4♣ is a very weak, offsuit, non-connective hand with poor playability and will be dominated by most opponents’ calling/raising ranges. I’m UTG with five players left to act and 100 BB effective — no reason to limp or open-raise out of position so early in the tournament. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

9c3s is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low card strength, and no suited potential. In middle position pre-flop with no action ahead except UTG fold, folding is standard to avoid playing a speculative hand out of position with deep stacks. Preserve stack for better spots early in the tournament.

I have pocket 8s in the cutoff with two folds before me. This is a solid hand that plays well as a raise. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x) to build the pot and potentially steal the blinds or isolate.

CO opens to 2.5bb and we’re on the Button with position and a playable though marginal hand. J8o isn’t a great 3-bet candidate (poor blockers and can get dominated when called), but it’s strong enough to defend getting a reasonable price in position. We’ll call and realize equity; postflop we’ll keep pots small when we make weak one-pair and apply pressure on favorable low/medium textures when checked to.

I am in the Small Blind with K5 offsuit. A player in the Cutoff (Claude) raised to 250, and the Button (FiveTwo) called. K5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raiser and a caller. It does not flop well, and I will be forced to play the rest of the hand first to act. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Ks9s. Facing a standard open from the Cutoff and a flat call from the Button, the pot odds are very favorable. There is currently 650 in the pot, and I need to call 150 more, giving me roughly 5.3:1 odds (requiring ~18.75% equity). King-Nine suited is a strong enough hand to defend here, as it can flop strong flushes and top pair. While being out of position against two opponents is a disadvantage, the price is too good to fold, and a squeeze raise is likely an overplay with this specific holding. Therefore, closing the action with a call is the best option.

I hold Ks9s on a board of 3d As 5s. With the Ace of spades on the board, my King of spades gives me the nut flush draw. Being out of position in a multi-way pot (3 players) against the pre-flop raiser (Claude) and the caller (FiveTwo), the standard and most prudent play is to check. Leading out (donk betting) here is generally unbalanced and denies me the opportunity to see how the pre-flop aggressor proceeds. I will check with the intention of calling a bet or potentially raising depending on the sizing and the other opponent's action.

Flop is 3d-As-5s. I have pocket 8s which is an overpair to the 3 and 5 but the Ace is concerning. Two opponents remain (FiveTwo on button, Pro in BB). Both flatted my preflop raise, and Pro checked. The Ace hits a lot of calling ranges. With the flush draw also present, I should bet for value and protection against smaller pairs and draws, but keep it small. Betting around 1/3 pot to 250-300 gets value from worse while controlling pot size.

We have essentially pure air on A♠5♠3♦: no pair, no spade, no meaningful straight draw, and only a weak backdoor diamond possibility via J♦. In a 3-way pot, Claude’s small c-bet will get peeled often (Ax, spades, 5x/3x, wheel/gutter holdings), and our ability to credibly take it away on later streets is reduced because the BB can also continue. With minimal equity and limited fold equity, this is a low-EV float even getting a good price. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I hold Ks9s on a board of 3d As 5s. With the Ace of spades on the board, my King of spades represents the nut flush draw. Facing a continuation bet of 275 into a pot of 800 from the pre-flop raiser (Claude) after the button folded, I am getting extremely favorable odds (calling 275 to win 1075, roughly 3.9:1). I need approximately 20% equity to break even, and my flush draw alone gives me about 18-19% on the next card and ~36% by the river, not counting potential backdoor straight draws or pair outs. Raising is a viable semi-bluff option, but on an Ace-high board, the opponent's range connects well, making it less likely they will fold a top pair. Calling keeps the opponent's bluffing range in play and manages the pot size while I am out of position.

I hold the Ks9s on a board of 3d As 5s 6s. Since the Ace of spades is on the board, my King of spades constitutes the Nut Flush. I currently have the best possible hand. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) who c-bet the flop, my goal is to extract maximum value. Leading out (donk betting) on this scare card (which completes the obvious flush draw) would likely freeze the action and fold out bluffs or marginal hands like top pair (Ax) that lack a spade. By checking, I give the opponent a chance to continue bluffing or value betting hands like sets, two pair, or lower flushes (e.g., QsJ x). If he checks back, I can comfortably value bet the river. Checking is the optimal play to keep his range wide.

Turn brings the third spade (6s), completing flush draws. My 88 with no spade is now vulnerable. Pro has check-called preflop and flop - likely has an Ace or was on a flush draw that may have just hit. Betting here only gets called by better hands (Ax, flushes). Checking to control pot and potentially see a cheap showdown makes sense.

I hold the Ks9s on a board of 3d As 5s 6s 2d. With the Ace of spades on the board, my King of spades creates the Nut Flush (As Ks 9s 6s 5s), which is the absolute best possible hand. The opponent checked back the turn, which strongly suggests they do not hold a flush and are likely capping their range at Top Pair (Ax), Two Pair, or a Set. The river 2d puts a 4-card straight on the board (2-3-5-6), meaning any 4 makes a straight. Given the opponent's turn check, they are unlikely to value bet Top Pair if I check to them on this river texture. Therefore, I must bet to extract value. My sizing should be large enough to build the pot but small enough to entice a 'hero call' from a Top Pair hand that might suspect I'm bluffing or blocking with a weak pair. A bet of roughly half the pot (675 into 1350) balances getting value from Straights/Sets while remaining callable by strong Aces.

River brings 2d completing wheel straights (A4 has the nuts). Pro's line (check-call flop, check turn, lead river 1/3 pot) looks like thin value. Board has 3 spades and straight possibilities. My 88 with no spade is a weak bluff-catcher. Pro's small sizing suggests value more than bluff - wants a call. Getting 4:1 but I lose to flushes, straights (any 4), sets, two pair, and better pairs. Too many value hands in Pro's range here. Folding.

UTG in a 6-max game with K5d suited. This is a weak hand with marginal playability, poor equity multi-way, and difficult to play out of position. Standard play is to fold early position trash to preserve stack for premium spots. No limping as it invites multi-way pots OOP. Early in tournament (hand 3/150), patience is key to maximize stack long-term.
Q4o is a weak hand from middle position. Not playable - will be dominated by better queens and has poor postflop playability. Easy fold.

Unopened pot in the cutoff with A4 suited. This is a clear open-raise: we have strong playability, an ace blocker reduces the chance we run into premium aces, and we can profitably pressure the blinds in a 6-max lineup. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb sizing to build the pot with initiative while keeping risk controlled.

I am on the button with 8s 2s. While being in position is a significant advantage, 82s is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by a standard opening range from the cutoff. FiveTwo raised to 2.5x, and I don't have enough information on their play style yet to justify a speculative call or a 3-bet bluff. The most disciplined play here is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I hold 2d 5s in the Small Blind, facing a 2.5x raise from the Cutoff. This is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability and equity. Even though I am getting 3:1 pot odds, playing 25o out of position against a Cutoff open is significantly negative EV. The Big Blind is also yet to act. The clear and standard play is to fold.

Facing a cutoff open to 2.5x (250) from the big blind with 7♦2♣. To call is 150 into a 400 pot (≈27% pot odds). 7‑2 offsuit is the absolute bottom of the hand spectrum with very poor equity and postflop playability versus a CO opening range — I estimate its equity is well below the required ~27%. With deep stacks and early in the tournament there’s no reason to invest chips in such a dominated spot, and a 3‑bet bluff is unnecessary and unattractive here. Fold and wait for a better, more playable situation.
K9o from UTG is too weak to open in a 6-handed game. It's dominated by better kings and plays poorly out of position against the rest of the table. Standard fold.

UTG has folded and action is on me in MP. J6o is too weak and dominated to profitably open from this position in a 6-max configuration, and limping invites multiway pots where I realize equity poorly and get squeezed. With plenty of playable hands and deep stacks, I’ll pass and avoid marginal, dominated spots.

I am in the Cutoff position with Ah9c, which is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a 6-max game after the first two players folded. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop or put pressure on the blinds and button. A standard 2.5x big blind raise to 250 is appropriate to build the pot while maintaining a solid range.

I am on the Button with Q4o, facing a standard 2.5bb raise from the Cutoff (Flash). Q4o is a statistically weak hand with poor playability and significant reverse implied odds if I pair the Queen. It falls well outside standard calling or 3-betting ranges against a Cutoff open. With players still left to act in the blinds and no specific read suggesting Flash opens extremely wide or folds excessively to 3-bets, the correct strategic play is to fold.

I’m in the small blind with J♣2♣ facing a cutoff open to 250. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more (250 total) to continue. J2s is a very weak, often-dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position; calling requires ~33% equity (200 into a 600 pot) which I won’t reliably get versus a CO open. A light 3-bet to 400 as a bluff is also unattractive — I’d be OOP and will be behind vs most calling ranges. Small blind speculative calls with J2s are leak-prone early in the tourney, so I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Pocket eights is a strong pair, well ahead of the cutoff's wide raising range after multiple folds, especially with dead small blind money in the pot. Facing a min-raise (2.5BB), calling OOP risks playing post-flop out of position against a wide range. 3-betting takes the initiative, builds the pot for value, generates fold equity against marginal hands, and denies equity to Ax/draws. Sizing to 800 (3.2x their raise, ~2x pot after) is standard and balanced for deep stacks.

I opened from the Cutoff with Ah9c, a standard play, but I am now facing a 3-bet from Elon in the Big Blind to 800. While I have position on him for the rest of the hand, A9o is at the bottom of my opening range and is frequently dominated by his 3-betting range (AQ, AJ, AK, and middle-to-high pairs). Although the pot odds are roughly 2:1, calling with a dominated Ace often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I might lose a significant portion of my stack if an Ace hits. To preserve my stack early in this tournament and wait for a better spot, folding is the most disciplined and professional choice.

UTG in a 6-max game, Q5 offsuit is too weak to open profitably: it makes dominated top pairs (Qx) and has poor playability multiway versus blinds and late-position flats/3-bets. With no dead money beyond blinds, the best EV line is to fold and preserve stack for stronger opens.

I have pocket nines in middle position, which is a strong opening hand in 6-max play. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build a pot and exert pressure, while remaining cautious of potential 3-bets from players like Elon who showed aggression in the previous hand.

I hold KdQd (King-Queen suited) in the Cutoff. This is a premium broadway hand that plays very well post-flop. The action started with Flash in Middle Position raising to 300 (3BB). With 100BB effective stacks, KQs is strong enough to 3-bet, but flat calling is also a very standard and profitable line. Calling keeps the opener's range wider (including hands I dominate like KJ, QJ, or lower suited connectors) and allows me to utilize my position without bloating the pot immediately against a potentially strong MP opening range (which could include AK, AQ, AA-JJ that crush me). Given the early stage of the tournament, I prefer the lower variance route of flat calling to realize my equity in position.

I’m on the button with T8o facing a 3x open to 300 from middle and a call from the cutoff. Pot is 750 and it costs 300 to call (about 28.6% immediate equity required). T8o is a playable, positionally advantaged hand with decent equity and good postflop playability — especially against a single raiser and a caller. A min‑raise to 500 would have little fold equity and simply bloats a pot I don’t want to build out of position vs the blinds; 3‑betting thin here is unnecessary. I’ll call 300 to see a flop in position, keeping pot control and leveraging position to c‑bet or apply pressure only on favorable textures. If I face a large 3‑bet or heavy action before the flop I’ll fold; postflop I’ll proceed cautiously unless I connect with the board.

Preflop in SB with A5o facing a raise from MP (Flash) and calls from CO (Pro) and BTN (Minni), resulting in a multiway pot of 1050. Pot odds are about 4.2:1 (19% equity needed to call 250), but A5o plays poorly OOP multiway. Vulnerable to domination by better aces, behind pairs and suited connectors in calling ranges. No strong blockers or playability postflop justifies continuing. Folding preserves stack early in tournament (hand 5/150).
KTs is a solid hand in the BB with 3 players already in. Getting 5.25:1 pot odds on 200 to call. This hand plays well multiway with flush potential and strong top pair possibilities. Easy call.

Missed this flop completely with KTs on 6s Qh 6d. No pair, only backdoor draws. In a 4-way pot with a paired board, I have no reason to bet - K-high has almost no showdown value and bluffing into 3 opponents is -EV. Check and see what happens.

I have pocket nines on a paired board with one overcard (the Queen). This is a multi-way pot with three opponents. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, the presence of three callers increases the likelihood that someone connected with the Queen or potentially a Six. Checking here is a pot-control move that allows me to see the turn and gauge the strength of my opponents' hands based on their actions behind me.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a paired board (Q-6-6). The pot is 4-way, and the pre-flop raiser (Flash) has checked to me. This check often indicates weakness or pot control with a hand like a mid-pocket pair or missed high cards, though a trap is possible. Given the multi-way scenario, I should bet to extract value from worse Queens (QJ, QT) and pocket pairs (77-JJ) that might call one street. Checking back allows three opponents to realize equity for free, which is not ideal even on a relatively dry board. A bet of around 45% of the pot serves to deny equity and build a pot with what is likely the best hand.

I missed the flop entirely (T8o vs Qh 6s 6d). Pro's bet of 550 into the ~1250 pot demands roughly ~30% equity to continue, but I only have ~6 outs (~24% to pair by the river) and no real backdoor draws. This is a multiway spot and raising as a bluff is too risky against callers who can have Qx, a six, or any pair. My pre-flop plan was to proceed cautiously unless I connected — I didn't — so folding preserves chips for better opportunities rather than calling a marginal, likely -EV spot.

I have K-high on a paired board (6s Qh 6d). Pro's 550 bet into 1250 shows strength - likely Qx or better. I have no pair, no real draws (only extremely thin backdoor spade flush). Pot odds are ~3.3:1 but my equity is minimal with just K-high against a range that beats me. Easy fold.

I have pocket nines on a board of 6s-Qh-6d. After I checked the flop to control the pot, Pro bet 550 into three players. While the Queen is an overcard and the board is paired with sixes, a 550 bet into a 1200 pot is a relatively small sizing (less than half pot), which could easily be a positional stab from the Cutoff after the pre-flop aggressor (me) and the Big Blind checked. Folding pocket nines here would be too exploitable. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card and Pro's continued aggression, as I am out of position for the remainder of the hand.

I am out of position with pocket nines on a board that is paired (6-6) with a Queen overcard. After checking and calling a bet on the flop, I have signaled some strength but also a desire to control the pot. The turn 4d is a blank that shouldn't change the board texture significantly. Checking here is the most prudent play to see how Pro reacts. If Pro bets again, I will have to decide whether my hand is still ahead of his range, which includes potential semi-bluffs or positional stabs, versus hands like a Queen or a Six that have me beat.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a strong kicker and have picked up a flush draw on the turn (4d). The opponent, Flash, called my flop bet in a 4-way pot, which narrows his range to mostly Qx hands (QJ, QT, maybe AQ), pocket pairs (77-JJ), or potentially a slow-played 6. While betting for value is an option, checking back is superior for several reasons: 1) It controls the pot size with a one-pair hand on a paired board. 2) It eliminates the risk of being check-raised, which would put me in a tough spot despite my equity. 3) It disguises my hand strength, potentially inducing bluffs on the river or allowing me to get value from marginal hands that might fold to a second barrel now but call on the river. 4) I guarantee seeing the river card to potentially hit my flush or win at showdown.

The river 7d completes a potential diamond flush. After Pro checked back the turn, his range likely consists of medium-strength hands like a weak Queen, a smaller pair, or a draw that may have just hit. My pocket nines have solid showdown value, but the board is now quite dangerous with both a pair and a possible flush. Betting into Pro is unlikely to get many worse hands to call, while better hands will certainly call or raise. Checking is the best option to reach showdown cheaply and evaluate based on Pro's action.

I have improved to a King-high flush on the river. The board is paired (6s), making full houses a possibility, but the opponent's line (Check-Call Flop, Check Turn, Check River) heavily favors a one-pair hand like AQ, QJ, or a pocket pair rather than a full house or the nut flush (AdXd), which would likely show aggression. My check-back on the turn disguised my hand strength, making it look like I have a marginal made hand or gave up on a bluff. Therefore, I can comfortably value bet. A sizing of roughly 55% of the pot is large enough to extract meaningful value but small enough to get called by Top Pair (Qx) which might discount the flush possibility due to my turn check.

Pro's decision to check back the turn is a key indicator of his hand strength. A player with a Queen or a Six would typically bet the turn for value and to protect against the various draws available on the board. By checking, Pro's range is likely capped to marginal hands like middle pairs or missed draws. The 7d on the river completes a potential flush, making it a perfect card for a bluff. Given Pro's previous history of river aggression and the fact that my pocket nines are a strong bluff catcher in this scenario, I believe calling is the most profitable play to keep his range honest.

I am in the UTG position with Ten-Eight offsuit. This is a very weak hand to play from the earliest position at a 6-max table. Standard strategy dictates folding such hands to maintain stack integrity and wait for better spots where I have position or stronger card equity.

I hold Ad4d in Middle Position (Hijack) with 137 BB effective. In a 6-handed game, A4s is a standard opening hand due to its playability (nut flush potential, wheel straight potential) and blocker value. Given that UTG has folded, opening puts pressure on the remaining players. I will raise to 2.5x BB, which is a standard open size.

Pocket nines in the cutoff facing a middle‑position open to 250 is a standard 3‑bet spot. I want to isolate the opener, take the initiative, and charge weaker pairs, Axs and speculative hands from the button/blinds. A 3‑bet to 900 is large enough to fold out many speculative callers and give me good post‑flop leverage while leaving ~86–87bb behind to play. If I face a sizable 4‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate (fold to very large 4‑bets, consider calling or shove vs smaller 4‑bets); plan is to c‑bet favorable flops and control the pot on highly coordinated boards.

Qh4s is a trash hand: offsuit, poor high card value, no straight potential. Pre-flop on button facing MP raise to 250 and CO 3-bet to 900 (pot 1300, call 900). Minni's 3-bet range is strong (QQ+, AK, maybe some bluffs but rare early). Q4o equity vs tight 3-bet range is ~20-25%, requiring ~40% pot odds to call which we don't have. Even with position, better to fold and wait for premium hands to build stack. Pro and Minni both aggressive per history, but no showdown info favors playing speculative hands here.
K6o is too weak to call a 3-bet, especially out of position. Minni 3-bet to 900 over Pro's open, showing strength. K6o plays poorly multiway and heads-up against a 3-betting range. Easy fold.

Facing a raise and a sizable 3-bet from Minni, A4o plays poorly out of position: it’s often dominated when we make an ace, has weak playability, and we also risk Pro coming back over the top after we continue. The pot odds aren’t enough to justify taking a dominated, high reverse-implied-odds spot in a 3-bet pot. Not a good candidate for a cold 4-bet bluff either given positions and the likelihood of strong/continue ranges behind. Preserve stack and wait for better defend spots (suited Ax, pairs, better broadways).

I opened A4s from Middle Position and faced a 3-bet to 900 from the Cutoff (Minni). Although A4s is a decent opening hand and a candidate for a 4-bet bluff due to having an Ace blocker, playing it out of position against a 3-bet is generally -EV without specific reads on the opponent's aggressive tendencies. My equity against a standard 3-bet range (TT+, AQ+, AJs) is roughly 30-35%, and realizing that equity OOP is difficult. I am getting 2:1 pot odds, but the reverse implied odds (dominance issues with kickers) and positional disadvantage make folding the prudent, low-variance play early in the tournament.

I am in UTG (Under the Gun) with 6s8s. This is a suited one-gapper, which has some playability due to flush and straight potential, but it is generally too weak to open from early position in a 6-max game. With 5 players left to act behind me, the risk of running into a stronger hand or getting 3-bet is significant, and playing out of position with a marginal hand is difficult. Although I have a healthy stack (134 BBs), standard strategy dictates folding this hand from UTG to maintain a solid range.

I’m in middle position with K♦2♥ — a weak offsuit King with a terrible kicker. There are four players yet to act (cutoff, button, SB, BB), and several opponents have shown willingness to defend/3‑bet. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives me ~40% pot‑odds, but K2o is often dominated by better Ks and loses a lot of multiway equity — I don’t want to limp or call into a likely multiway pot out of position. A min‑raise to 200 is possible, but it’s thin with this hand and opens me up to 3‑bets or tough decisions postflop. With ~99BB effective and plenty of hands to wait for, the lowest‑variance +EV line is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Folded to me in cutoff with 57o offsuit, a speculative trash hand with poor playability, low equity, and easily dominated. Deep stacks early in tournament (hand 7/150), no strong steal incentive against competent blinds and button behind. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

On the button with T4o after three folds. While this is a weak hand, I have excellent position and only need to get through the blinds. This is a standard button steal opportunity. Raising to 2.5x to try to take down the blinds.

Claude is opening from the Button, so his range is wide. QJo is too strong to fold and plays awkwardly as a flat from the small blind (out of position, and it invites the BB to come along/squeeze). I prefer an aggressive 3-bet to isolate the Button, deny equity, and often win preflop. If Claude 4-bets, I’m generally folding; if he calls, we can proceed with a fairly standard c-bet strategy on good textures (high-card, disconnected boards) and slow down on unfavorable ones.

I am holding Ace-Seven suited in the Big Blind. The Button (Claude) opened for 2.5x, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 9x. While A7s has some value, cold-calling a 3-bet out of position against two players is generally a losing play, especially when my kicker is weak. The Small Blind's 3-bet range typically dominates A7s (containing hands like AJ+, 88+). Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack early in the tournament.
Facing a 3-bet to 900 with T4o. My button steal got reraised and I have a trash hand with no playability. This is a clear fold - T4o has no business continuing against a 3-bet.

UTG with 8♦5♦ — a weak, low, gap-suited hand with poor playability from early position. With ~100bb effective stacks and five players to act behind (some who have shown aggression in prior hands), this is not a hand to open-raise or even limp; it will often be dominated and has poor postflop prospects. Standard tight UTG strategy and pot preservation early in the tournament argue for folding. I fold preflop and wait for a better spot in position.

Preflop in middle position with 3h4d, an offsuit trash hand with no connectivity or high card value. No actions before me except UTG fold, pot is small at 150, but this hand has zero playability. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament against deep stacks.

J4o in the cutoff is a weak hand. While I'm in late position with only button and blinds to act, J4o is too weak to open-raise. My recent steal attempt in hand 7 got 3-bet, suggesting opponents may be willing to play back at me. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Folds to us on the Button with blinds 50/100, so this is a prime steal spot. 6d5h is not premium but has decent playability (connectivity/straight potential) and we realize equity well in position. Opening applies pressure to two ranges that are structurally weak (SB/BB) and lets us take initiative; if we face a sizable 3-bet we can comfortably fold this offsuit connector.

I am in the Small Blind with As 4s, which is a very strong hand for 3-betting or calling against a button open in a 6-max game. FiveTwo on the Button has shown aggression in previous hands, so his opening range is likely wide. By raising to 1000, I take control of the hand, potentially forcing a fold from both the Big Blind (Pro) and the Button, or at least narrowing the field while maintaining nut potential with my suited Ace.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo facing a Button open to 250 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 1000. While I have closed the action partially, QJo is a easily dominated hand that performs poorly against a 3-bet range (which likely includes AQ, AJ, KQ, JJ+). I would be calling 900 chips cold, often out of position relative to the original raiser, and sandwiching myself between two aggressors. The risk of being dominated or squeezed further is high, and my equity is likely quite low (around 30-35%). Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

We opened a marginal offsuit connector and now face a large SB 3-bet to 1000. Against a typical SB vs BTN 3-bet range (often value-heavy, especially with this sizing), 6d5h has poor playability (no suitedness, easily dominated, hard to realize equity even in position). Calling needs ~36% raw equity and we’ll frequently be put in tough spots on many flops. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here: we have no blockers, and with Flash’s ~64bb stack, a 4-bet risks getting jammed on and lighting chips on fire. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.

UTG position in a 6-handed game with 7h3s offsuit, one of the worst possible hands. No high card value, no straight or flush potential, dominated by any raising range. Players have shown aggression in previous hands, so folding avoids entering pots OOP with trash. Preserve stack for better spots.
87o in middle position is a weak hand. Not strong enough to open-raise profitably with 4 players behind me. Standard fold.

Td2d is too weak to open in a 6-handed CO against opponents who have shown willingness to 3-bet and apply pressure (Flash/Minni). Suited helps a bit, but it’s dominated and performs poorly versus 3-bets; limping is also unattractive because it invites an isolation raise and forces us to defend a weak hand out of position postflop. Preserve stack and wait for better steal candidates.

I am on the button with 3-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I have positional advantage, the hand has almost no potential to win at showdown and very poor playability if I am called. Given the current stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament, there is no need to take an unnecessary risk with a trash hand. I will fold and wait for a better opening.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Tens (Ts Tc). Action has folded to me. This is a very strong hand, particularly in a blind-versus-blind situation. Limping allows the Big Blind to realize equity for free, which is not ideal. I should raise to build the pot and define ranges. A standard raise size of 3x (300 chips) is appropriate to charge the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

I have A♠K♦ in the big blind facing a lone open to 300 from the small blind. This is a standard 3-bet spot: AKo is a premium hand that benefits from taking the initiative, building the pot, and folding out weaker broadways and small pairs. Opponent's open to 300 looks like a wide opening range from the SB, and stacks are deep (~98 BB effective), so I size to about 3x the open to get value and fold equity while leaving room to maneuver postflop. Raising to 900 (total) pressures the opener without overcommitting; if I face a 4-bet I can reassess based on size, but passively calling 200 would concede the initiative and allow a wide range to see a cheap flop in position. Therefore I 3-bet to 900.

I am facing a 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind after opening to 300 from the Small Blind. This is a Blind-vs-Blind scenario where ranges are generally wider. Minni has shown aggression previously (Hand #6). Pocket Tens is a very strong hand in this configuration, but 4-betting risks isolating myself against a range that crushes me (JJ+, QQ, KK, AA) or flipping against AK, while folding out the bluffs or weaker hands I dominate. Although I will be out of position post-flop, calling is the prudent play to keep the opponent's range wide and manage the pot size. I need to call 600 more to match the 900 total.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with second pair (Tens) on a monotone heart board (Kh 5h 3h). The King hits the 3-bettor's range (AK, KQ, KK) hard, and the monotone texture favors their range if they have the Ace of hearts or a made flush. Leading out would be a mistake as I likely only get action from better hands. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate their action.

I have A♠K♦ on a 3♥ K♥ 5♥ flop — top pair with the best kicker in a 3‑bet pot. As the preflop 3‑bettor I should continue to take the initiative: my opponent’s calling range contains a lot of Kx and medium pairs (and a few two‑heart combos that can make a flush), so a continuation bet gets value and charges draws. I size to about half pot (900) to extract value from worse kings/pairs, force equity out of drawing hands, and keep the pot controllable with deep stacks; if he check‑raises I’ll reassess for sets or a made flush and proceed cautiously.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with Pocket Tens on a board of King-Five-Three, all hearts. My opponent (Minni) c-bets half pot (900 into 1800). The board texture heavily favors the 3-bettor's range, which includes sets (KK), top pairs (AK, KQ), overpairs (AA, QQ, JJ), and strong flushes or flush draws (AhX). I hold no hearts, meaning I have no redraws to a flush and do not block any of their value flush combos. My hand is effectively a bluff-catcher that is currently behind a significant portion of their value range (any King, AA, QQ, JJ) and vulnerable to their semi-bluffs (AhX). Continuing here out of position with just second pair and no backup equity is a losing play. Folding is the disciplined decision.

JTo in UTG at 6-max. Decent holding but not premium. Standard open to 2.5x is appropriate here - hand plays well postflop with straight potential. Not strong enough to limp/trap but too good to fold at 6-max.

Claude’s 2.5x UTG open in 6-max is likely not ultra-tight given prior aggression, and 7h5h plays well enough with deep stacks due to suited/straight potential and strong implied odds when we connect hard. Calling keeps his range wide and avoids turning a marginal hand into a low-quality 3-bet bluff with poor blockers. I’ll proceed with a flat and play fit-or-fold postflop; if we face a sizeable squeeze from CO/BTN/blinds, we can comfortably fold.

Holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Cutoff, I am facing a raise from Claude (UTG) and a call from FiveTwo (MP). Against an early position open and a caller, ATo is a marginal hand that often find itself dominated by better Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) or pairs. Playing this hand multi-way increases the risk of being outkicked on Ace-high boards or facing difficult decisions out of position if the button or blinds squeeze. Given the stack depths and the action ahead, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

I am on the Button with Ac5c, a strong suited ace that plays well in position, particularly in multi-way pots due to its nut flush and wheel straight potential. UTG (Claude) opened to 2.5BB and Middle Position (FiveTwo) flat called. A squeeze (3-bet) here is risky against a UTG opening range which is typically strong. Calling allows me to realize my equity cheaply, keeps the pot manageable, and leverages my positional advantage. I am getting good direct pot odds (calling 250 into a pot that will be at least 900) and have excellent implied odds if I hit my draw.

I’m in the small blind with K♥3♥ facing an UTG open to 250 that’s already been called twice (pot 900, I must call 200). This is a marginal, easily dominated king with a weak kicker and I’ll be out of position against the raiser and likely multiple callers. Calling 200 to play a multiway pot OOP gives poor equity and large reverse-implied-odds; raising to isolate is risky and vulnerable to reraises. With deep stacks and plenty of better spots to invest chips, the prudent play is to fold and conserve my stack.

65o is a marginal speculative hand at best. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has shown strength in prior hands (e.g., Hand 2), and two callers (FiveTwo and Pro), the pot is multiway. While pot odds are favorable (150 into 900, ~7:1), 65 offsuit has poor playability multiway postflop—low equity against strong ranges, prone to domination, and limited straight potential without suitedness. Deep stacks early in tournament (Hand 10/150); better to preserve stack and wait for stronger spots rather than realize equity poorly in a bloated pot out of position preflop (though BB has postflop position). Folding maximizes EV long-term.

Flopped two pair (Jacks and Tens) on Th-Ad-Jc board. Excellent flop for my hand. Board is coordinated with straight possibilities (KQ has nuts), so I need to bet for value and protection. Two pair is strong but vulnerable to draws. Standard c-bet around 1/2-2/3 pot to get value from Ax, Jx, Tx hands and charge draws.

On A♦ J♣ T♥ versus a ~60% pot c-bet in a 3-way pot, 7♥5♥ has essentially no equity: no pair, no straight draw, and only a backdoor flush (needs two hearts). With Pro still to act behind, floating is high-risk/low-reward and a bluff-raise is unattractive since we block none of Claude’s strong continues (Ax/Jx/Tx) and have poor barreling prospects. Preserve stack and fold.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (A5s) on a wet board (Th Ad Jc). The UTG opener (Claude) c-bets 550 into 900, a sizable bet into two opponents, indicating strength or a semi-bluff with broadway draws (like KQ). While my kicker is weak and I am likely dominated by his value range (AK, AQ, AJ, AT, Sets), I am in position and getting decent odds (27.5%) to call. I also have a backdoor flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (though a King makes a 4-card straight on board). Folding Top Pair immediately to a single bet in position is too tight, but raising would only isolate me against better hands. I will call to re-evaluate on the turn; if he checks, I can check back, and if he barrels big again, I can release.

I have two pair (Jacks and Tens) on Th-Ad-Jc-3c. Turn 3c is a brick that adds a backdoor club draw. My hand is still very strong. Pro called my flop bet which could be Ax, a draw (KQ), or Jx/Tx. I need to continue betting for value and protection. Pot is 2000, a bet around 2/3 pot (~1300) will extract value from worse made hands and charge draws.

The turn brings the 3c, giving me the Nut Flush Draw to accompany my Top Pair (though with a weak kicker). The UTG player fires a second barrel of 1300 into a pot of 2000. This line represents significant strength, likely a straight (KQ), sets, or strong two-pair/top-pair hands (AK/AQ). Against this range, my Top Pair is likely no good, but my flush draw gives me about 18-20% equity. Pot odds are roughly 28% (1300 to call into 4600 total). While direct odds are slightly insufficient, the implied odds are excellent. If I hit the club on the river, I hold the nuts and can likely stack the villain who has ~4600 behind. Position allows me to see the river and decide. Raising achieves nothing but isolation against better hands. I call.

River Qd is a terrible card for my two pair. The board now shows Th-Ad-Jc-3c-Qd, meaning any King makes the nut Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T). Pro called my preflop raise, flop bet, and turn bet - hands like KQ (OESD on flop), AK, KJ are all in their range and all now beat me. Betting here is terrible as I only get called by hands that beat me. Must check and re-evaluate if Pro bets.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker (5) on a very coordinated board of [Th Ad Jc 3c Qd]. The board shows four cards to a Broadway straight (A-Q-J-T), meaning any King holds the nuts. Villain (UTG) showed significant strength by double-barreling the flop and turn but checked the river. His range includes sets, two pair, and strong Aces (AK/AQ), and potentially straights (KQ/KJ). My hand has some showdown value against missed bluffs, but I am dominated by almost all of his value range. Betting is unlikely to fold out better hands like sets or two-pair often enough to be profitable, and I risk getting check-raised or called by a King. Checking back is the prudent play to realize whatever showdown value I have without committing more chips in a spot where I am likely behind.

99 is strong enough to open UTG in 6-max and plays best with initiative. Limping invites multiway pots and makes it harder to realize equity; raising also lets us represent strong overpairs/AK on many boards. I’ll use a standard 2.5x open consistent with prior sizing, building the pot for value while keeping risk controlled this deep.

I am holding pocket Queens, a premium hand. FiveTwo opened from under-the-gun for 2.5x the big blind. I should 3-bet for value and to isolate the original raiser, preventing the button or blinds from entering the pot with speculative hands. A 3-bet to 850 is a standard sizing that builds the pot and exerts pressure.

I hold 3h 4c, a very weak off-suit connector. Facing an UTG raise and a subsequent 3-bet from Middle Position, my hand is dominated by virtually all opening and 3-betting ranges. The pot odds are insufficient to set-mine or speculate, and there are players left to act behind me. Folding is the only correct play.

Fold. I’m on the button with Th2c — a very weak, easily-dominated hand. The action shows an UTG open to 250 and a strong-looking MP 3-bet to 850. Pot is 1,250 and calling 850 is a large chunk of my 10,800 stack for very poor equity and implied odds against these ranges. There are still the SB and BB to act who can call or 4-bet, increasing variance and the chance I face larger commitment with a junk hand. A 4-bet bluff is possible but not attractive here (I have poor blockers and the 3-bet sizing is consistent with value). Best play is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot to use my button position. Therefore I fold.

Preflop in the small blind with 7c9d offsuit, a weak speculative hand. Facing a raise from UTG (FiveTwo) to 250 and a 3-bet from middle position (Flash) to 850, indicating strong ranges from both. Pro and Minni already folded. Pot is 1250, calling 800 more gives poor implied odds and direct pot odds (~2.3:1), but my hand is dominated and plays poorly postflop out of position. No value in calling, raising, or shoving with this trash hand against tight opening and 3-betting ranges. Standard fold to preserve stack.
58o is a very weak hand facing a 3-bet. Even with pot odds of ~1.67:1, this hand has terrible equity against Flash's 3-betting range. Clear fold.

Facing Flash’s 3-bet to 850 after our UTG open, 99 is a bit too strong to fold outright, especially given Flash has shown willingness to apply preflop pressure (notably the 3-bet in Hand #8) and may be wider than a pure value range here. 4-betting would mostly turn 99 into a bluff and risks getting jammed on by overpairs/AK, putting us in a tough spot. Calling keeps in his bluffs and worse pairs, and with an SPR around ~3 postflop we can play straightforwardly: look to realize equity/set-mine on favorable textures and be disciplined on high-card boards when he barrels.

We’re the preflop caller in a 3-bet pot and out of position with a medium-strength underpair (99) on a paired board (J66). This is not a great texture to lead into the 3-bettor—Flash retains a strong range advantage with overpairs and Jx, and our hand wants pot control while still allowing him to c-bet his AK/AQ-type hands. Checking keeps our range protected (we can still have 6x/sets sometimes) and lets us respond based on sizing rather than inflating the pot ourselves.

I hold pocket Queens on a Jack-high paired board (Js-6d-6s), giving me an overpair. FiveTwo called my pre-flop 3-bet out of position and has checked to me on the flop. This board texture is generally favorable for my range as the 3-bettor. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 43% of the pot to extract value from Jack-x hands (like AJ or KJ) and smaller pocket pairs (77-TT) that might call one street, while also protecting my hand against potential overcards on the turn.

In a 3-bet pot OOP on J66, 99 is a medium-strength bluff-catcher. Flash’s small c-bet size is consistent with a wide range that includes AK/AQ and some lower pairs that we beat, not just overpairs/Jx. We’re getting good immediate price (800 into 2650) and calling keeps his bluffs in while avoiding turning 99 into a risky bluff-raise at an SPR where he can shove and put us in a miserable spot. Plan: call once, then proceed cautiously—fold to heavy barreling on A/K/Q/T turns, continue on bricks/smaller sizing, and of course stack off if we spike a 9.

Turn brings the Tc, which is a fairly strong barreling card for the 3-bettor (adds overpairs/Tx/Jx interaction and gives him more credible pressure). With 99 we’re still mainly a bluff-catcher versus AK/AQ-type hands and possibly lower pairs, and we don’t want to bloat the pot OOP by leading into a range-advantaged opponent. Checking keeps our range protected (we can still have Jx/6x occasionally), allows Flash to continue bluffing, and lets us respond based on his sizing rather than committing chips unnecessarily.

I hold pocket Queens on a board of Js-6d-6s-Tc. My overpair remains very strong. FiveTwo has checked to me again after calling my flop continuation bet. While the Ten of Clubs is a somewhat coordinated card, it's unlikely to have helped FiveTwo's range unless they are holding exactly Ten-Ten (giving them a full house) or Jack-Ten (giving them a lesser two-pair). I am still ahead of Jack-x hands like AJ and KJ, which constitute a significant portion of FiveTwo's calling range. I will bet 1600 to extract value from those Jacks and to charge any straight draws like 98 or KQ that might be lingering. This sizing also sets up a potential shove on the river while leaving me room to navigate if the river is an Ace or King.

We’re in a 3-bet pot OOP with 99 on a J66T board versus an aggressive opponent who can plausibly double-barrel a lot of unpaired broadways (AK/AQ/KQ) and some backdoor spade/overcard pressure hands, not just pure value. His 1600 barrel into 5050 gives us solid pot odds (~24% needed), and folding turns our hand into an over-fold against a range that still contains many misses. Raising would be a bad bluff (we don’t block his value and get jammed on a lot), so the best line is to call and re-evaluate river: continue mostly versus small/medium bets on brick rivers, but be ready to fold to heavy river pressure on cards that heavily favor his value range (A/K/Q/spade) unless we improve to a full house with a 9.

River A♦ is a major range-shift in Flash’s favor in a 3-bet pot: a big chunk of his double-barrel bluffs (AK/AQ) just improved to top pair, and KQ gets there to Broadway. Our 99 has essentially no thin value and turning it into a block-bet/bluff just folds out the hands we beat while getting called/raised by all Ax/Jx/overpairs/straights. Best is to check and realize our showdown value versus his give-ups (smaller pairs/missed hands), and be prepared to fold if he applies meaningful river pressure.

The Ace of Diamonds on the river is a very poor card for my pocket Queens. While I have held the lead throughout the hand, the Ace now completes potential holdings in FiveTwo's range such as AJ, which would have called pre-flop, flop, and turn. Additionally, the Ace might scare off any Jack-X hands that I was previously extracting value from, meaning a bet is unlikely to be called by a worse hand. FiveTwo's check on the river indicates they are either giving up or planning to check-call with a hand like a Jack or an Ace. Given the pot size and the board texture, checking back is the safest play to realize my showdown value without risking a check-raise or losing more chips to a hand that improved on the river.

I am dealt pocket Kings (Kc Kh) in the UTG position. This is the second-best starting hand in Hold'em and requires a strong opening raise to build value and reduce the number of opponents in the pot. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is a standard and effective opening size given the table dynamics and my stack size.

I hold 5s 7d in Middle Position, facing a raise to 300 from Flash in UTG. This is an extremely weak, unsuited hand that falls well outside of a defensible range against an early position open. I have no equity to call and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary and risky here. The clear choice is to fold.

Call 300. A♥3♥ is a hand I want to see a flop with from the cutoff against an early-position 3x open: it has good playability (nut‑ace blocker + suitedness) and I will have positional advantage on the opener post‑flop. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity versus the blinds, and lets me realize equity without bloating the pot with a marginal 3‑bet that often runs into stronger UTG ranges. Plan: if I pair the Ace or pick up a strong heart draw I’ll continue aggressively; if I miss the flop I’ll use position to control pot size and fold to heavy aggression. If a big squeeze/4‑bet comes preflop I’ll let it go — the 300 call is a small portion of my 10,800 stack and an acceptable price to see a favorable flop.

2h5c is one of the worst possible hands offsuit, disconnected low cards with minimal straight potential and poor playability. Facing an UTG raise from Flash (typically strong range) and a call from Minni in cutoff, plus SB and BB yet to act. Pot odds to call 300 into 1050 (post-call pot) are about 3:1 requiring ~23% equity, which this hand lacks even multiway. Button position doesn't justify entering with trash; folding preserves stack for better spots in deepstack tournament.

J2o is a weak hand, especially out of position against a raiser and caller. Even with decent pot odds (250 to win 750), being OOP with a dominated jack and weak kicker makes this an easy fold. FiveTwo could also squeeze behind me.

We’re getting a very good price in the big blind (200 to win 750 now), and 87o has decent connectivity to realize equity multiway when we flop pairs+draws/straights. Versus an UTG open and a cold-call the ranges are stronger, so we don’t want to 3-bet bluff or inflate the pot out of position; just defend and see a flop with the odds.

We’re 3‑way out of position versus the UTG raiser and a cold-caller. 8♦7♥ gives us top pair but with a weak kicker and only a small backdoor flush component, so building a big pot by donk-betting is unnecessary and risks getting raised/put in tough spots by stronger 8x/overpairs. Best line is to check, keep ranges wide, and mostly continue versus a reasonable c-bet while controlling pot size multiway.

I have pocket Kings on a low board (3d-5h-8h), giving me a strong overpair. While the board is relatively safe, there are potential heart flush draws and some straight draws (like 46 or 67). FiveTwo has checked to me. I need to bet for value against middle pairs, smaller overpairs, and draws, while also protecting my hand. A bet of 600 into a pot of 950 is a standard continuation bet size (roughly 63% pot) that achieves these goals.

I have A♥3♥ on 3♦-5♥-8♥: top pair with the nut heart draw — one of the strongest flop combinations. Flash is an UTG opener who c-bet; calling keeps weaker hands and draws in, but raising here both extracts value from worse pairs/draws and protects my equity by charging chasing hands. I size the raise to 1,800 total (about 3x the bet) — big enough to charge heart and straight draws and to deny free cards, but not so large that I commit my stack unnecessarily. Plan: if called I’ll proceed aggressively on a heart or A turn and be willing to call reasonable resistance with these combined made+draw equities; if I face an unusually large 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate but I won’t fold lightly given the nut-flush outs and top pair.

Multiway on a wet 3d-5h-8h flop, our 8♦7♥ is top pair but a weak kicker with no strong draw (only backdoor hearts/straight possibilities). Minni’s cold-call preflop then large raise over the UTG c-bet is heavily weighted to strong value (sets/two pair/strong 8x) and strong combo-draws, and UTG still has the option to come back over the top. Calling 1800 out of position would bloat the pot and often leave us in a near-commitment spot on turns where we’re frequently dominated. Best for stack preservation is to let this go.

I have pocket Kings, an overpair to the board of 3d-5h-8h. I also hold the King of hearts, which blocks some potential heart flush draws. Minni's raise on the flop to 1800 is strong, representing a range that includes sets (88, 55, 33), straight draws (76s, 64s), flush draws, and perhaps smaller overpairs like 99, TT, or JJ. Folding is too weak given the strength of my hand. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (sets) and might push out the semi-bluffs or weaker value hands I want to keep in. Calling is the most balanced play, allowing me to control the size of the pot and see a turn card while keeping Minni's range wide.

The Ace on the turn is a very unfavorable card for my pocket Kings, especially after being raised by Minni on the flop. Her flop-raising range includes sets (88, 55, 33), straight draws, and flush draws. Crucially, any Ace-high flush draws she might have been semi-bluffing with (like Ah Qh or Ah Jh) have now paired the Ace to become top pair. By checking, I am exercising pot control and avoiding building a larger pot while potentially being behind. It also allows me to see how Minni reacts to the Ace; if she checks back, I might still have the best hand against her non-Ace draws or lower pairs, but if she bets, I have a difficult decision to make given the strength she showed on the flop.

Board: 3♦-5♥-8♥-A♠. I hold A♥3♥ — two pair (Aces and Threes) plus the nut heart draw (four hearts to the board). This is a very strong hand that is ahead of almost all hands that called my flop raise (weaker pairs, A‑x, and heart/straight draws) and only behind sets or rare two‑pair combos (e.g. A8). Flash checked the turn after calling my flop raise, so I should take the initiative to get value and deny free cards to draws. I size to ~2/3 pot (3,000): large enough to charge heart/straight draws and extract value from one‑pair hands and worse A‑x, but not so large that I immediately commit my entire stack to a single raise. If I get check‑raised big I will re‑evaluate (I can call reasonable sizing with two pair + nut draw; I will be cautious vs an overbet shove and fold only if the line and sizing strongly indicate an unlikely dominated/locked hand).

The Ace of Spades on the turn is an extremely dangerous card for my pocket Kings. Minni's aggressive raise on the flop (1800 into 950) already suggested a range of sets (88, 55, 33) or high-equity draws like AhQh or AhJh. If she had a set, I am currently drawing to only two outs. If she was semi-bluffing with an Ace-high flush draw, she has now improved to top pair with a top kicker, overtaking my Kings. Her 3000 chip bet on the turn represents a huge portion of the pot and signals extreme strength. Given that the Ace hits a significant part of her flop-raising range and I am now losing to any Ace or set, the most disciplined professional play is to fold and preserve my remaining stack.

I hold ThJh (Jack-Ten suited) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position at a 6-handed table. While JTs is often a standard opening raise in 6-max games due to its post-flop playability, it sits at the bottom of the UTG opening range. I must adjust to the table dynamics: Minni, seated directly to my left in Middle Position, has shown significant aggression, specifically 3-betting my opening raises in Hand #6 and Hand #9. Opening a speculative hand like JTs out of position into an aggressive player risks losing chips to a 3-bet or playing a difficult bloated pot out of position. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot or better position.

Holding 7♥4♠ is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand with poor playability. I'm in middle position with four players still to act (including aggressive players behind like Flash and FiveTwo), so limp-calling 100 into a 150 pot invites raises and multiway action where my hand has negligible equity. Stack is deep enough to wait for a better spot — folding preserves chips and avoids playing a marginal hand out of position. I will fold.

Preflop in cutoff position, action folded to me (UTG Pro and MP Minni folded). Holding J4o, an offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low playability, and dominated by any calling range containing better jacks or pairs. Not in standard late-position opening range (typically suited aces, broadway, connectors, but not weak offsuit like this). Players appear aggressive and call frequently based on history, reducing steal equity. Deep stacks (10k effective), early in tournament (hand 13/150), no need to risk with marginal hand. Standard fold to wait for better opportunities.
On the button with Q8o, folded to me. This is a standard button steal spot - Q8o plays well enough to open here with position. Raising to 250 (2.5x) to put pressure on the blinds.

Claude is opening the button to 2.5bb and we’re in the small blind out of position with 6-3 offsuit. This hand has poor playability, no relevant blockers, and we still have the big blind behind who can squeeze, making a defend even worse. With 60bb effective, there’s no need to take a low-EV spot—just release and preserve stack for better steal/3-bet candidates.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 6-max format. The button (Claude) has raised to 250. Against a button open, AQo is a clear 3-bet candidate for value and to seize the initiative, as playing out of position is easier as the aggressor. A standard 3-bet size from the blinds is roughly 3.5x to 4x the initial raise. I will raise to 900 to put pressure on Claude's wide button opening range and build a pot with my high equity.

Flash 3-bet me to 900 from the BB. Q8o is a decent button open but doesn't play well against a 3-bet - it's offsuit, lacks strong equity, and won't have good playability postflop. Not worth calling 650 into 1200 with this hand, and definitely not strong enough to 4-bet. Standard fold.

6♣3♥ offsuit is a very weak hand (bottom range). I'm UTG in a 6-handed game — early position — so I should play very tight. Calling 100 to see a flop out of position against multiple opponents would be speculative and likely costly; raising would be a pure bluff and ill-advised here. With a healthy stack, there's no need to gamble; I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop in middle position with 6s Jd offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor high-card strength and limited playability. Only UTG (Minni) has folded, but no value to raising or limping into a multiway pot. Standard play is to fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Deep stacks (101 BB) allow for patience over 150 hands.

K4o in cutoff with 2 folds ahead. This is a marginal stealing hand, but in late position with only 3 players left to act, it's a reasonable spot to open. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to steal the blinds. If I face a 3-bet, I can easily fold.

CO open to 2.5bb is a fairly strong range. T2s is dominated by most suited tens and broadways, has poor playability, and doesn’t make strong top pairs. Calling 2.5bb even in position will realize equity poorly and invites tough postflop spots; 3-bet bluffing is also bad since we block nothing relevant and have terrible coverage when called/4-bet. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better BTN opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Claude has opened from the Cutoff to 250. While an Ace has some raw value, A2o is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a standard late-position open. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against Claude's range and also leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from Pro in the Big Blind. Given the lack of playability and the positional disadvantage, the most profitable long-term play here is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 2s, facing a raise to 250 from the Cutoff (Claude). The pot is 400, and I need to call 150, offering me pot odds of roughly 27%. However, 5-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker with very poor playability and equity, even against a wide Cutoff range. Being out of position with such a trash hand makes realizing equity extremely difficult. Defending here would likely result in a long-term loss. The standard and correct play is to fold.

UTG with Tc5c is a standard fold. T5 suited has poor playability post-flop, low equity against typical calling ranges, and is easily dominated by better suited connectors or broadways. No position advantage, deep stacks favor playing premium hands early. Preserve stack for better opportunities in a tournament setting.

Pocket 8s in middle position is a solid hand. With only Elon folding before me, I should open raise to build the pot and thin the field. Standard 2.5x open to 250 is consistent with my previous raises.

Claude opens to 2.5bb from middle position. With 5♠3♣ offsuit in the cutoff, we’re too weak to call profitably (poor playability, dominated, and little ability to realize equity) and it’s not a good 3-bet candidate given lack of blockers and connectivity. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Q8o. Claude has raised to 250 from middle position. In previous hands, Claude has shown a willingness to open for 250 and fold to 3-bets (Hand #7, Hand #13), but has also shown aggression in other spots. Q8o is a relatively weak hand to call a 2.5x open with, especially when I have tight/aggressive players behind me in the blinds (Pro and Minni). Calling here leaves me playing a mediocre hand in position but against a range that likely dominates me. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack of 9275 chips and wait for a better opening.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 7d, an offsuit connector. Facing a raise to 250 from Claude in Middle Position, I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 400), but I would be playing a weak hand out of position for the rest of the hand against a range that likely dominates me. Additionally, the Big Blind is still left to act behind me, which creates the risk of a squeeze play or multi-way pot where I have the worst position. 67o does not have enough playability or equity to justify a call here, and it is not a strong candidate for a 3-bet bluff. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Facing a standard MP open to 2.5x (250) from middle position and I'm in the big blind with J♥8♣. This is a marginal but playable hand — too weak to 3-bet for value and a small 3-bet would have poor fold equity out of position, but it's worth defending given deep stacks (≈131 BB) and the small cost to see a flop (calling 150 more). I will call to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and proceed cautiously post‑flop (look to continue with pairs or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression with air).

I have top pair (J♥) with a weak 8 kicker on A♠ J♣ 7♠ against the MP opener. This is a vulnerable but valuable hand — good for showdown value vs worse Jx and some bluffs, but dominated by Ax and beaten by better Jx. I'm out of position and stacks are deep, so I don't want to inflate the pot OOP without more information. Checking controls the size of the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets the opener bluff or show strength first. Plan: check and call a small-to-medium c-bet (rough guide ≤ ~1/2 pot ≈ 275) to extract value; fold to very large bets or a check-raise that represents an Ace or sets. If checked to me on the turn I'll re-evaluate and look to extract thin value on safe runouts or fold to heavy aggression.

I have pocket 8s on a Jc 7s As board - an underpair to both the Ace and Jack. Minni called preflop and checked to me. This is a classic pot control spot. Betting doesn't accomplish much - better hands call (Ax, Jx), and worse hands fold. With two spades on board creating flush draw possibilities, I'd rather check back and keep the pot small with my medium-strength showdown value hand.

I have top pair (J♥) with a weak 8 kicker on J♣ 7♠ A♠ 3♦, out of position vs Claude (MP opener). He checked both the flop and turn, so his range contains Ax, some broadways, weaker Jx, and medium pairs/floats. Checking keeps the pot small but hands like QJ/KJ and some worse Jx will check behind — betting a small, value‑oriented size extracts thin value, denies free cards, and keeps the pot manageable OOP. I’ll bet about half the pot (~275 into 550) to target worse jacks and floating hands; if he raises strongly I will fold (my kicker is weak), if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously (check‑call small bets, fold to big aggression). Total committed = previous 250 + bet 275 = 525.

Minni check-called preflop, checked the flop, and now leads out on the turn for ~half pot. This betting pattern often indicates a hand that's now betting for value - likely Ax, Jx, or possibly a flush draw semi-bluff. My pocket 8s are an underpair to both the Ace and Jack. I'm behind Ax, Jx, sets, and even if I call, I face a difficult river decision. Getting 2:1 isn't enough when I'm likely dominated by most of Minni's value range. Folding is the disciplined play here.
Q9o from UTG is too weak to open. This hand plays poorly out of position and is dominated by many hands that would call or 3-bet. Standard fold from this position.

UTG has folded and action is on us in middle position facing only the big blind (to call 100). 6c4c is a low suited connector/gapper that plays best in late position or in situations where we can realize equity cheaply. From MP, opening it is too loose because we’ll often face squeezes/3-bets from CO/BTN and end up folding or playing a dominated, low-equity pot out of position against the blinds. Limping is also unattractive because it invites an isolation raise and we don’t have a hand that performs well versus strong iso ranges. Preserving stack and waiting for better MP opens is higher EV here.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Deuce suited (As2s). The action has folded to me. This is a standard opening hand in late position to steal the blinds or build a pot with a hand that has nut-flush potential. A raise to 2.5x the big blind is consistent with the standard opening sizes observed at this table.

I hold 5s Jc on the Button, facing a raise to 250 from Flash in the Cutoff. J5 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and is dominated by the opener's range. Although I have position, the hand strength does not justify a call or a 3-bet bluff, especially this early in the tournament (Hand 16/150). The correct and low-variance play is to fold.

Cutoff opened to 250. I'm in the small blind with A♥9♠ and a deep stack (13,500 ~135bb). A9o is worth defending against a CO open but it's not strong enough to 3-bet out-of-position — raising here bloats the pot OOP and invites difficult decisions. The raise size is small, so calling 200 more (250 total committed) is the best line: it keeps CO's range wide, gives me a chance to realize equity and play postflop for a reasonable price. If the big blind 3-bets large I'll fold; if we see a flop I'll look to continue when I hit an Ace or favorable texture, otherwise proceed cautiously.

J7o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability multiway. Facing a cutoff raise from Flash (who has shown aggression in prior hands) and a small blind call from Minni, the pot odds of calling 150 into 600 are decent (~4:1), but my hand lacks equity against likely calling ranges. It blocks few strong hands but is often dominated by better jacks or any pair/Ax. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this early tournament stage with 6 players remaining and deep stacks.

Check. I have A♣9♠ (A♥9♠) on J♠ T♠ 8♥ — only Ace-high, a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs, ≈16% to hit by the river) and a very small two-card backdoor spade possibility (~4% to make by river). Flash opened from the cutoff and I just defended from the small blind; I’m out of position and stacks are deep (I ~13,300, villain ~9,025) so I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand that’s currently behind a lot of the CO’s range (Jx, Tx, broadways, spade combos). Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me realise equity and see how the opponent reacts. Plan: check and call a small c-bet to chase my draws with correct pot odds, fold to a large raise, and if checked to me see the turn for an Ace/9/7 pickup or better spot to take the lead next street.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding the nut flush draw with the Ace of Spades. The board of 8h-Js-Ts is very coordinated, offering many straight possibilities, but it also provides me with the nut spade draw. Minni checked to me, which is a standard play for the caller. I will place a continuation bet of approximately two-thirds of the pot to build the value for my draw, exert pressure on any marginal hands Minni might be holding, and potentially take the pot down right now.

I have A♥9♠ on J♠ T♠ 8♥ — currently just Ace‑high with a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs, ≈16% to hit by the river), a small backdoor spade route (~4% by river) and ≈18% to pair my Ace by the river. Flash’s 400 into the 1,000 pot is a modest c‑bet (~40% pot), so the 400 call gives me ~28.6% immediate pot odds. Versus a wide CO continuation range (Jx/Tx, broadways, spade combos) my combined equity is borderline but roughly in line with the price. I’m out of position and deep-stacked, so I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with only Ace‑high — raising would be a mistake. I’ll call the 400 to realize equity, fold to a sizable raise, and then re-evaluate on the turn: continue for value/raise if I pair an Ace/9 or complete the straight/flush, otherwise proceed cautiously or fold to further aggression.

I paired the Ace on the turn, giving me top pair (A9) but with a mediocre kicker. Villain led the flop and then checked the turn, which often indicates a range of Jx/Tx, weaker A-x, missed spade draws, or pot‑control with stronger hands. I want to extract value from worse A-x and one‑pair hands while keeping the pot manageable out of position and denying equity to remaining draws. A modest bet of about 40–45% pot is the right compromise: it gets called by many worse hands and avoids bloating the pot where my kicker becomes a liability. If Flash check‑raises strongly I’ll respect that as significant strength and fold; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river for further value or protection.

The Turn Ace provides me with top pair, although with a very weak kicker, while I maintain the nut flush draw. Minni's lead of 600 into a 1400 pot after the Ace hits is a classic 'donk bet' which could signal an Ace, a made straight (like KQ), or a defensive blocking bet with a hand like JT or a draw. I have excellent pot odds to call (600 to win 2000) and significant equity with the nut flush draw plus my pair. Calling is better than raising here as it allows me to realize my equity in position and keep the pot size manageable in case she has a stronger Ace or a straight, while still giving me the chance to win a large pot if I hit my flush on the river.

I have A♥9♠ on J♠ T♠ 8♥ A♣ 6♦ — top pair with a mediocre 9 kicker. Villain (CO/Flash) opened pre, c‑bet the flop (400), I called, then led the turn with a modest bet (I raised to 600) and he called. That line is wide: Jx/TX, worse A‑x, some 9x/Q9/straight combos and missed draws. The river 6d is a largely “blank” — it helps only a few combos (pocket 6s, and some backdoor two‑pairs) and doesn’t complete any flushes. 

Checking gets to showdown for free but forfeits thin value from worse A‑x and weak pairs that will call a small river bet. Betting a modest fraction of the pot is the best compromise: it extracts value from worse A‑x and single pairs, charges missed draws, and keeps the pot manageable given my vulnerable kicker. With the pot ≈2600, a bet of ~1000 (~35–40% pot) is appropriate. If Villain check‑raises large I’ll fold — his check‑raise would polarize to two‑pair/straights/bluffs and my kicker is too weak to continue. 

Decision: small value bet to target worse Ax and weak pairs while folding to strong aggression.

On the river, the board is 8h-Js-Ts-Ac-6d. My hand is As-2s, giving me top pair with the lowest possible kicker. While I had the nut flush draw on the flop and turn, that draw has now missed. Minni's betting pattern is very concerning: she check-called the flop but then led out (donk bet) on the turn when the Ace appeared and has followed up with a 1,000 bet on the river. This sequence strongly indicates she has an Ace with a better kicker, a set (88, TT), or two pair (AJ, AT, A8, JT, J8, T8). Given there are no straights possible on this board, her range is concentrated on these high-value hands. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.6-to-1, my hand only beats a total bluff or splits with a few specific hands like A3-A5. Since I block some of her potential missed spade draws with the Ace of Spades, the likelihood of her bluffing is reduced. Saving the 1,000 chips is the more prudent tournament play.

UTG in a 6-max game, Q9o is too loose to open profitably: it’s frequently dominated when called, and it doesn’t play well versus the likely 3-bets from players who’ve shown willingness to re-raise pre. Limping just invites isolation and puts us in tough multiway spots. With 60bb, we can pass and wait for stronger, cleaner open/call/3-bet candidates.

I am holding Queen-Eight offsuit in Middle Position. This is a weak starting hand that does not meet the standards for an opening raise in a 6-max game. Given that I have a healthy stack and many hands remaining, there is no strategic reason to play this marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Cutoff with 9h 8c (98 offsuit). The action has folded to me. While the Cutoff is a steal position, 98o is generally too weak to open profitably, especially with active players behind me like Minni on the Button who has shown aggression (re-raising pre-flop and playing aggressively post-flop). Defending against a 3-bet or playing out of position if the Button calls would be difficult with this hand. Standard play is to fold.

I'm on the button with 2♦6♣ — a very weak, unsuited hand, but I have position and a deep stack (≈149 BB). This is a prime spot to apply pressure and pick up the blinds. I prefer to open here rather than limp: a 3x BB open (300) gives good fold equity against the blinds and is a standard size in this game. If I face a 3-bet I will most likely fold this hand; if I'm called I'll play postflop in position and c-bet selectively on favorable textures, otherwise check and control the pot. The raise costs very little relative to my stack and maximizes my opportunity to accumulate chips early.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 3x from Minni. My hand is 9s2h, which is offsuit, disconnected, and low – complete trash with no playability postflop. Button opens are wide but still profitable against a wide calling range, and defending SB with this hand risks too much equity out of position with dominated potential hands. Pot odds are not compelling enough to call or 3bet bluff. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I have pocket tens (TT) in the BB facing a button raise to 300. This is a premium hand that plays well as a 3-bet for value. Minni has position but TT is strong enough to 3-bet and take control. I'll 3-bet to around 950 to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop.

I opened to 300 with 2♦6♣ to steal the blinds, but Claude 3-bet to 950. 2-6 offsuit is too weak to continue versus a sizable 3-bet — it’s dominated and has very little playability postflop. I already planned to fold to a 3-bet with this hand, so I’ll preserve my deep stack and pick better spots later.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with Jack-Eight offsuit. This is a very weak hand to open with from early position in a 6-max game. My stack is healthy at 8025 chips (80 big blinds), so there is no strategic reason to take a high-risk, low-reward gamble with a marginal hand. Given the history of active 3-betting at this table, I expect to be challenged if I raise, and I will likely be dominated if I am called. Therefore, the standard and most profitable play is to fold.

I am in Middle Position with King-Six suited (K6s). After the UTG player folds, I am first to act. In a 6-handed game, K6s is generally too weak to open profitably from this position (Hijack), as it is easily dominated by better Kings and faces the risk of 3-bets from the players behind me (Cutoff, Button, Blinds). With a stack of over 100 BBs, there is no need to force action with marginal holdings. The standard play is to fold.

Cutoff with Jc8s — a playable but not premium hand. I want to seize position, apply pressure to the blinds, and avoid giving the button a cheap flop; opening is preferable to a limp. With a deep 146 BB stack a standard 3x sizing gives good fold equity while keeping post‑flop maneuvering room. Raise to 300 total (3x) to isolate and continuation-bet on many flops; fold to a large 3-bet, and consider calling small/isolating 3-bets by the SB/BB to play post-flop in position.

74 offsuit is a complete trash hand with no playability, suitedness, or high card strength.
