Facing a cutoff raise from Minni, who has shown aggression in prior hands (e.g., Hand 6, 12, 17), her range is wide but strong enough that 74o has zero equity against it. On the button with deep stacks (99.5BB), position doesn't justify calling or 3-betting this speculative hand this early in the tournament (Hand 18/150). Pot odds are poor (calling 300 into 450, ~33% implied odds needed but hand realizes nothing). Folding preserves stack for better spots.

9Ts is a decent connected hand with straight-making potential. Facing a min-raise from Minni in the cutoff, I'm getting good odds to call and see a flop. While I'll be out of position, the hand has good playability. Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting seems unnecessary against what could be a wide cutoff opening range. Calling 250 more to see a flop.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action: 200 to win a 700 pot (~28.6%). Although 54o is weak and can suffer reverse implied odds, the small blind cold-call increases our immediate pot odds and gives us reasonable implied odds when we flop strong (two pair/straights) in a multiway pot. A squeeze/3-bet bluff is unattractive into an opener plus a caller with our hand and stack depth. Take the cheap defend and realize equity.

I flopped trip nines with 9c on 9h9sKc board - a monster hand. With such a disguised hand, checking to trap is optimal. Minni raised preflop and may c-bet with a King or other holdings. FiveTwo is also in the pot. By checking, I can let them bet into me or catch a bluff. If they check behind, I can extract value on later streets. No need to lead out and potentially fold out worse hands.

We have complete air with 54o on K99 rainbow and essentially no useful backdoors. Multiway OOP, bluffing into the preflop raiser plus a caller is low fold-equity and sets us up to get floated/raised. Best is to check and realize whatever tiny equity we have, then respond to a bet based on sizing and action.

Flop: 9h 9s Kc. I raised preflop with Jc8s and both blinds checked to me — I completely missed. I have no pair and essentially no backdoors (only one club in hand; a flush is impossible from here), so my equity is negligible. This is a multiway pot (two opponents), which drastically reduces fold equity for a c-bet and makes bluffing costly. On a paired board with likely Kx/9x/pocket pairs in callers’ ranges, I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position to hands that beat me. I’ll check and control the pot, plan to fold to serious pressure, and re-evaluate on the turn — look to bluff or value-bet only if I pick up real equity or see a favorable turn runout/line from opponents.

I have trip nines - a monster hand. Everyone checked the flop, so they likely don't have strong holdings. If I check again, I risk giving a free card and getting no value. With two hearts on board now, there's a flush draw present. Time to bet for value - targeting Kings, pocket pairs, or flush draws. Betting around 50% pot to get called by weaker hands.

We have essentially no equity: 4-high with only a single river 2 as a real improvement, and pairs (4/5) are rarely good on K99x. Claude’s 1/3-pot lead into two players can be a probe, but with Minni still to act behind us our fold equity on a bluff-raise is very poor, and calling 450 to win 1800 (~25%) isn’t justified given our true winning chances and reverse implied odds. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I opened from the cutoff with Jc8s and checked the multiway flop (9h 9s Kc). After FiveTwo folded on the turn Claude leads for 450. I have no pair and essentially no meaningful equity (only six clean outs to make a one-pair hand that will often still be second-best vs Kx or pocket pairs). Calling 450 into a ~1350 pot (it will be ~2250 if I call) requires ~20% equity to break even; my hand is well below that against Claude’s likely range (Kx, pocket pairs, 9x, or other value hands). A raise would be a thin bluff — I was passive on the flop and have little fold equity — so the correct, stack-preserving play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with Ts6s. This is a weak suited disconnected hand. In a 6-handed game, opening this hand from early position is generally a losing play due to the high likelihood of being dominated or facing 3-bets from the aggressive players behind (like Claude or Minni). I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have pocket kings (Kh Kd) — a premium hand and an automatic open from middle. Stacks are deep (~143 BB) and the table’s standard open sizing has been around 250–300, so I want to build the pot and thin the field without bloating it. A 3x open to 300 extracts value from worse hands (AQ/AJ/KQ, medium pairs, suited aces) and discourages limp-calling multiway action. If I face a significant 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (KK normally continues vs. 3-bets — either call or 4-bet for value depending on opponent and sizing).

Q3o is a trash hand with no high card strength, connectivity, or suitedness. Minni's 3x open from middle position suggests strength, and she's shown aggression in prior hands. In cutoff, position is good but this hand has zero equity against her range. Folding preserves stack for premium spots in a deep-stacked tournament.
42 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a raise to 300, this is a clear fold even from the button. No need to defend with such a weak holding.

Facing Minni’s open to 300, 8♠5♥ offsuit is too weak and plays poorly out of position from the small blind. Calling creates a tough multiway spot with the big blind still to act, and 3-bet bluffing with this hand is too low-EV given poor blockers and limited postflop equity realization. Preserve stack and wait for better defend/3-bet candidates.

I have a strong pocket pair (88) in the Big Blind. Minni opened to 300 from Middle Position. While calling to see a flop is a viable option, 3-betting to 1000 allows me to take initiative, capitalize on the fold equity Minni showed in Hand #17, and protect my hand against overcards. If called, I still have a powerful hand that can flop well.

Flash 3-bet to 1000 from the big blind after my open to 300. I have KK — a premium that wants to get value and isolate. The 3-bet sizing is small and Flash has shown aggression, so his range is likely broad (AQ/AK, suited broadways, medium pairs and some bluffs), not just AA. I want to 4-bet to build a pot heads-up and charge worse hands while denying free equity to speculative holdings. The minimum 4-bet to 1700 is too small; I’ll make a meaningful 4-bet to ~3x the 3-bet. Raising to 3000 (~30BB, still leaving plenty behind on my 14,000 stack) balances extracting value and preserving fold equity. If he 5-bets jam I will strongly consider calling given KK’s equity vs a likely wide 3-bet/5-bet range, but I’ll re-evaluate if his profile/sizing polarizes to near-certain AA. Action: 4-bet to 3000.

I am currently facing a 4-bet from Minni. While pocket eights are a strong starting hand, Minni's 4-bet to 3000 after my 3-bet to 1000 represents a very narrow and strong range (typically TT+, AK). In Hand #12, I already folded a much stronger hand (KK) to Minni's aggression when an overcard hit, and in Hand #17, Minni showed respect to a 3-bet by folding. This 4-bet suggests she is either holding a premium hand or has identified me as a player who can be pushed off hands. Calling 2000 more (nearly 30% of my remaining stack) out of position with 88 is mathematically poor for 'set mining' and leaves me in a difficult spot on most flops. Shoving is a massive over-play. Therefore, the most disciplined tournament play is to fold and preserve my stack.

UTG with K♠7♥ — a marginal, easily-dominated offsuit king and poor out-of-position hand. In a 6-max game UTG opening ranges should be relatively tight; raising here invites 3-bets/squeezes from aggressive players at this table and creates difficult post‑flop decisions OOP. Limping/calling 100 just to see a flop is passive and likely to produce a multiway pot where K7o has low playability. With deep stacks and many capable raisers behind, fold and preserve chips for a better spot in position.

Preflop, pot unopened after UTG fold to me in middle position. QJs is a strong suited broadway hand suitable for a standard open-raise in 6-handed play. Raising builds the pot with position advantage and good playability postflop. Standard sizing around 2.5x BB (250 total). Deep stacks allow for flexible play.
T3 suited is too weak to defend against a middle position open. Poor hand strength, and 3 players behind me who could squeeze. Easy fold.

Elon opens to 2.5bb from middle position and we’re on the Button with 86o. This hand has poor playability and is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and it’s not a good 3-bet bluff candidate (no blockers, not suited). Calling also invites squeezes from the blinds and forces us to realize equity with a weak offsuit holding. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better spots in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Elon opened from Middle position with a standard 2.5x raise. A5o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against an active player. Calling often leads to difficult spots post-flop where I am dominated or playing a medium-strength hand with no position. While I could 3-bet as a bluff, Elon has shown aggression in previous hands (Hand 4), and I'd prefer a better candidate for a 3-bet bluff or a stronger hand to play for a pot. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with KhTh facing a standard 2.5x open from Elon in Middle Position. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 150 to win a pot that will be 550, offering roughly 3.6:1 odds (requiring ~27% equity). King-Ten suited is a strong defending hand that flops well and retains good equity against a typical MP opening range. 3-betting is an option but calling is standard to keep the opponent's range wide and realize equity with a hand that plays well post-flop, especially 100bb deep. I will defend my blind by calling.

I hold middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker and a backdoor heart flush draw on a wet board of [Ts 7s Qh]. The board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Queens, Overpairs, Sets), though I connect reasonably well. Being out of position, leading out (donk betting) is not optimal as it likely folds out worse hands and isolates me against better ones. Checking is the standard line to allow the opponent to c-bet or check back. If the opponent bets, my hand is strong enough to call given the pair and backdoor equity. If they check, I realize equity for free.

Post-flop on a Ts7sQh board, I have top pair top kicker with QdJs after raising preflop. The board has a spade flush draw and straight draw potential (e.g., 89, JT), but my hand blocks QJ and plays well. Pro's BB call preflop is wide, including Ax suited spades, weaker Queens, pairs, draws. With position and initiative, a standard continuation bet extracts value from worse hands (worse Qx, pairs) and protects against draws. Pot is 550; betting ~2/3 pot (375) builds it while denying equity to floats/draws. Deep stacks allow flexibility for turns.

I hold middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker on a [Ts 7s Qh] board. The opponent, who opened from MP, bets ~68% of the pot. While this board favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Queens, sets, flush draws), my hand is too strong to fold immediately. I beat bluffs (like AK, AJ, lower pocket pairs) and have decent equity against draws. I also have a backdoor heart flush draw and runner-runner straight possibilities. The price to call (375 to win 925) requires roughly 29% equity, which my hand easily has against a balanced range. Raising would isolate me against better hands, so calling is the standard play to keep his bluffing range in and realize my equity.

On the Turn, the board is [Ts 7s Qh 6c]. The 6c is largely a brick that doesn't change the texture significantly. I hold middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker. I acted passively on the flop (check-call), and standard strategy dictates checking again to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here would likely only get called by better hands (Queens, overpairs) and fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. Checking gives the opponent a chance to either check back (allowing me to realize equity cheaply) or fire a second barrel, at which point I can re-evaluate based on sizing. My hand has showdown value but is not a value betting hand on this board.

On the turn with QdJs on Ts7sQh6c board, I have top pair top kicker, which remains very strong. The board is draw-heavy with a live spade flush draw and now additional straight draws possible (e.g., 89 for straight). Pro's BB range after calling pre and my flop cbet includes weaker Qx (like Q9-QT), pairs (88,99,TT? unlikely), Ax suited spades, and some sets unlikely. Pro checked again, which could be weakness or trapping, but with position and initiative, betting for value from worse hands and to charge draws/protect equity is optimal. Pot is 1300; a ~2/3 pot bet of 900 builds the pot, denies equity to draws (spades have ~19% equity vs us), and represents strength. Deep stacks (my 9325) allow flexibility if raised. Checking risks giving free cards to draws that beat us.

On the Turn, the board is [Ts 7s Qh 6c]. The pot is 1300 and the opponent bets 900 (approx. 70% pot), which is a substantial second barrel. I hold middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker. While I called the flop c-bet, the opponent's continued aggression on a board that favors their range (containing Queens, sets, and strong draws) is concerning. The Queen is an overcard to my pair, and MP's opening range connects heavily with this board (AQ, KQ, QJ, QQ, TT). My hand is now effectively a bluff-catcher. The large sizing suggests value or a high-equity semi-bluff. Against a value range of Qx or better, I am drawing very thin. Against draws, I have equity but face a difficult river decision. The price to call (900 to win 3100, requiring ~29% equity) is marginal at best against a tight double-barreling range. Preserving my stack of ~95bb is preferable to making a high-variance call with a marginal hand.

UTG in 6-handed play with AQo offsuit, a premium hand warranting a standard open-raise. Raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 total) to build the pot with position advantage post-flop and thin value against calling ranges. No significant reads from history suggest deviation; opponents frequently raise to 250 pre-flop in similar spots.

T6o is a weak offsuit hand that's not worth defending against an UTG raise. Elon opened from early position, which typically indicates a stronger range. I'm in middle position with players still to act behind me. Folding is the clear play here - no reason to invest chips with this hand.

UTG open in 6-max represents a relatively strong, tight range. Q3o is heavily dominated (by all better queens and broadways), has poor playability postflop, and doesn’t realize equity well in position vs a strong opener. With no compelling squeeze dynamics and plenty of better spots, folding maximizes EV and preserves stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight of hearts, a decent suited gapper. Elon raised from UTG to 250. While UTG ranges are typically strong, J8s is a hand that can play well post-flop in position. My stack of nearly 70 big blinds gives me enough room to see a flop and potentially outplay the opener if I connect or if the board texture allows for a bluff. Folding is an option, but in a 6-max game, calling on the button with this hand is a standard speculative play to realize equity in position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Six offsuit (Ks 6h). An Under-the-Gun player (Elon) has raised to 2.5 BB, and the Button (Flash) has called. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a pot of roughly 850 if the BB folds), playing K6o out of position in a multi-way pot is a losing strategy. My hand is frequently dominated by the UTG opener's range (which includes stronger Kings), carrying high reverse implied odds. I will fold.

Facing an UTG open to 250 and a button call, I’m in the big blind with Q♦4♣ (Q4o). This hand is weak and heavily dominated by a typical UTG opening range and by the caller’s calling range. I’d be out of position against two players with virtually no fold equity and poor post‑flop playability. A min‑raise would be a speculative bluff that’s likely to get called or 3‑bet, and calling 150 to see a flop is a marginal spot with little long‑term EV. With a healthy stack (15,250) I’ll preserve chips for better opportunities and fold.

Flop of Ac Ah Tc gives me top set with AsQd (three aces), the nuts on this paired, draw-heavy board with club flush draw and gutshot possibilities like KQ. Flash called my UTG raise from the button, which could include Ax, Tx, flush draws, or floats. Pot is 650, I'm first to act with position heads-up. Betting for value to extract from worse aces (like AT/AJ), tens, draws, and induced bluffs. Sizing to 3/4 pot (500) balances value and protection against draws while keeping stacks committed (my stack 10375, Flash 6725). Larger size maximizes EV as opponents show aggression but fold to pressure when behind.

I am holding Jack-high on a board of Ace-Ace-Ten. The UTG opener, Elon, has led out for 500 into a pot of 650. Given Elon raised from UTG, his range is heavily weighted towards strong pairs and high cards, particularly Aces which have now trips on this board. My hand has no showdown value, no draws, and very little equity against his likely range. Continuing here, even in position, would be a mistake as I would be floating with almost no outs. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play.
AKs is a premium hand, one of the best starting hands in poker. From UTG, I should open raise to build the pot and define my hand. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB (250) is appropriate.

Claude’s UTG open should be relatively strong 6-handed, and 45o performs very poorly versus that range. Calling invites dominated situations and multiway pressure with little implied odds (offsuit, low connectivity), and a bluff 3-bet is too spewy given the tight opener and players left to act. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Queen offsuit, a premium starting hand in 6-max play. Claude (UTG) has shown a high frequency of opening for 250 in previous hands, suggesting a range that is wider than a standard tight UTG range. While calling is an option to see a flop, 3-betting allows me to take initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and isolate Claude. Given that players behind me (Pro and Minni) have been active, I want to discourage them from entering the pot with speculative hands. A raise to 800 (roughly 3.2x the open) is a standard sizing to apply pressure and define Claude's hand strength.

I am on the Button facing an UTG open from Claude and a 3-bet from Flash in the Cutoff. My hand, Jack-Ten offsuit, is not strong enough to cold call a 3-bet, especially when the initial raiser (UTG) is still left to act behind me. JTo is frequently dominated by the ranges of an UTG opener and a CO 3-bettor (likely AQ, AK, JJ+). Even though Flash has shown aggression previously, getting involved here with a marginal offsuit hand is negative EV. The correct play is to fold.

I’m in the small blind with 5♦2♣ — the absolute bottom of the barrel: no connectivity and no backdoor play. Claude opened UTG to 250 (a reasonably strong open) and Flash made a sizable 3‑bet to 800 from the cutoff. Facing that 3‑bet out of position (the button and big blind still to act) this hand has virtually no post‑flop equity or playability. Calling 750 to play OOP with 5‑2 off is a clear losing play, and a 4‑bet bluff to 1,350 is too thin and easy to fold out or get 4‑bet/isolated. Preserve the 15,200 stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

82o is one of the worst hands in poker, with no high card value, no suitedness, and no connectivity. Facing an UTG raise from Claude followed by a 3-bet to 800 from Flash in cutoff, who shows aggression in prior hands (frequent raises and 3-bets), indicates a strong range. Pro and Minni have folded, and assuming Claude folded the 3-bet (as action reached SB/BB without further action shown), the pot has 1200 including dead money (blinds + initial raises). Calling 700 OOP with this hand offers poor implied odds and equity realization against a likely polarized strong range. Raising has low fold equity against an aggressor, and shoving risks the stack for minimal gain. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament.

AKs is a premium hand that plays very well against a 3-bet range, especially from the cutoff. Flash 3-bet to 800 with only 5925 behind. I should 4-bet for value here - AKs is too strong to just call and too strong to fold. A 4-bet to around 2100-2200 puts pressure on Flash and sets up a potential all-in situation if they continue. With their stack size, they'll be pot-committed if they call.

Claude (UTG) has 4-bet to 2100 after my 3-bet. While I have position, Claude's 4-bet range from early position is typically very strong, often consisting of JJ+, AK, and occasionally AQs. My Ace-Queen offsuit is significantly behind this range. Previously in Hand 13, Claude folded to my 3-bet, which suggests that his decision to 4-bet now represents a much stronger holding rather than a light adjustment. Calling 1300 more would commit nearly 35% of my remaining stack to a pot where I am likely dominated, and shoving is not profitable against his likely continuing range. Folding allows me to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

6-max UTG with ~56bb, AhTd is strong enough to open (good high-card value, blocks premium Ax, can make top pair/top kicker-ish on many boards). Limping invites isolation and multiway pots where offsuit AT loses value. I’ll take the initiative with a standard 2.5x size that’s been consistent in the match, keeping the pot manageable versus 3-bets while still applying pressure to the blinds.

I am holding Queen-Three offsuit in middle position. This is a very weak hand, particularly after an under-the-gun raise to 250 from FiveTwo. Given the range of a UTG raiser and the fact that there are four players left to act behind me, calling or raising with this hand would be a significant mistake. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Cutoff with 2c 3c facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG. A UTG opening range is typically strong, often containing big pairs, strong aces, and broadways. My hand, being the lowest suited connector, has very low equity against this range and poor playability. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me (Button, SB, BB), increasing the risk of being squeezed or playing a multi-way pot out of position relative to the Button. Calling speculative hands like 23s requires deeper implied odds and ideally closing the action, neither of which applies here. Folding is the disciplined and standard play.

I’m on the button with Q♦ J♣ facing an UTG open to 250. With position and deep stacks, QJo is a good hand to play post‑flop — it has decent equity and blockers — so I prefer to flat-call and see a flop rather than 3‑bet bluff here. A small or thin 3‑bet invites cold 4‑bets from Elon/Claude (both capable) and isolates me against a likely strong UTG range; folding is too tight given position. Calling keeps the pot controllable and lets me use position to realize equity or apply aggression on favorable flops. I will call the 250.

T2 suited is a weak hand with poor playability. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo (who has been active but UTG suggests strength) and a button call from Minni, the combined range is strong. Calling 200 into 650 gives minimal odds (3.25:1), but postflop SPR will be high with awkward SB position out of position to three players. Better to fold and wait for stronger hands, preserving stack for tournament progression.

I have T9 suited in the BB facing a min-raise and a call. Pot is 650 and I only need to call 150 more, giving me over 4:1 pot odds. T9s is a strong suited connector with excellent playability - straight potential and flush draws. Easy call to close the action and see a flop in position relative to the raiser.

Flop is Kh-5d-Kc. I have T9 of clubs - completely missed this board. No pair, no flush draw (no clubs on board), no straight draw. I have zero equity and no reason to bet. Easy check and fold to any bet.

As the preflop raiser on K-K-5, I retain a meaningful range advantage (lots of strong Kx/overpairs) and can profitably apply pressure with a small multiway c-bet. My actual hand (AhTd) has little showdown value but does block some continuing Ax floats, and a small sizing targets folds from hands like 66–QQ, suited connectors/missed broadways, and random overcards. Multiway I don’t want to bloat the pot without equity, so I’ll use a controlled ~30% pot size and be prepared to shut down versus significant resistance.

Flop is K♥ 5♦ K♣; I have Q♦ J♣ and completely missed. FiveTwo (UTG opener) fires a very small c‑bet (250 into a 1050 pot). I’m on the button so I have position and can use it to realise equity or float and bluff later. Raising here would be a thin, multiway bluff against an UTG opener (and the big blind), and folding is too tight given the tiny bet and good pot odds. Call 250 to see a turn — if a Q/J or a harmless blanket shows up I can apply pressure or extract value; if opponents show real strength I can fold on later streets.

I have T9 of clubs on Kh-5d-Kc. Complete miss - no pair, no draw, only backdoor clubs at best. FiveTwo bet and Minni called, showing strength. No reason to put 250 more into this pot with zero equity. Easy fold.

Turn Jd is a reasonable barrel card for the preflop raiser on K-K-5: it adds more strong value combos to my perceived range (AK/KQ/KJ/AA/JJ) while my actual hand (AhTd) picks up a gutshot to the Q for Broadway. Minni’s flop-calling range contains a lot of pocket pairs/A-high floats that are still uncomfortable facing continued pressure, and I don’t want to give a free card in position when I have little showdown value. I’ll use a small, range-friendly sizing that applies fold equity without bloating the pot vs Kx/Jx that continue, and I can comfortably fold if raised.

I have Q♦J♣ on K♥5♦K♣J♦ — I just turned two pair (K & J). It’s heads‑up vs FiveTwo (the UTG opener). This board is dangerous: two kings are on board so any K in his hand is trips (or he can have full houses with a J or pocket pair), and UTG’s preflop range contains a lot of Kx and strong pairs. His line (small flop c‑bet, another small turn bet) is consistent with thin value betting Kx or probing. Raising here would be thin — it folds out worse hands but won’t make Kx fold and could get me committed when I’m behind. Calling 450 is a cheap way to keep the pot controlled (pot ≈1750; calling 450 requires ≈20% equity to be profitable), use position, and see the river before committing more. Plan: call the turn, then re‑evaluate on the river — bet for value if he checks a harmless card, or proceed cautiously/fold to heavy aggression if the river completes sets/full houses or he shows strong action.

River brings the 3rd diamond, which is a good theoretical bluff card, but after Minni has called flop and turn on K-K-5-J, her range is heavily weighted to hands with real showdown value (Kx, Jx, pocket pairs that now play as two-pair with the board), plus some diamond draws that just got there. Those portions won’t fold often enough to justify a bluff, and I have essentially 0 equity when called. Checking keeps me from burning chips with a low-percentage triple barrel; if she bets, I can comfortably fold this Ace-high.

I have Q♦J♣ giving me two pair (Kings and Jacks) on K♥-5♦-K♣-J♦-8♦. It's heads‑up vs FiveTwo who opened UTG, made a very small c‑bet on the flop and a small turn bet, then checked the river. That line is consistent with a range containing thin Kx, some medium pocket pairs and some weaker broadways. The river 8 is relatively harmless and I hold valuable blockers (Q and J) that reduce KQ/KJ combos that would crush me. I'm vulnerable to trips/full houses (any King, 55, JJ, some pocket pairs), so I don't want to overcommit — but I should still extract value from worse hands (A‑x, weaker Jx, Qx, some pocket pairs that make two‑pair worse than mine) that will call a modest bet. I will make a modest value bet (~900, ~40% of the pot) to charge worse hands while keeping the pot manageable; if FiveTwo raises big I will respect that as a strong Kx/full house line and fold.

We arrive at the river with pure Ace-high (no pair, no made hand) on a K-K-5-J-8 board where Minni has called two barrels and now bets 900 into 3100 after we check. Her line heavily concentrates on hands with real showdown value: any Kx (trips), Jx (two pair), pocket pairs (now two pair vs the board), and the diamond draws that just completed. There are very few natural bluffs available given the runout (no missed obvious draws), and we’d need her to be bluffing >22% of the time to justify a call (900 to win 4000). That’s unlikely, so calling burns chips; raising is even worse because her value range won’t fold. Best play is to fold and preserve stack.

I am in the UTG position with 6s 5c, which is a very weak hand to open from early position. My stack size of 59.25 big blinds is healthy enough that I do not need to take risks with marginal hands. Given the aggressive nature of the table seen in previous hands, particularly from players like Minni and Claude, folding is the standard and most correct play to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in Middle Position with 5h 6c, which is a weak offsuit connector (56o). With four players left to act behind me, including a large stack in the Cutoff, this hand is too weak to open profitably. The standard play here is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

Cutoff with 8♦7♥ offsuit — borderline but playable in late position. Limping hands this deep just gives the button a cheap opportunity to steal; I want to seize fold equity and avoid multiway limp pots. A standard open to 300 (≈3×BB) applies pressure to the button and the short-stacked big blind, while keeping the pot manageable if called. If I face a big 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I can play post‑flop in position and c‑bet favorable textures. Raise to 300 to try and take the blinds or play a single-raised pot in position.

J8h suited on the button facing a CO raise to 300 into a 450 pot. Excellent position, speculative hand with straight and flush potential that plays well postflop multiway or heads-up. Minni's CO range is wide, and players have shown aggression but also calling tendencies. Direct odds are slightly poor (1.5:1), but implied odds are strong due to stacks (mine 108BB, hers deeper) and position for postflop play. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks bloating pot OOP for blinds without strong equity. Flat calling to realize equity in position is optimal.

8Ts is a decent speculative hand. Getting 3:1 pot odds (250 to win 750) with a suited connector that can make straights and flushes. In the SB I'll be out of position but the implied odds are reasonable given stack depths. Worth a call to see a flop.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action (200 more to win a 1000 pot), and J9o has enough equity multiway to defend, especially with position disadvantage but implied odds when we flop strong pairs/straight draws. A squeeze would need to be large into multiple callers and is likely to get played back by deeper stacks, so we take the cheap defend.

I have 8s Tc on 7s 7c Qs - completely missed this flop. No pair, just a gutshot (need 9) and backdoor spade draw. With 3 opponents in the hand and no equity to speak of, I should check and see what develops. No reason to bluff into multiple players with this board texture.

Multiway on a paired board (Q77) as the BB, J9o has very little immediate equity and no strong draws (no spade, only a thin backdoor straight). Donk-bluffing into the preflop raiser and multiple callers is unlikely to generate enough folds because ranges contain plenty of Qx, pocket pairs, and some 7x. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and reassess versus sizing if someone bets.

I have 7h on a 7s-7c-Qs flop — trips. This is a very strong hand but it’s a multiway pot (Button, SB, BB called my open) so I want to both extract value from Qx/pair hands and charge any spade/backdoor draws without scaring everyone off. A ~2/3‑pot lead sizing gives me good value vs worse pairs and draws while still keeping worse hands in. I’ll bet 800 into 1200. If I face a large, polarized raise (overcommit), I’ll strongly consider that they have QQ or a full house and reevaluate/fold; I’ll call a small single raise to realize equity and keep extracting on later streets.

Flop board 7s7cQs is extremely dry and paired, favoring Minni's CO leading range which includes 77, Q7s, QXs (esp spades), pocket pairs, and bluffs/draws but heavily value-weighted given bet sizing (800 into 2000, 40% pot) after two checks. My J♥8♥ connects with nothing: jack-high no pair, no straight draw (gapped), hearts miss spade flush draw entirely. Equity vs her polarized range is poor (~12-18% max, less multiway). Pot odds 3.5:1 superficially ok but reverse implied odds disastrous—calling commits ~8% stack into pot where I'll rarely extract value and often face aggression. Multiway with SB/BB stacks (esp FiveTwo's 44BB), folding preserves stack for superior positions/postflop equity realizations. Consistent with GTO vs aggro: speculative BTN flat preflop but fold to c-bet when bricked.

I have 8s Tc on 7s 7c Qs - no pair, and my draws are extremely weak. I have a backdoor spade draw (only one spade) and a very weak gutshot needing a 9 for an 8-9-T which isn't even a made straight. Minni bet 800 into a pot showing strength on a paired board. I'm getting 2.5:1 but with essentially no equity and FiveTwo still to act behind me, this is an easy fold.

We’re now heads-up vs the preflop raiser, but the key issue is SPR: if we call 800, the pot becomes ~3600 and we’ll have ~3600 behind, meaning we’re essentially committing future streets with a hand that has very poor equity on Q77. J9o has no pair, no flush draw, and only very thin backdoor straight potential, plus it’s frequently dominated when we do improve to a J or 9. Minni also bet fairly large (800 into 2000) after a multiway preflop pot, which weights her toward Qx/overpairs/7x and reduces fold equity for any bluff-raise. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in the UTG position with 92o (Deuce of Hearts, Nine of Spades), which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the poor position and lack of hand strength (unsuited, unconnected, low cards), the only profitable play is to fold. There is no reason to enter the pot with this hand.

2♣5♦ is a very weak, unsuited, unconnected hand and is easily dominated. I’m in middle position with several players to act (Elon, Button, SB/BB), so calling 100 to see a flop is a marginal limp that will often face a raise or a c-bet where I have poor equity. Attempting a steal by raising from this spot with garbage is low EV given the players left to act and deep stacks. With a healthy stack (17,350 ≈ 173bb) I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Cutoff position in 6-handed play with UTG and MP folded provides a strong stealing opportunity. K7o is a playable hand in late position: king-high blocker reduces villain's KK/AK range, and it has post-flop potential with top pair/top kicker or draws. Stacks are deep (100+ BB), but stealing the 150 pot (dead money from blinds) has good equity even if called/3-bet. Standard raise size of 2.5x BB accounts for dead money and builds pot. Folding is too tight here; limping undesirable. Will evaluate 3-bets post-flop aggression from prior hands.

I have pocket 9s on the button facing a min-raise from the cutoff. This is a great spot to 3-bet for value - I have a strong hand with position, and 3-betting allows me to isolate Elon while building the pot. Standard 3-bet sizing would be around 3x, so approximately 750.

We’re in the small blind, out of position, facing a CO open and a BTN 3-bet. Q6o is far too weak to defend here: it plays poorly postflop (dominated by Qx), has little equity realization OOP, and 4-bet/squeeze bluffs are unattractive with this hand and ~43.5bb effective (would risk a big chunk of our stack into strong, uncapped ranges). Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the big blind with 76o. Elon (CO) opened to 250 and Claude (BTN) 3-bet to 750. My hand is very weak and does not play well out of position against a 3-bet range. Calling 650 more would commit over 10% of my stack to a pot where I am likely dominated and out of position. The correct strategic move is to fold.

Facing a 3-bet to 750 (needing to call 500 into 1150 pot) from Claude on the button after my cutoff open with K7o. K7o is a standard light open from CO but marginal vs 3-bet range. Claude's button 3-bet range includes QQ+, AK, AQs+, some bluffs due to position. King blocker helps slightly vs AK/KK, but poor playability OOP (we act first postflop). Pot odds ~2.3:1 require ~30% equity, which K7o likely has vs wide range (~35-40% vs linearized range like TT+, AQs+, AQo+), but realization is low OOP without position or strong postflop potential (dominated TPTK possible). 4-betting bluff risky deepstacked (~100BB) with this blocker-heavy hand and no strong history to exploit. From hand history, Claude aggressive but competent (e.g., Hand 22 4-bet). Standard fold preserves stack for 125+ hands remaining; better spots abound.

UTG with J♦5♠ — a weak, offsuit hand with poor playability from early position. There are five players yet to act and this table has shown regular preflop aggression, so limp/calling 100 would put me out of position into likely raises and multiway pots where J5o is usually dominated. Raising is not justified either. With a deep stack I should wait for a better spot with position or stronger holdings, so I fold to conserve chips and avoid marginal situations.

2s6s is a very weak suited hand with poor playability, low high-card strength, and significant gaps. In middle position pre-flop in an unraised pot, standard play is to fold. No fold equity needed, pot odds poor for speculating, and better to preserve stack for premium hands in a multi-table tournament dynamic. Opponent tendencies from history don't justify entering with trash.
AJo in the cutoff is a strong hand. With two folds in front of me, this is a clear raise spot. I'll make a standard 2.5x open to 250 to steal the blinds or isolate if called.

Claude is opening a fairly standard 2.5bb from the cutoff and has shown a willingness to play back (including 4-betting). With A9o on the button, we have decent equity and strong positional advantage, but it’s not strong enough to 3-bet for pure value, and a 3-bet bluff with a 43.5bb stack risks getting put in tough spots versus a capable opener and deep blinds behind. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity in position, and avoids getting dominated into a large pot preflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d Th, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude raised from the Cutoff and FiveTwo called on the Button. Although I only need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 850, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents. This hand has very poor playability and is likely dominated. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I hold Pocket Queens (QQ) in the Big Blind, a premium hand. I am facing a raise from the Cutoff (Claude) and a call from the Button (FiveTwo). This is an ideal spot for a 'squeeze' play (3-betting over an open and a call). Just calling would invite a multi-way pot where I am out of position, allowing opponents to realize equity with weaker hands. By raising, I extract value from Claude's wide opening range and FiveTwo's calling range, while also potentially isolating a single opponent or winning the pot immediately. Given the initial raise to 250 and one caller, a robust sizing is required to discourage calls. A raise to roughly 4.5x the initial bet (around 1100) charges them appropriately to see a flop and leverages the fact that FiveTwo has a shallower stack (41BB) which might induce a shove that I would happily call.

AJo facing a 3-bet from Pro in the BB after FiveTwo flatted. Pro's 3-bet to 1100 (4.4x) could be a squeeze play or genuine value. AJo is often dominated by AK/AQ, but getting ~2:1 pot odds (850 to win 1650). Folding is reasonable but feels too tight. 4-betting would only get called by better. I'll call and play postflop carefully - can fold to heavy action on bad boards but realize equity on favorable flops.

We’re getting a decent price (850 to win 2500, ~25%), but this is a squeezed 3-bet pot with the original raiser continuing by calling—both ranges are now substantially stronger and heavily weighted toward Ax that dominates A9o (AQ/AK) plus broadways and medium+ pairs. With A9 offsuit we have poor playability (no flush equity) and significant reverse implied odds when we make top pair, even with position. A 4-bet bluff is too risky at ~41bb effective and likely gets called or jammed on by stronger ranges. Best EV is to preserve the stack and fold.

I 3-bet pre-flop with QQ and was called by the Cutoff. The flop comes K-9-4 rainbow. The King is a significant overcard that connects very well with the Cutoff's flat-calling range (AK, KQ, KJs). While I am ahead of underpairs (JJ, TT), betting turns my hand into a bluff-catcher against his Kings and folds out the hands I beat. Checking is the best option to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from missed broadways or weaker pairs. I want to get to showdown without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

Pro 3-bet preflop and checked this K94 rainbow flop. He could be trapping with AK/KQ, pot controlling with QQ/JJ/TT, or giving up with air. I whiffed completely with AJo - just A-high. Betting to bluff is risky since his 3-bet range connects well with this K-high board. Checking back preserves my showdown value against his air, controls the pot, and gives me free chances to hit an A or J on later streets.

The turn card is the 6c, which is a relatively safe card that doesn't significantly change the board texture or complete obvious draws. The pot is 2500. I hold Pocket Queens, which is effectively second pair on this King-high board. After checking the flop and witnessing the opponent check back, the opponent's range is somewhat capped; he likely doesn't have a monster like a set or a very strong King (AK), as those often bet the flop, though a slow-played King is possible. However, his range still contains Kx hands that beat me. Betting here has 'reverse implied odds': worse hands (JJ, TT, AQ) likely fold, and better hands (Kx) call. Checking allows me to keep the pot manageable, keep his bluffing range alive (floated hands like AQ/AJ), and hopefully get to a cheap showdown. I will check and evaluate if he bets.

Pro 3-bet preflop and has now checked flop and turn on K964. This line is suspicious - either trapping with Kx/overpairs or giving up with missed hands like QJs/AQ. I still have just A-high. Betting here as a bluff is risky because: 1) His checking twice could be a trap, 2) If he has QQ/JJ he might call, 3) I have some showdown value against complete air. Better to check and take a free river card - I can hit an A or J for a likely winner.

I hold Pocket Queens on a board of K-9-4-6-T. The action has been checked through on both the flop and turn, which suggests the opponent likely does not hold a strong King or a set, though a slow-played top pair is possible. The river Ten brings a 3-flush and connects with hands like AT or JT, but mainly I am worried about a King. Betting here is unlikely to extract value from worse hands (like JJ or 9x) which would fold, while better hands (Kx) would definitely call. By checking, I can get to showdown cheaply against hands I beat (like JJ, 9x, or missed Ace-highs) and avoid value-owning myself against a King.

Pro 3-bet preflop then checked flop, turn, and river. This is a very passive line. He likely has: QQ/JJ that was pot-controlling, AQ that whiffed, or occasional traps. I have A-high - I beat his complete air like AQ. Betting as a bluff is risky since QQ/JJ might call. The Tc on river helps some draws but also makes TT into trips. Better to check and win at showdown against his missed hands.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 8c5s, an offsuit, low, gapped hand with poor playability and low equity against any raising range. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. No position advantage and deep stacks don't justify limping or raising this trash hand. Prioritizing tight pre-flop play to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.
4d 8s is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high card value. From middle position with players still to act behind me, this is an easy fold.

Folds to me in the cutoff with ~41bb. K9o is strong enough to open in 6-max, especially with dead blinds and position on the button. Raising is better than limping because it creates fold equity vs the blinds, lets me realize equity in position when called, and keeps my range aggressive; if I face a sizeable 3-bet from Flash/Pro I can comfortably fold this offsuit hand. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open size.

I am on the button with King-Six offsuit. FiveTwo has opened from the cutoff for 250 (2.5bb). While I am in position, K6o is a very weak hand to defend against an open raise, even from a late position. FiveTwo has a relatively short stack (38.5bb), which makes 3-bet bluffing less effective as they are more likely to commit. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 6s (offsuit connectors). The Cutoff (FiveTwo) has raised to 250 (2.5 BB). I have 108 BBs, but FiveTwo is the short stack with roughly 38 BBs. Playing 65o from the Small Blind against a late position open is generally a losing play due to being out of position and having a hand with weak showdown value and limited playability compared to suited connectors. The pot odds are roughly 200 to win 400 (33%), but realizing that equity out of position is difficult, and there is a risk of the Big Blind squeezing. Standard strategy dictates a fold here.

I’m in the big blind with A♠ J♦ facing a CO open to 250 (pot 400, I’ve got 100 in, to call 150). FiveTwo’s stack is 3850 (~38.5BB) so effective stacks are ~38BB. AJo has strong blocker value against many CO opening hands and plays well both as a value 3-bet and postflop. I don’t want to just call OOP and let a wide range see flops cheaply, nor do I want to make a tiny min-3-bet that gives the opener correct odds to continue. I’ll 3-bet to apply pressure, isolate the opener and take the initiative. Raising to 900 (≈3.6x the open) is big enough to fold out lots of weak broadways and small pairs while still preserving workable postflop play if called (approx. pot ~1850 and effective stack ~2950 -> SPR ≈1.6). Plan: take down the pot preflop when they fold; if called, continue with a straightforward value/attack plan on favorable flops; fold to a clear large 4-bet shove from CO unless it looks like a bluff. Action: 3-bet to 900.

We opened CO and face a BB 3-bet to 900. K9o is too dominated versus a typical BB 3-bet range (lots of better kings, strong broadways, and pairs), and while we have position, the SPR will be middling and we’ll frequently end up in tough, low-realization spots (dominated top pair, reverse implied odds). The price (650 to win 1200) requires ~35% raw equity and we’re unlikely to realize that cleanly. Best EV is to conserve the 41bb stack and continue with stronger continue/4-bet candidates.
5c 6s is a weak offsuit hand from UTG position. With 5 players still to act behind me, I need a much stronger hand to open. This is a clear fold.

We’re first in from middle position (no raise yet), but 8♣3♣ is far below a profitable MP opening range and plays poorly at ~39bb: it’s dominated when we make a pair and doesn’t realize equity well without position. Limping to “see a flop” also invites isolation from the Button/Cutoff and forces us into tough postflop spots. Best EV is to preserve chips and wait for better open/3-bet candidates.

I am holding 9h 5d in the Cutoff position. This is a very weak starting hand and does not meet the requirements for an opening raise, especially with three active players (Pro, Minni, and Elon) still to act behind me. Standard play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with 7c 6h (76 offsuit). The action has folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, 76o is a weak hand, typically at the very bottom or outside of a standard opening range. Furthermore, the player in the Small Blind (Minni) has shown considerable aggression, specifically 3-betting my opens in previous hands (e.g., Hand #6, #9). Opening with a weak hand against an aggressive opponent who covers me and has a history of re-raising me is -EV. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the small blind with 6♣4♠ after everyone folded to me; pot is 150 and I need 50 more to complete to the big blind (total 100). 6-4 offsuit is a weak, out-of-position hand — not a hand I want to inflate the pot with by raising. However the price to see a flop is cheap (50 to win 200, ~3:1 pot odds) and the hand has enough equity versus a defending BB range to justify a call. I’ll call the extra 50 to see the flop and proceed only with a pair, strong draw, or a favorable board; fold to significant aggression otherwise.

Folded to SB limp into BB with ATo offsuit, a strong hand with ace-high and good kicker that dominates many limping ranges. Raising isolates the limper, takes initiative, and builds the pot when ahead. Standard isolation sizing around 4x the BB at 400 total, given the dead money and deep effective stacks. Minni is aggressive but limped here, likely weak.

I completed the blind to 100 intending to see a cheap flop, but Elon then raised to 400, leaving me to call 300 into a 500 pot. That requires ~37.5% equity to justify a call. 6♣4♠ offsuit is a very weak, low-equity hand and is difficult to play out of position — it’s frequently dominated and offers poor post‑flop playability. There’s no reason to 3-bet this; calling 300 with this hand is a losing, marginal play given the pot odds and positional disadvantage. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG 6-handed with 63o is far too weak to open, and limping creates a multiway pot where we’re dominated and realize equity poorly. With a ~38bb stack, preserving chips for better steal/3-bet spots is higher EV than entering with a trash offsuit hand out of position.

I am holding Ten-Deuce offsuit in middle position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em with very little connectivity or high-card value. Given my current stack size of approximately 58 big blinds, there is no reason to play such a speculative and statistically disadvantaged hand from this position. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the Cutoff with QdTc (QTo). In a 6-handed game, this is a marginal opening hand, typically near the bottom of a standard opening range. However, the player on the button (Minni) is the chip leader and has demonstrated significant pre-flop aggression, frequently 3-betting openers (e.g., Hand #6, Hand #27). Opening a hand like QTo here exposes me to a high likelihood of being 3-bet, forcing a fold, or playing a dominated hand out of position. To minimize variance and avoid bleeding chips to the aggressive button, the disciplined adjustment is to tighten my opening range from this position. Therefore, I will fold.

I’m on the button with T♥6♣ — a marginal but playable hand in position against only the blinds. Raising is the best play to steal the blinds and avoid playing a multiway pot out of position. I’ll open to 250 chips (2.5x BB) — a standard-sized steal that preserves fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. If either blind 3-bets large I will fold (T6o performs poorly vs a polarized 3-bet); if called, I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or check/control on coordinated boards.

J7s is a speculative suited connector with good playability, potential for straights, flushes, and two-pair hands. Facing a button raise from aggressive Minni, who opens wide. Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 more into 400 pot (roughly 3:1 effective). Deep stacks allow for implied odds postflop. OOP but Claude may isolate or check back, keeping pot multiway. Folding is too tight; 3-betting too thin. Call to realize equity.

I have 5c7h in the BB facing a min-raise from Minni and a call from Elon. I need 150 to call into a pot of 600, giving me 4:1 odds. While 57o is weak, the pot odds are decent and I can potentially flop a straight draw (needs 4-6-8) or two pair. With position on Elon and decent odds, calling is marginally +EV here.

Flop completely misses my J7s suited. No pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw (board 2h4h5d doesn't connect with 7J). Pure bluff catcher with jack-high, no showdown value. Board is draw-heavy (hearts flush draw, wheel straight draws like 36o, 3T etc.). Multiway pot (3 players) OOP as SB first to act. Minni aggressive button raiser likely to bet wide for value/protection. Claude BB may check or bet draws. Best play is to check-fold to preserve stack and avoid difficult spots on turn/river. No profitable bluffing range here given coordination and SPR ~13 (pot 750, my stack ~10k).

I flopped top pair (5s) with a 7 kicker on a 4h2h5d board. Elon checked to me. While my kicker is weak, I have the best made hand likely. There's a flush draw on board that I need to charge. Betting around half pot for value and protection makes sense.

I opened to 250 on the button with T♥6♣ and the flop (4♥ 2♥ 5♦) is a very dry, low board that favors my preflop raising range. Claude (BB) leads for 375 into a 1125 pot (~33% pot) — a small probe that represents a wide range (floats, weak pairs like 5x/4x/2x, or pure air). Calling 375 gives me good pot odds (need ~20% equity) and I have enough showdown/backdoor outs (three tens, a six, and some two-street chances) plus the huge advantage of acting last on future streets. Raising to 750 would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and will often be called by better hands; folding is too passive after I opened. I’ll call 375, keep the pot manageable, and use position to evaluate the turn — if I pair up or get a clear fold equity spot I’ll press; if I face heavy turn aggression with a blank I’ll fold.

Flop completely misses my Jc7c: jack-high with no pair, no straight draw (board 245 rainbow-ish but connected), no flush draw (wrong suits). Board is extremely draw-heavy (heart flush draw, wheel straight draws like 3x, 36, AT etc., gutshots). Pre-flop: Minni BTN open wide, we defended multiway. Claude BB check-raise bets into checked pot – polarized range: value (sets like 22/44/55, two-pair 24/25/45) or strong draws (hearts, wheels). Minni BTN flat-call from late position suggests strength/draw, not folding weak. Pot odds 1500:375 (~4:1) require ~20% equity to call, but jack-high has <10% vs villain ranges (dominated, live cards few). Calling OOP multiway builds tough SPR spots on scary turns/rivers. Folding preserves stack (~10k deep), avoids spew. Check-fold pre-planned and correct here.

I have top pair (5s) with weak 7 kicker. Minni called my flop bet - could have flush draw, overpair, or better 5x. The Qs is mostly a blank but could have improved QQ. My hand has showdown value but is too weak to bet for value against their calling range. Checking for pot control makes sense - if I bet and get raised, I'm in trouble.

The turn Q♠ completely misses my T♥6♣. My backdoor heart possibility is dead and I only have a single- card gutshot to a 3 (≈4 outs, ~8.7% to improve). Claude led a small probe on the flop then checked the turn — a line consistent with a marginal made hand or pot-control with something like a small pair or a Q. Betting now would rely on limited fold equity (the Queen actually helps his range) and would bloat the pot with virtually no showdown value. I’ll control the size of the pot and take the free river card in position. If Claude fires the river I’ll fold to a large bet and only continue with a pair/if I hit my straight.

I have top pair 5s with weak 7 kicker. River 8d doesn't complete flush draws. Minni called flop and checked turn - could have missed hearts, weak pairs, or better 5x. My hand has showdown value but thin value betting is risky. If I bet, better hands call and worse hands fold. Checking to showdown is optimal.

Board: 4♥ 2♥ 5♦ Q♠ 8♦. I hold T♥6♣ — I missed completely (no pair, no straight, no flush). Claude led a small probe on the flop then checked turn and river, a line consistent with a small pair or pot control. Betting now would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity (many hands in his range — 4x/5x/2x or even Qx — will call a modest bet), and would bloat a 1,500-chip pot with almost no showdown value. I’ll take the free card and check to avoid unnecessary risk; if Claude had fired a big river bet I’d fold.

I am holding 8h 3d in the UTG position. This is a very weak hand with no straight or flush potential, and its high card value is low. In a 6-max tournament, playing such a hand from early position is unprofitable and risky. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in Middle Position (MP) with King-Seven offsuit (K7o) after the UTG player folded. K7o is a weak hand that typically falls outside the opening range for this position in a 6-handed game. It is easily dominated by hands that opponents behind me might play (like KQ, KJ, or better Kings, as well as pairs). Additionally, there are active players behind me (Minni in CO) and a short stack in the BB who could shove. Preserving my healthy stack of over 100 BB is preferable to playing a marginal spot. Therefore, the correct strategic play is to fold.

Q♣5♦ (Qh5d) is a weak offsuit, poorly connected hand that will be dominated by typical calling/3‑bet ranges from the Button and the blinds. There are three players left to act (Button Elon, SB Claude, BB FiveTwo) and several at this table have shown willingness to 3‑bet or raise preflop — raising from the cutoff with Q5o risks a 3‑bet and a difficult out‑of‑position postflop spot; simply calling 100 also leaves me OOP and likely dominated on many flops. With a healthy stack (16925) I can preserve chips and wait for a better spot to attack. Fold and conserve equity for a stronger hand/position.

Preflop on the button with Ac5h in an unopened pot after three folds (UTG, Middle, Cutoff). A5o is a profitable stealing hand from the button due to excellent position, ace blocker reducing opponent's AA/AK range, and suited potential for flush draws postflop. SB Claude is deep-stacked and active, BB FiveTwo is shorter (37.5BB) but has shown aggression. Raising to 3x the BB (300) applies pressure to steal blinds (pot odds favorable), builds pot when called, and maintains fold equity. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative; folding too tight in position. Standard tournament play dictates raising wide here to maximize stack growth over 150 hands.

I have pocket 8s in the SB facing a button open to 300. This is a solid hand against a likely wide button range. I can either flat call to set-mine or 3-bet for value/protection. With my stack size and being OOP, I'll 3-bet to around 950 to either take it down now or build a pot where I'm ahead of much of his button opening range.

Facing a BTN open and a small blind 3-bet, our 3c4h is at the absolute bottom of range with no suitedness, no high-card value, and no useful blockers to make a profitable 4-bet bluff. Calling 850 more with ~37.5bb effective creates a bloated pot out of position versus strong ranges (and likely at least one caller behind), where we’ll realize equity very poorly and get dominated on most runouts. Best EV is to abandon the blind and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3-bet to 950 from aggressive SB Claude after my BTN open to 300. Pot 1350, to call 650 (pot odds ~2:1, needing ~33% equity). Ac5h suited with Ace blocker plays well: decent equity (~35-45% vs typical JJ+/AK/AQ range, better vs lighter 3-bets which Claude shows in history like Hand 2,10). Excellent button position for postflop playability - can realize equity, bluff with Ace-high, fold equity on later streets. Folding too tight in position with good odds; 4-betting marginal (weak hand, risks stack vs premium). Calling isolates vs SB, builds pot OOP for him, maintains initiative flexibility. Tournament phase favors defending wide to maximize EV over 150 hands.

I have pocket 8s on a J93 board with two overcards. After 3-betting preflop and getting called, Elon's range includes many hands that connect with this board (Jx, 99, T9s, QTs). My 8s are an underpair here. I should check to control pot size and see what Elon does. If he checks back, I get a free card to potentially improve. If he bets, I can evaluate but likely fold to significant pressure.

Post-flop on 3h-Js-9c rainbow board (one heart). Facing Claude's check after his preflop 3-bet. Pot 2000, heads-up, button position with Ac5h (ace-high, ace blocker to his premium range). Equity solid (~40%+ vs 3-bet calling range including AK/AQ/JJ+/TT/AJs; beats bluffs/air he checks back). Board somewhat drawy (gutshots, wheel potential) but rainbow favors ace-high showdown. Claude's history shows aggression (bets flops in similar spots like Hand 2/10), check likely weakness/trap/weak value - exploit by c-betting for value/thin value/fold equity. Position allows controlling pot postflop if called. Betting 60% pot (1200) builds pot when ahead, denies equity to draws/worse, high fold equity vs checked range. Checking back too passive OOP for him, invites free cards, misses EV from initiative/range advantage. Tournament stack growth favors aggression in position over 150 hands.

I have pocket 8s on a J93 board - an underpair to both the Jack and Nine. Elon bet 1200 (60% pot) after I checked. His range after calling my 3-bet includes many hands that crush me here: Jx hands (AJ, KJ, QJ, JTs), overpairs (TT+), and sets (99, JJ). Even hands like T9s, QTs have good equity. My 88 has only 2 outs to improve and I'm out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling puts me in a tough spot on future streets where I'll face more pressure. This is a clear fold - I'm beat too often and don't have the equity or position to continue.

I am in the UTG position with Ad 8s (Ace-Eight offsuit). In a 6-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open profitably from early position. It is easily dominated by better Aces (AT+) that opponents will call or 3-bet with, and I will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. My stack is healthy (over 100 BB), so there is no desperation to make a move. The standard and disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or better position.

9♦2♠ is pure junk — worst offsuit, no backdoor potential, and will be heavily dominated. Facing a 100-chip call into a 150-pot (pot odds ≈1.5:1) I need ~40% equity to justify a call, which 9-2o does not have vs typical opening/defending ranges. There are several players left to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB) who are active and likely to raise; limp-calling or stealing from middle with this holding is a losing line and puts me out of position postflop. With a healthy stack (~169 BB) I can wait for much better spots. Fold and preserve chips.

Folded to me in the cutoff with K7o, a speculative hand with king blocker and good postflop playability due to position. With UTG and MP folding, this is a standard spot to open-raise for a steal or to build the pot in position against the blinds. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is a balanced size. Limping is weak, folding too tight for late position. Opponents behind have shown aggression but stacks are deep enough to proceed.

Holding 8c6d offsuit on the button facing a cutoff raise to 300. This is a weak offsuit gapped hand that doesn't play well even with position. 86o is not strong enough to call or 3-bet against a standard cutoff open. Easy fold.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3x cutoff open and will be out of position the rest of the hand with a very weak offsuit connector. 5-4o has poor playability and suffers from domination (we often make weak pairs/straight draws that are hard to realize OOP), and calling also invites the big blind to come along, worsening our realization. A bluff 3-bet doesn’t have great properties here either with this stack depth and no strong read that Elon over-folds. Best EV is to cut losses and preserve the 37bb stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit. Elon has opened from the Cutoff for 300. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call the remaining 200, J2o is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop and is significantly behind Elon's opening range. It's better to preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

UTG with A6o is a marginal hand — dominated by many Ax combinations and performs poorly out of position. Several big stacks behind (Pro, Elon, Claude) have shown willingness to 3‑bet and defend aggressively, and the button (FiveTwo) can 3‑bet or call. Pot is only blinds (150) and calling 100 or opening here would commit me to difficult postflop decisions with a weak kicker and often multiple opponents. With a deep but not desperate stack, I should preserve chips for better position or stronger aces. Standard, prudent play is to fold UTG A6o rather than open or limp.

9d6d is a suited connector but gapped and weak for middle position in a 6-handed game. Multiple players behind (Claude in cutoff, FiveTwo on button, blinds). Standard play is to fold speculative hands out of position to avoid multiway pots where equity realization is poor. Deep stacks but no need to speculate here; wait for stronger hands to maximize EV over tournament.
Ah2h in the cutoff with two folds before me. Suited ace is a good hand to open raise with position. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Claude’s cutoff open to 2.5bb is fairly wide, and on the button we can profitably defend with K8o thanks to position and decent top-pair potential. A light 3-bet is possible with a king blocker, but K8o plays poorly when called/4-bet and we’d rather avoid building a big pot at 37bb with a dominated offsuit kicker. Calling keeps ranges wide, realizes equity in position, and lets us outplay postflop.

Holding Queen-Five suited in the Small Blind against a cutoff raise and a button call puts me in a difficult spot. While the pot odds are mathematically tempting (calling 200 to win a total pot of 850), Q5s performs poorly in multi-way pots out of position. I face significant reverse implied odds if I hit a Queen and am out-kicked, or if I make a flush that is lower than my opponents'. Given my stack size and position, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c 8s, a very weak offsuit hand. The Cutoff raised to 2.5BB and the Button called. Although the pot odds are tempting (150 to win 650, approx 18.75% equity needed), playing 8-3 offsuit out of position against two opponents is a losing proposition. The hand has poor playability and significant reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard play.

Missed the flop completely with Ah2h on 8d Kc 5d. I have ace high with no flush draw (board is diamonds, I have hearts). While K-high boards favor the preflop raiser, I have zero made hand and only backdoor equity. Against a button caller, a c-bet here will often get called by Kx, 8x, diamond draws, and pocket pairs. Checking and giving up or potentially catching an ace on later streets is the prudent play.

We flop top two pair (K8) on a fairly dynamic K-8-5 board with a diamond flush draw and some straight/backdoor possibilities. After Claude checks, we should value-bet to start building a pot while also denying equity to hands like Kx (worse kickers), 8x, 5x, diamond draws, and gutshots. With ~34.5bb effective and a 650 pot (SPR ~5), using a solid sizing now sets up turn/river shove lines without overcomplicating the hand. If Claude is trapping with AK/KQ/sets, we’re still happy playing for stacks.

I have Ah2h - ace high with no pair and no diamond draw on 8d Kc 5d. FiveTwo bet 450 into a 650 pot. I'm getting about 3.4:1 pot odds needing ~23% equity. However, my ace high rarely wins at showdown - I'm only beating bluffs. I have no diamond draw, just a weak backdoor heart runner-runner. FiveTwo is short-stacked (3000), making floating to bluff later less effective. Folding is correct here.

Pocket threes is a playable hand from UTG in a 6-handed game. Small pairs like this have good implied odds for set mining with deep stacks (113BB effective against most). We raise to a standard 2.5x open (250) to take initiative, build the pot, and isolate weaker hands or limpers. Folding would be too tight early in the tournament when building stack over many hands. Limp is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative.
2s5h is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG raise with this trash hand in middle position, folding is the clear play.

Elon’s UTG open to 2.5x represents a relatively strong, tight range in 6-max. K4o is heavily dominated (by Kx, Ax, broadways) and plays poorly postflop, especially with players still behind (Button and blinds) who can squeeze or overcall. A 3-bet bluff is not attractive with this hand profile and our 41bb stack. Best EV is to release and preserve stack.

I am on the button with 7d 5d, a speculative suited one-gapper. Elon has opened from UTG for 2.5bb. While the button is the best position to play speculative hands, calling an UTG raise with 75s is generally marginal, especially with aggressive players like Pro and Minni in the blinds who might squeeze. Given my current stack size of 56bb, I prefer to fold and wait for a higher-equity situation rather than bleed chips in a hand where I am likely dominated or forced to hit a very specific board to continue.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine suited (Kh 9h). The action has folded to Elon in the UTG position, who raises to 250 (2.5 BB). An UTG opening range is typically strong, often containing hands that dominate K9s (like AK, KQ, KJ). Although K9s has some playability due to flush potential, playing out of position (OOP) against a UTG range is difficult and often leads to negative expected value, especially with reverse implied odds on King-high boards. Elon has been active, but calling here invites a squeeze from the Big Blind and leaves me playing a marginal hand OOP. The disciplined and standard professional play is to fold.

I'm in the big blind with QhJc facing an UTG open to 250. QJo is a playable hand but vulnerable vs an early-position raising range, so a 3-bet would be too thin here. Calling for 150 more (250 total committed) is cheap, lets me see a flop with decent equity and keeps the pot controllable out of position. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively with top-pair or strong draws, fold or pot-control against big aggression on unfavorable boards.

Flop: Ad 5c Ah. I hold Qh Jc — no pair, no real draws (flush impossible with only one heart on board), and the board has two aces. I’m heads-up to an UTG open whose range contains many Ax and strong hands that will continue for value. With essentially zero equity and out of position, I should control the pot and avoid bluffing into a polarized range. Check to see a free turn and fold to meaningful aggression. If Elon checks back I can reevaluate on the turn and only continue with a pair or a clear improvement; if he bets, fold to normal-sized bets (only consider calling very tiny probes).

Heads-up on a paired Ac Ad 5c flop after I raised pre from UTG with 33 and Minni defended wide from BB. My pocket 3s is now just an underpair, second bottom pair essentially, with no showdown value against her calling range which hits this board hard (any Ax, A5, 55, 33 blocked but rare). Board is extremely dry, low coordination beyond the pair, favoring check-check to realize equity or control pot. Betting has low EV: villain likely calls/raises with top pair+, folds too little trash for FE given her position. Checking first allows pot control, induces bluffs on turn if it bricks, and realizes when behind to Ax. Minni has shown aggression post-flop in history (e.g., Hand 12, 23) but also checks weak hands; we'll evaluate her bet sizing. Folding equity isn't there pre-turn. Deep stacks (111BB eff vs her 166BB), no need to bloat pot OOP with marginal hand early tournament.

Board is Ad 5c Ah 4h and I hold Qh Jc. UTG opened preflop (strong range, lots of Ax) and checked the flop — this is a classic spot where I have no pair and essentially zero equity vs his continuing range. With only one card to come I cannot make a flush and my only clean outs are Q or J (~6 outs) which still lose to any Ace or many stronger holdings. I should keep the pot small and avoid bluffing into an early-position, likely-polarized range. Plan: check to see the river for free and fold to any meaningful bet; only consider calling very tiny probes that give correct pot odds. Action now: check.

Heads-up pot on a paired Aces board (Ad 5c Ah 4h) after my UTG raise with 33 and Minni's BB defense. Pocket 3s is an underpair with minimal showdown value against her range, which includes all Ax, A5s, pocket pairs up to AA (unlikely), and some floats/speculative hands. Board remains dry post-turn 4h (adds a weak heart draw gutshot to wheel), no strong draws completed. Minni has checked flop and turn, showing weakness or trap, but given her history of passivity here and occasional post-flop aggression elsewhere (e.g., Hand 12), betting risks getting called by Ax or better while folding out bluffs we beat. Thin value betting 33 gets no calls from worse (she checks 22-JJ, low pairs) and gets raised by better; bluffing lacks fold equity vs her positional checking range which caps lower. OOP pot control is key deepstacked (111BB eff), check induces her bluffs on scary rivers or realizes equity cheaply when ahead vs trash. Pot 550 is small, no need to bloat early tournament (hand 33/150). Check to river maximizes EV by avoiding mistakes.

I have Q♠? sorry Q♥ J♣ (Qh Jc) on A♦ 5♣ A♥ 4♥ 7♠ — no pair, no realistic draw, essentially zero showdown value. Elon opened UTG (a strong range) and then checked the flop and turn; that passive line is consistent with either a medium/weak Ace, a missed broadway, or a pot-control line with a strong hand. There is very little fold equity for a river bluff — many of his holdings (any A, some pairs or Kx/Qx) will call — so a bluff would be thin and low EV. Pot is small (550) and my stack is large, so preserving chips and avoiding a marginal bluff is correct. I check to keep the pot small and only concede if he bets, folding to meaningful river aggression.

River 7s is a complete blank, pairing nothing relevant and missing draws. Heads-up OOP with 33 after check-check streets on paired Aces board (Ad5cAh4h). Minni's triple check BB defense vs UTG raise caps her range low: weak Ax (slowplay unlikely given passivity), medium-low pairs (22-TT fearing AA/Ax), missed suited connectors/draws (e.g., 23,67, hearts that bricked). We beat her trash/bluffs (e.g., KQ, JTs missed, pure air) but lose to any A or higher pair. Betting thin value gets no calls from worse (she folds low pairs/draws cheaply pre-river anyway); bluff lacks FE vs stubborn BB calling station tendencies seen in history (Hand 12 check-raise trap, but passive here). Checking realizes equity at showdown vs wide weak range, induces bluffs if she had them (none came), controls deepstacked pot (111BB eff, hand 33/150 - preserve stack). Pot tiny (550), no regret bluff spot. Check maximizes EV long-term.
6d4c is a very weak offsuit hand with no real playability. From UTG (earliest position), I need strong hands to open. This is an easy fold.

We’re facing an unopened pot (just blinds) from middle position with a 41bb stack. Js6s is too dominated to open profitably here—when we get action it’s often from better Jx and stronger broadways, and if we’re 3-bet by the button/cutoff (both capable), we can’t defend enough with our stack depth. I’d rather conserve chips for cleaner opens and better steal spots from later position.

I am in the Cutoff position with Ace-Seven offsuit. Since it has folded to me, this is a standard opening range hand for a 6-max game. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play the hand in position.

I am on the Button with QJo facing a raise from the Cutoff (Flash). While Flash has been active and opening a decent amount of pots, QJo is a hand that is easily dominated by hands like AQ, AJ, KQ, and KJ. Additionally, the Small Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has shown aggression, making a flat call risky as it invites a squeeze. Although position is favorable, the potential reverse implied odds and the threat from the blinds make folding the most disciplined and prudent option to preserve chips for better spots.

I have Ah5c in the small blind facing a CO open to 250 (pot 400). A5 offsuit is marginal — it can make top pair but is often dominated and has limited post‑flop playability out of position. Calling costs 200 more into a 400 pot (roughly 3:1) so defending is inexpensive and reasonable versus a likely steal. A 3‑bet bluff here is suboptimal with A5o (it’s better to 3‑bet polar or with suited aces); folding is too tight given the pot odds and my stack. Plan: call to 250 total to see a flop, proceed cautiously OOP — fold to strong pressure if I miss, control pot size with top‑pair (ace) because of the weak kicker, and only escalate if I improve or get a good read.

Preflop in BB with K9o facing CO raise to 250 and SB call. Pot 600, calling 150 more for ~25% pot odds. K9o has decent equity (~35-40%) against a wide CO opening range, blocks kings, and has some straight potential. Multiway pot possible, but OOP playability is acceptable given depth (115BB effective vs Flash's 54BB). Folding too tight in BB; raising risks bloating pot OOP or facing shove. Call and reassess postflop.

Check. I called preflop with A5o to see a cheap flop but this board (3c 4d 3d) is paired and largely disastrous for my hand — I only have an overcard (A) and a weak 5‑kicker with no backdoor flush. I’m out of position and don’t want to build a big pot with just Ace‑high; leading out would get raised off my hand too often and give away equity. Checking controls the pot, lets the preflop raiser barrel bluffs that I can call or exploit, and keeps the option to realise equity if an Ace or harmless card hits the turn. Plan: check, call small/medium continuation bets to see a turn when pot odds justify it, but fold to heavy pressure (big raises) since I’m likely behind to sets/two‑pair.

Faced with a flop of 3c-4d-3d, a paired, dry board that heavily favors sets, two-pair hands, or draws like 52, A5, etc., from opponents' ranges.
