My Kd9c is king-high with no pair, no flush draw (one club backdoor), and minimal straight potential. Preflop call was for BB defense with position and multiway potential, but this board misses my range badly while hitting Flash's CO opening range (sets, Ax, suited connectors) and Minni's calling range harder. Pot is 750, checked to me first postflop. Betting risks folding out equity or getting raised off my hand OOP. Checking induces bluffs from Flash, controls pot, and allows check-fold/call to continuation bets based on size and opponent tendencies. Multiway pot preflop but Pro folded; Flash is short-stacked at ~54BB and aggressive from history. Prioritize survival and stack preservation in tournament.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop (3c-4d-3d) is extremely dry, making it unlikely to have significantly helped the blinds' calling ranges. Since both Minni and Elon have checked to me, this is a textbook situation for a continuation bet. I will bet around 40% of the pot to capitalize on my range advantage and fold out their air, while still having the best hand (Ace-high) most of the time if called.

Call 300. Flash’s c‑bet is small (300 into 1,050 — you’re getting roughly 22% pot odds) and I have an Ace blocking some of his value combos, so seeing a turn is cheap and the right way to keep control of the pot. My hand (Ah5c) is currently only Ace‑high with a weak kicker and no real draw, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a raise and I’ll fold to heavy aggression. Plan: call to see a turn, fold to a sizable raise (min‑raise to 600 or larger likely indicates sets/two‑pair), and if I hit an Ace on the turn proceed cautiously for value; otherwise check‑fold to strong action.

Facing a flop bet of 300 into 1350 after checking through preflop callers, with Kd9c on a 3c4d3d board. My king-high has no pair, no flush draw (backdoor club at best), and negligible straight outs. Board heavily favors sets (33, A3s, 53s), two-pair (34s), or draws (diamonds like A5d, 2d5d, 52) from Flash's CO raising range and Minni's calling range. Preflop call was standard BB defense, but postflop this misses badly. Pot odds ~4.5:1 require ~18% equity, but I have far less (~10% or worse multiway OOP). Calling commits to bloated pot OOP with reverse implied odds and poor playability on turn/river. Opponents show strength; Flash aggressive but board hits him. Preserve deep stack (114BB) for premium spots in tournament.

Check. I have Ah5c — only Ace‑high with a weak kicker and no diamond, out of position to an aggressive preflop opener who c‑bet the paired flop. The turn (7♦) actually helps villain’s range (completes diamond draws for anyone holding two diamonds, and strong flop hands like 33/44/3x are still in range), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with nothing. Checking controls the pot and lets me gather information. Plan: check and fold to clear turn strength; call very small probe bets (cheap enough to see a river, e.g. ~≤300) because of pot odds and the chance he’s barreling bluffs, but fold to sizable turn bets or raises (~>600) since I’m almost always behind.

I have improved to top pair (Sevens) with an Ace kicker on the turn. The board is relatively safe, though a diamond flush draw has appeared. Minni has checked to me again, which often indicates a marginal made hand or a draw. I will bet for value, as I can still be called by Ace-high, smaller pocket pairs (55, 66), or 4x. A bet of 600 (slightly less than half the pot) is large enough to extract value and charge any diamond draws without over-committing if she happens to have trips.

I have Ah5c on 3c 4d 3d 7d facing a 600 turn bet. The pot is 1950 and a call would commit 600 more — I’d need roughly 23.5% equity to break even (600/(1950+600)). My clean outs are very limited (3 Aces + 3 fives = 6 outs → ~13% to hit the river), and several of those outs are non‑clean if Villain holds an Ace or a 5. Flash’s line (CO open, small c‑bet on a paired flop, second barrel when a diamond falls) is highly consistent with value (sets/two‑pair or now a made diamond), so I’m usually behind. Raising as a bluff OOP with A5o is a poor risk/reward — he still has ~3k behind and can call or shove. Per my preflop/flop plan to fold to substantial turn aggression with no real draw, the correct play is to fold and preserve the stack.

Th2h is well below an UTG opening/limping threshold in 6-max. With 41bb effective, entering multiway out of position with a dominated, low-connectivity hand is -EV, and the table has shown plenty of aggression/3-betting that will punish weak opens/limps. Preserve stack and wait for better UTG candidates.

I am in Middle Position with 7h6h, a solid suited connector. In a 6-max game, this is a standard opening hand to balance my range and provide board coverage on low-to-middling textures. With a stack of 6425 (64 BB), I have enough depth to play post-flop, but I want to keep the initial pot size controlled. I will open for a standard 2.5x raise to 250. This allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with momentum. If I face a significant 3-bet from the players behind (Pro or Minni) or the blinds, I can evaluate based on sizing, but usually, 76s can be folded to large 3-bets to preserve my stack.

I am in the Cutoff with 6d 8s, facing a raise to 250 from Flash in Middle Position. Flash has been active and aggressive, but 86o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against a pre-flop raise. The pot odds (requiring ~38% equity) do not justify a call given my likely equity is lower against a reasonable opening range, plus the threat of the Button (Minni, big stack) or blinds squeezing behind me. Folding is the only standard play here to preserve my stack of ~107 BBs.

I’m on the button with A♣? (Ah2s) facing a 250 MP open. My stack is deep (16,125) and the call is small (250 into a 400 pot) so I can leverage position. A2 offsuit is a weak Ace (bad kicker) — not a hand I want to 3-bet light and get into a bloated pot or face a 4-bet from the blinds or the opener. Flash has opened to 250 frequently and can be sticky, so a light 3-bet is likely to be called. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves positional advantage, and lets me see a flop where I can continue with top pair/strong draws or fold to heavy pressure on Ace-high boards. I’ll call 250 and play the flop in position rather than risking needless chips preflop.

AQo is a strong premium hand from the small blind facing a middle position raise to 2.5BB and a button call. Flash's opening range from middle is relatively wide, especially given the history of multiway pots and loose play. Minni's button call suggests a speculative range. Claude in BB may defend wide but 3-betting isolates the raiser and applies pressure. OOP calling leads to difficult postflop spots; better to 3-bet for value and fold equity. Sizing to 900 (9BB) is standard, about 3.6x the raise, balancing thin value and semi-bluffs while denying equity to worse Ax, KQ, suited connectors.

Q9o in the BB facing a 3-bet to 900 from Elon. Flash opened, Minni called, and Elon squeezed. I need to call 800 more into a pot of 1500 (~1.9:1 odds). Q9o plays poorly against a 3-betting range - I'll be dominated by better queens, and OOP multiway makes this even worse. Easy fold.

I am facing a 3-bet from Elon in the Small Blind after I opened and Minni called on the Button. The raise is to 900, meaning I need to call 650 more into a pot that is already 1500. This gives me pot odds of approximately 30%. 7h6h is a strong suited connector that plays well in 3-bet pots because it is rarely dominated and has high implied odds if I hit a straight or flush. Additionally, I will have position on the aggressor (Elon) post-flop, although Minni still acts behind me and has position on us both. Given the stack depths (over 60 BB), calling to see a flop is a standard play to realize equity and potentially stack an opponent if the board texture favors my range.

I originally planned to call the 250 open to use position, but Elon’s reraise to 900 (and Flash calling) turns this into a three‑way pot where I must call another 650 into a 2,150 pot — I need ~23% equity to break even. A2o is frequently dominated by a 3‑bettor’s range (AK/AQ/AJ and medium/large pairs) and multiway pots drastically reduce my ability to realize equity or extract value. A light 4‑bet/isolation with A2o is thin and likely to get called or 4‑bet by stronger hands, committing chips with a bad kicker. Given the price, the ranges, and that I can wait for a much better spot in position with deep stacks, folding is the correct +EV choice here.

Flop of 7c6s2d is very dry and disconnected, completely missing my AQo. No pair, no strong draw, just ace-high with live cards. Heads-up OOP against Flash who called a 9BB 3-bet from middle position, his range is strong: QQ+, AKo, AQs+, perhaps some AJs or sets, but unlikely to have direct hits like 76s or 87s given the sizing he faced. This board favors his range slightly more as it can connect with pocket pairs or suited connectors he might defend with. Betting would be a bluff with low equity and fold equity against a capped strong range, poor given low SPR (~2.5 for him) and my position. Checking allows me to realize equity with ace-high, see turn free if he checks weak hands, and fold to a bet without committing further. Pot control essential OOP with marginal hand.

I have flopped top two pair (7s and 6s) on a very dry rainbow board (7c-6s-2d). Pre-flop, Elon 3-bet from the small blind, which typically indicates a range of high overpairs (88-AA) or strong high cards like AK/AQ. His check on this flop suggests he either missed completely or is playing an overpair cautiously. Since I have a very strong hand that is currently way ahead of most of his range, I need to start building the pot to extract value. A bet of around 40% of the pot (850) is small enough to encourage a call from hands like AK, AQ, or 88-JJ, while still moving toward a potential stack-off on later streets. I am in position, so I can control the action on the turn and river.

Preflop 3-bet with AQo was standard for value and isolation. Flop 7c6s2d completely misses my range, leaving me with ace-high and negligible backdoor draws. Facing a check from OOP, Flash's 850 bet (~40% pot) into a pot controlled after his large preflop call suggests a strong polarized range: overpairs (QQ+), sets (22/66/77), or some bluffs like air or weak Ax, but his MP 3-bet calling range caps bluffs tightly. My ace-high has poor equity (~15-25%) against his value range and realizes little by calling OOP into low SPR (~3 post-call). Raising has low fold equity against strong hands and commits me further with a bluff catcher at best. Pot control via check-fold is optimal per preflop plan; folding saves stack for better spots. Pot odds insufficient given position and range disadvantage.

I am in the UTG position with Eight-Seven offsuit. This hand is significantly below the standard opening range for this position in a 6-max game. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for stronger hands where I have a better mathematical and positional advantage.

I am in Middle Position with Ad 5s (A5o). The UTG player has folded. In a 6-max game, A5o is a marginal hand to open from early/middle position. While it has an Ace blocker and straight potential, it is easily dominated by better Aces and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the aggressive deep-stacked players behind me (Minni on CO and Elon on BTN). Standard strategy dictates folding A5o from this position to avoid difficult spots. I will fold.

Folded to me in the cutoff with Q♣9♥ (Q9o) and a deep stack (~158 BB). Q9o is marginal but has good positional value and postflop playability, so I want to open and take the initiative rather than limp. I raise to 300 (3x BB) to apply pressure to the blinds and the Button, reduce the chance of multiway callers, and give myself position to play postflop. Plan: continuation-bet favorable flops and realize equity; fold to a large, committed 3-bet (e.g. a shove or a 4-bet-sized 3-bet) since Q9o is vulnerable; consider calling a small 3-bet from an aggressive Button depending on their tendencies and pot odds; if I pick up the blinds I win the pot outright.

J7o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability post-flop, low equity against a likely strong range from Minni's cutoff raise. On the button, defense is wider, but this hand is too weak, dominated by better Jx, 7x, or pairs. No need to speculate with 10k effective stacks; fold to preserve stack for premium spots.
2h4c is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from Minni in the cutoff, I'm out of position with a hand that plays poorly postflop and has minimal equity against any reasonable raising range. Easy fold.

CO open is likely fairly wide and we’re heads-up. QJs (suited broadway) plays well as a 3-bet: we pick up the pot a lot pre, deny equity to hands that realize well vs a flat, and take initiative with a hand that can make strong top-pair/straight/flush draws. With ~40bb, sizing up keeps their calling range more honest and sets an SPR where we can pressure effectively postflop.

I opened to 300 from the cutoff with Qc9h and the big blind (FiveTwo, 3,100 behind) 3-bet to 1,000. Calling 700 into a 1,350 pot gives ~34% break‑even equity — Q9o has reasonable equity vs a fairly wide BB 3‑bet range and plays very well in position. I don’t want to 4‑bet/shove with Q9o (too often dominated or called by better hands), and folding surrenders position and value. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, use my positional advantage to realize equity and apply pressure on favorable flops. Plan postflop: c‑bet and seize initiative on Q/9/broadway or single‑toned boards, fold to heavy resistance on dry, unhelpful boards unless I pick up clear equity or reads; respect a shove or a large 4‑bet from the short stack.

We 3-bet pre and go HU to a very low, dry flop where our range (AA/KK/AQ/KQ/QQ) has a clear advantage. With QJ we flop top pair and, given the ~1 SPR (pot 2050, ~2100 behind), we should start building a pot to get stacks in while denying equity to hands like AKo/AJo, suited broadways, and small pairs that can peel once. A small-to-medium c-bet accomplishes this and sets up an easy turn shove.

I call 650. I have top pair (Q♣9♥) on a Q♦-3♣-2♣ flop and I'm in position. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop from the BB (a reasonably wide/short‑stack 3‑bet range) and his small flop stab (650 into ~2700) looks like a probe/blocking bet rather than a polarized power move. Calling is cheap (650 into the effective pot gives ~19% break‑even) and keeps worse queens, mid pairs and draws in the hand while letting me use position to control the pot and gather information on the turn. Raising here risks bloating the pot and inducing a shove or getting called by better Qx/pocket pairs; I’ll call, evaluate the turn, and either extract thin value or fold to heavy turn aggression depending on the card and his action.

We 3-bet pre and got called, then c-bet this low Q-high flop and got a peel. With pot 3350 and only 2450 behind, the SPR is < 1, so there’s no real room to maneuver—our default with top pair (QJ) is to put the money in now.

The 9s turn adds some second-pair hands (Q9/99) and a few straight draws, but it also leaves plenty of worse continues in Minni’s range (KQ/QT, stubborn pocket pairs, and club draws like AcXc). Checking gives free equity to clubs and JTx-type hands and can create awkward river spots; shoving maximizes value vs worse queens/draws and denies equity while we’re still comfortably ahead of most of their flop-calling range.

Turn gives me Qc9h two pair (Qs + 9s). FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop from the BB, made a small flop probe (650) and then shoved the turn for 2,450 (effectively all‑in). Only hands that reliably beat my two pair are sets/full houses (QQ, 99, 33, 22) — a relatively small number of combos — while a large portion of his 3‑bet/shove range (AQ, KQ, AK, medium pairs, and bluffs) is behind me. Pot is 5,800; calling 2,450 requires ~29.7% equity (2,450 / (5,800+2,450)). Two pair on this runout has well above that vs a plausible shove range, and the call is a reasonable portion of my stack (~17%). This is +EV, so I call and get to showdown.

2♣9♦ offsuit is a clear fold UTG. In a 5‑handed game UTG opening ranges are wider than full ring, but 9‑2o has virtually no equity, poor connectivity and terrible post‑flop playability out of position against 4 opponents. Calling 100 to see a flop or attempting a blind steal here is dominated and will put me in marginal spots; preserve my deep 200bb stack and wait for a playable hand. Fold.

5-handed play with positions: Minni UTG folded, I'm UTG+1 (early-middle position). QsTc offsuit is a marginal hand with straight potential but no suited value or domination power. Preflop open ranges in 5-handed games can include QTo sometimes due to wider dynamics, but opponents are aggressive raisers/callers from history (e.g., frequent 3-bets, pot control issues). Stacks deep (~100BB), so folding avoids tough postflop spots OOP with a non-premium broadway. Better to wait for stronger holdings to build stack long-term over 150 hands rather than spew chips with thin value/protection.

K9o on the button with only the blinds left to act. This is a solid stealing opportunity - K9o is well above the threshold for button opens. I'll raise to 2.5x to steal the blinds or play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven suited. The Button (Claude) has opened with a standard 2.5x raise. While Ten-Seven suited is a speculative hand that can play well post-flop, playing it from the Small Blind puts me in the worst possible position for the rest of the hand. Against an aggressive player like Claude, who has a significant stack, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity where I either have a stronger starting hand or better position, especially since I am currently the shortest stack at the table with about 76 big blinds.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad9s facing a Button raise to 250 from Claude. The blinds are 50/100, so I have already posted 100. I need to call 150 more to win a pot of 400, offering me pot odds of roughly 2.6:1 (requires ~27% equity). A9o is well ahead of a standard wide Button opening range (often 45-55% of hands), having approximately 58% equity against a top 50% range. While a 3-bet is an option to seize initiative, calling is the standard, lower-variance play to realize equity and keep the opponent's range wide while playing out of position. I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad9s on a low flop of 6d 4c 2c. I have Ace-high with two overcards to the board. As the pre-flop caller out of position against the Button aggressor, the standard line is to check to the raiser. The board texture is somewhat dynamic with a flush draw present, but it doesn't heavily favor my range enough to warrant a leading range. I will check and re-evaluate based on the opponent's subsequent action.

Dry, low flop (6d 4c 2c) that missed my K9o but also likely missed Pro's BB defending range. This is a textbook c-bet spot - I have position, the aggressor's advantage, and this board heavily favors my opening range. A 1/2 pot c-bet should take this down often.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad9s on a flop of 6d 4c 2c. Facing a continuation bet of 275 into a pot of 550 from the Button (Claude), I am getting odds of 3:1 (requiring 25% equity). My hand holds significant value: I have two overcards (Ace and Nine), giving me 6 outs to top pair, and a gutshot straight draw (a 3 makes a straight). Against a wide Button c-betting range which includes many missed broadway hands and semi-bluffs, my Ace-high is frequently ahead, and even against pairs, I have decent equity. Folding would be too tight given the price and my hand's potential. Raising would isolate me against better hands. Therefore, calling is the optimal play to realize my equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad9s on a board of 6d 4c 2c Ts. The turn card (Ts) connects better with the Button's opening range (which includes many broadway hands like AT, KT, QT, JT) than my check-calling range, which is weighted towards low pairs and draws. I still hold Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (requiring a 3). Leading out (donk betting) here would accomplish little, as I would likely fold out worse hands and be called or raised by better ones. The standard play out of position is to check to the aggressor and re-evaluate based on their action.

Turn brought Ts which could have helped Pro's BB defense range (T9s, T8s, AT, KT, etc.). Pro called my c-bet, showing some interest in the pot. My K-high has no showdown value, but double-barreling into a caller when the turn card may have helped them seems risky. Better to check back, control pot size, and potentially improve to a pair on the river or give up cheaply.

I hold Ad9s (Ace-high) on a board of 6d 4c 2c Ts Jh. The opponent (Claude) raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, and checked back the turn. The river card is a Jack. The opponent's turn check signals weakness or pot control, potentially a small pair or a draw. The river Jack interacts with his broadway range (hands like KJ, QJ, AJ) which might have checked the turn. My Ace-high has some showdown value against missed club draws or pure air. Betting would likely only fold out hands I currently beat (missed draws) and get called by hands that beat me (pairs). Therefore, checking is the best option to realize equity at showdown without investing more chips or turning a marginal showdown hand into a bluff unnecessarily.

River completed Jh on a 6d 4c 2c Ts Jh board. I have K-high which has almost no showdown value. Pro checked, but they called my flop c-bet showing interest. The J and T are both cards that could have hit their BB defending range. Bluffing here is risky - Pro has shown they want to see showdown by calling flop and checking through. K-high loses to any pair, but bluffing is unlikely to work against someone who has stuck around. Check back and accept the loss.

Pocket sevens (77) is a strong hand from UTG in a 5-handed game with deep stacks (~100BB). It has excellent equity against typical calling ranges, disguises well, and benefits from taking the initiative preflop. Raising builds the pot when ahead and allows fold equity. Limping risks multiway pots where overcards hurt, and folding is too tight given position and hand strength. Opponents frequently open/3bet around 250-300 based on history, so standard 2.5BB raise to 250 isolates effectively while keeping pot manageable.

Q6 suited is a marginal hand. Facing an UTG raise from Elon, this is a clear fold. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, and Q6s doesn't play well against that range even with the suited aspect. Not worth calling or 3-betting here.

I am on the Button with King-Trey offsuit (K3o). Elon has opened from Under the Gun (UTG) for 250. K3o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by an UTG opening range, which typically consists of high cards and pairs. Even though I have the advantage of position, calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with low equity. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

The current blinds are 50/100. I am in the Small Blind holding 5s2s, a very weak suited hand. An UTG player (Elon) has raised to 250 (2.5bb). I am out of position against an opening range that dominates my hand. While I have deep stacks (112bb), calling 200 chips to play a pot out of position with 5-high suited is a losing play due to reverse implied odds and lack of playability. The standard and correct move is to fold.

Elon opened UTG to 250 and I’m in the big blind with 4♦5♦. Calling is only 150 more (250 total) to contest a 400 pot — roughly 27% equity needed — and stacks are very deep, so 4-5 suited has real implied odds and playability. I don’t want to 3-bet light out of position (that would commit more and invite 4-bets); calling keeps the pot small and lets me realize equity on flops with straights/flushes. Plan: fold to large c-bets on dry Ace/King-high boards, but continue/raise as a semi-bluff on strong diamond/straight draws and value-bet/raise with two pair or better.

Elon opened UTG and I flat-called in the BB with 4♦5♦. Flop 2♣7♣2♦ is a paired, fairly dry board that heavily favors the raiser’s UTG range (overpairs, 7x, some 2x combos). I have no pair and only a weak backdoor diamond possibility (needs runner-runner) and just 6 outs to a single pair — not enough equity to build a pot out of position with deep stacks. Betting here would be a thin bluff against a range that connects well; I’ll check to control the pot, fold to significant aggression, and only continue vs small C-bets to realize my modest equity.

Pocket 77 on a 2c-7c-2d flop gives us a full house (77722), an absolute monster disguised as a set. Heads-up vs Minni who defended BB OOP vs UTG raise and checked flop, representing weakness. Board has a flush draw and is paired, protecting our hand while charging draws, Ax, or weaker pairs/trips. Deep stacks (~100BB effective). Value bet 2/3 pot (~350-375) to build the pot, get called by 22 (quads unlikely), 7x, flush draws, or floats. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or missing value. No reason to slowplay with villain likely weak.

I defended the BB preflop with 4♦5♦ to see a flop, but the board 2♣7♣2♦ is very bad for my hand and heavily favors an UTG opening range (overpairs, 7x, some 2x). Elon’s c‑bet is 375 into a 550 pot (pot now 925) and my call would cost 375. Break‑even equity to justify a call = 375 / (925+375) = 375 / 1300 ≈ 28.8%. I have only ~24.1% to hit a 4 or 5 by the river (6 outs) and ~4.2% for a runner‑runner diamond flush — roughly ~28% total at best, and my effective equity will be lower versus an UTG continuing range. Raising as a bluff out of position is unattractive (invites big action), and calling would put me in difficult spots OOP with limited implied odds. Per my plan to fold to significant aggression on this dry, paired board, I will fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

Q9o from UTG is a weak holding. This hand is not in my opening range from early position - it's easily dominated by better queens and will play poorly out of position against multiple opponents. Folding is the standard play here.

I am holding 5-4 offsuit in UTG+1. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack depths and the tendency of players like Minni and Elon to defend or 3-bet, there is no value in entering the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold Pocket Aces [Ah, Ad] on the Button, which is the strongest starting hand in poker. With action folded to me, my clear objective is to build the pot for value. I will raise to 2.5BB (250), a standard opening size that balances my range and encourages the blinds (who have deep stacks) to defend or 3-bet.

I have 7♣2♦ — the worst starting hand. Facing a standard button open to 250 from the small blind, I'm out of position and would need to call 200 more (250 total) to see a flop. That requires roughly 33% equity to break even (200 to win ~600), but 2‑7 offsuit has far less equity vs an opener. A squeeze/3‑bet bluff (min to 400) is possible with my deep stack, but OOP vs the button and with the big blind yet to act makes it too risky — I’ll often get called or 3‑bet and be dominated post‑flop. Standard +EV play is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

73s is a suited connector with decent playability and good implied odds on deep stacks. Facing a button raise to 2.5BB, Pro's range is wide (likely 40-50% of hands). Pot odds are excellent: calling 150 into 400 (pot will be 550 post-call, ~3.7:1). Folding is too tight from BB; 3-betting is suboptimal OOP with this hand as it plays poorly postflop when called. Calling to see flop OOP, where we can flop strong draws or hit big. Pro has shown willingness to bluff but also value bets, so we'll realize equity.

Flop is Jd Kd Jh, a paired board with a diamond flush draw. My 73s has complete air: no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw. Pro's button raise range hits this flop hard (JJ, KJ, QJ, AJ, KQ, suited diamonds, etc.). OOP with zero equity and poor playability, check-fold is optimal. Betting has no fold equity against a strong range and commits me OOP. Checking allows a free card if he checks behind (possible with missed draws), but we'll fold to any bet as pot odds won't justify calling with air.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ah Ad) on a Jd Kd Jh flop. While I have an overpair, this board texture (paired Jacks with a King) connects very well with the Big Blind's defending range, which includes many Kx and Jx combinations. Furthermore, I have observed Elon (BB) taking aggressive lines post-flop in previous hands, specifically check-raising flops (Hand 30, Hand 38). Betting here risks getting check-raised, which would put me in a difficult spot where I am either way ahead or drawing very thin against trips. Checking back allows me to pot control, induces bluffs from this aggressive opponent on later streets, and avoids bloating the pot on a dangerous board. I also hold the Ad, giving me a redraw to the nut flush and blocking the nut flush draw, making it safer to give a free card.

On the turn, the board is Jd Kd Jh 9h. My 73s remains complete air: no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw. Pro checked behind on the flop, which could indicate weakness (missed draws, ace-high, weak pairs), but his button raising range still includes many strong hands like JJ, KJ, Jx, Kx, diamond draws that may have improved or not. The 9h adds heart flush draw possibilities and opens some straight draws (T8, Q10, etc.), but I have none. OOP with no equity, betting as a bluff is risky: low fold equity against a calling station or strong range, and if called/raised, I'm committed with nothing. Checking is optimal - pot control, realize any thin equity on river if he checks behind, and fold to bets without costing chips. Consistent with flop plan of check-fold, now check-check if possible.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ah Ad) on a board of Jd Kd Jh 9h. After checking back the flop for pot control, the turn card gives me additional equity. I now have an overpair plus two nut flush draws (Diamonds and Hearts) since I hold both the Ace of Diamonds and Ace of Hearts on a board with two of each suit. This gives me tremendous equity even if currently behind a random Jack or a straight (QT). However, I am likely ahead of Elon's range, which includes many Kings (Kx) and straight/flush draws that he would check-call. I need to start building the pot to extract value from these worse hands and to deny free equity to his draws. Checking again would be too passive given my hand's strength and potential. A bet of around 300 (approx. 55% pot) is a good size to get called by Kx and draws while setting up a river decision.

7s3s is pure air on this JdKdJh9h board: no pair, no straight draw (73 doesn't connect), no flush draw (spades missed). Pro's button raise range hits the flop hard (JJ, KJ, Jx suited diamonds, KQ, etc.) and checking behind flop could be trapping strong hands or weak, but turn bet 300 into 850 (1/3 pot) after checking flop suggests value or thin value/protection. OOP, calling 300 offers poor pot odds (~26%) with zero equity and bad reverse implied odds (no potential to improve ahead). Raising as bluff has low fold equity vs. Pro's range which calls light or raises stronger; stacks deep enough post-call but no showdown value. Consistent with flop/turn plan: check-fold air OOP on strong board. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I am holding Qd 3c in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low card strength. In a 5-handed game, opening from UTG requires a much stronger range to account for the players behind me. Given my current stack size of 76 BB, there is no reason to play such a weak hand out of position. I will fold.

I am in the Cutoff position in a 5-handed game with pocket Deuces (22). While 22 is technically an opening hand from this position in a standard vacuum, the player on the Button (Minni) is the chip leader and has shown a tendency to 3-bet aggressively against my opens in previous hands (e.g., Hand #6, Hand #9). Pocket Deuces have very poor playability post-flop if called, and I cannot call a 3-bet. Given the deep stacks (100BB+) and the active player behind me, the risk of bleeding chips or being forced to fold to a re-raise outweighs the marginal value of set-mining or stealing the blinds here. I choose the lower-variance line to preserve my stack.

Button with Q4o — a weak hand but in prime position with only the SB and BB to act. I want to take the initiative and steal the blinds rather than play passively. A standard 3x BB open (to 300 total) gives reasonable fold equity while still allowing me to continue postflop in position. If I face a sizable 3-bet I will fold Q4o; if called heads-up I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and play more cautiously if it becomes multiway. Folding is too tight here and just calling gives the blinds good odds to see a flop. Raising to 300 balances aggression and risk.

K7o is a weak king-high hand offsuit from the small blind facing a button raise from Minni, who has shown aggression in previous hands with frequent raises and involvement in pots. Calling out of position would be poor due to reverse implied odds and difficult post-flop play against her wide button range. 3-betting is not justified with this hand as it lacks blocking value or domination potential against her calling range. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stacked tournament.

5h6s offsuit in the BB facing a button raise to 300. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability postflop. Getting 2.25:1 on a call, but 56o is near the bottom of my defending range. Against a button steal I could sometimes defend wider, but this hand plays poorly out of position. Fold is the disciplined play.

I am in UTG position in a 5-handed game with Qc 4c. This is a weak suited hand, disconnected, and below the standard opening range even for short-handed play. With 115 BBs, there is no pressure to play marginal hands from early position. Opening risks domination or being 3-bet. The prudent and standard play is to fold.

Fold. Q♦7♥ is a weak, easily dominated hand from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. There are still multiple players to act including aggressive raisers; limping (calling 100) invites multiway pots out of position and raising to 200 is too loose from early position with this holding. Preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

Folds from UTG and UTG+1 leave me on the button facing unopened blinds in a 5-handed game. Pot is 150 with 100 to call the BB. 6c5h is a speculative offsuit connector with decent playability, capable of making straights and hitting two-pair or better. Deep stacks all around (100+ BB effective). Button is an ideal stealing spot with high fold equity against blinds, who have shown aggression but also folding tendencies in history. Raising small maintains fold equity while keeping pot manageable postflop where I have position. Standard sizing 2.25x BB to 225 total. EV positive from steal + implied odds if called/multiway.
T4o is a weak hand. Facing a button raise, I'm out of position with a hand that plays poorly postflop. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. Elon has raised from the Button to 225. Pro and Minni have folded, and Claude has folded from the Small Blind. I need to put in 125 more to call a total pot of 375, giving me 3:1 odds. JTo is a solid hand to defend the big blind with against a button open, especially with these odds. I will call to see a flop and evaluate the hand further.

I have top pair (Tens) with a Jack kicker on a board of Td-3s-7s. This is a strong hand, but I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Elon). Standard play in this situation is to check to the raiser to see their continuation bet. There are some draws present (spades and straight possibilities), but my hand is well-protected for now. I will check and plan to call most standard-sized continuation bets.

Flop is Td 3s 7s, a somewhat coordinated board with two spades for flush draw potential and possible straight draws. My 6c5h has a weak gutshot to the 3-4-5-6-7 straight (4 outs), but minimal showdown value otherwise. Flash defends BB vs button raise by calling pre, then checks flop, which is standard with a wide range including pairs, draws, floats, and weak hands. As preflop raiser on button with position, a continuation bet maintains my range advantage, applies pressure, and denies equity to draws/weak hands. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing Flash to rep strength later. Small c-bet ~40% pot balances value (overpairs, sets, T7 etc.) with bluffs like this. Sizing 200 protects against floats while keeping pot manageable OOP for opponent. High fold equity vs BB check expected given history of folds postflop.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a T-high board. While there is a flush draw present, Elon's small 200-chip bet into a 500-chip pot is a standard continuation bet from a button opener. I have a strong enough hand to continue, but raising here would likely isolate me against better hands (like AT, KT, or sets) and force out the bluffs I want to keep in. I will call to keep the pot controlled and see how the turn card changes the board texture.

The turn card is the Ten of Spades, giving me three of a kind (Tens). While this is a very strong hand, the Ten of Spades also completes potential flush draws that were present on the flop. Since I am out of position against Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most strategic move. It allows me to control the size of the pot and observe Elon's reaction to the board pairing and the flush completing. If Elon bets, I will likely call to see the river, as trips are too strong to fold here, but I want to avoid overcommitting until I have more information about the strength of his hand.

Board is Td 3s 7s Ts, extremely draw-heavy with paired tens and a completed spade flush draw (three spades on board). My 6c5h has zero equity: no pair, no draws, no gutshot anymore (turn didn't help). Preflop button raise c-bet flop as standard semi-bluff/steal, but Flash's flop call indicates medium strength (Tx, 7x, underpairs, spade draws), and turn check suggests caution—possibly pot control with top pair (Tx), or missed draw now improved to flush but slowplaying/trapping, or weakness. Continuing to barrel risks calling stations or monsters; fold equity diminishes after flop call on coordinated board. With deep stacks and position, checking behind realizes no equity loss, controls pot, sees free river card in position. Avoids bloating pot with pure air against BB defense range that's likely to continue in spots. Preserves stack for better spots in tournament.

On the river, the board is Td-3s-7s-Ts-6d. I have three of a kind (Tens) with a Jack kicker. The turn went check-check after the board paired and the flush draw completed, which suggests Elon might not have a flush or a very strong hand like a full house, as those would typically want to build the pot. However, it's also possible he's checking back for pot control or with a medium-strength hand like a pair of Sevens or Nines. I have a very strong hand that is likely the best here. I will bet for value, choosing a sizing of 500 chips into a 900-chip pot (slightly over half pot) to encourage a call from weaker pairs (88, 99, 7x) or potentially an Ace-high hand that thinks I am bluffing on the missed flush.

River board Td 3s 7s Ts 6d is highly coordinated with three spades (any two spades in hand makes the nut flush), paired tens, and possible straights or two pairs (T7s, 73s, etc.). My 6c5h is complete air: no pair, no draw, no improvement from turn. Flash's line—defending BB wide pre, calling flop c-bet on spade-heavy board (range: draws like spades, Tx, 7x, pairs), checking turn after spade comes (pot control with medium strength or missed draw), then leading river for 500 (1/3 pot)—represents value, likely Tx (top pair improved or not), 7x two-pair/straight draws completed, or made spade flush thin value betting. Pot odds ~3.8:1 but zero equity or SDV; calling is -EV. Raising as a bluff after checking turn lacks credibility (looks weak), low fold equity vs BB defender who called flop, and risks stacks deep. Folding preserves stack for better spots; consistent with turn check realizing equity.

Kc7s (K7o) is a marginal, easily-dominated hand in early position. In 5-handed play UTG is effectively first to act with four players behind — that increases the chance I face a 3-bet or multiway pot where my kicker and playability are poor. Stacks are deep (≈198bb), so there’s no need to gamble with a weak holding; preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the +EV play. Fold preflop.

T5o is a speculative trash hand with poor playability, low equity, and no suited or connected value. In UTG+1 position with 5 players, this is an automatic fold preflop to preserve stack and avoid difficult spots OOP.
Q9o on the button with two folds to me. This is a standard open spot - Q9o plays well in position and I should be raising here to take the blinds or play a pot with position. Standard 2.5x open.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 8c, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has opened from the Button to 2.5x the big blind. Calling out of position with such a low-equity hand against a button range is a losing play. The best course of action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Threes (3c 3h). The Button (Claude) has opened to 250 (2.5 BB). I am getting excellent pot odds to call: I need to contribute 150 more into a pot of 400, offering me odds of roughly 2.7 to 1 (requiring ~27% equity). Pocket pairs, even small ones, generally have sufficient equity against a wide button opening range to defend. Additionally, we are deep-stacked (~100 BB), which justifies calling to 'set mine'—hoping to flop a set and win a large pot. Folding is too tight given the price, and 3-betting is not ideal as it turns a hand with showroom value into a bluff. I will call and evaluate the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Threes (3c 3h) on a flop of 5d-Jc-2s. The board is dry and uncoordinated. I have an underpair to the board's high card but am ahead of missed high cards. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (Button). Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal here as it folds out bluffs I might beat and isolates me against better hands. I will check and evaluate based on Claude's action.

Flop is 5d Jc 2s - dry board that missed my Q9o. Pro checked to me. This is a standard c-bet spot with position on a dry board. I have two overcards and can represent hands that connect with this board. Betting around 1/3 pot for value/protection and to take it down.

I hold Pocket Threes on a J-5-2 rainbow board, giving me an underpair and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). The opponent (Claude) c-bets small (200 into 550, ~36% pot). This is a standard c-bet size on a dry texture, and his range includes many missed high cards (AQ, KQ, etc.) against which I am currently ahead. I am getting roughly 4.75:1 pot odds (requiring ~21% equity). With 4 outs to the straight and 2 outs to a set, plus the likelihood of currently leading against his bluffing range, I have sufficient equity to continue. Folding a pair with a draw to a small single bet is too tight. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the board is 5d-Jc-2s-Qs. The Queen is a scare card that favors the Button's range (AQ, KQ, QJ) more than my Big Blind defense range. My hand (Pocket Threes) is now effectively 4th pair and vulnerable. However, I still retain my gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). Leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose as I am unlikely to fold out better hands and I isolate myself against strength. My best option is to check to the aggressor. If he checks back, I realize my equity for free. If he bets, I will re-evaluate based on sizing, though folding becomes likely as my showdown value has diminished significantly with the arrival of the Queen.

The turn Qs gives me top pair with a 9 kicker. Pro has check-called preflop and flop, likely with a weak range. I now have value and should bet to extract from worse pairs and protect against draws. Betting around 2/3 pot is appropriate for value.

On the Turn, the board is 5d-Jc-2s-Qs. The pot is 950 chips, and the opponent (Claude) bets 600, making the pot 1550. I need to call 600, getting pot odds of roughly 2.6:1 (requiring ~28% equity). The Queen is a bad card for my hand (Pocket Threes) as it hits the Button's opening range hard (AQ, KQ, QJ, QTs). My hand is now likely behind any pair (Q, J, 5, 2) and vulnerable to overcards even if currently ahead of a bluff. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4), this only provides 4 outs (~9% equity). Combining my weak showdown value and limited draw equity, I do not reach the required 28% equity to justify a call against a 2/3 pot bet. The prudent play is to fold.

68o is a standard fold from UTG in 5-handed NLHE. Weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no high card strength, and first to act with multiple players behind. Deep stacks allow waiting for premium hands to maximize EV. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.
A7o in UTG+1 is a playable hand. With Elon folding, I have position considerations but A7o is strong enough to open raise. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am on the button with 9-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude has opened with a raise from the cutoff position. While I have position, this hand does not play well post-flop and lacks the equity to justify calling or the strength to 3-bet as a bluff against a solid opening range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I hold J4o in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. I am facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG+1. Being out of position with a trash hand against an early position open is a clear fold situation. I do not have the pot odds or equity to justify calling, and raising is not a viable bluffing strategy here given the strength of an UTG+1 range.

UTG+1 opened to 250. I’m in the BB with Qh8c — a marginal but defendable hand heads-up. To call I need 150 more (total committed = 250); the pot would be ~550 after my call, so I’m getting ~27% pot odds to see a flop. Claude’s 2.5bb open here has been common/standard and not super tight, so Q8o has reasonable equity to realize. A 3-bet bluff with Q8o is thin (few blockers, likely to face stronger hands or a 4-bet) and will create a bloated OOP pot. Plan: call to see the flop and then proceed cautiously — continue with top pair/decent draws or favorable textures, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards. Action: call, amount = 250.

I have Q♣? Wait: Qh8c on a K♠-6♣-2♥ flop — I completely missed and have no realistic draws (a heart backdoor is impossible to make a 5‑card flush from here). Claude was the preflop raiser and his range includes many Kx/Ax and broadways that beat my queen-high. The pot is small (550) relative to both stacks, so low-variance, position-aware play is best: check and let the opener show aggression. If Claude cbets a small probe (very small, ~≤1/3 pot) I can consider a call to see a cheap turn, but I will fold to meaningful pressure or a standard c-bet size — I won’t bluff-raise OOP with such a weak holding. Action: check to control the pot and preserve stack while gathering information.

I raised preflop with A7o and got called by Minni in the BB. Flop is 2h 6c Ks - a dry King-high board. Minni checked to me. I have ace-high with no pair but this is a standard c-bet spot. I can represent the King, and on this dry board Minni will fold a lot of their range that missed. A small c-bet of about 1/3 pot should get the job done.

Board K♠-6♣-2♥ vs my Qh8c: I have only queen-high with no real draws and am out of position. Claude (UTG+1) opened preflop so his range contains many Kx/Ax and broadways that dominate me, but his flop bet is tiny: 200 into a 750 pot (~27% pot). Calling 200 to win ~950 gives ~21% break-even equity, so a cheap turn is worth seeing to realize any pair-outs (Q or 8) or to pick up information. I will not raise OOP with such a weak hand — that’s a high-variance bluff with poor blockers. Plan: call the small c-bet, fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair or the turn is very favorable; if checked to on later streets I can consider a small delayed bet on a safe card.

Board K♠-6♣-2♥-J♥, my hand Qh8c: no pair, no meaningful draws (can't make a five‑card flush by river). Claude was the preflop raiser and made a tiny flop c‑bet that I called to realize equity; he checked the turn to me. I only have ~6 clean outs to improve (~13% on the river) and I’m out of position, so bluffing OOP here is thin — his checking range can include lots of hands that still beat Q‑high (Kx, Jx, Ax, broadways). Pot control is the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line: check and go to the river cheaply, then fold to significant aggression unless I pair or get a clear spot. Betting now would bloated the pot for little equity and create tough decisions on the river, so I check.

I c-bet the flop with A7o and Minni called. Turn is Jh, adding another overcard to my 7. I still just have ace-high. Minni calling flop and checking turn could indicate a King, a Jack that just improved, or a pocket pair. Betting again with no equity seems risky - I'd be turning my hand into a bluff and if called I'm likely behind. Better to check back, take a free card, and potentially hit an Ace on the river for showdown value.

River: I hold Qh8c on 2h-6c-Ks-Jh-4h. I have no pair, no straight and I don’t have a flush (only one heart in hand while the board has three). Claude was the preflop raiser, made a tiny flop c-bet and then checked the turn — a line consistent with Kx/Jx, medium pairs or missed hands; his range usually dominates my queen-high. The pot is small relative to stacks and there’s no realistic value to extract or a good bluff to run here (poor blockers, OOP, and risk of being called by a pair or better). Lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and fold to any meaningful bet; if he checks back we go to showdown cheaply. Action: check and conserve chips.

Minni called my flop c-bet and has checked turn and river. I only have ace-high. Betting here would be a pure bluff - I can't get called by worse hands and Minni's line suggests some showdown value (weak K, J, or small pair). Check back and see if A-high wins occasionally.
J6 offsuit in UTG is too weak to open. This hand is well outside any reasonable UTG opening range. Easy fold.

I am holding 2h 3h in UTG+1. While these are suited, they are the lowest possible suited connectors and carry very little equity. In a five-handed game, opening with such a weak hand from early position is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with 9s 4s. The action has folded to me. While the Button is an advantageous position to steal the blinds, 9-4 suited is a very weak hand, falling outside a standard opening range even for a wide button strategy. The Small Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has shown willingness to play pots, and the Big Blind (Elon) is also capable of defending. Opening here would be too loose and likely unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the disciplined decision.

I’m in the small blind with A♠6♥ and everyone folded to me — heads-up vs the big blind (Elon). Limping gives him great pot odds and cedes initiative OOP; A6 is strong enough (Ace-blocker, decent high-card equity) to open. I want to take the initiative and deny him cheap odds, but keep the sizing reasonable vs deep stacks. I’ll raise to ~3BB (300 total) to isolate and steal the blind. If Elon folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed postflop cautiously out of position; if he 3-bets big I’ll likely fold to preserve my deep stack.

Qd9d is a strong suited broadway hand for defending the BB against a SB raise, especially with Minni's history of aggression suggesting a wide range. Excellent pot odds (200 to call into 400 pot, plus implied odds with deep stacks). Equity realization postflop is feasible despite OOP. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks too much with this hand. Prefer calling to control pot and play flops.

Flop K♠ Q♣ 5♠ — I hold A♠6♥: Ace-high with only one spade (a backdoor flush possibility, not a one-card flush draw) and no pair. Elon defended the big blind to my 3BB open, so his continuing range is wide but contains a lot of Kx/Qx and other made hands that currently beat me. I’m out of position and don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand that has limited immediate equity; a c-bet here will often be called by better hands and put me in tough spots on later streets. I’ll check to control the pot and gather information — plan is to call a reasonable probe bet to see a turn (realize my equity and leverage my Ace blocker), but fold to a large raise.

Preflop call with Qd9d was standard as a strong suited broadway defender OOP vs SB open. Flop Qc Ks 5s hits top pair top kicker? No, top pair weak kicker (9), but good equity vs Minni's wide SB raising range which includes many Qx, worse pairs, draws. Board has Ks 5s (spade flush draw possible, K overcard scary). Minni checks as aggressor, representing possible weakness or trap, but her history shows aggression so likely to fold worse. Betting for value/protection denies equity to her draws (spades, gutshots) and worse hands like Ax, lower pairs. Pot 600, bet 2/3 pot (400) balances value/thin value while keeping pot manageable OOP. Avoids check-raise risk from monsters. Strong spot to build pot with top pair.

I checked the K♠ Q♣ 5♠ flop to control the pot OOP with A♠6♥. Elon’s 400 into a 1000 pot is a small probe/continuation bet that can include Kx/Qx, medium pairs, some draws and bluffs. I have Ace-high with a backdoor spade possibility and I block many Ax combos, so I have some showdown value and realistic equity against his wide defending range. Calling 400 keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity, and lets me see a turn card to re-evaluate — I’ll fold to a large raise on the turn unless I improve (pair an Ace or pick up a clear draw). Raising OOP with only Ace-high would be reckless, so calling is the best line here.

Turn: Qc Ks 5s 2s — I hold A♠6♥. The turn gives me a powerful draw: I now have the nut spade blocker and a 4-flush (9 outs to the nut flush) plus the possibility of pairing the Ace. Elon’s small flop c-bet (400) into a wide defending range means he can have Kx/Qx, medium pairs, some missed draws, and occasional two-card spade combos (which would already have a made flush). Betting here is the right semi-bluff — it builds the pot when I hit, extracts folds from medium strength hands (Kx/Qx/pairs), and denies free cards to some of his equity. I don’t want to overcommit out of position, so I choose a constructive half-to-two-thirds pot size to get fold equity while keeping my options: bet ~800 (≈55–60% pot). If he folds I take it down; if he calls I still have ~9 outs to the nut flush and can re-evaluate the river. If he raises large I will re-evaluate and likely fold to an overcommittal raise.

Facing a turn bet of 800 into 2200 after Minni check-called my flop c-bet with top pair weak kicker on a Qc Ks 5s board. The 2s turn completes the spade flush draw (three spades on board: Ks, 5s, 2s), making the board very scary. Minni's SB range was wide preflop, but her check-call flop then leading turn suggests she has improved to a flush (e.g., KXs, any two spades), a strong king (KQ, AK), or turned straight draw/value. My Qd9d has top pair but weak kicker, no flush or straight draw, and blocks only some Qx. Pot odds are 800 to 2200 (~3.75:1, needing ~21% equity), but against her likely strong range post-turn spade, my equity is lower than that OOP with reverse implied odds and poor playability. Stacks are deep (my 9400), but folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament; calling risks stacking off to better hands without enough equity or fold equity on raise. History shows Minni aggressive but capable of value betting. Best play is to fold and wait for better leverage.

I am UTG with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand and is not worth opening, especially from the earliest position at a five-handed table. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively middle position in a 5-handed game) with [2s, 4h]. This is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em, being unsuited, disconnected, and low rank. Facing a standard structure with no ante, there is no pot odds or playability justification to enter the pot. The only standard and profitable play is to fold immediately.

I’m on the button with 4♦6♥ — a weak offsuit hand but I have position and an opportunity to steal the blinds. The pot is 150 and the min-raise is 200; I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind). This size gives reasonable fold equity against the blinds while still being a small portion of my 20k+ stack. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold — I don’t want to inflate the pot with this hand. If called, I’ll use position to c-bet on dry, favorable flops and pot-control or fold on highly connected/face-card boards unless I improve.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). My hand is T3o, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Button ranges are wide but still crush this hand. Poor position postflop, multiway potential if BB defends. No fold equity on a raise here. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Pot odds insufficient given ~10-15% equity at best against button range.

I have 2h8c in the BB - one of the weakest possible holdings. Minni raised to 300 and I need to call 200 more. While I'm getting decent pot odds (~2.25:1), 28o plays terribly postflop with no connectivity or high card value. Easy fold.
I am in UTG position with 3s 5c, which is a very weak unsuited hand. With 110 big blinds, there is no reason to play this hand from early position. The correct play is to fold immediately.

5♠3♣ is a very weak, unconnected offsuit hand in early position (UTG+1) at a five-handed table. There's no raise yet so calling 100 would be a limp out of position into aggressive players (Button and the blinds) and will often put me in multi-way pots with poor equity and bad implied odds. Raising to the min (200) is thin and invites 3-bets or difficult postflop decisions. With a healthy 20k stack I should preserve chips and wait for a much stronger spot — fold this one.

Qc2d offsuit is a very weak hand with poor postflop playability and gets dominated by better queens or any ace. Even on the button with two folds ahead, it's not in a profitable stealing range against these opponents who defend aggressively in blinds based on history (Claude and Flash frequently raise/3bet pre and cbet). Deep stacks (93BB effective) favor patience; folding preserves stack for superior spots to maximize long-term EV.

Folded to me in the small blind with A6o. This is a clear raising hand heads up against the big blind. A6o is well above average for heads-up play and I should raise to try to take the blinds or play with initiative. Standard raise to 250.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, Queen-Ten offsuit is a very strong hand. Claude (Small Blind) raised to 250, and I have 150 more to call from the Big Blind. The pot odds (150 to win 500) and the fact that I will have position throughout the hand make this an easy call. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on Claude, calling allows me to play a pot in position against a likely wide Small Blind opening range without bloating the pot unnecessarily with a non-premium holding.

I raised preflop with A6o and got called by Flash in the BB. Flop is 9d Tc 3d - I've completely missed with just ace high and no diamond draw. This board connects well with BB calling ranges (Tx, 9x, pocket pairs, diamond draws). With no equity besides catching an ace, I'll check back for pot control and take a free card rather than c-betting into a board that likely hits my opponent.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen kicker. Claude, who raised pre-flop from the small blind, has checked to me. This board (9-10-3 with two diamonds) is fairly coordinated, offering several straight and flush draws. I should bet here for value against weaker pairs (like 9x or pocket pairs below tens) and draws, while also protecting my hand against potential overcards (Ace, King, Jack) that could appear on the turn. A bet of 300 in a 500-chip pot is a standard sizing to accomplish these goals.

I have ace-high with no pair, no diamond draw, and no real backdoor equity. Flash bet 300 into 500 on a 9dTc3d board that hits BB calling ranges well. I'm getting 2.67:1 odds but ace-high rarely has enough equity here against a range that includes Tx, 9x, pocket pairs, and diamond draws. Floating with just 3 aces as outs (and even those could lose to two pair on later streets) is -EV. Standard fold.

UTG with K2o is a very weak hand — poor kicker, unsuited, and gets crushed by any reasonable opener. In unopened action the standard is to open only a fairly tight early-position range. There are four players to act behind (including aggressive players who 3‑bet or defend frequently), so playing this hand out of position is a losing long-term spot. Calling 100 or min‑raising to 200 would commit chips in a marginal situation with little fold equity or postflop playability. With a healthy stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold preflop.

79s is a strong suited connector with excellent playability in a 5-handed game with deep stacks. Position is UTG+1, which is early but acceptable for speculative hands like this in short-handed play. Standard opening raise of 3x BB to take initiative, build the pot, and realize equity OOP. Folding would be too tight; limping is poor strategy. Raise size is standard at 300 into 150 pot.

I have pocket Aces - the best starting hand in poker. Elon raised to 300 from UTG+1. With AA on the button, I should 3-bet for value. Standard sizing would be around 3x, so 900-1000. I want to build the pot while still getting called by worse hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 3h, which is a very weak hand. There has been an open-raise from Elon and a 3-bet from Claude. 5-3 offsuit has almost no equity against the ranges of an early position raiser and a button 3-bettor. I should fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from UTG+1 and a 3-bet from the Button. My hand, Qs3s, is very weak and not suitable for calling a 3-bet, especially out of position against two opponents who have shown strength. The pot odds do not justify a call with a hand that is easily dominated and difficult to play post-flop. Folding is the only viable option to preserve my stack.

79s is a playable hand facing a button 3-bet after my UTG+1 open. Claude's range is wide and polarized given button position and history of aggression (frequent raises and 3-bets). Equity realization is strong: ~35-40% vs typical 3-bet range (88+, broadway suited, Ax suited, connectors), with suited spade offering nut flush and straight potential. Pot odds excellent: 650 to call into 1400 (3.15:1 immediate, plus deep stack implied odds ~100bb effective). OOP but can control pot postflop by check-fold misses or check-raise value/draws. Folding too nitty in 5-handed dynamic spot; 4-bet risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand. Call to realize equity and play postflop.

79s misses the Kd Jd 4s flop completely: no pair, no straight draw, only backdoor spade flush draw and negligible straight outs. Equity is low (~15-25%) against Claude's button 3-bet range, which connects well with Kx, Jx, AdXd, QJs+, sets. As OOP first to act in a 2050 pot with deep stacks, check to control pot size and induce bluffs/checks from his air (suited connectors, Ax suited). Betting risks raising into better or folding OOP; check-raise bluff too thin with poor playability. If he checks, free card to hit turn outs (spade, 10/8/5/6/2 for runner-runner straights/flushes). If bets small, can fold or peel if odds good; expect to fold most bets to realize fold equity later. Prioritizes survival and pot control over spewing with marginal bluff.

I have AA on a Kd Jd 4s board - an overpair. Elon checked to me after calling my 3-bet. This is a wet board with two diamonds (flush draw possible) and connected broadway cards. I need to bet for value and protection. Standard c-bet sizing around 60% pot (~1200) makes sense to charge draws and get value from Kx, Jx hands.

79s completely whiffs the KdJd4s flop: no pair, no straight draw (79 doesn't connect meaningfully with KJ4), only backdoor spade flush draw (need two running spades) and gut-wrench equity ~10-20% against Claude's polarized button 3-bet range which crushes this board (KJx, AdXd suited, QTs+, sets like 44/KK/JJ/AA). Facing a 1200 bet into ~2050 pot (~60% pot size) OOP in a 3250 pot, pot odds are ~2.7:1 effective (1200 to win 2050+1200=3250), requiring ~23% equity which we don't have, and implied odds are minimal with no hand to realize them postflop (will fold to turn aggression). Deep stacks but no fold equity later with air; check-fold is disciplined pot control as planned preflop/flop thoughts. Claude's history shows strength on coordinated boards (e.g., value bets with top pair+ post-3bet). Folding preserves 8400 stack for better spots in 5-handed tournament, avoiding spew vs aggressive button.

9hTc is a strong suited connector with good playability postflop. In 5-handed play, UTG opening range includes suited connectors like this for their implied odds and potential to make strong hands. Raising takes the initiative, builds the pot, and applies pressure on the blinds. Standard raise size of 3x the BB (300) is appropriate given the deep stacks and observed opening sizes in prior hands (250-300). Folding or limping UTG would be too passive.

A6o is a marginal hand facing an UTG raise. While I have position on Elon, A6 offsuit plays poorly against a typical UTG opening range - I'll often be dominated by better aces if I hit. Not worth calling or 3-betting here.

I am on the Button with Jd 7s. Elon, the UTG player, has opened for 300. J7o is a weak hand that does not play well against an early position opening range, especially when facing a 3x raise. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 2c, which is statistically one of the weakest hands in Hold'em. An opponent (Elon) has raised to 300 from UTG, indicating a potentially strong range. I am out of position and the pot odds do not justify calling with such poor equity and playability. The correct and standard play is to fold immediately.

Elon opened UTG to 300. I’m in the BB with 6♠7♣ — a marginal, connected offsuit hand. Pot is 450 and I need to add 200 (300 total) to call, which gives ~31% break-even equity and solid implied odds with deep stacks. I’m out of position postflop and Elon’s UTG range is relatively strong, so a light 3-bet is not ideal. I’ll call to see a flop cheaply and proceed cautiously: fold to significant aggression on dry boards, continue only with a pair+ or a clear draw (or very favorable runout), and otherwise give up OOP to preserve my deep stack for better spots.

Check. I missed this flop — 6♠7♣ on Q♠9♠4♣ is a blank: no pair and only a single spade (backdoor flush ~3.3% to make by river) with only low runner-runner straight potential. Elon was the UTG preflop raiser so his continuing range contains many Qx/9x, spade combos and pocket pairs that connect much better than my hand. I’m out of position and OOP bluffs here are poor value and vulnerable to calls or check‑raises. I’ll check to see the turn cheaply and preserve my deep stack; plan is to fold to meaningful c-bets and only continue if I pair a 6/7, pick up a clear draw, or Elon checks turn as well (re‑evaluate then). If he fires a very small probe (tiny % of pot) I may call for pot odds, but otherwise this is a fold‑to‑bet spot.

Middle pair with a decent Ten kicker on a Qs9s4c flop. The board has a spade flush draw, but my UTG raising range is perceived as strong, including QQ, 99, Q9, AQs, etc., which hits this board well. Minni's BB calling range is wide and often weak, as seen in prior hands where she defends light but folds to aggression. C-betting for value and protection against overcards, straight draws (e.g., JT, T8), and the flush draw. Half-pot size balances value from worse pairs/draws while keeping pot manageable with deep stacks. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or letting her bluff. Pot control secondary to realizing equity.

Elon (UTG) opened pre to 300 and I defended the BB with 6♠7♣. Flop Q♠9♠4♣ — I checked and Elon c-bet 325. The pot is 975 and I must call 325, so I need roughly 25% equity to break even (325 to win ~1300). My hand missed: no pair, only weak backdoor possibilities (runner‑runner flush/straight); the chance to make a pair by the river is about 24% and the backdoor flush adds only a few percent — overall my improvement equity is marginal and essentially at or below the break‑even threshold. I’m out of position versus an UTG raiser whose c‑bet range contains lots of Qx/9x/spade and pocket pairs that dominate me. Raising as a bluff OOP here is risky (I lack useful blockers and will be crushed by calls/3‑bets). With deep stacks but a dry board and little fold equity, the correct play is to preserve chips for better spots and fold this marginal spot.
KTo from UTG - a decent but not premium hand. Given my stack depth (121bb) and position, this is a standard open raise hand. I'll make a standard 2.5x open to 250.

I am in UTG+1 with 6h 4d, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised from UTG. Given my position and the strength of my hand relative to a standard UTG opening range, this is an easy fold. There is no reason to risk chips with a bottom-tier hand in this spot.

I hold Pocket Threes (3d 3h) on the Button. Claude raised to 250 from UTG. Given the deep stack sizes (effective stack ~109 big blinds) and the small raise size relative to the stacks (250 is roughly 2.3% of my stack), I have excellent implied odds to 'set mine'. I am in position, which allows me to control the pot size or bluff if checked to on favorable boards. Folding is too tight for a pocket pair in position, and 3-betting turns my hand into a bluff against a strong UTG range. Calling is the most profitable play to try and hit a set.

I have 4♣5♠ in the small blind facing an UTG open to 250 with the button calling. Pot is 650 and I must call 200 (min raise to 400). 4‑5 offsuit is a speculative hand that plays poorly out of position, especially multi‑way against an UTG open and a caller; postflop I’ll be dominated or out of position with little fold equity. Although the immediate pot odds are reasonable, the combination of range disadvantage, likely multi‑way action, and deep stacks makes calling or 3‑betting unprofitable here. I’ll fold and conserve chips for a better spot.

Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 250 and a button call from Pro. In the BB with 6d Td suited connectors, which has good playability, straight and flush potential, and solid implied odds in a multiway pot with deep stacks (mine 86.5BB, others similar). Pot odds are excellent: calling an additional 150 into 650 (total pot 800, ~4.3:1). Folding is too tight OOP vs these ranges; 3-betting not ideal as a bluff OOP with two opponents. Calling keeps the pot multiway, realizes equity well postflop.

On the flop of Kh6sJc rainbow, holding Td6d for bottom pair with a Ten kicker in a multiway pot (Claude UTG raise, Pro button call). OOP as BB acting first. Bottom pair has showdown value but is vulnerable to any King, Jack, overpair, or better. Leading OOP risks folding out worse hands while getting raised by better; better to check and pot control, induce bluffs from missed draws, or check-raise value if warranted. Excellent pot odds if faced with a bet, but check keeps options open multiway with deep stacks. Folding preflop equity was correct; now check to realize equity efficiently.

Flopped top pair with KT on Kh 6s Jc board. This is a strong hand - I have TPGK (top pair good kicker). Elon checked to me. I should bet for value to get called by worse kings (K9, K8, etc.), Jx hands, and any draws. Betting around 50% pot is standard here.

I hold Pocket Threes (3d 3h) and missed my set on a board of Kh 6s Jc. The flop connects heavily with the UTG opener's range (Kings, Jacks, broadway draws). Facing a half-pot c-bet of 400 into 800, I have essentially 2 outs (approx. 4% equity) and no showdown value. Floating is not profitable here as I am likely drawing nearly dead against top pair or better. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Facing Claude's UTG continuation bet of 400 (1/3 pot) into 1200 on a dry rainbow Kh6sJc flop. Holding Td6d for bottom pair (6s), decent Ten kicker, plus backdoor diamond flush draw. Pot odds are excellent: 400 to call into 1600 (~25% equity needed). Claude's UTG range (JJ+, AK, AQ+, KQ, AJs+, etc.) includes top pairs (K,J), sets, overpairs, but also some air he c-bets for value/protection. Board is fairly draw-light (no flush draws, limited straight draws like Q9o/T9s). Pro's button fold to the bet suggests weakness, leaving us heads-up deep (~85BB effective). Bottom pair has ~35% equity vs his value range, higher vs bluffs; folding OOP would be too tight after correct preflop defense with suited connector playability. Raising OOP is thin and risks stacking off light. Calling realizes equity efficiently, controls pot OOP, and keeps implied odds alive if we improve or he has worse (e.g., overpair, missed draw). Plan to check/call turn if reasonable or fold to aggression if board worsens.

Heads-up on turn vs Claude's UTG range after calling his flop c-bet of 400 into ~1200 pot, now pot 1600. Board Kh6sJc9h, holding Td6d for bottom pair + ten kicker, which has decent showdown value but vulnerable to his value range (KJ, KQ, AJs, TT+, QQ+, sets). The 9h turn brings possible QTs OESD and heart flush draws, slightly dynamic but he may continue betting value/protection. OOP first to act, leading out risks getting check-raised by better or folding out worse while bloating pot awkwardly. Checking realizes equity efficiently: induces bluffs/checks behind from missed draws/overpairs, pot controls, and allows calling a reasonable bet (~600-1000) where we have ~35% equity vs his range (blockers to straights/flushes minimal, but live T helps). Deep stacks (~80BB) support calling down lighter for implied odds if we hit river improvement (straight/flush unlikely but possible). Folding too weak after investing flop; raising too thin OOP. Plan: check, call reasonable bet or fold bomb, showdown if checked behind.

Turn brought the 9h, completing QT for a straight. Board now has K-J-9 which is quite connected. My top pair with T kicker is decent but vulnerable. Elon check-called flop and checked again - could have Kx, Jx, draws, or now two pair with 9x. Given the dangerous board texture and Elon's continued check-calling pattern, I should check back for pot control. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to build a big pot.

River 9s pairs the 9h from turn, making the board Kh6sJc9h9s (paired 9s). Holding Td6d, I now have two pair (99 and 66, using my 6d with board 6s and board 99), with T kicker. This is a strong hand on this board, ahead of most of Claude's UTG range that didn't bomb turn/river (Kx top pair, Jx, weaker pairs/draws that missed). His flop c-bet shows strength, turn check suggests caution/medium strength (not sets/monsters likely), but he will call value bets with overpairs (TT+/QQ+ make two pair TT99/QQ99 beating mine, but AxK/AQ often fold), top pairs, and some bluffs/draws. OOP first, checking risks missing value from worse (KJ, AT, air) and showdowns only. Value betting ~60% pot extracts from his calling range while protecting against unlikely 9x trips (rare in UTG raise). Deep stacks support sizing up 1000 (leaves room for calls/shoves if raised). Folds too weak; overbet unnecessary. Plan: bet 1000 for thin value/max EV.

River paired the 9s. I have KdTc for two pair (Kings and Nines). Elon check-called flop, we both checked turn, and now he donk-bets 1000 into 1600 after the board pairs. This line screams strength - specifically trips. His calling range from BB includes many 9x hands (T9, 98, 97, A9, Q9) that now have trips. The small donk bet looks like a value bet trying to get paid. I'm getting 3.6:1 odds (need ~28% equity), but his line is very consistent with having caught trips on the river. Two pair loses to all his 9x combos. Folding is prudent here.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Eight offsuit (Qs 8h). This is a weak starting hand, especially from early position in a 5-handed game. Standard opening ranges for UTG in this structure generally exclude Q8o, as it is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of players like Elon and Claude to be aggressive, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I hold 3h 7h in UTG+1 (effectively Cutoff in this 5-handed configuration). This is a very weak hand with low equity and poor playability. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from any position, especially early/middle position. There is no reason to enter the pot.

I’m on the button with Q♦6♦ — a marginal hand but strong enough to open in position. Action’s folded to me, so I want to apply pressure and steal the blinds while keeping the pot manageable. Table preflop open sizes have been ~250–300, so raising to 300 looks natural and gets decent fold equity from both blinds. If I get called I’ll use my positional advantage to C‑bet favorable flops; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold Q6o (it’s not worth playing a big pot OOP). Goal is to accumulate chips with low‑risk pressure — raise to 300.

KK is an extremely strong premium hand, second only to AA preflop. Facing a button raise after two folds, Minni's range is likely wide and includes many steals, suited connectors, Ax, broadway cards, etc. As SB OOP, calling keeps the pot multiway potentially and gives up initiative. 3-betting isolates Minni, builds a pot with massive equity advantage (~82% vs typical button open), and pressures her wide range. BB (Claude) may fold or call light but unlikely to 4-bet light. Sizing to 900 (3x) is standard OOP 3-bet size into 3bb open, balancing value and fold equity without overcommitting deep stacks (~100BB). Shoving too strong, calling weak.

T4o is a very weak hand facing a 3-bet. Elon 3-bet to 900 after Minni's open, showing significant strength. I'd need to call 800 into a 1300 pot with a hand that plays poorly post-flop and is dominated by any reasonable 3-betting range. Clear fold.

I opened to 300 from the button to apply pressure, but Elon’s quick 3‑bet to 900 puts me to a tough decision.
