I’d have to call 600 into a 1300 pot (pot after my call ~1900), so I’m getting roughly 31.6% required equity to break even. Q♦6♦ (offsuit in suits: Qh6d) is easily dominated by the value portion of a typical 3‑bet range and has poor multi‑street playability here. 4‑betting to the min (1500) is clearly the wrong move with this hand. Elon has shown the ability to 3‑bet for value and occasionally aggression, but I don’t have a reliable read he’s doing so light enough to justify calling. Preserve the stack, fold, and wait for a better spot in position.

I am in UTG with Pocket Tens (Td Th), a premium pair. With 5 players left and deep stacks (100+ BB), this is a mandatory open for value. I want to build the pot and discourage multi-way action to protect my hand against overcards. A standard opening size of 2.5x (250) is consistent with table dynamics and my previous play.

Facing an UTG open to 250 (50/100), pot is 400 and it costs me 250 to call. I have Q♣9♥ in UTG+1 — a marginal offsuit queen. Against an early-position opener the raiser’s range is relatively strong and contains many hands that dominate Q9o; postflop I’d often be out of position and have poor playability. A tiny 3-bet to 400 is ineffective and a larger 3-bet with this holding is too speculative. With a healthy 196BB stack I can wait for a better, position-friendly spot. Fold and conserve chips.

78o is a marginal speculative hand offsuit, but on the button with deep stacks and excellent position, it has good playability postflop for straight draws and potential floats. UTG raiser (Pro) has shown some wide ranges and calling tendencies in past hands. Pot odds are favorable (calling 250 into 400, ~3.6:1 implied odds), and multiway potential if SB/BB call, but position allows controlling the pot. Folding would be too tight; 3-betting not suitable for this hand. Calling to realize equity and implied odds while maximizing fold equity later.

KJo in the SB facing a UTG raise to 250 and button call. Getting good pot odds (200 to win 650). KJo plays decently multiway. A 3-bet squeeze would be aggressive but KJo is marginal for that against UTG raise. Calling to see a flop is the standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8c 4s. Pro raised from UTG to 250, and both the Button (Elon) and Small Blind (Claude) have called. While I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 1000), 84o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots and is difficult to play out of position. Defending too wide with junk hands like this often leads to 'chip bleeding' post-flop. Given that Pro raised from UTG, their range is likely strong, and the probability of 84o realizing its equity is low. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Flopped QQ2 with two diamonds. I have KJ with no pair, just overcards. On a paired board multiway, I should check. I have no equity worth betting - my best shot is to see free cards. If someone bets, I can fold without investing more chips. Checking is standard here with air.

I hold Pocket Tens on a Qd Qs 2d board in a 3-way pot. Although I have an overpair to the Deuce, the paired Queens heavily favor the callers' ranges (especially broadway hands containing a Queen). Betting here is risky as I likely only get called by better hands (Qx, JJ+) or strong draws, and I fold out worse hands that I beat (small pairs, random air). By checking, I exercise pot control, avoid getting raised, and can evaluate the turn. I am out of position against the Button (Elon), so checking to the potential aggressor is a standard play with a medium-strength showdown hand on a paired board.

Flop is Qd Qs 2d, a paired board with a diamond flush draw possible. My 7h8d has no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw—just complete air with minimal equity against the UTG raiser's (Pro) strong range and Claude's calling range. Both checked to me on the button, giving me position post-turn/river. Pot is 850 with deep stacks. Betting here risks getting raised by value hands like QQ, AQ, AK, KK+, or even sets/draws, and I have no fold equity reliably as villain checked UTG strength. Semi-bluffing is poor without equity. Checking preserves stack, sees turn for free (hoping to hit runner-runner or improve to bluff card), controls pot, and keeps initiative for future streets with position. Folding is unnecessary since unchecked. Maximize stack by avoiding spew.

Turn 4h doesn't change anything. Everyone checked the flop, suggesting nobody has a queen. With KJ high, I have showdown value if it checks through - K high could win. Betting here as a bluff is risky since I'd be targeting hands that might call (small pairs, Ax). Better to check and see a free river, potentially winning at showdown or taking a stab if a good card comes.

The flop was checked around 3-way, suggesting that no one has a strong Queen or is slow-playing. The turn is a brick (4h). While I have an overpair to the 2 and 4, my hand (TT) is vulnerable to a random Queen and finding value is thin. Betting here likely folds out worse hands (A-high, small pairs) and isolates me against Qx or strong draws. Being out of position against the Button (Elon), checking is the prudent play for pot control. It keeps the opponents' ranges wide, allowing me to potentially catch a bluff or get to showdown cheaply without inflating the pot with a marginal made hand.

On the turn with 7h8d on a QdQs2d4h board, I have complete air—no pair, no draws, just 78 offsuit with negligible equity against Pro's UTG raising range (likely QQ+, AK, AQ, etc.) and Claude's speculative calling range from SB. Both opponents have checked again, which could represent traps with strong hands or weakness, but the paired board favors value-heavy holdings that can call or raise a bluff. Pot is 850, my stack 9850 effective. Betting as a semi-bluff lacks fold equity given the board texture and opponents' tendencies (Pro has shown strength in past hands like #1, #26; Claude calls light but folds to aggression sometimes). Raising preflop callers with air risks spew. Checking preserves my stack, takes free river card (hoping for a scare card like an ace or diamond to bluff), maintains pot control in position, and avoids difficult spots. Long-term stack maximization prioritizes not bleeding chips in hopeless multiway spots—patience for better setups.

River on QQ274 board with KJ high. Everyone checked flop and turn, suggesting no one has a queen. K-high could actually win at showdown against hands like Ax or smaller unpaired cards. Betting as a bluff would only fold out hands I already beat while getting called by pocket pairs that beat me. Check and take it to showdown.

I hold Pocket Tens on a board of Qd-Qs-2d-4h-7s. The action has been checked through on both the flop and turn, which strongly suggests that neither opponent holds a Queen, as they likely would have bet for value or protection by now. My hand is effectively two pair (Queens and Tens) and is very likely the best hand. Betting here is thin value; I would only be targeted by hands like 88, 99, or a rivered pair of 7s, while folding out all air and missed draws (like diamonds). By checking, I induce the Button (Elon) to potentially bluff with missed draws or air, and I ensure I get to showdown without reopening the action to a difficult raise. It is a standard line to take showdown value in a checked-down pot.

River 7s pairs my 7h, giving me middle pair (sevens) with an 8 kicker on a Qd Qs 2d 4h 7s board. This has decent showdown value against UTG raiser Pro's range (likely QQ, AQ, KQ, AK but checked twice suggesting not nuts) and SB Claude's wide calling range. Many hands like worse Qx (QJ-Q6), Ax high, missed diamond/spade draws (spades now complete but if they had spade draw without Qs might have bet), or lower pairs can call a bet. All checks pre-river indicate caution, so half-pot bet extracts thin value while pot is small (850). Position protects if raised. Checking misses value opportunities against villains' passive tendencies seen in history (e.g., Hand #2, #10). Betting maximizes EV and stack growth.

Elon bet 425 into 1275 after everyone checked flop and turn. With KJ high, I beat nothing that's value betting. His small bet (33% pot) looks like thin value - likely a pocket pair like 55-JJ that checked back earlier and now extracts value, or possibly a Queen slowplaying. K-high can't call profitably here - I only beat bluffs, and his line doesn't scream bluff. Fold.

The hand was checked through on the Flop and Turn, strongly suggesting that Elon does not hold a Queen, as he would likely have bet for value or protection on the draw-heavy board. His delayed aggression on the River (betting ~1/3 pot) likely represents a stab with missed draws (diamonds) or thin value with a hand like 88, 99, or a rivered pair of 7s. My Pocket Tens are effectively the best hand here against this range. Raising would isolate me against better hands and fold out his bluffs, so a call is the correct decision to capture value from his bluffs and weaker made hands.

Five‑handed, deep stacks (~196 BB) — open a much wider UTG range than full ring. Q9s is a playable, suited Broadway‑ish hand with good postflop playability and useful blockers. I want to take the initiative, deny cheap multiway pots, and build a pot I can play postflop, so I’ll open to a standard 3x size. Raise to 300 total (3x the BB). If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold; I’ll evaluate smaller 3‑bets based on who and sizing and continue postflop with position plans and pot control.

5-handed game with deep stacks (I'm at ~94BB). Q9d suited is a speculative hand with good playability, straight and flush potential. Facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has shown aggression in prior hands (frequent raises preflop). In short-handed play, UTG ranges are wider. I have position on the raiser and am getting reasonable odds (450 pot to 300 call). Calling allows me to realize equity postflop, potentially multiway or heads-up, with implied odds against Minni's deep stack. Folding is too tight; 3-betting is possible as a bluff but Q9s prefers seeing flops. Plan to play postflop cautiously.
7-3 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even with button position, this hand has poor playability and equity against a raise and a call. Not worth investing 300 chips here.

I am in the Small Blind holding K3o. Minni raised from UTG, and Elon called from UTG+1. K3o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot against an early position raiser. Calling would require investing 250 more into a pot where I am likely dominated or will struggle to realize my equity post-flop. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 6h facing a raise from UTG (Minni) to 300 and a call from UTG+1 (Elon). The pot is 750 and it costs me 200 to call, offering pot odds of about 21%. However, 96o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two players with likely strong ranges. The risk of domination and the difficulty of realizing equity make this a clear fold despite the attractive pot odds.

I opened UTG and was called by one opponent, Elon. I hold Q♥9♥ and the flop is 3♣-5♠-4♣ — a very dry, low, rainbow-ish board that misses most of a caller’s typical calling range (broadways, A/Kx, many pocket pairs). As the preflop aggressor I should take the initiative and c‑bet to fold out those missed hands and deny free cards. I don’t have a pair or a strong draw (only a weak backdoor heart potential), so I’ll use a small‑to‑medium sizing to preserve pot control while keeping decent fold equity. Bet 300 (≈0.4 pot). Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls, re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue with equity or a good turn card; fold to a sizable raise since my hand has very little showdown value here.

Heads-up pot on a 3c-5s-4c flop after calling Minni's UTG raise with Qd9d suited. My hand has no pair, no straight draw (board offers wheel and 6-7 open-enders, but Q9d doesn't connect), no flush draw (wrong suits), just queen-high with backdoor diamond potential at best. Minni's UTG range likely contains pairs (44-66, maybe 77+), sets (33,44,55), or strong draws (AcXc, 62s, 76s), and her small 300 bet into 1050 pot (28% pot) smells like protection/value/thin value rather than a bluff. Pot odds are ~3.5:1, but with minimal equity (~20-30% vs her range max) and poor implied odds going forward (my hand has little showdown value), folding preserves stack in a deep-stack tournament. Position helps, but this spot is a clear fold to avoid spew.

Ac6s is a weak ace from UTG in 5-handed play with deep stacks (~91BB). Suited but low kicker, easily dominated by AQo+, ATs+, etc. Poor post-flop playability OOP against likely 3-bettors or calls. Opponents deep and aggressive based on history (frequent raises). Standard GTO fold; preserve stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.
Q2o is a very weak hand, especially from UTG+1 position. This is well outside any reasonable opening range from early position. Easy fold.

I am on the button with 8h 6d. While the button is a prime stealing position, 86 offsuit is quite weak. Looking at the players in the blinds, Minni has a massive stack and has shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet, while Pro has also been active. Given the stack depths and the tendency of the blinds to contest pots, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

I am in the Small Blind with T3o, a very weak hand. Action has folded to me. The Big Blind (Minni) has a large stack and has shown aggression recently. Playing this hand out of position is -EV. Even though it only costs 50 to call, the reverse implied odds and lack of playability make folding the correct disciplined decision.
AJo is a solid opening hand from UTG. Standard raise to 2.5x BB to build the pot and take control of the hand.

I am holding Ten of Hearts and Trey of Diamonds, which is a very weak hand (T3o). Claude has raised to 250 from UTG, showing strength or at least a standard opening range from early position. Playing such a weak hand out of position against an UTG open is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with Q7o facing a raise to 250 from Claude in the UTG position. An UTG opening range is typically tight and strong (pairs, strong aces, broadways), heavily dominating my Q7o. Although I have position, Q7o plays poorly post-flop and lacks the equity to call profitably or the blockers to 3-bet bluff effectively against an early position raise. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from 5 players, I’m in the small blind with K♠4♣. This is a weak, easily dominated hand (many of the raiser’s hands contain a King with a better kicker or stronger broadways/pairs). Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% pot odds, but K4o won’t have that equity vs an UTG opening range and is hard to play out of position, especially with the big blind still to act. A bluff 3-bet here is unnecessary and risky with a poor kicker. Preserve stack and wait for a better, more playable spot — fold.

Pocket nines is a strong premium hand preflop, especially in the big blind facing an isolated UTG raise from Claude after folds from Flash, Pro, and Minni (SB). Claude's UTG range is likely strong (e.g., 88+, AJs+, KQs+, AKo), but 99 has excellent equity against it (~45-55% vs typical ranges) and good postflop playability. With deep stacks (~90BB effective), 3-betting builds the pot for value, applies fold equity (Claude may fold medium-strength hands), and isolates us heads-up. Calling risks multiway pots if reraised and plays awkwardly out of position. Folding is too tight. Sizing to 900 (3.6x the raise) is standard for value/protection without overcommitting.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon in the BB with AJo. This is a standard spot - AJo is too strong to fold but not strong enough to 4-bet for value. I'm getting decent pot odds (650 to win 1200) and have position in a heads-up pot. Calling to see a flop is the standard play here.

Flopped top set with 99 on Qh9c4s, an excellent board for us with some backdoor draws and possible flush draws but mostly favors our hand. Claude's check after calling our 3-bet pre-flop suggests he doesn't have QQ (likely would check-raise or lead), AQ/KQ might check or bet, but his range includes overpairs (AA-JJ, TT?), top pair hands, and some bluffs. We bet for value against those, to protect against overcards or live draws (e.g., spade flush draw, gutshots like T-J), and to build the pot with our nuts. Pot is 1850, betting ~2/3 pot (1250) is standard sizing: large enough for value/protection without folding out too many calls, while keeping SPR manageable post-flop. Deep stacks allow flexibility. Avoid check-raising setup since we lead.

I have AJo on a Qh9c4s board - completely missed. Elon 3-bet preflop and is now c-betting 1250 into a ~1850 pot. I have just ace-high with no pair, no real draws except backdoor gutshot. The board favors his 3-betting range more than my calling range. With 1250 to call into 3100, I need about 29% equity, but I'm essentially drawing to 3 aces (6 outs at best) against a range that likely contains many queens, pocket pairs, and better hands. Clear fold.

I am UTG with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). This is one of the weakest hands in poker and should never be played from the earliest position, even in a short-handed 5-player game. With a healthy stack of 81 big blinds, there is no reason to take unnecessary risks with junk hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold Pocket Tens (Th Td) in UTG+1 (effectively UTG after Flash folded). This is a strong starting hand that sits comfortably near the top of my range for this position. With stacks deep (100bb+), I want to raise to build the pot and define ranges, rather than limp which invites multi-way action where Tens are vulnerable to overcards. A standard open size of 2.5bb (250) has been effective in previous hands (e.g., Hand 1, Hand 39, Hand 51) and balances my opening range.

Hand: K♦4♠ on the button facing an UTG+1 open to 250 (pot 400, to call 250). Pro’s early-position open represents a fairly strong, value-heavy range that dominates most Kx hands. K4o has very poor playability post‑flop (frequently dominated, few backdoor draws) and calling 250 into this pot invites multistreet decisions out of a marginal holding. A min 3‑bet to 400 would be too small to fold out better hands and too thin as a bluff; a larger 3‑bet is unnecessary with such a weak hand. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Preflop in the small blind with 6d2c, one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, low cards, no connectivity or straight potential. Pro raised to 250 from early position (UTG+1), indicating a strong range. Facing a to-call of 200 into 400 pot gives ~3:1 odds, but even with dead money, the hand has zero equity against a typical raising range and plays terribly out of position post-flop. No fold equity for a squeeze given the stacks and my position. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

KTs is a strong suited hand in the BB facing a single raise from Pro. Getting good pot odds (150 to win 400). This is an easy defend - I could 3-bet but calling keeps their range wide and KTs plays well postflop with straight and flush potential.

I flopped the nut flush draw (Ks Ts) on A88 with two spades. The As is on board so my Ks gives me the best possible flush draw. I'm first to act against Pro who opened preflop. Semi-bluffing here is good - I can take it down now or build a pot with 9 clean outs to the nuts. Betting around 1/3 pot as a probe bet.

I hold Pocket Tens on a flop of 8h As 8s. The board contains an Ace, which is a scare card for my hand, and a paired 8. My opponent, Claude, who defended the Big Blind, leads out (donk bets) for 200 into a 550 pot. This small lead often indicates a weak Ace, a draw (spades), or a blocker bet with a mid-pair, though it could also be a trap with trips. Raising here would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Ax, 8x) and fold out hands I beat (bluffs, small pairs). Folding is too weak given the small bet size and the possibility that my opponent is probing or on a draw. Calling allows me to use my position to evaluate the turn, keeps the pot manageable, and keeps his bluffing range in play.

Turn brought the Kh giving me top pair Kings plus the nut flush draw. This is a huge improvement - I have a made hand now plus 9 outs to the nuts if I'm behind an Ace. Pro called my flop bet which could indicate Ax, a pocket pair, or their own draw. I should bet for value and protection - around 2/3 pot to charge draws and get value from weaker pairs while building the pot with my strong combo draw.

The turn card is the King of Hearts, making the board 8h As 8s Kh. This is a very bad card for my Pocket Tens (Th Td). The board now contains two overcards to my pair. My opponent, Claude, who led the flop, now fires a second barrel of 625 into a pot of 950. His range for defending the Big Blind includes many Kx and Ax hands, as well as 8x, all of which beat me. While he could have a flush draw (spades or hearts), he is betting into a UTG+1 opener's range that should be heavy in Ax and Kx. Betting into that strength usually indicates a strong hand. I am effectively beating only pure bluffs, and with two overcards on the board, my hand is too weak to call a ~66% pot bet. The risk-to-reward ratio is poor, and I likely have very little equity against his value range.

I am in UTG (first to act) in a 5-handed game with pocket Sixes (6h 6c). With 110bb deep stacks, this is a standard opening hand in a short-handed format. I want to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially win the blinds or play for set value post-flop. A standard open raise to 2.5bb (250 chips) is consistent with my previous play and balances my range.

Deep stacks (~200 BB) and a standard 2.5x UTG open from Pro. 6♠5♠ is a suited connector with strong implied odds and good multiway/flop playability — calling is the default here. 3-betting from UTG+1 with a speculative hand is too loose; folding is overly tight given the cheap price to see a flop. I will call the 250 to realize equity and look for straights/flushes or favorable spots to apply pressure postflop. If faced with a large 3-bet (heavy isolation), I will fold; otherwise proceed cautiously and play aggressively when I connect.

93s is a weak suited hand with significant gaps, making it difficult to realize equity post-flop. Facing an UTG raise from Pro, who often shows strength in early position, and a call from Minni, the effective ranges are strong. Even on the button with position, calling commits chips to a pot where I'll be out of position against raiser post-flop and multiway dynamics reduce fold equity. Pot odds are marginal (pot 650, call 250 into ~900), but EV is negative long-term in tournament. Folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize over 150 hands.

J8o in the SB facing a UTG raise and a cold call. This is a weak hand that plays poorly multiway and out of position. Getting ~3.25:1 odds but J8o doesn't have the playability to justify calling here - it rarely flops strong and will be difficult to navigate postflop OOP against two opponents. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c5h. The pot has been raised to 250 by Pro (UTG) and called by Minni (UTG+1). I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 800. While my hand is very weak, the pot odds are 4.33-to-1. However, being out of position against two players, one of whom is the chip leader (Minni) and the other an aggressive UTG raiser, makes this hand very difficult to play post-flop. 35-offsuit has poor playability and realization of equity. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have flopped a set of Sixes (Bottom Set) on a board of Ah-6d-Ks. This is a very strong hand, essentially the nuts given that AA and KK likely 3-bet pre-flop. The board texture is 'dry' but hits broadway ranges hard. I am out of position against Minni, who has shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands (raising post-flop, betting when checked to). On this A-K-x board, my opponent will expect me to c-bet my strong Ax hands. By checking, I can disguise the strength of my hand, protecting my checking range (which includes hands like QQ-99), and potentially induce a bluff or a value bet from a weaker Ace or King. If Minni checks back, I don't lose much value as the board is static, and I can bet the turn.

Flop is A♥ 6♦ K♠; I hold 6♠5♠ — only middle pair with a weak kicker and a two-card spade holding (a backdoor flush requires both turn and river). Pro was the UTG opener and checked the flop, so his range contains lots of Ax/Kx and stronger hands; my pair is frequently behind and vulnerable to aggression. With deep stacks and a single opponent I prefer pot control here: betting thin risks getting called by better hands (or check-raised) while only folding out a few worse hands. Plan: check and see the turn, call small-to-medium bets to realize equity or fold to clear strength; if Pro checks back a safe turn I can probe for thin value or barrel if I improve.

I hold a set of sixes on a board of Ah-6d-Ks-5c. The flop was checked through, which suggests Minni likely has a marginal hand like a weak Ace, King, or an underpair, rather than a monster, or complete air. On this turn, checking again risks Minni checking back to realize equity or pot control with hands like Kx or QJ. To extract value from these marginal hands and deny free equity to gutshots, a delayed continuation bet is the best line. I will bet slightly over half pot to get called by Kings or weak Aces.

Turn pairs my 6♠5♠ with the 5♣ — I now have two pair (6s & 5s). Pro was the UTG opener (strong preflop range), checked the flop and now fires a small probe bet of 350 into ~1000. That sizing is consistent with a wide portion of his range (single-pair A/K hands, some Kx/Ax thin value, occasional smaller pairs or bluffs) and is beat by my two pair more often than not. Folding here would be too nitty; simply calling keeps weaker hands in but leaves value on the table. I want to extract value from Ax/Kx and other worse pairs while not bloating the pot unnecessarily, so I’ll apply a minimum raise to 700 — large enough to charge hands that would otherwise call a small bet, but small enough to keep many calling ranges in and to avoid overcommitting against rare sets/two-pair combos that beat me. If Pro 3-bets big, I’ll re-evaluate (those lines will often indicate sets/two-pair A/K and I can proceed cautiously).

I hold a set of sixes (bottom set) on an Ah-6d-Ks-5c board. I bet 350 on the turn and was min-raised to 700 by Minni. This min-raise is suspicious; it often indicates a strong value hand like two pair (A5, A6, K6) or a blocking raise with a draw. I crush two pair and am only beaten by a set of 5s (unlikely given pre-flop action) or higher sets (AA/KK which likely 3-bet pre). Re-raising here would likely fold out Minni's bluffs and isolate me against the very top of their range. Since I am out of position, calling is the optimal play to conceal the strength of my hand, keep the pot manageable, and allow Minni to continue with weaker value or bluffs on the river. I need to call 350 more to match the 700 bet.

I hold a set of Sixes on an Ah-6d-Ks-5c-2d board. The action on the turn involved me betting and calling a min-raise from Minni. The river card is a relative blank (unless she holds 43 suited for a straight). Because I only called the turn raise, my range appears capped (like weak Ax or Kx), whereas Minni has represented significant strength. As the out-of-position player facing a previous street aggressor, checking is the standard and optimal play. This allows Minni to bet her value hands (Two Pair, Sets) or continue bluffing with missed draws. Leading out would likely fold out her bluffs and might scare away marginal value hands. I plan to check-raise if she bets to extract maximum value.

I have two pair (6s & 5s) on A♥ 6♦ K♠ 5♣ 2♦. Pro (UTG) opened pre, checked the flop, fired a small turn probe (350) and then called my raise to 700 — that line looks like many Ax/Kx and medium single-pair hands rather than frequent sets/two-pair combos that would usually reraise or shove. On the river he checks, which invites a value probe. Checking behind guarantees showdown but leaves value on the table against Kx and weaker Ax that will gladly call a small bet. I will bet small — ~1/3 pot — to extract from worse one-pair hands while keeping the pot manageable against the stronger two-pair/set combos. If he makes a large river raise I will treat it as a polarized/very strong line and fold or re-evaluate based on sizing; against a flat or call I expect to win or split. Bet 700 into the 2050 pot to maximise thin value.

I hold a set of Sixes on a board of Ah-6d-Ks-5c-2d. The action has been checked to the turn where I bet and Minni min-raised, which I called. On the river, I checked and Minni bet 700 into a pot of 2050. This small bet sizing (~1/3 pot) typically indicates thin value (like two pair A5, A2) or a blocking bet. While a straight (43) is possible, it is less likely given Minni's pre-flop flat call range compared to two-pair combinations like A5, A2, or A6s. Since I beat all two-pair hands and lower sets, and top sets (AA/KK) would likely have 3-bet pre-flop, my hand is extremely strong. I am losing only to 55 or 43. I should raise for value to target her two-pair holdings that might cry-call a raise. A raise to 2200 extracts significant value while allowing her to call with her strong but second-best hands.

I have two pair (6s & 5s) on A♥6♦K♠5♣2♦. Pro’s line (UTG open, check flop, small turn bet 350, called my min-raise to 700, checked river, then 3-bet my small 700 to 2200) is aggressive and polarized — it can contain strong two-pair/trips (A6, K6, AK, 66, 55, AA/KK/22) but also a fair share of single-pair A/K combos and some bluffs. The math favors a call: I must add 1500 to win a ~6450 pot (need ≈23% equity), and my two pair is ahead of most A/K one-pair hands that are likely in his calling/raising range. Raising myself would commit more chips and polarize me into a tougher decision vs a very small subset of better hands; folding is too nitty. I’ll call the 1500 more (2200 total committed this street) to realize equity and get to showdown.

5-handed table, UTG with Kh8c: in short-handed play my opening range should be wider than full-ring, and K8o is playable for a standard open. I want fold equity and initiative — a 3xBB open (to 300) is a clean, table-consistent sizing that pressures the blinds and avoids a cheap multiway pot. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (don’t bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand); if called proceed cautiously postflop — C-bet when I connect or have good equity, otherwise control the pot and look to realize equity. 300 gives good balance between fold equity and commitment with this holding.

Pocket kings (KsKd) is an extremely strong premium hand, second only to AA preflop. Minni's UTG raise to 300 (3x BB) suggests a strong range, but she has shown aggression in previous hands (frequent raises and 3-bets). As UTG+1 with deep stacks (~10k effective), standard play is to 3-bet for value and isolation, narrowing her calling range to worse hands like AQ+, JJ, or bluffs. Sizing to 900 (3x her raise) builds the pot while leaving room for 4-bets or postflop play. Folding or flat-calling is suboptimal with this hand in position relative to blinds.
75 suited facing a 3-bet. This is a speculative hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet range. Even with position, calling 900 with 7d5d is too loose. Clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. The action involves an open raise from Minni (UTG) to 300 and a 3-bet from Elon (UTG+1) to 900. Given the strength indicated by a raise and a 3-bet from early positions, my hand is extremely weak and does not justify a call or a bluff. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with AhTh facing a UTG open to 300 and a UTG+1 3-bet to 900. This is a classic 'sandwich' spot against strong ranges. The 3-bet from UTG+1 against a UTG open typically represents a very strong range (QQ+, AK, maybe JJ/AQs) which dominates my hand. Additionally, the original raiser (Minni) is still to act behind me and could 4-bet. Calling 800 chips here would be -EV due to reverse implied odds (domination by AK/AQ) and being out of position. My equity against a standard tight 3-bet range is likely below the ~37% required to call, ignoring the threat of the UTG player. The prudent play is to preserve my stack and fold.

I opened UTG to 300 with K8o and Elon 3-bet to 900. I’m out of position and K8o is a marginal, easily-dominated hand against a standard UTG+1 3‑bet range (AK, AQ, broadway Ks, pairs). Calling 600 OOP commits me to a difficult postflop spot with poor playability and insufficient equity (calling 600 into ~1350 requires ~30% equity). 4-betting as a bluff is too thin without strong blockers or a read that Elon 3-bets extremely wide. With healthy stacks and better spots ahead, folding and preserving my stack is the +EV play here.

UTG position in a 5-handed game with TsJd offsuit. This hand has straight potential but no suited connectivity, making it marginal at best. Standard UTG opening range even short-handed prefers suited connectors, broadways suited, or pairs. Playing OOP against 4 opponents risks difficult post-flop spots with a weak ace-high hand at best. Deep stack (100BB+), no need to force action. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities.
2h 6d is a very weak hand - unsuited, disconnected, low cards. From UTG+1, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with this holding.

I am on the Button with 7-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although it's a folding situation for most players, being on the button often offers a stealing opportunity; however, both the Small Blind (Pro) and Big Blind (Minni) have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to play pots. There is no strategic reason to invest chips with such a poor hand here.

In a blind-vs-blind situation with Pocket Deuces (22), I have a pair which is ahead of a random hand but very vulnerable. Being out of position against the chip leader (Minni), I prefer to keep the pot small. Raising bloats the pot with a hand that is difficult to navigate post-flop if I don't hit a set. Folding is too tight given the 3:1 pot odds to see a flop. Therefore, completing the blind (limping) is the most prudent play to realize equity cheaply.

I’m in the big blind with 8♣5♠ and only the small blind (Pro) has completed to 100. This is a weak, easily dominated hand and there’s no reason to inflate the pot preflop with marginal cards against a player who has shown aggression in prior hands. I’ll check and see a flop for free; if Pro leads out heavily I’ll fold most misses and only continue with a real pair or good draw. A min-raise to 200 could sometimes take it down, but it’s too speculative with 8-5 offsuit, so checking preserves my stack and keeps options open.

I hold Pocket Deuces (22) on a flop of [4c 4h Ah] in a limped blind-vs-blind pot. The board texture heavily favors ranges containing an Ace or a 4, or simply higher pocket pairs. Being out of position with an underpair that has minimal improvement potential (2 outs), my hand has very low playability. Betting accomplishes little as better hands (Ax, 4x, higher pairs) will call, and worse hands (total air) might just fold or float. Checking is the standard prudent play to control the pot size and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold to significant aggression.

I have 8♣5♠ on 4♣–4♥–A♥: no pair, no heart, no meaningful draw. It’s heads-up vs Pro who checked preflop and checked the flop, so a bet here would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity against Ax or a 4x. With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. I’ll check to control the pot, see a turn, and fold to any sizable bet. I may call a very small probe-bet depending on sizing and reads, but won’t barrel as the aggressor with this holding.

I have turned a full house (Deuces full of Fours) on a board of [4c 4h Ah 2s]. The pot is small (200 chips) and limped pre-flop. Minni checked back the flop, which typically indicates a marginal made hand (like weak Ax, small pair) or pot control, rather than a strong hand or air (which might have bet). My goal is to extract value. Checking risks the turn going Check-Check again, losing a street of value from her passive showdown value hands (Ax). By betting small (100), I target her Ax and pocket pairs that will likely call a 'probe' bet but might not bet themselves. It also disguises my hand strength as a simple stab.

I’m on 8♣5♠ facing a small 100 turn bet into a ~300 pot from the SB who checked the flop. I have no pair and no draw; raising here would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and limited fold equity. The sizing makes this a cheap probe — calling 100 preserves stack, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in their range, and lets me reevaluate on the river. I’ll fold to significant turn/river aggression but call this small bet to control the pot and see one more card.

I hold a Full House (Deuces full of Fours) on a board of [4c 4h Ah 2s 5d]. The river 5d completes a wheel straight for any 3x holding, and might improve 5x to two pair or a set (55). I am currently only losing to 55, A4, 42, or AA. Given the limped pot and Minni's check-call line, she likely holds a marginal Ax, a 3x (straight), or perhaps a 4x (trips). My hand beats straights, trips, and two pair. Checking risks the opponent checking back with showdown value like Ax. I need to bet for value. A bet of slightly over half pot (225 into 400) targets Ax and straights which will find it hard to fold, while not being so large that it isolates me only against better full houses.

I hold 8♣5♠ and the 5♦ on the river gives me a pair of fives on 4♣–4♥–A♥–2♠–5♦. The pot is 625 and Pro bets 225, so calling 225 into the 850 total pot requires only ~26% equity. Pro checked the flop then bet a small 100 on the turn (I called) and now makes a small-ish river bet — a line consistent with thin value (A‑x), a blocking/targeted bet, or a bluff. My pair beats missed draws, small pairs (2s/3s) and most bluffs, while losing to Ax and to pocket fours/quads. Given the cheap price to see showdown, folding would be too tight and raising would be thin and needlessly risky (I lack strong blockers and could face a re‑raise). Calling is the +EV, low-variance play here to realize my showdown equity.
Ac8c is a playable suited ace from UTG. It has decent equity with the nut flush potential and an ace blocker. Standard open to 2.5x BB.

I am holding 2s 5h, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has opened with a raise to 250 from UTG. Given my position and the strength of my hand, there is no strategic reason to call or raise. This is a clear fold.

I hold A8o on the Button. The player in UTG (Claude) has raised to 250 (2.5bb). A8o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a UTG opening range (which typically includes AT+, AJ+, AQ+, AK). While I have position, the hand suffers from reverse implied odds and does not play well post-flop against a strong range. With a healthy stack of 147bb, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot. The standard play here is to fold.

Claude opened UTG to 250 — an early‑position raise representing a relatively strong range. I’m in the small blind with 4♣3♠ offsuit: a very weak, easily dominated hand with poor playability out of position. The big blind still acts and could 3‑bet or call, turning this into a multiway pot where my equity and implied odds are terrible. Calling 200 here is a negative‑EV defense; I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot or position. Fold.

Claude's UTG raise to 250 is a strong range, but with folds from Flash, Pro (button), and Minni (SB), this is a prime squeeze spot in the BB. A4s is an ideal 3-bet candidate here: ace blocker to his AA/AK/AQs value range, suited for strong playability OOP (nut flush draws, wheel/straight draws, top pair potential). Pot odds for a call are great (~27% equity needed), but 3-betting extracts dead money (SB blind + my BB + his raise) and applies pressure. Sizing to 800 (3.2x his raise) is balanced, builds pot when ahead, and fold equity is high given his position and history of folding to aggression (e.g., Hand #57, #49). Stacks are deep (~100BB), so if called we play post-flop profitably; if 4-bet, we have blockers and fold equity vs his nuts. This maximizes EV in a tournament setting aiming to build stack over 150 hands.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon in the BB. Ac8c is a suited ace with nut flush potential. Getting about 2:1 odds (550 to win 1100). This hand plays well postflop with position and has good implied odds when we hit. Too weak to 4-bet, too strong to fold given the odds and playability. Calling is the best option.

Preflop 3-bet with A4hh was a strong squeeze play, giving us range advantage and initiative heads-up. Flop 3c7s8s is a good spot to c-bet: board is drawy (spade flush draw, open-enders like 56, 95), but our 3-bet range (overpairs QQ+, sets, combo draws) hits harder than his UTG calling range (QQ+, AKo/s, AQs+). A4hh blocks AA/AK/AQs, has ace-high SDV, backdoor heart flush draw, and backdoor straight potential (e.g., 25 for wheelish). C-betting ~2/3 pot applies max pressure OOP, denies equity to his floats/draws, builds pot when ahead, and fold equity is high given his history of folding to postflop aggression (e.g., Hand 49 river fold, Hand 57 prefold). Effective SPR low (~6:1 post-bet), we win at showdown or via folds. Check risks free card to his SDV/draws and lets him rep strength cheaply.

I flopped middle pair (8s) with ace kicker and a backdoor nut flush draw in clubs. Board is 3c7s8s - somewhat wet with spade draw possible. Elon c-bet 2/3 pot after 3-betting preflop. He could have overpairs, AK/AQ that missed, or draws. Getting ~2.5:1 odds (1100 to win 2750). With middle pair + backdoor flush, I have enough equity to continue. Can improve with an 8, ace, or running clubs. Folding is too tight; raising commits too much with just middle pair. Calling to re-evaluate turn.

Preflop squeeze 3-bet with A4hh was strong, blocking aces and suited playability. Flop c-bet on drawy 378ss board leveraged range advantage (sets, two-pair, overpairs > his calling range), and he called OOP, indicating strength: likely 77/88/87s, QJ+/KJ suited spades draws, AKs. Turn Qd is somewhat neutral/bricky for us but blocks QTs/QJs in our range and completes some straights like T9 (possible in his UTG calling range). We hold pure ace-high air now (no pair, no completed draw, gutshot unlikely), with minimal showdown value OOP vs his capped strong range. SPR ~2:1 post-turn makes betting risky: thin value unlikely (he folds worse? No), bluffs face low fold equity after his flop hero-call (history shows he sticks with hands like Hand #2 river fold but postflop callers persist, e.g., Hand #10). Betting commits us further into likely better hands or gets called light/weak. Checking realizes equity (free river card if behind, pot control), pots up only if he value bets (fold), or checks behind vs cautious overpairs/draws (win). Optimal discipline in tournament: avoid spew, preserve ~100BB stack for better spots over 150 hands.

Elon checked the turn after c-betting flop on a 3-bet pot. The Qd is a scary card that could have improved his range (AQ, KQ, QQ). His check could indicate: (1) giving up with AK, (2) trapping with strong hands, or (3) pot control with medium strength. I have middle pair top kicker - decent showdown value but vulnerable. Betting opens me to check-raises from overpairs/sets. Check back for pot control and showdown value. If he's bluffing we win; if he has an overpair we minimize losses.

Preflop 3-bet squeeze with A4hh was strong, leveraging position and blockers. Flop c-bet on draw-heavy 378ss board capitalized on range advantage (sets, overpairs, combo draws > his UTG calling range of QQ+, AK, AQs+), extracting value/fold equity while he called OOP, narrowing him to strong made hands (77/88/87, slowplayed sets), persistent spade draws (KJss, ATss), or floats with SDV like QJ/KT. Turn Qd bricky/neutral (blocks some of our Qx value, completes limited straights like T9), checked both ways realizing equity OOP with pure ace-high air (no pair/draw, backdoors dried up), capping our range somewhat but avoiding commitment into his strength. River 9c completes nut straight 56 (possible in his stubborn calling range), pairs 99 (unlikely preflop), adds flush draw outs for clubs but irrelevant here; doesn't hit our hand but caps villain further by improving floats/draws selectively. Now pure ace-high SDV OOP (~king-high equity vs his range post-call/check). Pot 3850, SPR ~2.2:1, betting thin value absent (ace-high rarely best vs 77+), pure bluff low EV after turn check signals weakness and his flop call shows commitment (fold equity crushed; history: Hand #49 river fold but to larger bet post-check, here he'd call/raise light). Checking maximizes EV: wins unimproved overpairs/draws/missed floats unchecked behind (e.g., KJss, QJ), pot controls, induces value from beaten made hands unlikely to bet river OOP after checks, faces reasonable callsizes if he bluffs/value thin (ace-high crushable). Tournament mid-stage (hand 59/150), preserve deep stack (~85BB) for superior spots vs spewing into cooler potential (56, 87, sets). Discipline over aggression builds stack long-term.

Elon has checked both turn and river after c-betting flop in a 3-bet pot. This line usually indicates: (1) giving up with AK/AJ type hands, (2) pot controlling with medium strength, or (3) occasionally trapping. The 9c river completed some gutshots but doesn't change much. My pair of 8s with ace kicker has decent showdown value. Betting for thin value is risky - better hands call, worse hands fold. The double-check line suggests weakness, so I should check back and take the free showdown.

I am in the UTG position with Ten of Hearts and Eight of Diamonds. This is a weak, off-suit hand that does not play well from early position. Given the stack sizes and the aggressiveness of the opponents at the table, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Cutoff position with 6d 4s, which is a very weak offsuit gapper. With three players remaining behind me and no prior action to attack, this hand has negative expected value to play. My stack is healthy (147 BB), so there is no pressure to make loose moves. Folding is the standard and correct play.

On the button with K♠4♣ I have position and a good spot to attempt a light steal. The hand is marginal but raising the minimum (to 200) buys fold equity against the blinds while keeping my risk small versus the table. If I get isolated heads-up or called, I can play the flop in position; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold this weak holding. Raise to 200 to try to take the pot now or see a flop with positional advantage.

Qh8s is offsuit Queen-Eight from small blind facing a button raise to 200 (150 to call into 350 pot). Pot odds require ~30% equity, but Q8o is marginal against a wide button range. Poor postflop playability OOP with bad kicker and no suited potential. Standard fold to avoid tough spots. Preserve stack for better opportunities in tournament.
JTo in the BB facing a button min-raise. Getting 3.5:1 pot odds to call 100. This is an easy defend with a connected broadway hand. No need to 3-bet, just call and see a flop in position.

I have JTo on Q62hh - completely missed the flop with no pair and only a weak backdoor straight draw. I'm first to act in the BB. No reason to lead into the preflop aggressor with air. Standard check and see what Minni does.

I opened to 200 from the button with K♠4♣ and got one caller. Flop Q♦ 6♥ 2♥ is fairly dry (though two hearts), and Claude’s check shows weakness or a wide calling range. As the preflop aggressor in position I should c‑bet to seize the initiative and fold out small pairs/weak broadways and charge heart draws. I size to ~2/3 pot (300 into 450) — big enough to apply pressure but not commit me with a marginal K‑high. Plan: fold to a large check‑raise, but if called re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue with improvement or clear bluffing opportunities.
JTo on Q62hh - I completely missed. No pair, no flush draw (no hearts), just a gutshot if K comes. Facing a 75% pot bet from the preflop aggressor, I'm OOP with no equity. Easy fold.

I am holding 4c 7d in UTG position. This is an extremely weak hand (7-high, unsuited, gapped) with very poor playability. Being the first to act with a trash hand and many players behind left to act, the only correct play is to fold immediately. I have a healthy stack and should wait for better spots.

QcJh (QJo offsuit) UTG+1 at a 5-handed table is within a standard opening range. I want the initiative and to avoid a multiway pot out of position, so I’ll open-raise rather than just call the 100. A 3x BB sizing to 300 gives reasonable fold equity without over-committing given my 164bb stack. Plan: C-bet and seize initiative on many flops; proceed cautiously/fold to large 3-bets (or re-evaluate vs known 3-bettors) and value-bet top pair or better.

2s5c is one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, low, poorly connected, minimal showdown value or straight potential. Facing an early/middle position raise (UTG+1 after UTG fold) from Minni, who has shown aggression in prior hands. Even on the button with deep stacks, calling risks multiway pots or difficult OOP decisions if isolated. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in this tournament phase.

Q7 suited in SB facing a min-raise from Minni. Getting 1.8:1 pot odds to call 250 into 450. Q7s has playability with flush potential and can hit top pair. Against a likely wide UTG+1 range from Minni (who has been active), this is a reasonable defend. Folding is fine too but the suited aspect gives us implied odds.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7s 3c, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Minni raised to 300 from early position and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting favorable pot odds to see a flop, this hand has terrible playability and I would be out of position against two opponents. It is more prudent to fold and preserve my stack.

I have Q7h on a 3h-5s-Js board. I completely missed - just queen high with a backdoor heart flush draw. The board is low/disconnected with two spades. Check to the preflop aggressor and likely fold to a bet. No reason to lead with this air.

I hold QcJh — top pair (a Jack) with a Queen kicker on 3h-5s-Js. I opened preflop and Claude (who called) is my only opponent, so this is heads-up. The flop is fairly dry: two low cards and a Jack; there are two spades but no completed flush on the flop. My hand is ahead of a large portion of Claude’s calling range (weaker Jx, many broadways, medium pocket pairs). I want value and to deny equity to any backdoor draws, so I’ll c-bet to seize the initiative. A half-pot bet (350 into 700) extracts value from worse Jx and smaller pairs, charges any two-card spade combos, and doesn’t overcommit my 16,100 stack. Plan: if he calls, re-evaluate the turn and continue for value or pot control; if he puts in a large check-raise I’ll give credit to sets/two-pair/AJ and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

I have Q7 hearts on 3h-5s-Js. Complete miss - just queen high with only a backdoor heart draw (one heart on board). Minni bets 350 into 700, giving me 3:1 odds needing ~25% equity. With just Q-high and a weak backdoor draw, I don't have the equity to continue. No pair, no real draws, out of position. Clear fold.

UTG in a 5-handed game with K♠9♠ — suited, reasonably playable and worth opening. With a deep stack (168 BB) I don't want to limp; I want initiative and to thin the field. A 3x open to 300 is standard here: it reduces multiway action, gets value from worse hands, and keeps draws/floating harder for opponents. If I face a small 3-bet I can call and use position/stack depth postflop; if confronted with a big 3-bet or 4-bet I will fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing and opponent.

T2o is a complete trash hand: low, offsuit, poor connectivity, and minimal showdown value. Minni's UTG raise indicates a strong range, especially in an early position spot. My stack is healthy at 8525 (85BB), so no need to speculate with this hand. Folding preserves chips for better spots in a tournament with 88 hands left. No blockers or positional advantage to justify calling or 3-betting.

Q5s on the button facing UTG raise to 300. Minni's UTG range should be fairly strong. Q5s is too weak to 3-bet and calling OOP against a strong range isn't profitable. The queen is likely dominated and 5 kicker is poor. Fold is the disciplined play here.

Minni opened from UTG (earliest position in this 5-handed game) to 300. My hand, Ace-Six offsuit, is too weak to profitably call or three-bet from the Small Blind out of position against a range that is likely to contain better Aces and pocket pairs. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities where I have positional advantage or a stronger range.

I am in the Big Blind with [5d, Ac]. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG. I need to call 200 chips to win a pot of 450, giving me pot odds of roughly 30.7%. Minni has been very active, raising frequently from all positions (e.g., Hand #50, #52, #57, #60, #61), suggesting a wider opening range than a typical tight UTG player. Against a loose range, A5o has approximately 43% equity, which justifies a call given the odds, despite being out of position. A 3-bet is risky against a UTG open and potentially isolates me against a stronger range. I will call and defend my blind.

On this [7h, 4s, 5h] flop, I have middle pair with a top kicker (Ac 5d). I am out of position against the UTG pre-flop raiser, Minni, who has been playing an active and aggressive game. The board texture is wet with straight and flush draws. Standard play dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they continue. My hand is strong enough to call a continuation bet (bluff-catch) but not strong enough to lead out or check-raise for value, as I would likely only get action from better hands (overpairs, sets, strong 7s) or strong draws. Therefore, I will check and evaluate Minni's action.

I opened UTG to 300 and got one caller (BB). Flop 7♥-4♠-5♥ is very dry and favorable for a continuation bet — my preflop aggressor range is strong here and the BB’s calling range contains a lot of missed broadways and weak holdings. I have K♠9♠: two overcards + a backdoor spade draw and I block some spade/K combos. I want to apply pressure and take the pot down while keeping the size manageable, so I’ll c-bet about 1/2 pot. Plan: bet 325 to fold out air and charge draws; if he folds I win the 650 pot. If called I’ll re-evaluate the turn — continue only with improvement (K/9/spade) or good odds, otherwise pot-control and often check behind; fold to a significant raise since I currently only have K-high.

Pot is 975 chips after Minni's 325 bet into a 650 pot. I need to call 325, offering 25% pot odds. I hold Middle Pair (5s) with Top Kicker (Ace). Minni is a loose-aggressive player who opens wide and c-bets frequently. Their range includes overpairs and sets, but also many flush draws, straight draws (like 66, 86s), and missed high cards (AK, AQ) that they might c-bet with. Against this wide range, my hand has significantly more than 25% equity. Raising would likely only get action from better hands, while folding is too weak given the price and opponent tendencies. Calling allows me to keep their bluffing range in and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold [5d, Ac] on a board of [7h, 4s, 5h, 4d]. I have two pair (Fives and Fours) with an Ace kicker. The Turn 4d pairs the board, which is a relatively safe card unless Minni holds a 4 or a full house, though it counterfeits my two pair if I were up against a higher two pair, but here it effectively leaves me with my pair of 5s playing. I am out of position against an aggressive opponent (Minni). Leading out (donk betting) has little merit as I would likely only be called by better hands (7x, 88+, 66) and would fold out the bluffs that I am currently beating (flush draws, straight draws, Ax). Checking allows Minni to fire a second barrel with bluffs or check back for pot control. I intend to check and evaluate their action.

I c-bet the dry flop and was called; the turn (4♦) pairs the board which increases the chance villain has a 4 (now trips) and generally strengthens the range that called the flop (7s, 5s, small pairs, some Ax). My K♠9♠ has very little equity now (only ~6 outs to pair on the river ≈ 13%) and a flush is impossible after the turn. Betting as a bluff here would be thin — the caller on the flop is likely to continue with many hands that beat K-high and will often call a turn barrel, so I don't want to bloat the pot with air. I will check for pot control, see the river and then decide (raise/fold to sizable aggression on the turn now is unnecessary; if villain leads into me on the river I will fold without improvement, if I pair up I can consider a value bet).

On the River, the board is [7h, 4s, 5h, 4d, 8d]. I hold [5d, Ac], giving me two pair (Fives and Fours) with an Ace kicker. The 8d is a bad card for me as it completes a straight for any 6 (e.g., 66, A6s, 65s, 76s, 86s) and gives higher pairs to any 8 or 7. Minni checked back the turn, which usually indicates a marginal hand or a draw. Betting here achieves nothing; worse hands (like missed heart draws or AK/AQ) will fold, and better hands (straights, trips, higher pairs) will call or raise. Checking allows me to try to get to showdown cheaply against her missed draws or high cards. If she bets, I will have to evaluate based on size, but I will likely fold given the dangerous board texture.

I opened UTG, c-bet the dry 7♥-4♠-5♥ flop and was called. Turn paired the board with 4♦ and both of us checked; the river 8♦ doesn't improve K♠9♠ — I have only K-high. Villain’s line (call flop, then passive checks on turn and river) is consistent with any made pair (7x, 5x), small pocket pairs or a turned set/trips (4x) and has very little reason to fold to a river bluff. Pot is only 1,300 and betting here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity and unnecessary risk. I check to control the pot and will fold to any river aggression; if checked to showdown I’ll show/call down but expect to be behind.

UTG in 5-handed NLHE with 4c2h offsuit. This is one of the worst possible starting hands: no high card value, poor connectivity, offsuit. Playing it out of position risks committing chips with minimal equity against any defense. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Deep stacks don't change this.
3h6d is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high card value. From UTG+1, this is a clear fold - way too weak to open from early position.

I am on the button with 2c 4d, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While it is folded to me, the blinds (Pro and Minni) are both the table's big stacks and have been relatively active. There is no strategic value in attempting a steal with such a low-equity hand when I can wait for better spots to utilize my 78BB stack.

Action folds to me in the Small Blind with King-Jack offsuit (KJo). This is a very strong hand in a Blind vs Blind configuration, significantly ahead of the Big Blind's random range. My strategy is to raise to steal the pot or build value against weaker calling hands. I choose a standard size of 2.5BB (250), consistent with my previous opens (e.g., Hand #1, #6, #56). Although the Big Blind (Minni) has shown 3-bet aggression in previous hands (#6, #9), playing passively by limping allows them to realize equity with junk or attack my weakness. I prefer to take the initiative.

I’m in the big blind with T♠5♣ facing a 250 open from the small blind (pot 350). To call I need to put in 150 more (total committed 250). Folding is too tight given the price and I have position postflop — calling gets ~30% break-even equity (150 into a 500 pot). T5o is weak but has some showdown value and can hit a ten or two‑pair; I’ll defend in position and play the flop cautiously, folding to heavy aggression if I miss. I won’t three‑bet a marginal hand out of position against a likely wide SB open, so call and re-evaluate on the flop.

I hold King-Jack offsuit on a dry Q-7-3 rainbow flop. While I have the pre-flop initiative, I have missed the board completely. I have two overcards and some backdoor straight potential, but out of position against a range that likely defends the Big Blind with many pairs (Qx, 7x, 3x) and low pocket pairs, my fold equity might be limited on a single street. A continuation bet might fold out total air, but K-high has enough showdown value to try and get to a cheap showdown or hit a pair on the turn. Checking protects my checking range and keeps the pot small. If the opponent shows aggression, I can easily fold.

Check. I missed the Q‑7‑3 rainbow with T5o — no pair, no realistic draw and only weak showdown value. I called preflop because it was cheap; now facing a checked flop from the preflop raiser I prefer pot control and to use position. Checking keeps the pot small, lets Pro bluff first, and allows me to fold to meaningful aggression or call a very small probe to realize equity. If checked through I’ll see the turn and only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw; I’ll fold to any sizable bet on this street.

Pre-flop, I raised with KJo and got called by the Big Blind. On the Q-7-3 rainbow flop, I checked, and the opponent checked back, suggesting they likely don't have a strong Queen and are capped at weak pairs or Ace-high. The turn is the 4s, which gives me a Jack-high flush draw (Js) along with my two overcards (King, Jack). This significantly increases my equity. A delayed continuation bet here is effective for two reasons: 1) It generates fold equity against Ace-high hands that are currently beating me, and 2) It builds the pot for when I hit my draw. Given the opponent's check-back on the flop, I perceive weakness, and a bet of roughly 55% of the pot puts pressure on their marginal holdings while I have strong outs if called.

I called the flop for price and checked the turn to control the pot. The board (7♥ 3♦ Q♠ 4♠) gives me no pair and essentially no realistic draw — only 6 outs (three Tens, three Fives) to make a pair on the river (~13% equity). Pro’s 275 into the 500 pot (775 total) requires ~35% equity to justify a call. With so little equity and facing a player who opened and now bets the turn, calling is mathematically incorrect and a raise/bluff from my hand is too speculative given my lack of blockers and uncertain fold equity. I’ll conserve chips and fold.

6h Th suited is a marginal hand that's too weak to open from UTG. From early position, I need a tighter range. This hand lacks the strength to profitably open-raise here, and limping is generally a losing strategy. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 8c 4h in UTG+1. This is a very weak hand that does not warrant an opening raise, especially with four players left to act behind me, including two large stacks. Folding is the standard and most prudent play in this position.

I am on the Button with K6s (King-Six suited), which is a standard opening hand from this position due to the potential to steal the blinds and having position post-flop. The action has folded to me. My opponents in the blinds (Minni and Elon) have shown some aggression, particularly Elon 3-betting from the BB recently, but K6s is strong enough to open. If I face significant aggression (3-bet), I can comfortably fold, but often I will take down the pot pre-flop or play a pot in position with a flush potential. I will raise to 2.5BB (250) to apply pressure.

KsTs (spades) in the small blind vs a button open to 250 is a hand I want to play for value and initiative. I'm out of position, but this is strong enough and has useful blockers to 3-bet; calling just concedes the initiative and a tiny min-3bet to 400 would invite too many unfavorable scenarios. With deep stacks I can risk a modest-sized 3-bet to apply pressure, isolate the button and deny correct pot odds to the big blind. I’ll raise to 900 total — large enough to fold out many marginal hands and get heads-up often, but small relative to my stack so the risk is controlled. If called I’ll play the flop aggressively on favorable textures and exercise pot control when I face strong resistance.

J2o is a complete trash hand in the big blind facing a button open-raise to 2.5BB followed by a strong small blind 3-bet to 9BB. My hand has very low equity against the likely 3-bet range of Minni (premium pairs, strong aces, broadway combos), and even worse against Pro's calling range if I flat. Pot odds are about 1.56:1 (800 to call into 2050), requiring ~33% equity, which J2o doesn't have OOP. No position postflop, deep stacks make multiway pots disastrous with this hand. Folding preserves my stack (~84BB) for premium spots in this tournament phase.

I opened to 2.5BB with K6s on the Button, and Minni in the Small Blind 3-bet to 9BB (900). This is the exact same line Minni took in Hand #6 where I folded A4s. K6s is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a strong 3-betting range (typically 99+, AJs+, AQ+, KQs), often being dominated by better Kings. Although I would have position, the pot odds (calling 650 to win 1250, ~34%) are not compelling enough given the reverse implied odds of being outkicked. Without a strong read that Minni is bluffing excessively wide, the disciplined play is to fold.

In a 5-handed game, King-Ten offsuit is a standard opening hand from Under The Gun (UTG). While it is on the lower end of the opening range, the stack depth (78 BB) allows for post-flop play. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to 250 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds, while being prepared to evaluate if I face 3-bet aggression from the larger stacks like Minni or Pro.

I hold 7h 5h in UTG+1. The UTG player (Flash) has opened to 250. While suited gappers have potential, calling here is risky for several reasons: 1) I am in early position relative to the remaining players, leaving me vulnerable to 3-bets from aggressive opponents like Minni behind me. 2) I do not close the action. 3) The hand often makes second-best hands (weak flushes, lower pairs). The standard play against an UTG open from this position with this holding is to fold.

I’m on the button with Qc9h facing an UTG open to 250 (2.5x). Q9o is marginal but gains a lot from position — calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a flop in position, and avoids getting isolated OOP against a likely stronger UTG range. There are active players left to act (SB/BB) who often defend, so a 3-bet could just build a bloated pot out of position or run into a 4-bet. I’ll call 250 to keep my deep stack flexible, plan to fold to a large 3‑bet, and use position to c-bet or apply pressure only when the flop favors my holding (top/second pair, good draws, or very dry textures).

73s is a speculative hand at best, but very weak, especially multiway OOP from SB facing an UTG raise from Flash (tight range) and a button call from Minni (potentially wide but still). Pot odds are 200 to call into 650 (~3.25:1), but implied odds are poor with this hand postflop OOP, low playability, and reverse implied odds if we hit. BB still to act. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. No history suggests exploiting here.

K7o in the BB facing a 2.5x open from UTG and a call from Button. Pot is 650 and I need 150 to call, giving me 4.3:1 odds. K7o is marginal but with these pot odds and closing the action, it's a reasonable call to see a flop.

Flopped top pair with K7 on K-T-4 with two spades. I'm first to act with a marginal made hand - top pair weak kicker. Standard play is to check here to the preflop raiser (Flash). My hand is decent but vulnerable, and betting OOP into two opponents would only get called/raised by better hands or strong draws. Let Flash c-bet and I can decide whether to call or check-raise based on sizing.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Tens) on a board of 4s-Kc-Ts. This is a very strong hand, but the board is coordinated with a flush draw and several straight draws (like QJ or AJ). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Claude has checked to me, I must bet for value and protection. I want to get called by weaker Kings, Tens, or drawing hands. A bet of 500 into the 800 pot (62.5% pot) is a standard sizing that extracts value while making it expensive for opponents to draw.

Call 500. Flash (UTG) made a small continuation bet (500 into 1300) representing a fairly wide c-betting range. I missed the flop with Qc9h (K‑T‑4 rainbow with two spades) but have a 4‑out straight draw to a J (≈8.5% on the turn, ≈16.5% by the river) and I’m on the button so I can leverage position post‑turn. The immediate pot odds to call are ~28% (500/(1300+500)), and with deep stacks the implied odds when I hit are attractive. A raise would be too thin (I’m often dominated and have little fold equity), and folding is too nitty given the small bet and positional edge. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to large turn aggression unless I improve (or unless the action and board texture give a clear reason to continue); if a J hits I can extract value.

Top pair weak kicker on K-T-4 two spades. Flash c-bet 500 into 750, Minni called. I'm getting 3.6:1 (500 to win 1800). My hand is vulnerable - could be dominated by better Kx or facing draws - but folding top pair to a standard c-bet with these odds is too weak. Call and reassess turn.

Excellent turn card! I just made two pair (Kings and Sevens) on the 7d. This is a huge improvement from my marginal top pair. The board has a flush draw (two spades) and some straight possibilities. With two opponents who showed interest on the flop, I need to bet for value and protection. Flash c-bet and Minni called - they likely have draws, Kx hands, or Tx hands that will pay me off. Betting around 65% pot (~1500) extracts value from worse hands and charges draws appropriately.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Tens), which is an extremely strong hand on this board. Claude has led out for 1500 into a 2300 pot (roughly 65% pot) after check-calling on the flop. This 'donk lead' usually indicates a strong hand like a set of 7s, a turned two-pair (like K7 or T7), or perhaps a semi-bluff with a strong draw like QJ of spades. While 77 is a possibility, I cannot fold top two pair here given the pot odds and the strength of my hand. However, raising might only get called by hands that beat me (sets) and fold out the hands I am beating (single-pair Kings or draws). Furthermore, Minni is still to act behind me. By calling, I keep the pot manageable, allow Minni to stay in with inferior hands, and can evaluate the river based on the final card and the action from both opponents.

I’m on the button with Qc9h on Kc–Ts–4s–7d facing a 1500 raise into a 5300 pot with two opponents (Flash + Claude) still in. Calling 1500 gives ~22% pot odds (1500/(5300+1500) ≈ 22%). Raw river outs to improve: 3 queens + 3 nines + 4 jacks = 10 outs → 10/46 ≈ 21.7%, but many of those outs (Qs and 9s) are “dirty” — they still lose to hands containing a king or ten. Only the jacks are reliably clean straights. With two opponents who have shown strength (UTG c-bet and a turn raise/call), my true equity is well below the raw number. A raise here would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity and would bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win; calling is marginal at best. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

River Td is a terrible card for me. My turned two pair (Kings and Sevens) is now effectively just Kings and Tens with a 7 kicker since the board paired tens. Flash called my 1500 turn bet - likely has Kx, Tx, or a flush draw. If Flash has any Ten, they now have trips and crush me. Flush draws missed but won't call a bet anyway. Value betting is risky: worse hands fold, better hands (trips, full houses) raise/call. Best play is to check and evaluate if Flash bets. At showdown I beat missed draws and lose to trips/boats.

I have effectively the second-best possible hand: Tens full of Kings (Tc-Ts-Td-Kh-Kc). The board is 4s-Kc-Ts-7d-Td. There are two Tens on the board and I hold one, leaving only one Ten remaining in the deck, which makes Quads (TTTT) impossible for any opponent. The only hand that beats me is a pocket pair of Kings (KK), which would make Kings full of Tens. Given the action, Claude led into me on the turn after I bet the flop, which typically indicates a very strong hand like a set (77 or 44) or two-pair (K7). Now that the river has paired the Ten, any set Claude had has become a full house (7s full or 4s full), and any Ten he might have been semi-bluffing with has become trips. After Claude checks the river, I have 3,300 chips remaining in my stack with 5,300 already in the pot. This is a mandatory value bet. A shove is likely to be called by any Ten, any lower full house (77, 44), and potentially even strong Kings that Claude doesn't want to fold given the pot odds. I will commit my remaining stack to maximize value from my near-nut hand.

River Td is brutal. My two pair (K-7) now just plays as K-K-T-T-7 since the board paired tens. Flash called my 1500 turn bet then shoved 5550 into 10850 when the Ten paired. 

Pot odds: 5550 to win 16400 = need ~34% equity.

Flash's UTG range contains many Tens: ATs, KTs, QTs, JTs, T9s. Any of these now has trips and crushes me. The line (call turn, bomb river when board pairs) screams value from a made hand that improved. 

I only beat missed spade flush draws and worse Kx (K6 or lower). But would those hands shove all-in as a bluff here? Unlikely. Flash's all-in sizing screams "pay me off with your Kx."

Can't justify calling 5550 into this spot when so much of Flash's logical range (Tx) crushes me. Fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Four offsuit (Td 4s). This is a very weak starting hand with no connectivity or flush potential. UTG requires a strong opening range because I have to act first on every post-flop street and have 4 opponents left to act pre-flop who could wake up with a strong hand. Even with a healthy stack, there is no strategic merit in playing this hand. The correct action is to fold immediately.

We're 5-handed and deep-stacked (~155 BB). Th7h is marginal in early position but playable in short-handed play — I don't want to limp and give the button/BB cheap option to take initiative. I will open-raise to seize initiative, get fold equity, and narrow the field. Size = 3xBB (300) — matches table open sizes and discourages multiway pots while keeping postflop decisions manageable. Plan: fold to a large/standard 3-bet (likely ~900+); call only small 3-bets with a clear read or favorable pot odds. If called, c-bet dry boards, realize equity on heart/connected flops, and play cautiously OOP against heavy aggression.

Jd2c is a trash hand offsuit with no connectivity or high card strength. Minni's raise from UTG+1 indicates a strong range. Even on the button with position, calling or raising is unprofitable as it's dominated against their likely holdings. Pot odds are not sufficient (calling 300 into ~450 pot for implied odds that don't justify with this hand), and folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament.

A3o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a UTG+1 raise. Out of position with a weak kicker, this hand plays poorly postflop. Minni has shown aggression throughout the session. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 5s. Minni opened from UTG+1 to 300. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), 9-5 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Against an early-position raiser, it is best to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Holding 2♠7♣ UTG — the worst unsuited starting hand. Out of position with four players to act behind (including deep, aggressive stacks: Pro and Flash), this hand has almost no equity or postflop playability and will be dominated or forced to fold to pressure. Calling 100 to limp in just invites multiway action and difficult decisions; raising is nonsense. Preserve my healthy stack (15,675) for better spots — fold.

3s4d is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. In UTG+1 position with 5 players, this is a clear fold preflop to preserve stack for premium spots. Pot is small (150), dead money minimal, and risking 100 (1.2% of stack) for no equity is -EV. Multiway pots favor stronger ranges.
J6o on the button with just the blinds left to act. This is a reasonable button steal spot. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to try to take down the blinds or play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 3d. Claude has opened from the Button with a raise to 250. 53o is one of the weakest hands in poker and does not play well out of position, especially against an aggressive Button open. There is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff 3-bet here. Folding is the standard and most profitable play in the long run.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Sixes (6h 6s). Claude raised to 250 from the Button. This is a standard button open. I have excellent pot odds to call (calling 150 to win a pot of 400, requiring ~27% equity). Pocket pairs have good playability as I can set-mine (flop a set approx 12% of the time) or continue on low-card textures. 3-betting is an option but calling is lower variance and keeps the pot manageable while out of position. I will call and see a flop.

The flop [Kc 2d Qd] connects heavily with the Button's opening range (lots of Kx, Qx, broadway draws). As the Big Blind defender with Pocket Sixes, I have a weak underpair and am out of position. Leading out (donk betting) is not profitable as I fold out only worse hands and get called or raised by better. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If checked back to, I see a free turn. If faced with a bet, I will likely have to fold, but for now, checking is the mandatory first step.

Flop is K-Q-2 with two diamonds. I have J6 with the 6d for a backdoor flush draw. This board connects well with Pro's BB calling range (KX, QX hands). I have no pair and just a weak gutshot/backdoor draw. C-betting here is risky as I'll often get called or raised by hands that beat me. Better to check back, preserve pot control, and see a free card that might improve my hand.

The turn card is the Jack of Spades, making the board [Kc 2d Qd Js]. This is a very coordinated 'broadway' board that connects extremely well with a standard Button opening range (which includes many combinations of Kx, Qx, Jx, Ax, Tx). My pocket Sixes are now significantly weaker, counterfeited by higher pairs or straights (AT, T9). Although the opponent checked back the flop, this turn card likely improves their hand or gives them significant equity.
