Leading out would be turning my hand into a bluff with little hope of folding out better hands (like a random K, Q, or J). My showdown value is minimal. The best course of action is to check. If the opponent bets, I will fold easily. If they check back, I can re-evaluate on the river.

Turn brought me a Jack, giving me middle pair. Board is K-Q-2-J with two diamonds. While Pro could have Kx or Qx that beats me, I improved and should bet for value against worse pairs and to charge draws. A ~45% pot bet should work - gets value from weaker hands while not over-committing with third pair.

On the turn, the board is [Kc 2d Qd Js]. This texture is extremely unfavorable for my pocket Sixes (6h 6s). The board contains three broadway cards, all overcards to my pair, and connects heavily with a standard Button opening range (straights with AT, T9; pairs with Kx, Qx, Jx). Claude bet 250 into a pot of 550 after checking the flop. While the bet is small, my hand has very little showdown value and virtually no potential to improve to the best hand on the river. I am drawing to 2 outs at best, and even hitting a set might not be enough against a straight. Floating out of position on this texture is losing strategy. Folding is the correct decision.

6c8d is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and no high card strength. Playing from UTG in a 5-handed game risks difficult post-flop situations against ranges that include better hands. Opponents have shown aggression historically, making a fold optimal to preserve stack for better spots. Standard tight pre-flop strategy maximizes long-term EV.

7s6s is a suited connector with decent playability, but from UTG+1 I'm in early position with 4 players left to act behind me. This hand plays better in late position where I can see action before committing and have better implied odds. From EP, the risk of facing a raise and being out of position makes this a fold.

I am on the button with 8c 5d. While this is a potential stealing position, 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand. The blinds, Pro and Minni, have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet in previous hands. Raising with such a weak holding into active opponents is unnecessary given my current stack size and the tournament stage. Folding is the most prudent play.

I am in the Small Blind with Q3 offsuit. The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Minni) has been active, showing willingness to defend and 3-bet in previous hands. Q3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position with deep stacks (150 BB). The standard play is to fold this hand to avoid bleeding chips in a marginal spot.
96o in UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, well below any reasonable opening range from the earliest position.

Holding Ace-Trey offsuit in the Hijack position (UTG+1 in 5-handed) is a marginal spot. While it is an Ace, the weak kicker makes it vulnerable to being dominated by calling ranges and 3-bets from the active players behind me (Pro, Minni, and Elon). Standard opening ranges for this position usually exclude the lower Ace-rag offsuit hands. Given the stack depths and the aggressiveness of the table, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid being put in difficult post-flop situations with a weak top pair or no pair at all.

I am on the Button with 3h 2s, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unsuited connectors with the lowest possible ranks). Although being on the Button allows for a wide opening range to steal the blinds, 32o falls well outside even the loosest standard stealing ranges. My opponents in the blinds (Minni and Elon) have shown recent aggression and willingness to defend or 3-bet, making a pure bluff steal with zero equity negative EV. I have a healthy stack of over 150 BBs, so there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The correct play is to simply fold.

Q8h is a playable, suited queen with backdoor hearts and a useful Q-blocker. In the SB vs lone BB I want initiative and fold equity rather than limp/calling out of position, so I’ll open-raise. A raise to 300 (3x BB) pressures weak defenders, prices out some speculative hands, and keeps pot size reasonable with deep stacks. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (hand is dominated vs strong ranges), call small 3-bets very selectively; if called, c-bet favorable flops (Q-high, heart-heavy or otherwise wet) and pot-control on dry/unfavorable boards.

Preflop as BB facing a 3x raise from SB after all others folded. My hand is K5o, offsuit with a poor kicker. While SB range is wide and pot odds are favorable (200 to call into 400 pot, requiring ~33% equity), K5o has marginal equity (~35-40% vs wide SB open) but terrible postflop playability OOP: dominated if top pair, few draws. Standard GTO defend range vs SB open includes stronger kings (K9o+/K5s+), but K5o is a clear fold to avoid difficult spots deepstacked. Preserve stack for premium hands in tournament.

In a 5-handed game, UTG is effectively the Lo-Jack position. While hand values increase as the table shrinks, Jack-Ten offsuit remains a marginal hand that often underperforms when facing resistance from the remaining four players, three of whom will have position on me post-flop. Given the aggressive nature of the table seen in previous hands (e.g., frequent 3-betting and active post-flop play from Minni and Pro), JTo is a disciplined fold to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position with a dominated or mediocre hand. I will wait for better spots to commit chips.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively the cutoff in a 5-handed game since UTG folded) with [6d, 3d]. This is a very weak hand, being low and disconnected (one-gapper), despite being suited. My stack is healthy at ~150BB. There is no strategic merit in opening this hand from this position as it dominates nothing and plays poorly post-flop. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Button with A♣7♠ — this is a hand I want to play in position. I have an ace blocker and decent post‑flop playability, and there are two players left to act (SB and BB). Open‑raising applies pressure to the blinds, creates fold equity, and lets me take the initiative with position. I’ll make a standard button open to 300 (in line with table sizing). Plan: if both blinds fold I take the pot; if called I play postflop aggressively in position (c‑bet favorable, control size on ace‑heavy boards). If I face a large 3‑bet (significantly above ~2.5–3x my raise) I will lean to folding A7o to preserve my deep stack; I may call smallish 3‑bets occasionally depending on reads.

Preflop in SB facing a button min-raise to 300 (3x BB) after UTG and UTG+1 folds. Holding 4s3s, suited connectors but very low and gappy for strong play OOP. Button range (Minni) is wide but strong enough to defend vs calls/3bets. Calling OOP gives poor pot odds (250 to win 700, but multiway potential with BB, and hand plays poorly postflop with reverse implied odds). 3betting as bluff has merit vs wide button range, but 43s has low equity if called (bad vs typical calling range) and poor playability. Defending range in SB vs button should include better suited connectors (65s+), Ax suited, pairs. Folding maximizes EV long-term in tournament with deep stacks (~80BB). Prioritize position and hand strength.

Holding 9s2h offsuit in the BB facing a button raise to 300. This is a very weak hand with no connectivity or suits. Even though I'm getting ~2.25:1 odds (200 to win 450), 92o plays terribly postflop and is too weak to defend here. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position with 6h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand (disconnected, off-suit, low cards). With 150 big blinds, there is no pressure to make moves with garbage hands from early position. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG+1 with K♠5♣ is a weak, easily dominated hand — unsuited, poor kicker, and little post‑flop playability from an early position. Right now there’s just the blinds in the pot (150) and calling 100 would require ~29% equity to break even, but against the BTN/SB/BB calling/3‑betting ranges K5o will be behind far more often than not. Raising to 200 to try and steal is also bad here: out of position vs the button and the blinds, I’ll face frequent resistance and possible 3‑bets from players who have been aggressive. With deep stacks I can wait for much better hands (suited kings, broadways, pairs, suited connectors) in position. Fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

Folded to me on the button with KQo offsuit, a strong hand in late position with excellent playability, high card strength, and steal potential. Blinds are deep-stacked (Claude ~100BB, Flash ~106BB), and history shows aggressive play but unopened pot favors raising for value/thin value and fold equity against blinds. Standard raise size of 3x BB (300 total) balances range, builds pot when called, and applies pressure. Calling (limping) is weak OOP later; folding too tight.

T2o is a very weak hand in the small blind facing a button raise to 300. This hand has poor playability and is dominated by most of Elon's raising range. Easy fold - no need to defend with such a weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind with QJ offsuit. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. QJo is a standard defending hand in this spot, as it has good equity against a wide Button opening range and the pot odds (200 to call into a pot that will be 650) are very favorable. While I will be out of position, the strength of my hand justifies seeing a flop. I will call the raise.

I have completely missed this Ace-high flop with Queen-Jack offsuit. The board significantly favors Elon's range as the pre-flop raiser from the Button, as he is much more likely to hold an Ace in his opening range. Since I am out of position and have no pair or direct draw, I will check to the aggressor. If he follows through with a continuation bet, I will most likely fold as I have very little equity to continue in the hand.

Preflop raise with KQo from button is standard for value, position, and steal potential against deep blinds. Flop Ac7c5s completely misses my range—no pair, no draws, just king-high. However, as the preflop aggressor in position versus a single BB caller (who often defends wide), a continuation bet is standard to maintain aggression, deny equity to weaker hands, and exploit fold equity. Flash's check suggests weakness; BB calling range includes many Ax, suited broadways, pairs that missed or floats. Board has some draws (clubs, gutshots like 68/34/46), but not hitting villain's range too hard. My KQ blocks some Kx/AQ+ and has fold equity. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws/OESD that beat king-high and faces tough river spot OOP for villain. Small bet ~1/3 pot pressures without overcommitting stack (79BB deep). Sizing 225 balances range, allows fold if raised, continues profitably long-term vs BB calling stations seen in history (e.g., Hand 65 aggression but also folds). Goal: maximize EV by taking pots uncontested frequently.

I am in the Big Blind with QJ offsuit on an Ace-high flop (Ac 7c 5s). I checked to the pre-flop aggressor, Elon, who bet 225 into a 650 pot. This board heavily favors his range, as a Button raiser is very likely to have an Ace. I have no pair and no draw, and even hitting a Queen or Jack on the turn would not give me the best hand if he has any Ace. Continuing out of position with virtually no equity would be a waste of chips. Therefore, I will fold.

UTG in a 5-handed game I must be very selective. 6♣3♦ is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand that plays terribly out of position and will be dominated or have no fold equity if I try to limp/raise. Calling 100 to limp into multiple players creates a bloated multiway pot with almost no chance to realize equity or get paid when I hit a small pair. Several opponents at this table have shown aggression, so preserving my deep stack for stronger spots is preferable. Fold preflop.

Q♣8♠ is a marginal offsuit hand with weak high card strength and poor postflop playability. From UTG+1 in a 5-handed game, facing the big blind with no prior action other than UTG fold, this hand is not strong enough to open or defend. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in a tournament setting where position and hand selection are crucial for long-term stack growth.

On the button with Qh7h suited, two players already folded. This is a solid hand to open from the button - suited queen with backdoor flush potential. Standard button open to 250 to steal the blinds or play in position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. While King-high has some raw equity against a wide button opening range, K7o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling would often lead to difficult post-flop scenarios or invite a squeeze from the Big Blind (Pro), who has a large stack. Given the stage of the tournament and my position, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for more advantageous situations.

I hold Ad Ks (Ace-King offsuit) in the Big Blind, facing a standard raise to 250 from Claude on the Button. AKo is a premium starting hand that is significantly ahead of a typical Button opening range. Being out of position, simply calling allows the opponent to realize equity cheaply and puts me at a disadvantage post-flop. A 3-bet (re-raise) is the optimal strategy here to extract value from dominated hands (like AQ, AJ, KQ) and pairs, as well as to take the initiative. Based on previous hands in this tournament (e.g., Hand #7, #13), a 3-bet size of roughly 3.5x to 4x the open is standard. Raising to 950 (3.8x) applies appropriate pressure while building a pot with a strong hand.

Pro 3-bet me from the BB to 950. Qh7h is too weak to continue against a 3-bet - I have a suited queen but 7 kicker is weak. I'm getting 1250:700 odds (~1.8:1), needing about 36% equity. Against a typical 3-bet range, Qh7h doesn't have enough equity to profitably call, and it's not strong enough to 4-bet bluff. Standard fold.

UTG in a 5-handed game with Q6o (offsuit, Queen-high with poor kicker). This hand has very low equity against typical raising ranges and is not suited or connected enough for speculative play from early position. Standard tournament strategy dictates folding weak hands like this pre-flop to avoid difficult post-flop spots, especially with deep stacks (my 8575 ~86BB). Preserves stack for better opportunities. No reads from this hand yet, but history shows aggressive opens from multiple players; better to wait for premium hands.
A2o from UTG+1 is too weak to open. Even with the ace, this hand plays poorly postflop and will often be dominated by better aces when called. Standard fold from early position.

I am on the Button with 85 suited. After the players in early position folded, it is a standard situation to open the pot to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. 85 suited has some post-flop playability and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. A 2.5x raise to 250 is a standard sizing in this tournament structure.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c Ts, facing a raise to 250 from Flash on the Button. The pot odds are enticing (calling 150 to win a pot that will be at least 600, requiring ~25% equity), but T7o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressor. Additionally, the Big Blind is still left to act behind me, creating a risk of a squeeze or a multi-way pot where I am sandwiched. Given the lack of playability and poor position, folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve chips.

Fold. 2♠9♦ is a junk hand with almost no postflop playability and will be dominated by the button’s standard open range. Calling 150 into a 400 pot to play out of position against an aggressive opener is not worth it — the hand has poor equity and very little chance to realize fold equity or favorable turns. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive because I have no meaningful blockers and would put myself in awkward OOP spots with a marginal holding. Preserve my healthy stack and wait for a better spot in position.

K2o is a weak hand from UTG position. Even in a 5-handed game, this is not a profitable open from early position - the king is nice but the deuce kicker creates too many dominated situations. Folding is the clear play here.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings (Kd, Kh), which is an extremely strong starting hand. My goal is to build value and reduce the number of opponents. Claude has already folded from UTG. I will put in a standard opening raise of 300 chips, which is consistent with my previous strong openings (like Hand #12 where I also had Kings) and helps protect the hand while inviting calls from weaker kings, queens, or middle pairs.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Js Jd) on the Button. Flash has opened to 300 from UTG+1. Reviewing history, Flash has opened to 300 with speculative hands like 5s7d (Hand #12) and strong hands like QdKd (Hand #5), suggesting a wide opening range that is not size-dependent. JJ is a premium hand that is well ahead of Flash's range. Flatting would invite the blinds into the pot and leave me vulnerable to overcards in a multi-way pot. A 3-bet is the optimal play to isolate the loose opener, build value, and take the initiative in position. A standard 3-bet sizing in position is around 3x the open. I will raise to 950.

Fold. I’m in the small blind out of position with 5♠8♥ facing a button 3-bet after an UTG+1 open. This hand has very poor equity vs typical opening and 3‑bet ranges and is often dominated. Calling 900 into a 1,400 pot requires roughly ~39% equity (900/(1400+900)), which 5‑8o doesn’t have, and a call likely creates a multiway, out-of-position pot with little implied odds. A 4‑bet bluff is exploitable and unnecessary with a full stack — preserve chips for better spots.

3h5d is a complete trash hand, offsuit, low, disconnected. Facing a raise from UTG+1 (Flash) and a 3-bet from the button (Pro), who is in position. Pot odds are ~1.65:1 but equity against their ranges is abysmal. No draws or playability postflop warrants calling or raising. Standard fold to preserve stack in a tournament with 76 hands left.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings (Kd, Kh), which is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I opened from UTG+1 for 300, and Pro 3-bet me to 950 from the Button. Given our stack depths (approx 100BB), I should 4-bet for value to build the pot and protect my hand. A call would leave me out of position in a bloated pot. A raise to 2,500 is a standard 4-bet size that applies pressure to Pro's 3-betting range, which likely includes hands like JJ+, AK, and some bluffs. If Pro jams, I am never folding Kings pre-flop.

I hold Pocket Jacks on the Button and am facing a 4-bet to 2500 from Flash. Flash started the hand with 80BB and has shown loose-aggressive tendencies (opening 57o previously), which implies their range might be wider than a typical tight player, though a 4-bet is still strong. The pot odds are favorable; I need to call 1550 to win a total pot of 5150, requiring roughly 30% equity. Against a very strong range of {QQ+, AK}, JJ has approximately 36% equity, making a call +EV mathematically. Shoving would likely only get called by hands that beat me (QQ+), while folding is too conservative given the price and player profile. Calling allows me to use my position and re-evaluate on the flop with an SPR of roughly 1:1. I will call to see the board.

I hold an overpair (Pocket Kings) on a very safe, dry board (9-7-4). Pre-flop, I 4-bet and Pro called, which likely narrow's Pro's range to hands like JJ, TT, 99, 88, or AK/AQs. The board does not contain any overcards to my Kings. With a pot of 5150 and a remaining stack of 8000, the SPR is low (approximately 1.55). I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws or overcards like Ace-King. A bet of 1750 (roughly 1/3 of the pot) is large enough to build the pot toward an eventual shove while keeping Pro's range wide enough to call with weaker pairs or even high-card hands like AK.

I am holding Pocket Jacks on a 9h-7d-4c board in a 4-bet pot. The opponent (Flash) has shown loose tendencies but a 4-bet usually narrows a range to {QQ+, AK, perhaps TT/JJ}. On this low, dry texture, I am ahead of AK (16 combos) and TT, but behind QQ+ (18 combos). Against this composite range, I have roughly 40% equity. The pot odds require me to call 1750 to win a total pot of 6900, meaning I need only about 20% equity to continue. The bet size is small (~1/3 pot), and the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) is effectively ~1. Raising (shoving) would likely fold out hands I beat (AK) and isolate me against overpairs that crush me. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to bluff or value own themselves with worse, while allowing me to re-evaluate on the turn (e.g., if an Ace or King falls).

I am holding Pocket Kings in a 4-bet pot. The board is relatively dry (9-7-4-T), although the Ten on the turn does connect with potential pocket Tens in Pro's range. However, given the pre-flop and flop action, Pro's range is heavily weighted toward JJ, QQ, and perhaps AK or pocket 88/99. With the pot at 8650 and my remaining stack at 6250, the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) is approximately 0.72. At this depth, I am mathematically committed to the hand with an overpair. Moving all-in (betting my remaining stack) is the most effective way to realize my equity and extract maximum value from hands like JJ or QQ that may feel obligated to call given the pot odds. Checking risks allowing Pro to check back marginal hands or realize a free card with an Ace or straight draw.

I hold Pocket Jacks (JsJd) on a board of 9h-7d-4c-Tc. The opponent, Flash, has 4-bet pre-flop and now shoves the turn for 6250 into a pot of roughly 8650. Flash has previously demonstrated loose-aggressive tendencies (opening 5s7d, raising wide), which suggests their range is not limited to just premiums. While a standard tight 4-bet range is {QQ+, AK}, Flash likely includes more combinations of AK, AQs, or even erratic bluffs/lower pairs. Against a range of {QQ+, AK}, JJ has approximately 47% equity (dominating the 16 combos of AK while trailing the 18 combos of overpairs). The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 6250 to win a total pot of 21150, requiring only ~29.5% equity. Since my equity against a realistic range (~40-47%) significantly exceeds the required pot odds, and considering Flash's capability for aggressive plays with non-nut hands, calling is the positive expected value decision.

I am currently the chip leader with over 210 big blinds. In this hand, I am Under the Gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game holding Queen-Five suited (Qd 5d). While suited Queens have some playability, Q5s is far too weak to open from the earliest position, especially considering I want to maintain my chip lead and avoid unnecessary confrontations with players who might be looking to double up, like Pro who is relatively short-stacked. Standard opening ranges for 5-max UTG would typically start at QTs or QJs at the minimum. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with 48 BBs, currently the shortest stack at the table after a significant loss in the previous hand. T7s is a marginal hand that is typically a fold from this position, especially in a high-stakes tournament environment where preserving chips is crucial. While it has some playability as a suited gapper, the potential to be dominated or face 3-bets from the active players behind (Minni, Elon, Claude) makes it a negative expectation play to open here. I will fold and wait for a better spot to rebuild my stack.

I'm on the button with Qc9d — a playable, positionally advantaged hand that's a good candidate to open-steal in a 5-handed game. Action is folded to me with SB (Elon) and BB (Claude) left; both have medium/deep stacks and I have ~158BB, so I can comfortably pressure the blinds without overcommitting. I will open to a standard 3x the big blind (300) to generate fold equity and to play postflop in position if called. Plan: if I'm called I'll use position and c-bet appropriate flops; if I face a large 3-bet (size well above a single-reraise) I'll fold; I may flat a small, non-committal 3-bet to see a flop. Raise to 300 to steal/attack the blinds and keep options postflop.

Td2d is a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity against Minni's button opening range which includes many better suited connectors, broadways, and pairs. In SB facing a 3x raise, pot odds require calling 250 into 450 (pot after call would be 700, needing ~36% equity), but T2s has poor postflop potential, dominated often, and reverse implied odds. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no history suggests Minni is super wide, but even so, this is trash. Prioritize tight defense in early position out of position.

Facing a button raise with 97o in the BB. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). This is a marginal hand but has decent playability with straight potential. Against a button raise, I should defend fairly wide from the BB. Calling is standard here.

Flop is A-J-4 with two hearts. I have 97o with no hearts - complete air. No pair, no draw, nothing. This board heavily favors the preflop raiser's range (Minni). I should check and likely fold to any bet. No reason to bluff this texture.

Flop: Ac Jh 4h. I opened to 300 on the button with Qc9d and Claude (BB) just checked. I’ve missed this flop (queen-high) and have no realistic draw, but I have position and a wide preflop raising range, so a continuation bet is the correct play to pressure Claude’s calling range (many KQ, KJ, small pairs and connectors miss this board). I’ll make a modest c-bet (~1/2 pot) to fold out those missed hands while keeping my risk controlled. Bet 325; if Claude check-raises big I’ll fold (I don’t want to play a bloated pot with Q-high). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and only continue with improvement or a favorable card for a second barrel.

I have 97o with no hearts on an A-J-4 two-heart board. Complete air - no pair, no draw, no backdoor equity. This board heavily favors Minni's opening range. Getting 3:1 isn't enough to justify continuing with nothing. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position with Jd 6h, which is a very weak starting hand (unsuited, unconnected, low high-card value). With 48 BBs, there is no need to get creative or splashy from early position. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to conserve chips for better spots.

UTG+1 in a 5‑handed game is effectively an early position. Q♣5♠ is a weak, offsuit, poorly connected hand that will be heavily dominated by the ranges of players yet to act and plays badly in multi‑way pots. Facing the 100 to call (or a min‑raise to 200), limp/calling invites multi‑way action and limp‑folding postflop; an open‑raise will frequently get 3‑bet or called by better hands (Flash on the BB is deep and capable of squeezing). I have a healthy stack (16,175) so there’s no need to gamble with marginal holdings — fold and wait for a better spot or position to apply pressure.

Preflop, folded to me on the button with T3o, a speculative but weak offsuit hand. Button position allows a wide stealing range against the blinds. Pot is 150, BB is 100, min raise 200. A small raise to 2.5BB (250 total) has positive EV if blinds fold often enough, which they do in multiway pots historically. T3o has some playability postflop with straight potential. Stacks are deep enough to realize equity if called. Folding is too tight from button; limping risks multiway pots OOP. Raise to steal blinds and build stack.

Q9s is a playable suited hand facing a button open from Elon. His button range should be wide, making my Q9s a reasonable defend. Suited hands play well postflop with straight and flush potential. Calling 200 more to see a flop is better than folding or 3-betting this marginal hand out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 8c. The Button (Elon) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I need to commit 150 more to a pot that is currently 600. This gives me 5:1 pot odds (150 to win a total of 750). Given these excellent odds and the fact that 98o has reasonable playability in a multi-way pot, a call is the most standard and mathematically sound play. I am also the chip leader, so the 150 chips represent a negligible portion of my stack.

Flopped nothing - just queen high with a weak backdoor spade draw (need runner-runner). Ace-high board connects well with Elon's button opening range. No reason to donk bet with air here. Check and likely fold to any significant action.

On this Ace-high board (Ad 3h 2s), I have 9-high with no draws. The pre-flop raiser (Elon) is very likely to have an Ace or better in his range, and this is a classic continuation betting board for him. Claude (Small Blind) has already checked. Since there is no reason to bluff into two opponents on such a dry board that likely hits the aggressor, I will check and plan to fold to any significant action.

Preflop raised with T3o from button as a steal, got two callers (SB and BB), creating a 750 pot. Flop Ad 3h 2s is dry rainbow, hits my hand with bottom pair (3s) and positions me well against their wide calling ranges which include weaker Ax, overcards, gutshots (like A4, 45), and bluffs. Both checked, giving me initiative as preflop raiser. Continuation bet exploits my range advantage here multiway in position; half-pot sizing (around 375-400) balances value from worse pairs/draws while folding out pure air/misses. Avoids checking which risks giving free cards to draws or letting them realize equity cheaply. Deep stacks allow postflop playability if raised. Betting builds pot with showdown value and denies equity.

I have queen-high on an A32 board with no real draws - just a very weak backdoor spade draw. Elon's c-bet of 400 into 750 is standard and he'll have plenty of Ax hands and better in his range. No reason to continue with complete air here.

I have 9-high on an Ace-high board with no draws or significant equity. The pre-flop aggressor (Elon) has made a standard continuation bet that likely connects with his range (containing many Aces, pocket pairs, or wheel draws). Since I have nothing to continue with and no reasonable way to win the hand without a massive bluff against the original raiser, I will fold.

UTG with 3h8c offsuit — a very weak, non-connected, unsuited hand with poor playability out of position. There are four players to act behind (including big blind and aggressive, deep stacks), so calling 100 to play multiway is a losing long-run line and open-raising to 200 from UTG would be far too loose. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

Qs3c is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor high card value, no straight or flush potential, and dominated in early position (UTG+1). Folding preflop is standard to avoid difficult postflop situations and preserve stack for better spots. Pot odds don't justify calling 100 into 150, and raising would be spew with this holding against deep stacks behind.

72o is one of the worst hands in poker. Even on the button with just blinds to act, this hand has almost no playability and very little equity. If I attempt a steal and get 3-bet, I'm in a terrible spot. With a comfortable stack at 9175, no need to take marginal spots with trash hands. Folding is correct.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, 75o is a very weak hand. While I have a significant chip lead overall, Pro (the Big Blind) has a stack of 4,725 (approx. 47 BBs), which gives them plenty of room to defend aggressively or 3-bet shove over a steal attempt. Given the hand's poor post-flop playability and the fact that I want to avoid unnecessary confrontations with a relatively short but dangerous stack like Pro's right now, folding is the most disciplined play.

UTG with Th5c offsuit is a complete trash hand with no playability, straight potential, or suited value. Standard pre-flop ranges dictate a fold from early position to avoid difficult post-flop situations out of position. Pot is small at 150, but entering with this hand risks spew and doesn't maximize EV over the long tournament. Opponent stacks are deep, but no need to engage with marginal holdings pre-flop.
2d4s is a very weak offsuit hand in early position (UTG+1). No reason to open-raise with this garbage. Easy fold.

I am holding AJ offsuit on the button in a 5-handed game. This is a very strong starting hand in this position. Since the action has folded to me, I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300) to take control of the hand and build the pot while having positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 8d, facing a raise to 300 from the Button (Flash). My hand is an unsuited three-gapper with very low playability and equity, even against a wide button opening range. I am out of position and calling would require committing a significant portion of the current pot without the odds or hand strength to justify it. Preserving my stack of ~48 big blinds is more important than defending with trash hands. The correct action is to fold.

I’m in the Big Blind with 6♠8♣ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450). Calling requires 200 more (total commit 300) which gives ~31% pot odds to see a flop. 6-8 offsuit is marginal but has reasonable equity vs a wide button opening range and is worth defending from the BB with deep stacks. 3-betting as a bluff is too risky with this holding and folding is too tight given the cheap price. I’ll call to see the flop and then proceed cautiously — fold to heavy, well-sized pressure on dry boards but continue with any pair, two-pair, or meaningful draws and look for opportunities to bluff or extract value if the flop dynamics favor me.

I called preflop with 6♠8♣ and the flop is J♠ A♣ 3♥. I have no pair and no realistic draw — this is a very dry, Ace‑high board that heavily favors the button’s opening range. I don’t block A or J and have no business barreling out of position with air, so I’ll keep the pot under control and gather info. Plan: check to the opener. If they fire a large/standard c‑bet (~1/2 pot or bigger) I’ll fold; I may call a very small probe (≈≤1/3 pot) to see a turn against a player who c‑bets a lot, but otherwise preserve my stack and re‑evaluate on the turn.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Jacks) on a relatively dry board. This is an extremely strong hand in a heads-up pot against the Big Blind. Minni checked, which is expected. I will place a value bet of roughly half the pot (300) to build the pot and get called by weaker hands such as an Ace with a lower kicker, a Jack, or broadway gutshot draws like King-Queen.

Flash (button) shoved a small c‑bet of 300 into a 950 pot (~1/3 pot). I hold 8♣6♠ — no pair, only very weak backdoor potential — and I’m out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot or bluff-raise. However the bet is cheap: calling 300 into the 1250 total (950+300) requires only ~24% equity. Flash opens a wide button range often and a small probe like this can be called to see a turn without committing a large portion of my stack. Plan: call the 300, keep the pot controlled, re-evaluate on the turn — fold to substantial turn aggression if I don’t improve, and pursue value or a check‑back/stop line only if I pick up a pair or a real draw.

I have 6♠8♣ on a paired Ace board (J♠ A♣ 3♥ A♠) with no pair and virtually no meaningful draw. I’m out of position to the Button who c‑bet the flop and I don’t block Ax or strong Jx combinations, so bluffing the turn is low EV and would risk a large portion of my stack for little fold equity. My stack is healthy relative to the pot (15,575 vs pot 1,250) so preserving chips and seeing a cheap river when appropriate is preferable. I’ll keep the pot under control and check — fold to large aggression on the turn, consider calling a very small probe but otherwise avoid committing without real equity.

I have turned a full house (Aces full of Jacks) on a board of Js-Ac-3h-As. This is a monster hand and I am almost certainly ahead. Minni checked the turn after calling my flop bet. I want to continue extracting value from her range, which likely includes weaker Aces, Jacks, or pocket pairs. A bet of 750 (60% of the pot) is large enough to build the pot while still being a price that an Ace or a Jack might feel compelled to call.

Board: Js Ac 3h As. I hold 8♣6♠ — no pair, no meaningful draw, and I don’t block A or J. Flash’s line (button open > flop raise > turn barrel) strongly favors made Ax/Jx and selective value hands; calling 750 into a 2000 pot requires roughly 21–22% equity. I have essentially no equity here and am out of position, so bluff‑raising would be a large, low‑EV commitment (min raise to 1500) and calling to see a river is not justified. Preserve a healthy stack for better spots and fold.

7h6h is a suited connector but too weak to open from UTG. Early position requires a tighter range, and 76s doesn't make the cut. With aggressive players behind who can 3-bet, folding is the disciplined play here.

I am holding King-Ten offsuit in UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. This is a standard opening hand in this position. I have a significant chip lead despite a major loss in Hand #74. I will open for 300 (3x BB) to maintain aggression and take control of the pot early. My stack size allows me to apply pressure on the shorter stacks like Pro.

I am on the Button with 7s 5h, a weak off-suit one-gapper. Facing a raise to 300 from the Cutoff (Flash), who has a significant chip lead. My hand has very poor playability and equity against an opening range. Calling is losing, and 3-betting as a bluff is ill-advised with this specific holding given the stack depths and opponent. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Fold. A♦3♥ is a weak offsuit ace from the small blind facing an UTG+1 open to 3bb. I’ll be out of position vs the raiser and the big blind, and my ace is frequently dominated by better A‑x hands in the opener’s range. Calling 250 to play OOP in a multiway or heads‑up pot against aggressive opponents is poor value, and a 3‑bet bluff here is too risky with deep stacks. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Preflop in BB with A9o facing a raise to 300 (200 to call into 450 pot, excellent odds ~3:1). Flash raised from early-middle position (UTG+1 in 5-handed), but history shows Flash is aggressive and plays large pots frequently (e.g., Hand 74 massive pot). A9o has solid equity (~40-45% vs typical MP open range: blockers Ace, plays well multiway but SB folded). Position postflop is first-to-act, but deep stacks (88BB effective vs my stack) favor realizing equity OOP with strong Ax. 3-bet risks too much OOP with offsuit; folding too tight for BB defense. Call to see flop, control pot OOP, fold to c-bet if miss, proceed if hit.

Facing flop 7c-8d-Td OOP heads-up vs Flash's UTG+1 open. Hold Ah9c: ace-high + gutshot straight draw (3 outs to 7-8-9-10 straight via remaining 9s). No pair, no flush draw, backdoors only. Flash's range (aggro player per history: large pots in H11, H12, H65, H74) hits this board decently (98s, T9s, JTs, ATs, sets, overpairs). Equity ~25-35% vs polarized c-bet range. OOP first to act, pot 650 (86BB effective). Standard check to pot control/realize equity; donk too thin/risky OOP deepstacked. Expect c-bet ~60-70% freq; can fold to large bet, call small/medium if odds good. Folding pre was option but defended wide BB; continue realizing equity selectively. Check preserves stack, induces bluffs/checks from missed draws.

I have hit top pair on a very coordinated board featuring both straight and flush draws. As the pre-flop aggressor, I need to follow up with a continuation bet to extract value from Elon's calling range, which likely includes weaker pairs (like 7x or 8x) and various draws. Betting 400 into the 650 pot (approx 60% pot) is a standard sizing that builds the pot while making it expensive for draws to continue.

Facing a 400 bet into a ~650 pot (66% pot) on a draw-heavy 7c-8d-Td flop OOP with Ah9c, which gives ace-high plus an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: 4 sixes + 4 jacks for 6-7-8-9-10 or 7-8-9-10-J, ~32% equity vs typical c-bet range). Flash is an aggressive player who frequently plays large pots (e.g., H11, H12, H65, H74 with massive commitments) and c-bets wide from UTG+1. My hand has strong playability, blocks AA/AK/AT, and realizes equity well multiway but heads-up OOP. Pot odds are favorable (400 into 1450 post-call, ~28% needed), deep effective stacks (86BB) support calling to control pot size, induce bluffs on turn, and realize implied odds if hit. Raising OOP risks too much variance (fold equity ok but faces 3-bet shove potential from sets/T9/JT); folding too tight for BB defense with this equity vs aggro range. Call small/medium bet as planned preflop, re-evaluate turn (fold if brick and he barrels big, continue if draw completes or he checks). Preserves stack for better spots while exploiting his aggression.

Turn board 7c-8d-Td-5c pairs nothing, adds club bringing two clubs to board (I hold one club for weak flush draw blocker), keeps my open-ended straight draw alive (8 outs to nuts: 6 or J, ~32% equity HU vs aggro range). Ace-high has some showdown value and blocks top of his range (ATs, AJs). Flash aggro history (big pots H11,12,65,74; frequent barrels), likely to c-bet turn ~70% freq with wide range including missed draws, overpairs, weak Tx. OOP check standard to realize equity, control pot, induce bluffs/bets we can call profitably (pot 1450, deep eff 82BB favors calls with 30%+ equity). Donk too thin OOP deep; bet/raise risks variance without fold equity vs his stubbornness. Check, call small-med bet (favorable odds), fold large if no fold equity, raise monsters. Exploits his aggression while preserving stack for 71 hands left.

On the turn, the 5c is a relatively safe card, though the board remains highly connected with multiple straight and flush draws available. I still hold top pair with a strong kicker (KsTh). Elon has checked to me again after calling my flop bet, suggesting a range that includes middle pairs, weaker Tens, or draws. By betting again, I continue to extract value from those weaker hands and charge the numerous draws (diamonds, open-ended straights with a 9, etc.) to see the river. A bet of 750 into the 1450 pot is a solid value sizing that keeps the pressure on Elon's middle-of-the-road range while maintaining pot control.

Turn board 7c-8d-Td-5c brings a low club (two clubs now, I hold one more but no flush draw) completing gutshots and straight draws while keeping my two gutshots alive (4x6 for 5-6-7-8-9 straight, 4xJ for 7-8-9-T-J broadway; 8 clean outs ~32% equity HU vs Flash's wide aggro range including missed diamond/club draws, weak Tx/8x/77/55/76s/J9s/overpairs). Ace-high has showdown value and blocks AT/AJ/AK/AA. Flash (aggressive per history: massive pots H11 quadruple, H12 turn shove, H65 river bomb 5550, H74 huge runout commitment) likely barrels wide ~70% on turn with polarized range (value + bluffs/draws). Pot odds excellent: 750 to call into 2200 (25% needed equity, we have 30%+ plus implied from deep eff stacks 82BB my side/200BB his). OOP call standard to realize equity cheaply, induce further bluffs/ships on river if miss, exploit overbarreling. Raising OOP min 1500 invites 3-bet/shove from stubborn value range (sets/straights); folding too tight post-flop call with superior equity/exploits aggro. Call preserves stack flexibility with 71 hands left, re-eval river (fold bricks to bombs, value bet/call hits).

River bricks my open-ended straight draw (no 6 or J), leaving ace-high with 9 kicker (blocks AT, AQo value top pair top kicker, and 9x straight combos like 98s/T9s). Board 7c-8d-Td-5c-3d completes some gutshots (e.g., 69s to straight) and low diamond draw (3 diamonds total), but Flash's double-barrel (flop 400 ~33% pot, turn 750 ~33% into 2200) vs aggro history (big pots H11/H12/H65/H74, wide range from UTG+1) is polarized: strong value (TT/88/77 sets, 98/T9/JT/67 straights ~20-25% range, Tx top pair) or missed draws/bluffs (overpairs QQ+, AdXd, clubs, air). OOP first-to-act deep (74BB my stack/200BB his), pot 2950: betting thin value (A-high beats bluffs/weak Tx sometimes) risks raises from better (straights/sets/Tx deep), pure bluff unnecessary vs his stubbornness (check-raises poorly OOP). Checking exploits by inducing check-behind from worse showdowns (missed draws, cautious Tx/overpairs fearing straights/draws) or small value bets callable profitably (~25-30% equity still vs pol range + implied + fold equity on calls), preserves stack flexibility with 71 hands left in tournament. GTO baseline ~0% bet strength here with marginal blocker hand; exploit aggro's overbarreling tendencies conservatively.

I have top pair with a King kicker. The river 3d completes a possible diamond flush. Elon has check-called two streets. While I might get value from a weaker Ten, checking back is safer to avoid a check-raise from a flush or a slow-played set, ensuring I win the pot against his missed draws and weaker pairs.

I am currently the chip leader with a substantial stack. Hand #80 finds me in the UTG position (first to act) in a 5-handed game. My hand, Jack-Five of Spades, is too weak to justify an open-raise from early position. While suited, the gap between the Jack and the Five is too large, and there are four players behind me who could easily hold stronger hands or 3-bet. Given my objective to maximize my stack and the tournament progress (hand 80 of 150), I should avoid playing marginal hands out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG+1 with 6s3s, a very weak suited gapper. With 48 BBs, there is no reason to enter the pot from early position with such a marginal hand. The risk of being dominated or 3-bet is high, and the hand has poor playability post-flop out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with T♦6♣ — a weak, but positionally powerful hand. Only the SB (Elon) and BB (Claude) left to act and the pot is small (150, 100 to call). I’ll open-raise to 300 (~3x the BB) to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds while keeping risk controlled. This sizing gives decent fold equity without committing a large portion of my deep stack; I’ll fold to a big 3‑bet and, if called, play aggressively in position — c‑bet when I connect (top pair, good draw) and control the pot when I miss. Limping/calling is too passive from the button against these opponents, and the minimum raise (200) is too small to accomplish the steal reliably.

98o is a weak offsuit hand from the small blind facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive historically. Positionally disadvantaged OOP, and while button ranges are wide, 98o lacks suitedness or strong playability postflop with deep stacks. Pot odds are decent (450:250), but equity and implied odds don't justify calling multiway or heads-up. BB can still act, but folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 70BB effective. Standard fold to avoid difficult OOP spots.

A8o in the BB vs button raise. This is a standard defend - too strong to fold, not quite strong enough to 3-bet. Calling 200 into a pot of 450 gives me good odds to see a flop with a decent Ace-high hand.

Flopped ace-high on a 994 board with two hearts. I have backdoor hearts but no made hand. Standard check here - I have showdown value with ace-high if it checks through, and I'm not strong enough to bet for value. Will evaluate if Minni bets.

I opened pre and Claude (BB) just checked on a very dry flop 9h-9c-4h. I have T♦6♣ — no pair and no meaningful draw, so this is a pure bluff/continuation-bet spot. My preflop raising range credibly contains many overcards and some pocket pairs/9x, so a small c-bet can fold out lots of his weaker holdings and win the pot now. I’ll make a modest c-bet (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping future commitment low. If Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn but will usually give up to significant aggression unless I pick up equity.

I have Ah8d on a 9h9c4h board. Minni's small c-bet (225 into 650, ~35% pot) looks like a standard continuation bet from the button. I have ace-high with the nut backdoor flush draw. Getting almost 4:1 odds to call. My ace-high has some showdown value, and if a heart comes on the turn I'll have the nut flush draw. Easy call given pot odds and implied odds.

With 999 on the board, everyone has trip nines. My ace is the nut kicker unless Minni has a 9 (quads) or a pocket pair (full house). Betting here is problematic - I only get called by hands that beat me or chop. Check for pot control and to see a free river. If Minni bets, I'll need to evaluate sizing.

The turn puts a third nine on board (9h-9c-4h-9s). I hold T♦6♣ with no pair or draw — nothing improved. Claude’s line (BB call pre, check‑call a small flop c‑bet) is consistent with many pocket pairs or medium strength hands that now become full houses on this turn, or with a slowplayed nine that becomes quads. Betting here would be a high‑risk bluff into a range that largely beats me and could get check‑raised or called for value; I have no fold equity vs the hands that matter. I’ll check to control the pot and be prepared to fold to any meaningful bet. If checked through to the river I’ll re‑evaluate then, but I won’t invest chips on this scary board without a hand.

Board is 9-9-9-4-7. Everyone plays trip 9s. My Ace is the best possible kicker - I have 9-9-9-A-8. Minni checked the turn after a small flop bet, suggesting either pot control with a medium kicker or potentially a trap with a boat/quads. Betting here is risky: worse kickers likely fold, and I only get raised by hands that beat me (quads, full houses). Checking and showing down the nut kicker is the safest line to guarantee I win against Kx, Qx, etc.

Board: 9h 9c 4h 9s 7c. I hold Td6c — no pair, no blocker to the hands that matter. Claude called pre and the small flop c‑bet, then checked the turn and river. That line is consistent with pocket pairs (44,77,88, TT, etc.) or very occasionally a slowplayed nine — many of which now have full houses/quads. Betting here would be an expensive bluff into a range that almost never folds; my fold equity is effectively zero and I have no showdown value. Pot is only 1,100 vs my 15,000 stack — no reason to risk chips with a worthless hand. I’ll check and concede the pot to any meaningful river bet (fold to value), preserving my stack for better spots.

I am holding 3c 5d in the UTG position, which is one of the weakest starting hands possible. With 48 big blinds, there is no strategic merit in opening the pot from early position with trash cards. My stack is healthy enough to wait for better opportunities. The correct and standard play is to fold immediately.

2♥3♠ offsuit in UTG+1 is a garbage hand — very poor playability and no reason to invest 100 into a 150 pot from early position. With aggressive players behind (Button, SB, BB) and being out of position postflop, limp-calling or raising is inviting multiway action and big pressure. Deep stack means I can wait for better spots to open from position or 3-bet with a strong hand. Fold to conserve chips and pick a better spot later.

Folded to me on the button in a 5-handed game with 93o. While button opening ranges are wide (typically 45-55% of hands), 93o is too weak: poor high card strength, bad kicker, gapped, offsuit with low playability for straights or draws. Equity realization postflop is low against calling ranges from SB/BB, which include Ax and better 9x. Deep stacks (74BB effective vs SB) mean multi-street play, where this hand struggles. EV from stealing blinds marginal at best due to risk of calls/3bets from aggro opponents like Claude and Flash (history shows aggression). Better to wait for playable hands to maximize long-term stack growth over remaining 69 hands.

I have pocket tens in the small blind, and everyone has folded to me. This is a premium hand heads-up against the big blind. I should raise for value. Standard sizing here is around 3BB. Flash has a big stack but that doesn't change my strategy with TT - I want to build a pot.

Claude has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. While I have the benefit of position for the rest of the hand and the pot odds are roughly 3:1, Q6o is a weak hand that does not play particularly well post-flop. Given my significant chip lead, I prefer to fold this marginal hand rather than get involved in a potentially tricky pot against a medium stack like Claude.

UTG with AsAd — the single best starting hand. Open for value and to thin the field. Table standard is a 3x BB open and many players here habitually open to 300, so I’ll raise to 300 (total). That builds the pot, prices out speculative multiway callers, and keeps flexibility with my 15000 (≈150 BB) stack. If I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on who and sizing: against a small/medium 3-bet I’ll consider a 4-bet for value (or jam vs short stacks like Pro); against a very large 3-bet I can still call and play postflop. If called I’ll proceed to extract value with c-bets on favorable boards.

5-handed play with deep stacks (74BB effective). Jd9d is a suited connector with excellent playability, capable of making straights, flushes, two pairs, and sets. Facing an UTG min-raise to 300 (3x BB) from Minni, who has been aggressive historically. Getting about 1.5:1 on a call into 450 pot (11% equity needed), with strong implied odds if I hit. OOP but in short-handed game, ranges are wider, and speculative hands like this perform well multiway or heads-up postflop. Folding would be too tight; 3-betting risks too much with blockers but no premium; calling balances range and realizes equity effectively. Will play postflop cautiously based on board texture.

72 suited is a very weak hand. Even on the button with position, calling 300 with this hand against a raise and a call is not profitable. The suited aspect gives minimal flush potential but not enough to justify the call. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c-2s, one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni raised from UTG and Elon called. Even though I am getting decent pot odds to call, this hand has very poor playability post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with KcTd facing a raise to 300 from UTG and a call from UTG+1. The pot is 750 (including my 100 posted blind and the SB), and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 200/950 or roughly 21%. Although KTo can be dominated by an UTG opening range (AK, KQ, AT), the price is extremely favorable to see a flop. I am closing the action and the stack-to-pot ratio will be around 5, allowing for some maneuverability or an easy fold if I miss. A squeeze is too risky against an UTG range and a caller with my stack size. Therefore, calling is the most +EV decision.

I hold Top Pair with a Ten kicker on a wet board (Kd 7d Qh). I am out of position in a 3-way pot against an UTG raiser and a caller. The board texture heavily favors the UTG opener's range (AK, KQ, AA, QQ, sets). Donk betting here is ill-advised as I isolate myself against better hands and fold out worse ones. Checking is the standard line to evaluate the action from the pre-flop aggressor. If they bet, I can decide whether to call based on sizing, but for now, checking to the raiser is the most prudent move.

I have pocket Aces (As Ad) on a Kd–Qh–7d flop — a very strong overpair to the board. I raised preflop and got two callers (Elon and Pro), so both villains’ ranges will contain Kx/Qx, pocket pairs, and some suited diamond combos or connectors that can pick up equity. The board has two diamonds, so there are backdoor/flush possibilities and some straight-turn outs (e.g. T/J combos). I want to both extract value from worse hands (Ks, Kx, Qx, medium pairs) and charge/price out drawing hands, so I’ll make a sizable c-bet of roughly 60% pot. That size (600 into 950) should get value from worse made hands and charge diamond/straight draws while still keeping weaker hands to call. If I get raised I’ll re-evaluate based on who raised and the sizing (small raise -> likely set / strong K and I will lean to commit; large raise -> more caution and a fold or a shove depending on stack and read). If called, I’ll continue based on the turn card: bet again for value on blanks, and pot-control or proceed cautiously if a diamond or a king/queen appears.

Flop board 7d Kd Qh is extremely draw-heavy with two diamonds, pairing my Jd for a nut flush draw (9 outs) and gutshot straight draw to the 10 for broadway (4 outs, non-clean). Combined equity is around 35-40% against Minni's UTG range, which likely includes top pairs like Kx, Qx, some sets, but also air/overpairs that fold to pressure. Pro checked behind preflop callers, suggesting weakness and unlikely to continue strongly. Pot 1550, facing 600 bet (pot odds ~2.6:1, easily met). Calling OOP keeps me passive multiway and vulnerable to turn aggression; folding is a mistake with this equity/implied odds. Raising semi-bluffs for fold equity against Minni's aggressive history (folds to 3bets/resistance often), builds pot when ahead, and charges draws. Sizing to ~2.2x pot raise (2100 total into 1550+600=2150 if flat, but sizing on her bet): 2100 commits 2100, leaves me ~5000 behind vs Minni's deep stack and Pro's shorter. Balances with value raises like KK/QQ. With 68BB effective, pressure now maximizes EV in short-handed play.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker on a wet board (Kd 7d Qh). However, the action is extremely ominous. Minni (UTG) c-bet 600, and Elon (UTG+1) raised to 2100. A raise from UTG+1 against an UTG opener in a 3-way pot represents immense strength, likely Two Pair (KQ), a Set (77, QQ), or at worst a very strong draw (AdJd) or AK. My hand is dominated by the vast majority of Elon's value range (AK, KQ, Sets). Additionally, Minni is still left to act behind me and could re-raise. Calling would commit nearly half my remaining stack (2100 of 4525) with a hand that is likely drawing very thin or dead. This is a clear fold to preserve chips.

I have AA — the best possible starting hand and an overpair to this Kd–Qh–7d flop. I c‑bet 600 and Elon check‑raised big to 2100. That sizing is polarized: it includes strong value (sets, KQ/two‑pair, some strong Kx/Qx) and bluffs/semi‑bluffs (two‑card diamond draws, turned bluffs). Raising now would polarize my range, fold out many K/Q hands that will pay me off, and commit a lot of chips when his line can be value heavy. Calling 1500 (to 2100 total) gives me ~29% pot odds (1500 into a 5150 pot) — my overpair plus blocker (Ad reduces some nut‑diamond combos) has more than enough equity versus a mixed check‑raise range to justify a call. Plan: call to keep his bluffs and weaker K/Q hands in, then re‑evaluate on the turn. If a blank (non‑diamond, non‑K/Q) comes I will look to extract value; if a diamond or a K/Q lands and he shows heavy aggression I will proceed cautiously (likely pot‑control or fold to extreme turn shove depending on read). For now: call and see the turn.

I’m heads‑up with AsAd on 7d–Kd–Qh–5s after calling Elon’s big flop check‑raise. The 5s is a blank — it doesn’t add a diamond or pair the board — and I hold the Ad which removes some of his nut‑diamond semi‑bluff combos. Elon’s flop check‑raise is polarized (sets/two‑pair value + diamond/semi‑bluff). On this turn I want to extract value from Kx/Qx and charge diamond draws while keeping weaker parts of his range working. Betting ~3000 into the 5150 pot (~58%) is a size that: 1) gets called by worse made hands (single K/Q, medium pairs) and by some draws, 2) charges and can fold out pure air, and 3) leaves room to re‑evaluate if he responds with a large raise. If he raises very large or jams I’ll re‑assess (that line is mostly strong value), but barring an overbearing raise I prefer to bet for value now rather than check and give him a free river card. Action: bet 3000.

Turn board brings 5s, a complete blank that doesn't improve my strong diamond flush draw (still 9 clean outs for ~35% equity) and doesn't complete any obvious straights beyond unlikely combos. Pot is 8150, facing Minni's large donk bet of 3000 (~37% pot size), which polarizes her range after calling my flop raise on the Kd7dQh diamond-heavy board: likely strong value like KT/KQ/KK/QQ/sets/two-pair, or stubborn draws/protection bets. Pure calls would be weak here OOP. Pot odds for a call are excellent (3000 to win 11150, needing only ~21% equity, which we crush), but committing 3000 leaves just 2025 behind into a 14150 pot, making us pot-committed and vulnerable to river overbets/shoves from her value range. Folding wastes massive equity and implied odds in a tournament ICM spot with 68 hands left. Raising all-in to 5025 (additional 2025 over) maximizes fold equity against her polarized range: marginal made hands (worse Kx, Qx, overpairs) and weaker draws fold, while getting called by only true monsters we have equity against (sets ~20-25%, top two ~32%). Balances our image as the flop aggressor with diamond blocker/heavy draw. Minni's UTG opening + stubborn flop call + turn donk suggests strength but history shows she folds to post-flop resistance (e.g., Hand 12, 56, 75 where she folds to aggression). Shoving exploits her range, builds stack or realizes equity heads-up deep effective, aligning with aggressive short-handed play to maximize EV over remaining hands.

Elon shoved to 5025 after I bet 3000 on the turn. I only need to add 2025 more (total commitment on the turn = 5025) to call. The current pot is 13175, so calling 2025 gives me ~13.3% required equity (2025 / (13175+2025)). With AsAd I have overwhelming equity vs a polarized check‑raise/all‑in range that will include lots of Kx/Qx, some two‑pair and sets, and some diamond semi‑bluffs. Sets/two‑pair are possible and would beat me, but they are a relatively small portion of his range; against the bulk of his calling/raising combos my Aces are a large favorite. Also, because Elon is all‑in, further raising is pointless (any extra chips would form a side‑pot he cannot win). Folding would relinquish huge positive EV. Conclusion: call the all‑in (match the 5025 total) and go to showdown.
72o from UTG is one of the worst hands in poker. Easy fold - no reason to play this garbage from early position.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a premium hand in a 4-handed game. Since Claude folded, I am opening for 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and exert positional pressure on the blinds. Given the stack sizes, specifically Pro's shorter stack, I want to play a standard opening range that allows for a fold if they shove or to maintain control post-flop against Minni.

I hold 6h 4c in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand (unsuited one-gapper). Flash raised to 300 from the Button. Although Button opening ranges are wide, 64o has poor equity and plays terrible out of position. The pot odds (calling 250 to win a total pot of 700, ~36%) do not justify a call given the playability disadvantage. My stack is 4475 (45 BB), so I should preserve chips and fold this trash hand.

Heads-up after the SB folded: Flash opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the BB with 5♠6♣. Pot is 450 and I must call 200 (300 total commitment including my BB). 5‑6o is a marginal hand but against a wide button open it has sufficient equity to meet the pot odds (200 into 450 → ~30.8% required) and the stacks are very deep (~225bb), giving good implied odds when I hit straights or two pair. I don’t want to 3‑bet here — I have poor blockers and would be risking a big stack with little fold equity — but folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call and play the flop cautiously out of position; continue on low/connected flops or with strong pairs/draws, otherwise use pot control and fold to heavy aggression if I miss. Action: call.

Out of position with 5♠6♣ on 4♥ Q♣ 3♦ — I have an open‑ended straight draw (2s/7s = 8 outs, ~31% to hit by the river) and no pair. Stacks are very deep (~225bb) so I want to realize equity and keep the pot controlled. Checking is best: it lets the button c‑bet so I can call a standard small/medium barrel and use my implied odds if I hit, while avoiding bloating the pot OOP with a semi‑bluff. Plan: check; call normal c‑bets to see a turn, fold or re‑evaluate to very large overbets/shoves, and start building if I complete the straight or improve to a pair.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a very dry board of 4-Q-3 rainbow. After Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, I should bet for value. My goal is to get called by weaker Queens (like QJ, QT, or Q9), middle pocket pairs (like 88-JJ), or potentially some straight draws like 56. Given the pot size of 650, a bet of 400 (roughly 60% of the pot) is a standard size to build the pot while my hand is significantly ahead.

I have 5♠6♣ on 4♥ Q♣ 3♦ — an open‑ended straight draw (8 outs, ≈31% to hit by the river). Flash's c‑bet is 400 into a 650 pot (pot shown 1050). Calling 400 risks 400 to win 1450, so my required equity is ≈27.6% and my draw (~31%) is slightly ahead — +EV. Stacks are very deep, so implied odds are good if I hit. I'm out of position, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a semi‑bluff/raise that has limited fold equity and poor blockers. Plan: call to realize equity, re‑evaluate on the turn (continue with completed straight or reasonable improvement, fold to very large turn aggression if I miss).

I check. Rationale: I have 5♠6♣ on 4♥ Q♣ 3♦ T♠ — an OESD (2s/7s complete the straight). On the turn I have 8 clean outs to the straight (8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river) and 14 cards to at least pair or better (14/46 ≈ 30.4%). Flash opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, which looks like pot‑control with a marginal Q/T or a medium pair — many of his hands will call a river but are vulnerable to a made straight. We’re out of position with deep stacks, so semi‑bluffing the turn would bloat the pot with limited fold equity and poor blockers. Checking preserves fold equity on later streets, lets us realize equity on the river, and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: if he bets the river I’ll evaluate sizing — call small bets with decent equity and fold to huge overbets if I miss; if I complete the straight (2 or 7) I’ll bet for value; if I pair a 5 or 6 I’ll proceed cautiously (check/call small bets, fold to big shoves).

I have top pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively safe board. The Ten on the turn doesn't change the board texture significantly, although it does complete some unlikely gutshots and potentially gives Minni a pair if she was holding something like JTs or T9s. Since Minni checked again, I will continue to bet for value. I expect to be called by weaker Queens (like QJ or Q9) or middle pocket pairs that are suspicious of my flop continuation bet. A bet of 900 into a 1450 pot (approximately 62% pot) is a solid size to build the pot further while I likely hold the best hand.

Fold. The pot is 2350 and I must call 900 (would make the total pot 3250), so I need roughly 900/3250 ≈ 27.7% equity to break even. I have an OESD (2s/7s = 8 outs → 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the straight) and 6 additional outs to just pair (14/46 ≈ 30.4% to pair or better), but most pair-outs (a 5 or 6) will still lose to the kinds of hands Flash is representing. Flash opened wide on the button, c‑bet the flop, and now throws a large turn bet — a line that heavily favors value (Qx, Tx, sets, overpairs) and contains relatively few pure bluffs. Versus that value-heavy range my realistic equity is much closer to the straight-only number (~17%) which is well below the ~27.7% needed. Raising here would be a speculative bluff out of position with poor fold equity and a bad PRICE to realize my outs if called. With deep stacks and good spots ahead, folding and preserving 22k+ chips is the correct +EV choice.

Q8o is a weak offsuit hand to open from UTG, even in a 4-handed game. With a short stack like Pro on the button and two capable stacks in the blinds, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve the stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with Ks 7h. The action is folded to me. There are 4 active players, so this is effectively a 3-handed situation currently. K7 offsuit is a marginal hand. While opening from the Button is standard with a wide range, K7o is near the bottom of that range. Against a massive stack in the Small Blind and a solid stack in the Big Blind, opening this hand risks getting 3-bet or called by dominating hands (K8+, Ax), leading to difficult post-flop situations. Given my stack of ~45BB, I prefer to fold this weak offsuit King and wait for a better spot to accumulate chips.

I’m in the small blind with K♥T♥ — a strong, suited broadway that plays well heads-up but will be out of position postflop. Only the big blind (Claude) remains. Limping (calling the extra 50) surrenders initiative and gives Claude cheap odds to see flops; folding is overly tight with this hand. I want to take the initiative and apply fold equity to isolate Claude, so I’ll raise to a standard 3x-BB open (raise to 300 total). This sizing is big enough to fold out weak BB holdings and to get value/isolation if called, but not so large as to commit my deep stack unnecessarily. Plan: if Claude calls we’ll c-bet favourable flops and use positional pressure; if he 3-bets large I’ll reassess and often release KTs unless the sizing/read justify a call or 4-bet.

Holding 3d6d in the BB facing a min-raise from Minni. This is a very weak suited hand - low cards with limited straight potential. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400), 36s plays poorly post-flop against a raising range. Not worth defending this weak holding.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with a stack of 4475 chips (approx. 45 BB). My hand is 3s 6c, which is unsuited, unconnected, and low value. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the short-handed table requiring wider ranges, 63o is far too weak to open profitably from any position, especially first to act. I will fold immediately.

8♠2♠ is a very weak hand but I’m on the button with deep stacks and only the blinds to target. I want to take the initiative and deny them a cheap flop, but avoid the tiny min-raise that invites lots of callers. I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300) to apply pressure and isolate; if I get a clean fold I pick up the pot, if I get one caller I play in position and proceed cautiously post‑flop, and if I face a 3‑bet I’ll let this one go (no desire to get stuck with 8‑2s out of position). The size is big enough to get fold equity but small relative to my stack, which preserves flexibility.
T3o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Out of position with poor playability - this is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 95o. Minni raised to 300 from the Button. While the price to call is only 200 chips to win a pot of 450 (offering 2.25:1 odds), 95o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given that I am the chip leader and have over 23,000 chips, I don't need to defend my blind with marginal garbage. I'll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

K♠J♠ is a strong opening hand in a 4-handed game and worth taking the initiative. Table tendencies show frequent 250–300 opens, so a standard 3x-ish raise to 300 achieves several goals: builds the pot with a hand that plays well postflop, gains fold equity to isolate weaker holdings, and keeps the sizing reasonable against Flash’s deep stack and Pro’s shorter stack. Plan: proceed cautiously if met with a sizable 3-bet (fold or re-evaluate vs shove depending on size and opponent); continuation-bet favorable flops and use our spade/backdoor equity to realize equity when called.

J6o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. Even with button position, this hand doesn't play well postflop and is too weak to profitably call or 3-bet. Minni has been active but UTG raises still represent decent strength. Easy fold.

Minni has been opening very frequently recently, suggesting a wide range. However, 98o is a marginal hand to defend from the small blind, especially since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. With a short-stacked player (Pro) in the big blind who may look to shove over a call, and given my current chip lead, I prefer to fold and wait for better spots rather than play a mediocre hand out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 4-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Facing a raise to 300 from Minni (UTG), I would need to call 200 chips into a pot of 450. The pot odds are approximately 30.7%, but 4-2 offsuit has very poor playability and equity realization, especially out of position against a large stack. Even with decent stack depth (43 BBs), defending this hand is -EV. I will fold.
6c9d offsuit from UTG is a weak hand. Not worth opening from early position against 3 opponents. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Q4o. While opening ranges widen in short-handed play (4 players), Q4o is still a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability. Both Pro in the small blind and Minni in the big blind have been active, with Pro being short-stacked and potentially looking for a shoving opportunity. Risking chips on a weak hand like Q4o when I currently hold the chip lead is unnecessary. I will fold and wait for a more favorable hand.

Action is folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold Kh 2c. While a King has some high-card value, K2o is at the bottom of the range and plays poorly out of position, especially against the Big Blind (Minni) who has a commanding chip lead (over 220 BBs compared to my 43 BBs) and has been showing significant aggression in recent hands. Limping invites a raise or puts me in a difficult post-flop spot with a weak kicker, and raising is a loose bluff against a big stack. Folding is the prudent play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game, which is effectively the Cutoff position. J9o is a marginal hand that is generally a fold in this position, especially with a short stack (Pro) behind me who might look to shove and a very active big stack (Minni) in the blinds. Maintaining my chip lead by avoiding unnecessary marginal spots is the best strategy here.

I am on the Button with 94o in a 4-handed game. The UTG player folded. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, 94o is simply too weak to open profitably, especially with a 43BB stack where I am not desperate. The blinds are deep-stacked and capable of defending or 3-betting. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I'm in the small blind with J♠4♥ and both UTG (Flash) and the Button (Pro) folded — heads-up against Claude in the big blind (9600). The cost to see the flop is only 50 into a 150 pot, so I'll take the cheap price to realize equity with a marginal hand rather than iso-raising and playing a bigger pot out of position (risking a 3-bet). Plan: call to see the flop (total commitment 100). If I hit a jack or get good draw/runout, I'll proceed; otherwise I'll exercise pot control and fold to heavy aggression.
I have Q2o in the big blind with a limped pot. This is a weak hand but I get to see the flop for free. No reason to raise with this marginal holding - I'll check and see the flop.

I called preflop to realize equity with J♠4♥. The flop 5♠ 3♥ 2♦ is very dry — I have no pair, only an overcard (J), a backdoor heart and a gutshot to a 6. Distinct outs: 3 jacks + 3 fours + 4 sixes ≈ 10 outs (roughly 38% to improve to a pair/straight by the river), with only a small backdoor flush possibility. I’m out of position and the pot is tiny relative to my stack, so I prefer pot control and to see a free turn. I’ll check to realize equity; I’ll call a small probe (≈≤half‑pot ≈100) but fold to pot‑sized or larger aggression unless I improve on the turn.

I flopped bottom pair with Q2o on a 5-3-2 board in a limped pot. Minni checked to me. I have showdown value but a vulnerable hand. A small bet (~50% pot) makes sense for thin value against Ax/Kx high and to protect against overcards. If raised, I can fold.

Call 100. The flop is very dry and I have no pair with only backdoor heart and a gutshot; however Claude's bet is small (100 into a 300 pot ≈ 1/3 pot) so the pot odds are favorable. I have roughly ~10 outs (~35–40% to improve by the river), while calling 100 to win ~400 requires only ~20–25% equity, so a call is +EV. I want to keep the pot controlled out of position and realize equity — I will call the probe to see a turn and reassess if I pair or pick up additional outs. I won't lead or raise OOP with J4; I will fold to pot‑sized or larger aggression on the turn unless I improve.

Out of position with J♠4♥ on 5♠-3♥-2♦-8♦ — I have no pair and only spare outs. My clean outs on the river are roughly 10 cards (3 Jacks + 3 Fours + 4 Aces) ≈ 22% to improve. The pot is only 400 and there’s no bet to call now, so checking to keep the pot small and see a cheap river is the prudent play. I’ll check and fold to significant river aggression (pot-sized or larger, >~200) unless I improve; I will call small-to-medium river bets (~≤150) because the pot odds would justify it.

I have bottom pair with a weak kicker on a 5-3-2-8 board. The turn 8d doesn't change much. Minni called my flop bet and checked again. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Betting again risks building a pot where I only get called by better hands. Check for pot control and try to see a cheap showdown.

Board is 5♠-3♥-2♦-8♦-8♠ and I hold J♠4♥ — I have no pair and didn’t improve on the river. I called flop to realize equity and checked the turn; the line (small flop probe then turn check) looks like weakness or a marginal made hand from Claude. Pot is only 400 and there’s currently no bet to respond to. Betting as a bluff is possible but would need to be a sizable bet to fold out many hands, and Claude’s line plus his deep stack make that bluff low EV. I prefer to conserve chips and take the cheap showdown: check. If Claude bets small on my check I’ll consider a thin call (<=~150) for pot odds, but I’ll fold to pot-sized or larger river aggression.

River with Q2o on 5-3-2-8-8 board. I have two pair (8s and 2s) with Q kicker. Minni has shown weakness throughout - limped pre, check-called flop, checked turn and river. While I could bet thin for value against weaker holdings like A-high or K-high, my hand is marginal and Minni could be trapping. The pot is small at 400, so the risk/reward of betting doesn't justify it. Check back for safe showdown.

I am in UTG position (4-handed) with 43 BBs, currently the short stack. My hand is 9d Tc (offsuit connector). While ranges widen in short-handed play, T9o is still at the bottom of or outside a standard opening range from UTG. Opening risks engaging with the larger stacks (Minni and Flash) out of position with a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop. Preserving chips for a better spot is the prudent strategy here.

I'm on the button with J♢J♣ (JTo off-suit) and only the blinds to act. This is a standard open-raise spot: raise for fold equity, to isolate the big blind, and to take the initiative with position postflop.
