Table tendencies show SB (Claude) often gives up to opens and BB (Flash) is capable of defending but I prefer to play pots in position. With a deep 22k stack I can size comfortably. I'll raise to 300 (3x BB) — a size consistent with prior opens here. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet or re-evaluate if it's a small 3-bet (call in position vs a modest 3-bet depending on sizing and villain). If called, proceed to use positional advantage and c-bet selectively on favorable flops.
Q6o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Out of position with a dominated hand that lacks connectivity - clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 7d. Minni, who has a similar stack size, raised to 3x from the button. After Claude folds in the Small Blind, the pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call. This gives me excellent pot odds (over 3-to-1), and J7 suited is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard button opening range. While I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the playability of suited cards makes this a standard call to see a flop.

I have completely missed this flop with my Jack-Seven of diamonds. The board is eight-high and I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. My hand has very little equity here, with only one overcard to the highest board card and a remote backdoor flush draw. Standard procedure in the Big Blind against a button raiser on a dry flop is to check to the raiser to see their continuation bet. I have no reason to lead out and will likely fold if faced with a significant bet.

I opened to 300 from the button and the BB called. Flop 3c‑8c‑4h with Jc‑Td: I have two overcards and a backdoor (runner‑runner) club draw, and my preflop raising range is weighted toward many broadways and missed hands on this dry, low flop. Flash (BB) checked, so this is a favorable spot to take the initiative and apply pressure — many of his calling combos will fold to a credible c‑bet. I’ll make a half‑pot continuation bet (≈50% pot = 325) to get folds from unpaired hands and to charge draws, while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (bet for value if I pair or improve, check/control if a blank). If he check‑raises big I’ll give up unless I pick up a strong improvement.

I missed the flop completely with my Jack-Seven of diamonds. The board is eight-high (3c, 8c, 4h) and I have no draws, not even a backdoor diamond flush draw since there are no diamonds on the board. Minni is the pre-flop aggressor and has followed through with a half-pot continuation bet. While I have one overcard (the Jack) to the top card on the board, my hand is currently just Jack-high with very little equity or playability out of position. There is no reason to put more chips into this pot when I have zero showdown value and no significant drawing potential.

UTG with Kc2c is a marginal, frequently dominated hand (many better Kx and broadways). In 4-handed position I’ll be out of position to three players (Button, SB and BB) and both the Button (Claude, ~98k) and SB (Flash, very deep) have shown willingness to 3‑bet/raise — so an open here would often face a shove or a big 3‑bet. Calling 100 to limp is passive and gives up position and fold equity. Preserving a healthy stack and waiting for a better spot is the highest EV line, so I fold.

On the button with Q2o after UTG folds. This is a marginal spot - Q2o is weak but button steal opportunities are valuable. Against just the blinds, I can attempt a steal with a standard 2.5x raise. Flash has a big stack and Pro is shorter. Worth a try to pick up the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 5c, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. Defending such a weak hand out of position against a button open is generally a losing play, as 8-5 offsuit has poor playability and realization of equity. Given the stack sizes and the strength of my hand, folding is the standard and most prudent decision.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). My hand is J6o. The pot odds are roughly 27% (150 to call into a total pot of 550), but J6o performs poorly against a standard opening range and suffers significantly from being out of position. It is difficult to realize equity with this hand, and it is easily dominated. Defending here is likely -EV compared to folding and preserving chips for a better spot.

Pocket 9s in UTG is a strong hand in a 4-handed game. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB. This hand plays well against likely calling ranges and I can get value preflop while having position decisions on later streets.

I am on the button with 9c 5c. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG (which is the cutoff in this 4-handed game). While my hand is suited, 9-5 suited is far too weak to call a 2.5x open, even with positional advantage. The risk of being squeezed by the blinds (especially Pro who is relatively short-stacked) is significant. There's no reason to contest this pot with such a marginal hand when I have a comfortable chip lead. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce offsuit facing a raise from Claude in the UTG position. UTG opening ranges are typically stronger, dominating my weak King. Furthermore, playing K2o out of position is a losing strategy due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. My stack is 41.75 BB, so there is no desperation to play junk hands. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I'm in the big blind with A♠ T♥ facing an UTG open to 250 in a short-handed table. ATo is strong heads-up, blocks a lot of Claude's best Ax/AK combos, and plays well postflop — so I want to apply pressure and isolate. I 3-bet to 1,000 (~4x the open): big enough to fold out weaker holdings and price out speculative callers, but small relative to both stacks so we keep postflop maneuverability. If Claude folds we take the pot; if he calls I'll play aggressively on favorable textures; if he 4-bets large I'll respect it and fold ATo (or re-evaluate by sizing).

Pocket 9s facing a 3-bet from Minni. Getting roughly 1.7:1 pot odds to call 750 into 1300. Minni has been active with 3-bets throughout the tournament. With set mining potential (~7.5:1 implied odds needed) and my remaining stack of ~9k behind, I have decent implied odds if I hit. 99 is too strong to fold here given the pot odds, but 4-betting would commit too much of my stack OOP. Calling to see a flop is the best line.

I 3‑bet preflop and Claude called, so my perceived range is strong. On 3♠‑8♣‑6♠ I hold A♠ T♥ — no pair but I have the ace blocker and a very slim backdoor spade route. Claude’s calling range contains a lot of missed broadways and medium hands that will fold to pressure, while only sets/overpairs and some 8/6 pairs strongly continue. Out of position, I want to take the initiative without bloating the pot — a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) gives good fold equity and keeps options on later streets. I’ll bet 700 to price out pure air and deny free cards; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and be willing to fold to a significant check‑raise.

I have pocket 9s - an overpair on this 3s 8c 6s board. Minni's small 700 bet into ~2050 pot (about 1/3 pot) is a standard c-bet sizing. My overpair is ahead of most of their 3-bet range that missed (AK, AQ, KQ) and ahead of draws. However, I'm behind bigger overpairs (TT-AA) which are definitely in Minni's 3-bet range. The board has a flush draw. Calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me see a turn card, and keeps their bluffs in. Raising would build the pot but risks getting blown off my hand if they have TT+. Given stack depth and position (OOP), calling to see the turn is the prudent play.

Board: 3♠-8♣-6♠-8♠. I hold A♠T♥ — no pair but I block a lot of Ax combos and I now have a one-card nut-flush draw (As + three spades on board → ~9 outs ≈ 19–20% to hit the river). Claude called my 3‑bet and my small flop c‑bet, so his calling range includes Ax, medium pairs, some sets/overpairs and two‑spade combos. The turn pairs the board and brings more spade/boat risk (so my fold equity is reduced), but there are still many missed hands (AK/AQ/KQ, smaller pairs) that I can fold out. I’ll fire a modest turn stab to deny free cards, charge draws and keep the pot manageable — sizing small so I can fold to a sizable check‑raise. If he raises big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

Turn brought 8s - pairing the board and completing the flush draw. I have 9s9h for two pair (99 over 88). Minni's 1200 bet into 3450 is ~35% pot. Problem: I'm behind overpairs (TT-AA have better two pair) and any flush. Minni's 3-bet range is heavily weighted to big pairs and hands like AK/AQs. If they have AKs/AQs with spades, they just made a flush. Against their likely range, I'm in bad shape - only beating AK/AQ without spades that's bluffing. Pot odds are ~20% needed, but my equity vs their value range is poor. The smart play is to fold here and save the 1200+ chips rather than chase a losing situation OOP.

I am holding 2d 8s in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands and offers no significant equity or playability. Given the tournament context and my current stack size, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with Qc 5c. Action folded to me. While the Button is an advantageous position to steal the blinds, my hand is on the weaker end of the opening range (Q5 suited). The Small Blind (Minni) is the chip leader with a massive stack and has shown aggression recently, making it likely she will defend or 3-bet, putting me in a difficult spot with a marginal hand. The Big Blind also covers me significantly. With ~42 BBs, I am the shortest stack at the table (though still deep), so preserving my stack for better opportunities where I have higher equity or fold equity is preferable to opening loose-marginal hands against aggressive big stacks. Therefore, I will fold.

UTG and Button folded — I'm effectively heads-up vs Claude in the big blind. I hold A♦3♥: not a premium hand but an Ace that fares well against a BB's calling range and gives me decent playability. Raising to the min (200 total) is the best line: it applies steal/fold equity, gains initiative postflop, and keeps the sizing small so I can fold to a big 3‑bet without committing many chips. Completing to 100 is too passive out of position; folding forfeits too much equity. I'll raise to 200 to try to take the pot or play the flop aggressively if called.

Facing a min-raise from Minni in the SB with 86o. Getting 3:1 pot odds (100 to win 300). While the odds are decent, 86o is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Minni is the chip leader with 24k+ and has been aggressive. This hand has minimal equity and poor post-flop playability. Folding to preserve chips.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed) with Ah 4d. With a stack of 41.75 BB, I am the shortest stack at the table. A4o is a marginal hand that is typically folded from early position, even in 4-handed play, due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. With aggressive, deep-stacked opponents behind me (particularly Minni on the Button who has been raising frequently), opening this hand risks getting 3-bet or called while out of position with a weak kicker. Preserving my stack for better spots is the optimal strategy here.

I’m on the button with A♠6♥ in a 4-handed game — great position and an Ace blocker. Blinds are 50/100 (pot 150) and it’s only 100 to call, but opening is standard here. Claude (SB) has ~8.1k and Flash (BB) 23k — both deep enough to defend, but I want to take the initiative and keep the pot manageable. I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300 total) — small, standard sizing that gets fold equity, widens my BTN opening range, and lets me play post‑flop with position. If I face a big 3‑bet I’ll fold most non‑premium hands given the weak kicker; I’ll call smaller 3‑bets to realize position and leverage the Ace blocker.
J7o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. I'm out of position with a marginal holding. Even though Minni is active, J7o doesn't play well postflop OOP. Clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Jack of hearts, a very strong drawing hand and a solid starting hand in general. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, which is a standard opening range. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 more to win a total pot of 450) and have a deep stack. While I could 3-bet to take control of the hand, calling allows me to see a flop and utilize the post-flop playability of suited connectors/broadway cards without bloating the pot out of position against another big stack.

I have Queen-Jack of hearts on a low, scattered flop of 7-2-5 with two clubs. As the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise from the button (Minni), my standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand has two overcards and a backdoor heart flush draw, but it is currently weak. I will check to see how Minni proceeds before deciding whether to continue in the hand.

Heads‑up on a very dry flop (7♣ 2♥ 5♣). I have A♠6♥ — only ace‑high but with an important Ace blocker that reduces opponent A‑x combos. BB defended a wide range preflop, so many of his holdings are unpaired overcards or weak connected suited hands that I can fold out with pressure. I want to take the initiative and charge floats/club draws while keeping the pot manageable with my weak kicker, so I’ll make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot). This sizing gets value/folds from worse hands and keeps control if I get called. Plan if called: pot‑control on most turns and re‑evaluate (barrel only on favorable/blank turns or when villain shows weakness). If I face a sizable raise, I’ll let go — my hand has poor showdown value versus a raise.

On this 7-2-5 rainbow flop (with two clubs), Minni has placed a small continuation bet of 225 into a 650 pot. My current hand consists of two overcards (Queen and Jack) and a backdoor heart flush draw. The pot odds are very favorable, requiring me to call 225 to win a potential 1100, which is approximately 20.5%. Given the small sizing and the fact that many of Minni's high-card hands missed this flop, I have enough equity with my overcards and backdoor potential to see a turn card. Folding would be too tight against a 1/3 pot bet, and raising is unnecessary given my position and hand strength. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

The 8 of hearts on the turn is a great card for my hand, as it picks up a heart flush draw to go along with my two overcards (Queen and Jack). In a 1100 chip pot, I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop aggressor. My standard play here is to check to the aggressor. If Minni bets, I have significant equity (9 flush outs plus potentially 6 overcard outs) to call and see the river. If Minni checks back, I get a free card to potentially hit my flush or a high pair. Leading (donking) here doesn't serve much purpose as it allows Minni to fold her bluffs and potentially raise with her strong value hands, whereas checking keeps her range wide.

I check. I only have ace‑high (A♠6♥) and after barreling the flop I was called — Flash’s range contains many pairs (7s/5s/2s), some slowplayed stronger hands, club draws and floating hands. The turn 8♥ is a relatively blank card for my range but creates pair/heart combinations that beat me; betting here would build a pot I’m usually behind in and will be called by the hands that beat me. With position and a healthy stack I should control the pot size, see the river for free, and keep the option to call small turn/river bets or fold to large pressure. Checking preserves fold equity on river and avoids getting raised off a marginal hand, so I’ll check back.

On the river, the board has paired with eights, resulting in a final board of 7c-2h-5c-8h-8d. My Queen-Jack high has very little showdown value. However, Minni checked back on the turn after I checked to her, which suggests she might be holding a marginal hand like Ace-high or a small pocket pair, or perhaps a missed draw. While betting could potentially force a fold from Ace-high, I have played the hand passively thus far and leading out now might not be credible. I will check and hope to either win a cheap showdown against a worse high card or simply concede the pot if she bets, as preserving my significant stack is a priority at this stage of the tournament.

The river paired the board (7♣ 2♥ 5♣ 8♥ 8♦). I have A♠6♥ — only ace‑high with a weak kicker. Flash defended wide preflop and called my small flop bet, then went passive on turn and river; that line is consistent with a single pair (7/5/2), some missed club draws, or a medium holding that wants showdown. Betting here has very little fold equity against the hands that beat me and will often be called (or raised) by pairs — I’m usually behind and building a pot I don’t want. I’ll check to keep the pot small and go to showdown. If faced with a meaningful river bet I’ll fold to pressure (call only a very small probe).

AQs UTG in a 4‑handed game is a clear open-raise for value and to avoid a multiway pot. Stacks are deep (I have ~251bb) so I want a standard, respectable sizing that matches table tendencies (many opens at 250–300) — 3x the BB is appropriate. Raising to 300 builds the pot with a strong hand, denies cheap odds to the blinds, and keeps weaker Ax/broadway hands and dominated queens from seeing cheap flops. If I face a small 3‑bet I can continue (call or 4‑bet depending on sizing); versus a large, polarized 3‑bet or shove I’ll re-evaluate based on the opponent, but preflop the correct action is to open to 300.

Q4o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. While I'm on the button, Q4o doesn't play well postflop - it's not suited, not connected, and has poor kicker. Minni has been active but calling or 3-betting here with this hand is -EV. Easy fold.

I am holding 4d 2s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni opened with a raise to 300 from under the gun (effectively the cutoff in a 4-handed game). Calling from the small blind would put me out of position for the remainder of the hand with zero equity and poor playability. There is no reason to defend this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h7d facing a 3bb raise from Minni in UTG (4-handed). Minni has been opening very frequently with this sizing, suggesting a wide range rather than just premium hands. The pot offers me odds of 200 to call for a pot of 650 (approx 30.7%). My hand, an off-suit connector, has decent equity (around 38-40%) against a wide 4-handed opening range. Although playing out of position is difficult, the price is good enough to defend my blind and see a flop. I will call.

I defended the Big Blind with 8h7d against a UTG open. The flop comes 6c 3s Td. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and two weak overcards to the 6 and 3. As the pre-flop defender acting out of position, the standard line is to check to the aggressor. The board texture is relatively dry but favors the opener's range (broadways, sets, overpairs). I will check and evaluate based on whether Minni bets and the size of that bet. Donk betting here accomplishes little as I likely won't fold out better hands and don't want to build a pot out of position with just a gutshot.

I opened UTG with A♠Q♥ and got heads‑up to the BB who just checked. The flop (T♦ 6♣ 3♠) is very dry — I have two overcards and I’m in position. As the preflop aggressor I should continue to apply pressure: a c‑bet will fold a lot of the BB’s missed calling range (weak broadways, ragged suited connectors, small pairs) and protects my equity. I’ll size to roughly 50% pot (325 into 650) — big enough to deny equity and pick up the pot often, but not so large as to needlessly commit chips versus a short (~39bb) opponent. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously and look to barrel when an A or Q appears; if he raises/shoves I’ll give credit to a very strong made hand and fold rather than get into a flip with just overcards.

I hold 8h7d on a 6c 3s Td flop. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) which gives me 4 clean outs. Facing a half-pot c-bet (325 into 650) from the UTG opener, I am getting 25% pot odds. My direct equity to hit the straight on the turn is approximately 8.5%. Even considering two streets, it is roughly 17%. While I have some backdoor equity and implied odds if I hit the disguised straight, playing a naked gutshot out of position against a strong range that likely connects with the Ten or holds overpairs is unprofitable. The price is not cheap enough to justify a float OOP.
T3o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. No reason to open this from early position against 3 opponents.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. While it is tempting to steal the blinds from this position, especially after Claude folded, Ten-Four is a very weak hand. Pro in the Small Blind has a relatively short stack and might be looking for an opportunity to shove, and Minni in the Big Blind has a large stack and has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Risking chips on a low-equity steal attempt is not optimal here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (AKo) in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand. The action has folded to me. My stack is roughly 38 big blinds. Facing the Big Blind (Minni) who has a massive chip lead, I want to build the pot with my strong holding rather than limp and let them see a free flop with a random hand. Raising also gives me the initiative. A standard raise size of 3 big blinds (300 chips) is appropriate here to extract value and deny equity to weak hands, while not committing too much of my stack pre-flop if I encounter heavy resistance, though I am willing to play for stacks with this hand given my stack depth.

I’m in the big blind with A♥Q♦ facing a 3BB open to 300 from the small blind. AQo is a strong heads‑up hand and I have a massive stack advantage (25,350 vs 3,575), so I want to isolate and take the initiative rather than just call and give them control. A 3‑bet to 1,000 (~3.3x the open) gives good fold equity, builds the pot when I’m likely ahead, and is small enough to keep weaker hands and calls in. If Pro jams I can evaluate/call given my equity; folding is too weak here. Raise to 1,000 to pressure and extract value.

I hold Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind. I raised to 300 and the Big Blind (Minni), who is the chip leader, 3-bet to 1000. My remaining stack is 3575. Given the Blind vs Blind dynamic, Minni's 3-bet range is likely wide, trying to bully the short stack. AKo is a premium hand that blocks AA and KK. With a Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) that would be less than 1.5 if I just call, playing post-flop out of position is difficult if I miss. Shoving (4-betting all-in) maximizes fold equity against Minni's bluffs and ensures I realize my full equity against their calling range. I am currently the shortest stack at the table and this is a prime opportunity to double up.

Pro 4-bet to 3,575 after my 3-bet to 1,000. I have A♥Q♦ — a very strong heads‑up hand that does well vs a shove range of AK/AQ/pp and some bluffs. The pot is 4,575; calling costs 2,575 and would make the pot 7,150, so I need ~36% equity to justify a call (2,575 / 7,150 ≈ 36%). AQo typically has roughly that equity vs a realistic shove range (and does much better if the range contains any bluffs or weaker broadways). I have a massive stack advantage, so there’s no need to 5‑bet shove and I shouldn’t fold a hand that has close to the right price. Calling preserves my post‑flop options and leverages my equity — I will call the 4‑bet.

I hold Ace-King on an Ace-high flop (Ac Th 3c), giving me Top Pair Top Kicker. After the pre-flop leveling war resulted in a massive pot of 7,150, I have only 300 chips remaining in my stack. With an SPR of virtually zero (0.04), I am completely committed to this hand. Checking risks giving a free card or confusing the action, whereas betting the final 300 chips guarantees the money goes in now. My opponent gets incredible odds to call, so I'm not inducing a fold, but I ensure I realize my equity immediately. There is no strategic merit to doing anything other than putting the last chips in.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♥Q♦) on A♣ T♥ 3♣. Preflop I already committed to the 4‑bet pot and the current bet is tiny (300 into a 7,450 pot) — essentially a probe/blocking bet. Calling is the clear, low-variance line: it gets the correct price to keep worse hands and bluffs in, avoids turning the pot into a bloated commitment versus sets or A‑K, and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn with position and a big stack. I will call the 300 to see the next card and extract value or fold later if the board or action dictates.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 5s 2s. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak to open from early position, especially with active players like Minni and Claude behind me. Given my healthy stack size, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a bottom-tier starting hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I am on the Button with 74o, which is a very weak hand. UTG folded. With a comfortable stack of 77BB, there is no need to try to steal the blinds with such poor equity, especially against a large stack in the SB and a capable BB. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Heads-up vs Claude (BB) with 4h8h suited. In heads-up the SB is on the button (positional advantage post-flop), and 4-8 suited has good playability and equity versus a wide defending range. I have a large stack advantage, so I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure while still preserving maneuverability. Raising gives fold equity and lets me play post-flop in position if called. I’ll raise to 300 (roughly 3x the BB) — big enough to discourage some light defenses but not overly commit. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, c-bet on dry/advantageous flops and realize equity on connected/hearty boards.

Holding 3c6h - one of the weakest hands in poker. Even though I'm getting 2:1 odds to call 200, this hand has terrible playability post-flop. Against Minni's raising range from SB, I'll be dominated often and struggle to realize equity. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position (first to act in a 4-handed game) with J7 offsuit. This hand is statistically very weak and falls well outside of a standard profitable opening range, even in a short-handed format. With a comfortable stack of ~77 BBs, there is no pressure to make loose speculative plays. Opening here risks getting 3-bet by the aggressive big stacks behind me or playing a weak hand out of position. The correct strategic move is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with A♥2♠ (offsuit) and only the blinds to act. With deep stacks (~217bb) and position, this is a standard open: the hand has good blocker value vs other A-x and plays well post‑flop in position. Raising takes the initiative, applies pressure to the small blind and big blind, and denies the correct odds for many speculative hands to call. I’ll open to ~3x the BB (300 total) — big enough to discourage loose defenses but small relative to stacks so I can fold to a large 3‑bet or continue postflop if called. If I face a big 3‑bet from Flash I’ll likely fold; versus a small 3‑bet or a call I’ll use position to c‑bet favorable boards and extract value where appropriate.

K6 suited in the SB vs a button open. Minni has been active with raises. K6s has some playability with flush potential and top pair possibilities. The pot odds are decent (450:250). Folding is reasonable but I can defend here occasionally with suited broadway. Calling to see a flop.

I am in the big blind with King-Seven offsuit. Minni, who has been active and raising frequently from late positions, raised to 300 from the button. Claude called from the small blind. I am getting very good pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 900) to see a flop. King-high has decent equity against button opening ranges, and folding here would be too tight given the price. I will call and see a flop.

Monotone club flop (8c4cAc) and I have Kd6d - zero equity here. No pair, no flush draw, no backdoor anything useful. This is an easy check. I have no business betting into this board with complete air. If someone bets, I'll fold.

I have King-high with no clubs on a monotone Ace-high board. This is a very poor board for my specific hand. Claude has checked to me. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, is behind me. I will check and likely fold to any bet, as I have no draw and little showdown value against two opponents on this texture.

I was the preflop raiser and I hold A♥2♠ on A♣8♣4♣ — top pair but no club. This is a monotone flop with a real flush risk (any opponent with two clubs already has a made flush, one-club combos have ~9 outs). Both opponents checked to me, which is a sign of weakness/suspect range and gives me an opportunity to both extract value and deny equity. Betting ~2/3 pot (600 into 900) prices many one-club draws out (caller must invest 600 to win 2100 → needs ~28.6% equity; a one-card club draw has only ~19% on the flop) while still getting value from worse A-x, medium pairs and some club+Ace combos. I’m using position and initiative to take the lead; if I get a simple call I’ll proceed carefully on the turn (pot-control vs runouts and re-evaluate to fold to large raises since I’m often behind to made flushes or better A-kickers). If I face a big raise I’ll assume I’m beat and fold; vs a small raise I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing/player. Bet 600.
Monotone club board (8c4cAc) and I hold Kd6d - complete air with zero clubs. No pair, no draw, no equity. Minni bet 600 and I have absolutely nothing to continue with. Clear fold.

I have King-high with no clubs on an Ace-high monotone board. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 600 into a 900 pot. I have zero clubs in my hand, so I have no flush draw, and King-high is significantly behind any Ace or any made flush. Given the board texture and the bet sizing, I have no reason to continue.

2♠5♦ is one of the weakest offsuit hands — no playability and poor implied odds. I'm UTG in a 4‑hand game, so I'd be out of position against the Button and both blinds. It costs 100 to see the flop and stealing here with junk is a losing line (vulnerable to 3‑bets and dominated hands). Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

On the button with 68o after UTG folds. This is a reasonable spot to attempt a steal with position, though the hand is weak. A standard 2.5x raise should pressure the blinds. Flash has a big stack though, so I need to be prepared to fold if faced with significant resistance.

I am in the small blind with 3h 6s, a very weak hand. Claude, on the button, has raised to 250. Although I have a large stack, calling out of position with such a poor hand is mathematically incorrect and strategically unwise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad 7c facing a 2.5x raise from the Button. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 150 to win a pot of 400, requiring roughly 27% equity. A7o performs well against a wide button opening range, having approximately 54% equity. Given I will be out of position post-flop and we are deep-stacked (75 BBs), flat-calling to see a flop is the standard and most prudent play to realize my equity without over-committing or isolating myself against a stronger range.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with Ace-high (Ad 7c) on a 9h-4c-2h flop. This is a fairly dry board for high cards, but connects with sets or random low pairs. As the pre-flop caller, standard strategy is to check to the aggressor (Button) to see if they continue. Leading out (donk betting) here is unnecessary as I want to keep their bluffing range in the hand and not fold out worse hands that I currently beat. I will check and evaluate their action.

Completely missed this flop with 68o - no pair, no draw. However, this is a dry board (2h 9h 4c) and Pro checked to me as the preflop raiser. A small c-bet of about 1/3 pot should take this down often enough to be profitable. Pro's check indicates weakness most of the time.

I am facing a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550 (roughly 36% pot) on a 9h-4c-2h board. I hold Ace-high (Ad 7c). The pot odds offered are attractive, requiring only about 21% equity to call (200 to win 950). On this low, somewhat disconnected texture, my Ace-high is ahead of my opponent's pure bluffs (like KQ, QJ, JT) and has live overcard outs against their value pairs. Given the wide range of a Button opener and the small sizing, folding Ace-high here would be too tight. I will call to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the Turn, the board is [2h 9h 4c 4d]. The 4d pairs the board, which is generally a neutral to slightly favorable card for the Big Blind's defending range compared to the Button's opening range, as I can have more random 4x combos. However, with Ace-high (Ad 7c), my primary objective is to get to showdown cheaply. I am currently beating all unpaired bluffing hands (like JT, QJ, flush draws). Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands or force folds from hands I am already beating. Therefore, checking is the correct play to keep the opponent's range wide and potentially induce a check-back or a bluff that I can evaluate. I will check and react to the opponent's action.

I have 68o with no pair, no draw on a 2h9h4c4d board. Pro check-called my flop c-bet and is checking again. Firing a second barrel with complete air against a player who's shown interest is risky. The 4 pairing doesn't change much - Pro could have a 9, a 4, or a heart draw. Check behind for pot control and give up on this bluff.

The river is the Ace of Clubs, making the final board [2h 9h 4c 4d Ac]. I now have top pair (Aces) with a 7 kicker. Due to the board containing a 9 and a pair of 4s, my hand is effectively A-A-4-4-9. This means I chop the pot with any opponent holding an Ace with a kicker of 8 or lower (A2-A8), as their kickers are counterfeited by the 9 on the board. I lose to AT+ and beat all non-Ace hands (like 9x or pocket pairs). The opponent checked back the turn, which suggests a capped range likely consisting of marginal value (like a 9 or low pair) or a draw. Betting here achieves little because worse hands (like a 9) will likely fold on an Ace river, and better hands will call. Checking allows me to realize my equity (winning or chopping) without bloating the pot against better hands, and potentially induces a bluff from missed draws.

I have 68o with no pair - just 6 high. Pro checked to me on the river after the Ac came. While the Ace could be a good scare card to bluff, Pro already showed interest by calling my flop c-bet. Bluffing into a player who's shown resistance with complete air is -EV. I'll check behind and give up this small pot.
9Th offsuit in UTG at 4-handed table. Marginal but playable hand. With 73 BBs, I can open raise to 2.5x. This hand has decent playability postflop with straight potential.

I am on the button with AQs, a premium hand. Claude (UTG) has opened for 2.5bb. Claude has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets in previous hands (Hands 13, 22, 72). By raising here, I take the initiative, isolate a shorter stack, and potentially end the hand immediately. A standard 3-bet size from the button is around 3x the original raise.

I hold 3h 5s in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. The action involves an UTG open from Claude and a 3-bet from Flash on the Button. Facing this aggression with trash cards and being out of position, I have no viable option other than to fold. Conserving chips for better spots is the only logical play.

I’m in the big blind with 6♦3♣. Claude opened UTG to 250 and Flash 3-bet to 750; pot is 1150 and it costs me 650 to call (≈36% pot odds). 6-3 offsuit has very poor equity against an UTG open and a button 3-bet (likely well below the needed ~36%), and I’d be playing a multiway or out-of-position pot against deeper stacks. 4-betting as a bluff is too loose here. Preserve a healthy stack for better spots — fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash with 9To offsuit out of position. I need to call 500 into 1150 (~30% equity needed). 9To is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet range, especially OOP. Flash has shown aggression throughout the tournament. This is a standard fold - the hand doesn't have enough equity or playability to continue.

I am holding As5s, a strong suited ace, in a four-handed game. As the chip leader, I want to maintain pressure and exploit my position. Opening from UTG with this hand is standard as it has significant pre-flop equity and excellent post-flop playability, including flush and straight potential. A standard 2.5x raise to 250 is appropriate to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds.

I am holding [6d, Th] on the Button facing a raise to 250 from Flash in UTG. T6o is a very weak hand with poor playability and significant reverse implied odds, especially against an UTG opening range which is typically stronger. Despite having position, the hand strength does not justify calling or 3-betting. Conserving chips for a better spot is the optimal strategy.

Facing an early-position open to 250, A♣5♦ is an Ace but with a very weak kicker and is often dominated by the raiser’s UTG range (AQ+, AJ, AK, medium/strong pairs). Folding would be too tight given the cheap price to see a flop; a min-3-bet to 400 as a bluff is suboptimal because I lack strong blockers and won’t get many better hands to fold. Calling (putting a total of 250 into the pot) is the best play: it’s a small defense from the small blind, keeps pot control with deep stacks, preserves postflop playability (can hit top pair, backdoor draws), and lets me re-evaluate if the big blind fights or the raiser shows aggression. I’ll call 200 more (250 total committed) and proceed cautiously on the flop.

K7o in BB facing a min-raise from UTG with SB calling. Getting 4:1 pot odds (150 to call into 600). K7o is marginal but the price is right to see a flop. Not strong enough to squeeze, but closing the action with decent pot odds makes calling reasonable.

I have top pair (A♣) but a very weak 5 kicker on a three-way flop (A♥ 2♥ Q♣). Flash was the preflop raiser (strong-ish range) and Claude called — both can have Ax hands, Qx, or medium pairs. With two opponents I want to take the initiative: a half‑pot bet charges worse aces, Qx and pocket pairs, and makes heart-heavy and other drawing hands pay to see the turn. Betting also denies free cards that could improve opponents into hands that beat my weak kicker. I’ll make a controlled value/protection bet of roughly half the pot (375). If I face a large raise I’ll strongly consider folding; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.
Missed flop completely with K7o on Ah 2h Qc. No pair, no flush draw (only 7h), no straight draw. Facing a half-pot bet with zero equity and no showdown value. Easy fold.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board of Ah-2h-Qc. My kicker (5s) is weak, but top pair is a strong hand in a 4-handed game. Minni led into me (the pre-flop raiser) with a half-pot bet. This 'donk bet' could represent a flush draw, a Queen, or a weak Ace. Raising here might force out her bluffs and put me in a tough spot if she reraises, especially since her range could include stronger Aces (AK, AJ, AT) or sets. Calling is the most balanced play, keeping the pot manageable, allowing her to continue with bluffs, and utilizing my position to see how the turn develops.

I have top pair (A♣) but a very weak 5 kicker. Flash was the UTG raiser and then called my flop bet, so his continuing range contains many Ax (some with better kickers), Qx, and medium pocket pairs — plus some heart draws. The turn 7♠ is a blank that doesn’t improve my hand and betting again just bloats the pot and opens me to check‑raises or river shove decisions when I’m often dominated. I want pot control and to avoid committing more chips with a marginal holding: check to see how he reacts on the turn and re‑evaluate on the river (call small bets for value vs worse aces/Qx, fold to large, polarized aggression).

I have top pair with Aces, but my kicker (5) is very weak. Minni's check on the turn after leading the flop suggests she is either slowing down with a marginal hand (like a Queen) or is on a draw (like hearts). By checking behind, I control the pot size against the only other deep stack, avoid a potential check-raise that would force me to fold my equity, and can safely evaluate the river. Checking also keeps her range wide, potentially inducing a bluff on the river if her draws miss.

I have top pair A♣5♦ but a very weak kicker. Flash opened UTG, called my half‑pot flop bet and then checked the turn — a line consistent with worse Ax, Qx or a medium pocket pair (slowplayed two‑pair/set is possible but less likely given the passive turn). The river 8♠ is a blank for my hand and betting now just bloats the pot and subjects me to tough decisions vs better A‑kickert hands (AK/AQ) or check‑raises. I want pot control: check to see his action. If he checks back I take showdown; if he bets a small/medium sizing I will call for value vs worse aces/Qx but fold to a large, polarized shove or raise. Checking preserves chips with a marginal holding and keeps weaker hands in that will pay off small bets.

On the river, the board is Ah-2h-Qc-7s-8s. I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (5) is very weak. Minni led the flop and then checked both the turn and the river. This sequence often indicates a hand with marginal showdown value like a Queen, or a missed heart draw. While I might get value from a Queen if I bet, I also risk being check-raised by a better Ace that Minni played cautiously, or a slow-played set. Given that I have a hand that will win at showdown a high percentage of the time against her range and the pot is already a decent size, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and avoid unnecessary risk against the other big stack.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Five offsuit (Td 5h), which is a statistically very weak hand. Playing this hand out of position against three opponents, two of whom have very deep stacks, is negative EV. There is no strategic reason to enter the pot. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I'm on the button with Qc6h — a marginal hand but in great position with two players in the blinds. Pot is 150, to-call 100, min-raise 200. Rather than limp/passively call, I want to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds while keeping the pot manageable. Claude (SB) is somewhat active and Flash (BB) is deep and willing to defend, so a standard 3x-ish open to 300 gives reasonable fold equity without overcommitting with a weak hand. If Claude 3-bets or shoves, Q6o is easy to fold; if I get called I'll c-bet favorable dry flops and pot-control on coordinated/high-card boards. Raise to 300 to take the initiative and use position postflop.

K4o in the SB facing a button raise. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Minni has been active throughout the tournament. K4o doesn't have good playability postflop OOP - it's dominated by better kings and won't flop well often enough to justify calling. Standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd Jd, a strong suited broadway hand. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a typical button opening range. With a hand that plays well post-flop, calling is a standard and effective way to defend the blind without bloating the pot out of position against another big stack. I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (8s and Ks) along with two overcards. On this coordinated 9-7-T board, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is the standard and most strategic play. This allows me to see her action and potentially realize my equity or check-raise if the situation warrants it, while keeping the pot controlled if she also chooses to check.

I was the preflop raiser and I have position, while Flash (BB) checked the flop. The 9♠-7♣-T♥ board is relatively dry and misses a large portion of a BB calling range (many overcards, weak pairs, missed connectors). Q♣6♥ has almost no showdown/value equity, so this is a pure continuation-bet/bluff spot to seize the initiative and pick up the pot. I’ll make a small c-bet (~1/3–40% pot) to maximize fold equity without bloating the pot; if I get called I’ll exercise pot control (check turn when unimproved) and fold to strong aggression. If I face a check-raise or a large shove, Q6o is easy to fold.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw with overcards on a board of 9s-7c-Th. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 250 into a 650 pot (approximately 38% of the pot). I am getting nearly 3.6-to-1 odds to call, which is excellent given my equity. With 8 outs to a straight (any 8 or King), I have roughly a 32% chance of completing the straight by the river. Calling is the most prudent play to realize my equity while out of position, keeping the pot manageable and allowing me to potentially extract value if I hit one of my outs on the turn or river.

The turn brings an Ace of Clubs, which is a scare card that generally favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I still hold an open-ended straight draw (needing an 8 or a King for a straight). At this stage, checking is the most strategic move. It allows me to see how Minni reacts to the Ace. If she checks back, I get a free card to hit my straight on the river. If she bets, I can calculate if the pot odds are favorable enough to continue drawing to my 8 outs. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on an Ace is risky, so checking to realize equity or fold if the price is too high is the preferred line.

Turn: 9♠-7♣-T♥-A♣, I hold Q♣6♥. I initiated preflop and c‑bet the flop but the Ace on the turn is a scare card that hugely reduces my equity — I basically have queen‑high and no realistic draw (with only one club in hand I cannot make a flush with one card to come). Flash is a sticky defender (called my flop c‑bet) so my fold equity on a turn bluff is limited; a small bet will be called by many hands that currently beat me (T/9/pairs) and a large bet simply overcommits a marginal holding. With the pot already 1,150, the prudent play is pot control: check behind, see the river for free, and evaluate whether a river bluff is warranted or whether to fold to aggression. This minimizes loss vs value hands and preserves chips against a calling opponent.

On the river, the board is 9s-7c-Th-Ac-Jh. I have made a pair of Jacks with a Queen kicker. The Ace on the turn was a scare card that Minni checked behind, which suggests she likely does not have a strong Ace or was pot-controlling. The river Jack improved my hand to a pair, but it doesn't change the fact that an Ace is on the board. Since I am out of position and have decent showdown value, checking is the most strategic move. It allows me to see if Minni will check behind with a weaker hand or if she will attempt a bluff with her missed straight draws (like an 8 or a King). If she bets, I will evaluate the size, but a check-call is often appropriate here given the action.

Check. I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry 9♠‑7♣‑T♥ flop, but the A♣ on the turn and the J♥ on the river leave me with only Q‑high and almost no fold equity. Flash called the flop and then checked turn (and checked the river), which is a sticky line — likely weak pairs (T/9/7), a turned Ace, or missed draws that will call a river bluff. There’s no realistic value to extract and a bluff is low‑EV here, so I’ll control the pot and preserve chips by checking to showdown (or folding to significant aggression).

Short-handed (4 players) so I can open a wider UTG range. 5♠8♠ is a suited connector/gapper with decent flop playability and good implied odds with deep stacks (I ~217bb, Flash SB ~232bb, Pro BB ~81bb, Claude BTN ~67bb). I want initiative and fold equity versus the blinds, so I’ll open to a standard 3x the BB (300). That sizing pressures the blinds, keeps pot-to-stack ratio manageable, and lets me play postflop with position advantage if I get called. If I face a large 3-bet or an all-in from Claude I’ll likely fold this marginal hand; I’ll re-evaluate vs a small 3-bet depending on sizing and who it comes from.
J6o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Even on the button, this hand doesn't play well enough to call or 3-bet. Easy fold.

I have Ace-Jack suited in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand in a 4-handed game. Minni (UTG) has been very active and aggressive, raising frequently. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take the initiative, define her range, and potentially force a fold from the Big Blind to play the hand heads-up if Minni calls. This also leverages my stack size to put pressure on the opener.

I am facing a significant display of strength: an UTG open from Minni followed by a 3-bet from Flash in the Small Blind. A cold 3-bet from the SB against an UTG open typically represents a very strong range (TT+, AQ+, often QQ+ or AK). My hand, Ace-Ten offsuit, is likely dominated by both opponents' ranges. Additionally, I do not close the action; Minni could still 4-bet, which would force me out after investing chips. Calling 900 chips (about 11% of my stack) in this spot is negative EV. The prudent play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Call (total 1000). 5♠8♠ is a speculative, suited connector with strong postflop playability and with the button folded I will be last to act on the flop (positional advantage). Flash’s 3‑bet to 1000 is a normal-sized 3‑bet and Flash has shown aggressive tendencies, so the range will include bluffs and broadways — calling keeps those hands in and lets me realize equity. Calling 700 more into a 1400 pot gives ~33% immediate pot odds and, with both stacks deep (~214bb vs 223bb), strong implied odds if I hit a spade or a pair. A 4‑bet here is too thin and folding gives up too much equity. Plan: call to see the flop; continue aggressively with a pair/strong draw, fold to heavy action if I miss and the price/line looks unfavorable.

I have the nut flush draw (Ac Jc on a board with Kc 2c) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). As the pre-flop 3-better, I have the range advantage and the initiative. This board (K-Q-2) connects well with my perceived range (AK, AA, KK, QQ). A continuation bet here puts pressure on Minni's medium pocket pairs (77-JJ) and allows me to build a pot for when I hit my draws. Given my massive stack and equity, I want to continue being aggressive.

I have 5♠8♠ on K♣ 2♣ Q♦ — no pair and no immediate nut draw. Flash is a 3‑better preflop and now bets 1050 into the pot; folding surrenders good implied‑odds potential and raising as a bluff is too thin vs a 3‑bettor who can have a lot of K/Q/strong hands. I need to compare pot odds to my chance of improving: calling 1050 into a 3150 pot gives ~25% immediate pot odds. I have ~8 clean outs to make a pair (~31% to hit by river) plus a small runner‑runner spade flush possibility, so my raw chance to get to a reasonable hand by showdown is at or above the pot‑odds threshold. With position and deep stacks I can realize implied odds if I hit and can fold to heavy turn action if I miss. Plan: call the flop to see the turn; continue aggressively only if I pair or pick up a real draw, otherwise concede to strong aggression.

The turn 2d is a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Having bet the flop and been called, I have to respect that Minni likely has a King or a Queen. Since I have the nut flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (12 clean outs, plus potentially the remaining Aces), I have roughly 25-30% equity. Checking here is the most balanced play: it allows me to see a free river if she checks behind, or it gives me the opportunity to call a bet and realize my equity, as I have the pot odds to continue with my draws against her range.

I have 5♠8♠ on K♣ 2♣ Q♦ 2♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw. With only ~4 clean outs (two 5s, two 8s) my chance to improve on the river is very small. Flash was the preflop 3‑better and barreled the flop, so his range is heavily weighted toward Kx/Qx and club combos that dominate me; his check on the turn reads as pot‑control or caution after the board paired. Bluffing here has poor fold equity (he’s shown aggression and I lack blockers), and a bet risks a lot of chips with very little equity. I’m in position, so I should keep the pot controllable, realize equity cheaply, and gather river information — check and re‑evaluate on the river (look to bluff only if a favorable card and a clear giveaway from the opponent).

The river Jack of Spades improves my hand to a pair of Jacks. However, the board still contains a King and a Queen, and Minni called a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, which strongly suggests she has at least a piece of this board (likely a King or a Queen). While I have showdown value now, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet. Checking is the most prudent play to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible. It also allows me to potentially catch a bluff from missed draws, although my possession of the Ace of Clubs makes it less likely she was drawing to the nut flush.

I missed completely with 5♠8♠ on K♣ 2♣ Q♦ 2♦ J♠ — no pair, no meaningful blocker to K/Q/J, and no river equity to improve. Flash was the preflop 3‑better and c‑bettor on the flop; his turn check suggests pot control or a cautious line with a strong Kx/Qx/2x-heavy range. He checked the river too, so his range contains many hands that will call a river bluff. Given the poor fold equity and the small pot (4,200), betting is a negative‑EV play. I’ll keep the pot small and preserve chips: check and fold to any substantial river bet.
J5o is a weak hand from UTG position. No reason to open this - it's well outside a standard UTG opening range. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Queen-Trey offsuit. While I have positional advantage, this hand is extremely weak and prone to domination. Minni (Big Blind) and Pro (Small Blind) have been relatively active, making a steal attempt with such a poor hand unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the Small Blind with [4h Qd], a very weak off-suit hand (Q4o). The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader with a large stack and has shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands. While the price to complete is cheap (50 chips), playing a weak hand out of position against an aggressive big stack is generally a losing strategy due to poor equity realization. I have a healthy stack of roughly 80 big blinds and can afford to wait for better spots. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) is a strong enough hand to open from under-the-gun. I am the chip leader with over 250 big blinds, allowing me to play aggressively and put pressure on the shorter stacks (Pro and Claude) and the second-largest stack (Minni). A standard raise to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a balanced opening size that maintains control of the pot while providing room to fold if one of the short stacks shoves with a premium range.

The current game is 4-handed, meaning the UTG player (Flash) is effectively in the Cutoff position, suggesting a wider opening range than a full-ring UTG. With [Kd Jh] on the Button, I have a solid broadway hand that plays well in position. Folding would be too conservative given the reduced number of players and my position. 3-betting is an option, but KJo is easily dominated by calling ranges (AK, KQ, AJ) and cannot withstand a 4-bet. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, utilize my positional advantage post-flop, and evaluate based on the texture. The price is cheap (250 chips, ~3% of stack) to see a flop with a decent hand.

Facing an UTG raise to 250 with the button calling, I'm in the small blind with K♠4♥ — a weak, easily dominated offsuit King, and I'll be out of position postflop. Calling 200 into a 650 pot requires ~23.5% equity; multiway against an UTG opener + caller my equity is typically below that and playability is poor. A raise here would be a marginal squeeze with little fold equity and a high chance of a reraise. I fold to conserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Holding T3o in the BB facing a raise to 250 with a caller. Getting 650:150 (~4.3:1) pot odds but T3o is very weak with poor playability multiway. Hard to realize equity out of position against two opponents. Fold is the disciplined play.

I have top pair with a mediocre kicker (8) on a very coordinated board (Ac-Td-Kc). This board texture is likely to have connected well with the Button's pre-flop calling range, which often includes high cards, suited connectors, and broadway hands like QJ, KQ, or AJ. Betting here risks bloating the pot when I am likely either marginally ahead or significantly behind. By checking, I exercise pot control, allow the opponent to bluff with weaker holdings, and avoid value-owning myself against better Aces or made straights.

The flop comes Ac Td Kc, giving me middle pair (Kings) and a gutshot straight draw (I need a Queen). This is a very broadway-heavy board that hits the UTG opener's range quite hard (sets of AA/KK/TT, AK, AQ, AJ, QJ). My opponent checked to me. Betting here is risky because worse hands (like QQ or JJ) will likely fold, and better hands (any Ax, two pair, sets, straights) will call or raise. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity with the gutshot, and can re-evaluate on the turn. I have enough showdown value to try to get to showdown cheaply rather than bloating the pot.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (8) on a highly coordinated board (Ac-Td-Kc-9s). The flop check-check suggests the opponent likely has some showdown value or a draw but didn't want to bloat the pot. The 9s on the turn completes some straights (QJ already there, but now some J8/Q8 possibilities, though unlikely). Betting here doesn't achieve much; I won't get called by many worse hands, and I risk being raised by better Aces or made straights. I will check again to exercise pot control and see the river for free.

I hold [Kd Jh] on a board of [Ac Td Kc 9s]. I have second pair (Kings) and a double gutshot straight draw (any Queen makes a straight). The UTG opener checked the flop and now checks the turn. The board is extremely coordinated and connects well with an UTG opening range (lots of Ax, two pairs, sets, and QJ straights). Betting here is risky because better hands (Ax, two pair) will call, and worse hands (underpairs) will likely fold. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, trying to hit a Queen on the river or get to showdown cheaply with my pair of Kings. Pot control is paramount with a marginal made hand on this wet texture.

The board is quite coordinated with potential straights (any Jack) and now a possible club flush after the 5c river. My top pair with an 8 kicker has decent showdown value, but betting is unlikely to get called by many worse hands; most hands that call will have me beaten (better Aces, straights, or flushes). Pro's passivity on the flop and turn suggests a hand with some showdown value or a missed draw. By checking, I can reach a cheap showdown and potentially pick up a win against lower pairs or missed draws that might check behind, while also avoiding a difficult decision if Pro raises a lead-out bet.

Action is on the River with the board reading [Ac Td Kc 9s 5c]. I hold [Kd Jh] for second pair (Kings). My opponent, Flash, raised pre-flop but has checked the Flop, Turn, and now River. The board is quite wet with broadway cards and a potential flush draw completing on the river. Flash's line of triple-checking suggests either a weak made hand (like an underpair QQ/JJ, a weak Ten) or a pot-controlled weak Ace that beats me. If I bet, better hands (Ax) will call, and worse hands (missed draws, underpairs) will likely fold, making a value bet very thin and risky. I have sufficient showdown value to check back and hope to win against underpairs or missed broadways. There is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff.

I am in UTG position (4-handed) with 8d Tc. This is a weak off-suit one-gapper. Even in a short-handed game, this hand is below the threshold for an opening range from the first position. It is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. With a healthy stack of 78 BBs, there is no need to get involved with marginal junk hands. The standard play is to fold.

QJo on the button is a clear open-raise candidate versus the blinds — good playability and position. I want to steal the blinds and isolate the short-stacked small blind while pricing out some of BB's speculative holdings. A standard 3x open (300) is appropriate: big enough to pick up the pot uncontested a fair amount, but not so large that I’m overcommitted if Claude (SB, 6600) shoves or Flash (BB) 3-bets. If called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on connected/draw-heavy boards. Versus a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (fold to a polarized shove from Flash; comfortable calling a short-stack shove from Claude depending on sizing and read).
9-3 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a button raise from Minni, I'm out of position with poor card strength and no real playability. This is a clear fold.

I am holding 5h 2s in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), 52o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given my significant chip lead and the remaining hands in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to defend with such a marginal holding.

3♣6♣ UTG is too weak to open. Out of position to the button and blinds, this hand is frequently dominated and performs poorly multiway. Flash and Claude have shown aggression; limp/calling invites pressure and a likely 3‑bet or multiway pot where our equity is low. With a healthy stack, preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.
Button with K4o and only blinds left to act. This is a standard steal spot - K-high has decent equity and I have position. Open raising to 2.5x to try to take down the blinds.

I am holding Jc Tc in the Small Blind. Claude has raised from the button, which is typically a wide range. As the chip leader, I want to maintain aggression and isolate the button. 3-betting to 850 puts pressure on both the Big Blind and the original raiser. If called, JTs has excellent playability and equity against most of the button's opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with Js 5h, a very weak off-suit hand. The action involves a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 850. Facing a 3-bet with trash cards is an automatic fold. There is no strategic merit in calling or raising here given the strength shown by the Small Blind and my poor holdings.

Flash 3-bet me to 850 from the SB. K4o is a marginal steal hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range. I'm getting 2:1 odds (600 to win 1200) but K4o lacks the playability and equity to continue profitably. I'll often be dominated by better kings, and my kicker is terrible. Disciplined fold here.
JTs is a solid hand in UTG, especially with only 4 players. It has good playability with straight and flush potential. Standard open raise to 2.5x.

I am holding Queen of Diamonds and Five of Hearts (Q5o) on the button. Claude has opened for 2.5x from UTG. Q5o is a very weak hand and does not play well against an UTG opening range. Despite having the largest stack at the table, there is no strategic value in calling or raising here, as it would likely lead to losing chips unnecessarily. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with A4o facing a raise to 250 from Claude (UTG/CO in 4-handed). While 4-handed play widens ranges, A4o is easily dominated by an opening range (AT+, AJ+, AQ+, AK, pairs). Playing out of position with a weak off-suit Ace is generally negative EV. I do not have a good price to call given the reverse implied odds, and a 3-bet bluff is risky against a 60BB stack that has shown willingness to 4-bet or call in previous hands, although he has folded to 3-bets recently. The most prudent play to preserve my 76BB stack is to fold.

Claude opened UTG to 250. I have 4♠8♣ in the big blind — a very weak hand, but facing a single raiser heads‑up calling 150 into a 400 pot gives me ~27% break‑even equity. Claude opens a lot from early position, so his range is wide enough to justify defending occasionally. I will not 3‑bet with 48o (poor blockers, dominated by most of his value range). I'll call the 150 (total 250) to see a flop and proceed very cautiously: fold to strong continuation bets on dry boards, only continue with real equity (pair/two pair/good draws) or if I can credibly exploit later streets.

Check. I hold 4♠8♣ with no pair and only a backdoor spade draw on K♦5♠Q♠ — essentially negligible equity. Claude opened UTG so his range contains a lot of Kx/Qx and spade combinations that connect this flop; against that range I have almost nothing to continue with. No reason to lead — pot control and information are best here. I’ll check and fold to a standard continuation bet; only consider calling a very small probe (to see a turn) or continuing if I pick up a pair or a clear draw. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot given my deep stack vs his.

I have JTs on Kd5sQs board giving me an open-ended straight draw (any 9 or A makes the nuts/near-nuts). I also have backdoor spade potential. This is a good board to c-bet - I can represent hitting K or Q, and I have solid equity if called. Small c-bet around 40% pot.

I defended 4♠8♣ preflop intending to check the flop and fold to a standard c‑bet. On K♦ 5♠ Q♠ I have no pair and only a backdoor spade (would need both turn+river spades — extremely unlikely). Claude opened UTG (range weighted toward Kx/Qx and spade combos that dominate me) and now bets 225 into a 775 pot. Calling 225 would mean risking 225 to win a pot of about 1,225, so I need roughly 18.4% equity to justify a call; my realistic equity here is far below that. I have poor blockers and negligible fold equity to make a profitable bluff‑raise, and Claude is short‑stacked (more likely to shove/continue). Fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I am in the UTG position with 3d 5c, which is a very weak starting hand. Given that there are still three players to act behind me and my current stack size is healthy, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I am on the Button with Pocket Nines (9h 9c), which is a strong starting hand, especially 4-handed. The UTG player folded. I have a healthy stack of roughly 76 big blinds. Standard strategy dictates opening the pot to steal the blinds or play for value in position. Limping is passive and invites multi-way pots or squeezes. I will raise to 2.5BB (250 chips), consistent with standard opening sizes in this tournament, to isolate the blinds and define the action.

Pro opened to 250 from the button. I’m in the small blind with Qs7h — a weak, offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position and is frequently dominated by a button opener. Calling 200 more (250 total) gives marginal pot odds but leads to tough post‑flop decisions (and the big blind still acts behind me); 3‑betting as a bluff is thin here. With deep stacks and no reason to force the issue, I preserve chips and fold.

KJs is a premium hand in the BB facing a button open. With suited broadway cards, I should 3-bet for value and to take initiative. Pro opened to 250, so a 3-bet to around 750-800 is appropriate. This prices out speculative hands while building a pot with a strong holding.

I hold Pocket Nines (99) on the Button, a strong hand in a 4-handed game. After opening to 250, the Big Blind (Claude) 3-bet to 750. Given the effective stack size of roughly 65 Big Blinds and my positional advantage, folding is too conservative. 4-betting might isolate me against a range that dominates me (TT+, JJ+, QQ+, KK, AA, AK). Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wider, including bluffs and lower pairs, and lets me use my position to navigate post-flop. The price to call (500 chips) offers good pot odds considering the hand's strength and playability.

I have KsJs on 3c 9s Td. As the preflop 3-bettor, I should c-bet this board. I have two overcards, a gutshot to the nut straight (need Q), and a backdoor spade flush draw. The flop connects with some of Pro's calling range (Tx, 99), but I have enough equity and should continue aggression. A ~1/3 pot c-bet around 500 is efficient - gets folds from air while keeping the pot manageable if called.

I hold Pocket Nines on a [3c 9s Td] board, giving me a middle set. This is a monster hand in a 3-bet pot. The only hand beating me is Pocket Tens. My opponent (Claude) led out for 500 (about 1/3 pot). His range includes overpairs (JJ-AA), Top Pair (AT), straight draws (QJ, KJ), and missed high cards (AK, AQ). Raising here would get value from made hands but likely fold out his bluffs. Since I am in position and the board is rainbow (no flush draws), I can afford to slow play to keep his bluffs in and allow him to catch up or barrel the turn. The Stack-to-Pot Ratio will be less than 2 on the turn, making it easy to get stacks in later. Therefore, a flat call is the most profitable line to extract maximum value.

Turn paired the Ten, which is concerning. Pro called my preflop 3-bet and flop c-bet - their range includes Tx hands, 99, and pocket pairs. If they have a ten, they now have trips. I only have K-high with a gutshot (need Q). Continuing to barrel here accomplishes nothing - I can't get better hands to fold and I'm not getting value. Check and potentially realize my equity on the river.

I hold a Full House (Nines full of Tens) on a board of [3c 9s Td Tc]. This is effectively the nuts, losing only to Quad Tens (unlikely) or T9 (unlikely given preflop). My opponent, the preflop 3-bettor, bet the flop but checked the turn when the Ten paired. This check likely indicates pot control with an overpair (AA-JJ) or a trap with Trip Tens. By checking back, I accomplish several things: 1) I disguise the strength of my hand, looking like a medium pair or a float giving up. 2) I keep his bluffs in play for the river. 3) I allow him to value bet worse hands (like Overpairs) on the river that might fold to a turn bet. 4) Since the board is paired, straight draws are drawing dead against my boat, so I don't need to protect. The SPR is around 2, so I can comfortably get stacks in on the river if he has a strong hand, but checking ensures I extract maximum value from his entire range.

River 2d is a blank. I have K-high with KsJs. Pro checked back the turn which suggests some weakness, but I still can't value bet K-high - only worse hands fold and better hands call. No bluffing opportunity either since Pro's check-call line likely includes pocket pairs and possibly trips. Check and showdown.

I hold a Full House (Nines full of Tens) on a board of [3c 9s Td Tc 2d]. The turn and river actions (check-check) have disguised the strength of my hand. My opponent (Claude) checked the river, likely holding an overpair (JJ-AA) or missed high cards (AK/AQ) given his pre-flop 3-bet and flop continuation bet followed by slowing down. Hands like AK/AQ will likely fold to any bet, so I am targeting his overpairs for value. A bet of roughly half the pot is large enough to extract significant value but small enough to induce a call from hands like KK or AA which might be skeptical of the Ten pairing the board. I will bet 1250 chips into the 2550 pot.

Pro bet 1250 into 2500 on the river after checking turn. Their line (call 3-bet, call flop, check turn, bet river) suggests a hand with showdown value - likely trips with Tx, pocket pairs like JJ/QQ, or possibly 99. I only have K-high with a missed gutshot. I need to be right ~25% of the time to call, but Pro's delayed betting line doesn't indicate enough bluffs. Folding K-high here.

Playing 4-handed, UTG is effectively the Cutoff position. Ac9c is a strong suited ace that falls well within the standard opening range for this position. Raising allows me to take initiative, potentially steal the blinds, or play a pot in position against the blinds. I will open to 2.5BB (250), which is a standard sizing consistent with my previous play.

Facing an UTG open to 250 with 8♣6♠ (offsuit) on the button, this is a clear fold. Pot is 400 so calling 250 requires ≈38.5% equity to be +EV heads‑up — 8‑6 offsuit is unlikely to have that vs an UTG open, and it plays poorly multiway. With two players (SB/BB) yet to act there’s a real risk of a 3‑bet or isolation by the blinds, and a thin postflop equity/position profile. I’ll preserve my healthy stack and wait for a better spot on the button (suited connectors, pairs, or broadways).
A6o in the SB facing a UTG raise. Marginal hand but getting 2:1 odds to call. Ace gives some playability and I can see a flop cheaply. Not strong enough to 3-bet, but worth a call to see if I hit.

I am holding pocket Jacks, a premium hand in a 4-handed game. The UTG player (Pro) opened for 2.5x and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I am in the Big Blind with a significant chip lead. I want to 3-bet here for value and to thin the field, as Jacks play much better heads-up than in a multi-way pot. A raise to 1000 (a 4x squeeze) is a standard sizing to put pressure on the original raiser and the short-stacked caller, potentially isolating one of them or taking down the pot immediately.

I opened with Ac9c from UTG (Cutoff in 4-handed) to 250. The Small Blind called, and the Big Blind (Flash, chip leader) squeezed to 1000. While I have position on the 3-better, A9s is a marginal hand to defend against a squeeze, especially one sized at 4x the open. My hand is often dominated by the 3-bettor's value range (AK, AQ, AJ, AT), leading to reverse implied odds if an Ace flops. Additionally, the Small Blind is still left to act behind me and could potentially shove with a stack of ~50BB. Conserving my healthy stack of ~87BB for better spots is the disciplined play here.

Flash 3-bet to 1000 from the BB after Pro opened and I flatted. A6o is a weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet, especially out of position. My ace could easily be dominated by AK, AQ, AJ, AT in Flash's 3-betting range. Getting 2:1 odds but OOP with a weak kicker makes this an easy fold.

9♦3♦ UTG is a clear fold. This is a very weak, low-suited rag with poor playability out of position against three opponents (Button, SB, BB). Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives poor pot odds versus the range I’m facing and I’m likely dominated or crushed by 3-bets/shoves from stronger players (Flash/Pro/Claude). Preserve my 19,550 stack and wait for a better spot in position or with real equity.

On the button with Q2 suited hearts. Minni folded UTG. This is a standard button steal spot - I have position and suited cards give me some playability if called. Q2s is marginal but playable from the button. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. Claude, who is on the Button and playing a relatively short stack of 4,900, has raised to 250. Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, and there is still the Big Blind (Pro) to act behind me. Given my significant chip lead and the weakness of the hand, there is no reason to defend or contest this pot. I will fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 3c, facing a raise to 250 from Claude on the Button. The Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 to win a total pot of 550, offering me pot odds of roughly 27%. However, 93o is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em with very poor playability and equity, even against a wide Button opening range. Being out of position with such a weak hand makes it difficult to realize any equity or win the pot post-flop. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve chips.

Q7s from UTG with 4 players. Marginal hand but suited with some playability. With 53BB stack, I can make a standard open raise. It's borderline but in a 4-handed game positions are tighter, and Q-high suited has some value. I'll raise to 2.5x.

I am on the button with QJs, a very strong drawing and high-card hand in a 4-handed game. Claude (UTG) has raised to 250 with a relatively short stack of about 50BB. While calling is an option to keep the pot smaller and play in position, a 3-bet allows me to take control of the hand, potentially isolate Claude, and discourage the blinds from entering. Given my significant chip lead, I can afford to apply pressure here.

I hold [5c Ts] in the Small Blind. The action started with a raise from UTG (Claude) to 250, followed by a 3-bet from the Button (Flash) to 850. My hand is extremely weak (disconnected, offsuit, low ranks) and performs poorly against the strong ranges implied by an open and a 3-bet. I am also out of position against both opponents. With a stack of 85.5 BBs, there is absolutely no incentive to get involved in this pot. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips.

Preflop I’m facing a squeeze: UTG Claude opened to 250 and Button Flash 3‑bet to 850. I’m in the big blind with 2♦3♥ offsuit. Pot = 1250, to call = 750 (pot odds ≈ 37.5%). This hand has very poor equity and almost no post‑flop playability out of position versus a 3‑betting button and an opener in front. Raising to isolate would be reckless with 2‑3o. Calling is marginal and will put me in tough spots on most flops. Prefer to conserve chips for a better spot — fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash with Q7s. This is a weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet, especially out of position. Flash has a big stack and has shown aggression. Q7s doesn't have enough equity to call profitably here - easy fold.

I am UTG at a 4-handed table with Deuce-Four offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible hands in poker. Even as the chip leader, there is no strategic reason to open this hand from early position. Standard play is to fold and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with 9d 2d, a very weak suited hand. While the Button is an advantageous position to steal blinds, the hand has poor playability and low equity if called. The Small Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has been active, increasing the risk of a 3-bet or call. With 85 BBs, I have plenty of time to wait for better spots to accumulate chips rather than forcing a steal with junk. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

I have 6♣6♦ in the small blind with UTG and the button folded — heads-up vs Claude in the big blind. This is a standard spot to raise and take the initiative rather than limp/call out of position. A 3x BB open to 300 (total committed) achieves three things: (1) isolates the BB and narrows his calling range, (2) avoids giving him good odds to see a flop for free (important for a small pocket pair), and (3) builds a pot when I have a made hand while preserving post‑flop maneuverability with my 19400 stack.
