Plan: c-bet favorable flops; if Claude 3-bets a small-to-medium size I can call to set‑mine or play postflop, but I’ll respect a large 3-bet/shove and reassess based on sizing and his tendencies.

Holding 9c6h in the BB facing a SB raise to 300. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400) but 96o doesn't play well enough out of position against an active opponent. Easy fold.

I am in UTG (Cutoff in 4-handed play) with a stack of 85.5 BBs. My hand is 6c 5s, an unsuited connector. While 4-handed play encourages wider ranges, 65o is still too weak to open profitably from the first position, especially with active and deep-stacked opponents behind me who can easily 3-bet or flat call with superior ranges. There is no strategic merit in attempting a steal here given my healthy stack size and the poor playability of the hand. The standard and correct play is to fold.

8♣4♥ is one of the worst hands — very little showdown or postflop playability and frequent dominated scenarios. Facing a 100 call into 150 on the button, calling is a marginal speculative play and a min-raise to 200 is a thin steal that will often get Flash (very active) to defend or Claude (≈49bb) to call/shove, leaving me in tough spots. With deep stacks and many better spots to invest, preserve chips and fold preflop.

Heads up against the BB with Kc4c. Suited king is a decent hand for a steal attempt. Completing would be weak and puts me in a tough spot postflop out of position. Raising to 250 (2.5x) applies pressure and can win the blinds outright or build a pot with position on a decent hand.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, Queen-Ten offsuit is significantly stronger than the average range Claude would raise from the Small Blind. I am getting excellent pot odds (150 to call into a 350 pot) and I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on Claude's 47 BB stack, calling allows me to play a controlled pot and evaluate the flop, keeping his range wider and using my position to outplay him post-flop.

Flopped middle pair with K4c on AKT board. This is a marginal hand - I have second pair with a bad kicker. The board is very connected and hits a lot of Flash's calling range (broadways, aces, kings). Betting for value is risky as I'll mostly get called by better. Checking to control pot size and potentially get to showdown cheaply is the right play here.

I have middle pair (Tens) with an open-ended straight draw on a very coordinated board. Claude checked from the Small Blind, which could indicate weakness or a trap with a marginal hand. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I see a turn card for free, which allows me to realize my equity and potentially hit my straight while keeping my pair's showdown value intact against his air or smaller pairs.

Turn brings 6h, completing no draws. I have second pair with a weak kicker on AKT6 board. Flash checked behind on flop which could indicate weakness or a trapping hand. With K4, I'm often behind if called. Checking again to control pot size and get to showdown cheaply is the prudent play. Betting would only fold out hands I beat and get called by better.

On the turn 6h, the board remains very coordinated (A-K-T-6). I have middle pair (Tens) and an open-ended straight draw. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked for the second time. While this often signals weakness, this specific board texture (A-K-T) hits a pre-flop raiser's range very hard, so his double-check could occasionally be a trap or a marginal hand like a weak King. By checking back, I ensure I see the river for free, allowing me to realize the equity of my straight draw and preserve the showdown value of my Tens without risking a check-raise from a short-stacked opponent.

River Kh gives me trip Kings! This is a strong hand now. Flash checked both flop and turn, indicating likely weakness. I should value bet to extract from worse hands like Ax, Tx, or weaker Kx. A small bet of around 1/3 pot (175) should get called by marginal hands that would fold to larger sizing.

Claude raised from the small blind and checked both the flop and turn on a board (A-K-T-6) that heavily favors a pre-flop raiser's range. This passive line makes it statistically less likely he holds a strong Ace or a King, which would typically bet for value or protection. On the river, the King pairs. Claude's small bet of 175 into a 500 pot (offering me 4:1 odds) looks like a thin value bet with a small pocket pair or a 'blocking bet' with a marginal hand. My hand (Tens and Kings with an Ace kicker) beats all his bluffs and any lower pocket pairs he might have played this way. Given the price and his inconsistent line, calling is the most profitable decision.

Fold. 7♣5♠ is a weak, unsuited, disconnected holding and I'm first to act out of position in a 4-handed game. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives poor immediate odds and will likely lead to multiway action OOP where this hand has low equity. Raising to try and steal is unnecessary with three competent opponents behind (one very deep, one mid-deep, one decent stack) who can 3-bet or defend aggressively. With a healthy 19,550 stack (~195 BB) it's better to preserve chips and wait for a clearer, positionally advantaged spot.
Q8o on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a standard button open - the hand is marginal but position is excellent. I'll raise to 2.5x to steal the blinds or play in position postflop.

I am holding King-Seven suited (Kh 7h) in the Small Blind. The button (Claude) has raised to 250. Claude has a relatively short stack of approximately 51 big blinds. In previous hands (#106 and #111), Claude raised to 250 and folded to my 3-bets of 850. Given this pattern and the strength of my suited King, I will put in a 3-bet to 850 to apply pressure and attempt to win the pot pre-flop or isolate him while having a hand that plays well if called.

The player 'Flash' in the Small Blind has put in a significant 3-bet to 850 chips (8.5 big blinds) over a Button raise. While QJs is a reasonable hand, calling a cold 3-bet from the Big Blind is generally a losing play due to being out of position post-flop against the Button (if they call) or generally acting early, and the risk that the Button (Claude) may come over the top with a 4-bet. My hand is often dominated by the value portion of a 3-bet range (AQ, KQ, JJ+). The pot odds (requiring ~38% equity) are not attractive enough to justify the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage. Folding is the most prudent long-term decision.

Flash 3-bet me to 850 from SB. Q8o is a weak hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet range. I'd be calling 600 into 1200 pot (2:1), needing ~33% equity. Q8o doesn't have sufficient equity against a 3-bet range and plays poorly postflop OOP. Easy fold.
2d7d is a very weak hand, even suited. From UTG position with 3 opponents, this is a clear fold. No reason to open with one of the weakest starting hands in poker.

I am on the button with Kd 9c in a 4-handed game. After the UTG player (Claude) folds, it is standard to open a wide range of hands from the button to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. K9o is well within the opening range here. I will raise to 250, which is 2.5 times the big blind, to put pressure on the blinds while maintaining a manageable pot size if called.

I hold 6h5h in the Small Blind facing a standard 2.5x raise from the Button (Flash), who is the chip leader and has been active. While suited connectors have post-flop potential, playing them out of position is difficult, and flat-calling in the Small Blind is generally a strategic leak that invites a squeeze from the Big Blind. 3-betting is a viable aggressive option, but given the deep stacks (84 BB) and Flash's aggression, I prefer to avoid bloating the pot with a speculative hand OOP. Folding preserves my stack and minimizes variance.

Flash opened to 250 from the button; pot is 400 and I need 150 to call. I have A♠J♦ — a strong heads‑up hand. With deep stacks (~195bb) and the button likely opening a wide range, I prefer to take the initiative and 3‑bet for value and fold equity rather than play a bloated pot OOP. I’ll raise to 900 (total) — big enough to isolate and price out speculative hands but leaves room to maneuver postflop. If Flash 4‑bets large I can fold AJo; if he calls I can continue with A‑high boards and use positional cues postflop.

I opened K9o from the button, which is a standard wide steal attempt in a 4-handed game. However, Minni in the big blind has responded with a 3-bet to 900. While I have position, K9o is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a typical 3-betting range (which usually includes better Kings, Aces, and pocket pairs). Given the current stack sizes and the 3.6x 3-bet sizing, calling here to see a flop with a hand that has poor playability is likely a negative EV play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am the chip leader with over 26,000 chips. Holding Ace-Trey suited (As 3s) in a four-handed game is a strong opening hand from any position. Opening for a standard 2.5x raise allows me to put pressure on the smaller stacks while maintaining a hand that has good post-flop playability and nut-flush potential.

I hold 7s 4c on the Button, which is an extremely weak hand (7-high, unsuited, disconnected). Flash has opened to 250 from UTG, indicating a potentially strong range. Despite having position, there is no justification for entering the pot with this hand against an early position raise. The correct play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

UTG (Flash) opened to 250 and I’m in the small blind with A♦6♦. This is a suited ace with decent postflop playability but a weak kicker against an early-position opener — and I’ll be out of position versus Flash. The pot is 400 and it’s 200 to call (≈33% immediate equity required), so rather than inflating the pot OOP with a marginal hand I’ll call and see a flop. A min‑raise to 400 risks a 3‑bet or isolating stronger A‑x hands and is suboptimal here; if Claude or Flash puts significant 3‑bet pressure I’ll fold. Plan: call 250 total, play the flop cautiously, look to realize equity with backdoor flush/straight possibilities, and only continue aggressively on clearly favorable textures or when I pick up the nut or strong draw.

I have 9h7c in the BB. Flash raised from UTG to 250, Minni called. I need 150 to call into a 600 pot, getting 4:1 odds. While 97o is marginal, the pot odds are good and I'm closing the action. Worth seeing a flop at this price.

I have top pair (6) with the Ace kicker but I’m out of position in a three-way pot against an UTG opener and the BB. The flop (6♥-5♠-2♥) is very dry and there are no diamonds on board, so I don’t have a draw to justify building the pot OOP. Pot control is the priority — checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me reevaluate on the turn. If an opponent bets a small/medium amount I’ll call for value and re-evaluate; I’ll be wary of heavy raises or a shove from the short-stacked BB but won’t bloat the pot voluntarily.

Flopped a gutshot straight draw (need an 8 for 9-8-7-6-5) with 9h7c on 6h5s2h. Also have backdoor hearts. It's checked to me and I can see a free card. With only 4 outs for my gutshot, no reason to bet into two players - I'll take the free card and see what develops on the turn.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw on a low, connected board (6-5-2) that likely connects with the defending ranges of Minni and Claude. Since both opponents checked to me, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and see a turn card. Betting into two players on this board texture is risky, as I'm likely to be called by any pair or draw, and Claude's shorter stack makes him prone to check-jamming.

I have A♦6♦ on a 6♥-5♠-2♥-2♦ board — that’s two pair (6s and 2s) with the Ace kicker. I checked the dry flop for pot control; the turn paired the board which increases the chance someone holds a deuce (trips) or a set, so my holding is strong but vulnerable. In a three-way pot I want thin value from one-pair hands and A-highs while not bloating the pot out of position. A small-to-medium value bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) accomplishes that: it charges worse hands, denies a free river card, and keeps weaker callers in. I size to 300 into a 750 pot to get called by single-pair hands and some Ax hands but avoid committing too much if I face big resistance — if I face a large check-raise or shove I’ll heavily discount my equity and likely fold to avoid losing a big chunk to trips/sets.

Turn brought the 2d pairing the board. I have a gutshot straight draw needing an 8 (for 9-8-7-6-5). Minni bets 300 into ~750. Pot odds are 300 to win 1050 = 3.5:1 (~22% needed). My gutshot is only ~8.5% to hit. The math doesn't support calling - I need much better odds for a 4-outer. Additionally, the paired board means even if I hit my straight, I could be beaten by a full house. Easy fold.

On the turn, the deuce of diamonds pairs the bottom card of the flop. Minni leads out with a bet of 300 into a pot of 750 after the flop went check-around. This small lead often signifies a 'probe' bet with a marginal made hand (like a pair of fives or sixes) or a draw. With Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing any four), I am getting excellent pot odds (3.5:1) to see a river card. My Ace also provides some showdown value if the river goes check-check, or it could be the best hand if Minni is semi-bluffing with a heart draw or a straight draw like 87. I will call the 300 to realize my equity.

I have two pair (6s and 2s) with the Ace kicker, but the board is paired and my hand is very vulnerable to any 2 (trips), a 6+2 combination or pocket 6s (full house). Flash’s line — UTG open, passive check on the flop, then check‑call of my small turn bet — is consistent with a range that contains Ax, single 6x, small pairs and the occasional slowplayed 2. The river 4♠ is largely a blank: it doesn’t create many new worse hands that would reliably call a value bet, but it does leave me open to being crushed by trips/full houses if I get check‑raised. Betting thin here would risk a raise I don’t want to face OOP for a marginal-made hand; checking keeps the pot under control, allows me to induce bluffs, and lets me see a showdown cheaply if he checks back. Therefore I check.

On the river, the board is 6h-5s-2h-2d-4s and I hold As-3s, which gives me a 6-high straight (2-3-4-5-6). The action went check-around on the flop, and Minni led the turn for 300 into a pot of 750, which I called with my gutshot and Ace-high. The river check from Minni suggests she has a hand with showdown value that she is now looking to check-call or check-fold, such as trip deuces or a pair of sixes, or that she was semi-bluffing with a missed heart draw. Given that I have a very strong hand, I must bet for value. A bet of 800 is a standard value size that targets trips or top pairs while allowing her to potentially hero-call with a missed draw or a single pair.

I have two pair (6s and 2s) with the Ace kicker — a strong but vulnerable hand on a paired board. Flash’s line (UTG open, passive flop, check‑call a small 300 turn) looks like a lot of Ax and medium pocket pairs that want a cheap showdown; his 800 river bet is small enough to be a mix of thin value and bluffs. Pot is 2150 and it’s 800 to call — calling costs ~27% of the final pot (800 / (2150+800) ≈ 27%), so I only need ~27% equity to break even. While any 2 or a 6 (or pocket pairs turned into full houses/quads) crushes me, those exact combos are a minority of his range — many reasonable holdings (A‑x, worse pairs) will call or even raise a small turn bet and will be called down here. Raising is high variance and likely only gets called by better full houses or folds out bluffs I want to extract value from; folding surrenders too much VC. So I call 800 to get value from worse hands and beat bluffs, but I won’t bloat the pot further.

I am holding 5d 2h in UTG, which is one of the worst starting hands in poker (unsuited, unconnected, low cards). With 84 big blinds, there is absolutely no incentive to play this hand from early position. My strategy is to fold immediately and wait for a better spot.

On the button with pocket Tens (Ts Tc), no raise yet and the pot is just the blinds (150). This is a standard spot to open for value and to take the initiative — TT plays very well in position against the SB/BB. I’ll use a 3x sizing (300 total) to get fold equity, narrow the field, and build a pot when I’m likely ahead. If I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (call medium 3-bets, 4-bet or fold to very large/committing 3-bets) and proceed post-flop in position.
Q2o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Out of position with poor playability - folding is the right play here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Button (Minni) has opened with a 3x raise. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), K3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position and is easily dominated by the Button's opening range. With a large stack and many hands remaining, I prefer to fold and wait for better spots rather than defending with marginal offsuit Kings.

AcKd is a premium hand — open for value and fold equity. At 4-handed UTG we want a size that discourages limp/calls and gives us good postflop playability, so I'll open to a standard 3x BB (300). That size is consistent with table norms (250–300) and is large enough to reduce multiway pots but small relative to my deep stack. Plan: if I get a single call, continue on favorable flops with a c-bet; if I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate by opponent and sizing (against the short-stacked button I’ll lean toward a 4-bet/shove for fold equity/value; vs Flash’s big stack I may flat-call to realize equity and play postflop).

78 offsuit on the button facing UTG raise. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against a UTG raising range. Without being suited, I lose flush potential and the hand doesn't have enough playability to justify calling. With ~48BB stack, I should wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with K♣ 6♦. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG in a 4-handed game. While King-high has some value, K6o is quite weak for calling a raise from the Small Blind, especially against a UTG opener. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a dominated range (hands like AK, KQ, KJ, or even K7+ are in her raising range). With a healthy stack and 32 hands remaining, there is no need to play a marginal hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with T3o facing a 3x raise from Minni (UTG). The pot odds are approximately 30.7% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). However, T3o is one of the weakest hands in the deck with very poor playability and reverse implied odds, especially playing out of position against an aggressive chip leader. Even against a wide opening range, this hand does not have sufficient equity or realizability to justify a call. Folding is the only prudent option to preserve chips for better spots.
K5o is too weak to open from UTG position. Even with only 4 players, this hand has poor playability and will often be dominated by better kings. Standard fold.

I am currently the chip leader with 28,075 chips. I am on the button with 2s6s, a very weak hand. While opening from the button is a standard aggressive play, 2s6s is too weak to justify a raise against Pro and Minni, both of whom have shown they are willing to defend their blinds and play back. With only 31 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to risk chips on a high-variance, low-equity steal attempt. Folding is the most disciplined and safe option here to maintain my lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 3s, which is a very weak offsuit hand. The action has folded to me. The Big Blind (Minni) has a significant chip advantage (18,700 vs my 8,250) and has shown aggression. Even though it only costs 50 chips to call, playing 63o out of position against a large stack is a losing proposition in the long run. I have a healthy stack of 82.5 BBs and do not need to take unnecessary risks with trash hands. Folding is the correct strategic decision here.

In a 4-handed game, Queen-Jack offsuit is a strong starting hand that should be opened for a raise from any position. As the chip leader with 280 big blinds, I want to maintain aggression and apply pressure to the shorter stacks, particularly Claude who is under 50 big blinds. A standard raise to 250 (2.5x) is consistent with my previous play and allows me to see where I stand while building a pot with a solid semi-premium hand.

I am on the Button with King-Three offsuit (Kd 3h). The UTG player (Flash), who is the chip leader and playing aggressively, has opened to 250. Despite having position, K3o is a very weak hand that is often dominated by a UTG opening range (e.g., K9+, A-highs, pairs). Calling is unprofitable due to poor post-flop playability and reverse implied odds. 3-betting is too loose given the hand strength. Folding is the standard and disciplined play to preserve my 82.5 BB stack for better opportunities.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from Flash, I’m in the small blind with K♣4♣ (Kh4c) — a weak, easily dominated hand out of position. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call; calling OOP against an UTG range (and with the BB still to act) will often put me in a multiway pot with poor equity and marginal playability. A 3-bet bluff here is thin (players behind can defend or isolate) and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a dominated hand. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

As7o in the BB facing a 2.5x UTG raise. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). A7o is a reasonable defend - we have an ace and can flop top pair. Against UTG range it's marginal but pot odds make calling profitable. 3-betting is too thin, folding too tight.

Flopped nothing - just ace high on a paired queen board (Qc9sQh). Flash opened UTG so has a strong range. No reason to bluff here - checking to see what happens is clearly the best play. If Flash bets, I'll fold my ace high.

On this flop of Qc-9s-Qh, I have flopped trips with a Jack kicker. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Claude checked to me, a continuation bet is standard. I want to extract value from any 9, pocket pairs, or straight draws like JT. A bet of around 40% of the pot (225) is a good sizing to keep his range wide and entice a call or a check-raise from a curious opponent.

Flash opened UTG (strong range) and c-bet small on Qc9sQh. I have ace high with no draw. Against UTG range on a paired queen board, my A7 has almost no equity - Flash likely has Qx, pairs, or better overcards frequently. No reason to continue with ace high here.

I am in the UTG position with 73 suited, which is a very weak hand. Even in a 4-handed game, this hand is below the threshold for a profitable opening raise, especially with the chip leaders acting behind me. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

On the button with 4♠5♣ — a weak, but positionally playable hand. There's no raiser in front of me, so calling 100 keeps the pot small and lets me play postflop in position against the blinds. A min-raise (to 200) could attempt a steal, but this hand is too marginal to unilaterally inflate the pot and risk a 3-bet from Flash or an isolation by Claude. I’ll call to realize equity and use positional advantage: proceed with cautious c-bets only with clear equity (pair/draw); fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

J8o in the small blind facing a button limp. This is a marginal hand but getting 5:1 to complete (50 into 250 pot). Completing is standard here to see a cheap flop rather than folding a reasonable hand or bloating the pot with a raise when I'm out of position with a mediocre holding.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The action has been limped by Minni on the Button and Claude in the Small Blind. Given the relatively weak strength of my hand and my position as the chip leader, there is no need to bloat the pot pre-flop. Checking allows me to see a free flop and realize my equity without additional risk.
Completely missed this flop with J8o on Ah Qd 3c. I have jack high with no draws. Standard check here - no reason to bluff into multiple opponents with air on a board that hits many calling ranges.

I have bottom pair (threes) on an Ace-Queen-Trey board. This is a multi-way limped pot. While I have some showdown value, the board texture heavily favors the ranges of the opponents who limped, especially with an Ace and a Queen present. Betting here is unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. Checking is the most prudent play to see a turn card cheaply and evaluate the action from Minni on the button.

Check. I have 4♠5♣ on A♥ Q♦ 3♣ — no pair and only a backdoor wheel (four 2s as clean outs), ~8.7% to hit on the turn and ~17% to the river. Two opponents checked; this is a dry, high-card flop where my hand has very little showdown value and poor multi-street equity. A small c-bet could sometimes take it down, but I don’t want to build a pot out of position with almost no fold equity if called. I’ll keep the pot small and realize equity in position: check to see the turn and fold to significant aggression, call small turn bets only to try to see a cheap river or if a 2 appears I’ll proceed aggressively.

Still have complete air with J8 high on Ah Qd 3c 4d. No pair, no draws. Checked around on the flop, now turn brings another brick. With two opponents still in and no equity, I should just check again and hope to get to showdown cheaply or fold to any aggression.

I have bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker on a board of Ace-Queen-Trey-Four. The flop was checked by everyone, suggesting that nobody is currently holding a strong hand like an Ace or a Queen. Claude has checked again on the turn. By checking here, I allow the action to move to Minni on the button. If it checks through again, my pair of threes has decent showdown value in this small, limped pot. If Minni bets, I can evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the safest way to control the pot size with a marginal hand.

I turned a weak middle pair (4♠5♣ on A♥ Q♦ 3♣ 4♦). Both opponents checked flop and turn and the pot is tiny (300). With two opponents and two overcards (A/Q) on board, betting now will often get called by better hands (Ax/Qx) and will unnecessarily bloat a pot I’m not happy building with a marginal pair. Pot control is the priority — check down and see the river, call small bets cheaply, and only get aggressive if the river significantly improves my hand or an opponent shows clear weakness. I’ll fold to substantial turn aggression but otherwise keep things under control and re-evaluate on the river.

River pairs the queens on AhQd3c4dQs. I have J8 high - complete air. Pot is only 300 and both opponents have checked every street, showing extreme weakness. While I could bluff, the pot is tiny and my opponents' checking patterns suggest they either have weak made hands (small pairs) or air like me. No need to risk chips bluffing into a small pot. Just check and accept I'll likely lose at showdown.

The river pairs the Queen, giving me two pair (Queens and Threes) with a King kicker. The action has been checked all the way through on the flop and turn, and Claude has checked again on the river. This passive line from all players suggests that it is highly unlikely anyone is holding an Ace or a Queen. In a small, limped pot, my pair of Threes has decent showdown value. There is little to be gained by betting, as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands or to fold out better ones. I will check and take the hand to showdown.

Board is A♥ Q♦ 3♣ 4♦ Q♠ and I hold 4♠5♣ — my best hand is two pair (Qs and 4s), but it’s a very weak two-pair in a three-way spot. Both opponents checked through every street and the pot is tiny (300). Many hands in their checking ranges beat me (any A, any Q for trips/full house, and most pocket pairs 5+ make a better two-pair), while a bluff or thin value bet will often be called by better or tied by 4x. With two opponents and almost no fold equity, I should control the pot and take showdown. I’ll check and show down rather than bloating the pot with a marginal bet.

4-handed, UTG I should be opening much wider than full-ring. Q♦7♥ is a borderline hand in full-ring but perfectly playable in 4-max: it has decent high-card value and postflop playability. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than limp. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is my choice — it’s the table’s common sizing, gives good fold equity, and keeps the pot controllable with 190BB effective. Plan: if I get a single caller I’ll continuation-bet on dry boards and exercise pot control on dangerous, connected or overcard-heavy textures. If I face a sizable 3-bet or shove (likely from Flash or a committed Claude), Q7o is often dominated so I’ll fold; versus a small squeeze I’ll re-evaluate based on stack/actor but will mostly fold to large 3-bets. Overall raising to 300 maximizes fold equity and gives the best long-term EV from this seat and stack depth.

T7o is a weak offsuit hand facing an UTG raise from Minni. Even with button position, this hand plays poorly against a range that opened from early position. Not suited, poor connectivity, and will often be dominated. Clear fold.

I am holding Ten-Five suited in the Small Blind. Minni opened with a raise from UTG, which typically indicates a stronger range than a button or late-position open. Being out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal speculative hand like T5s is not ideal. Since I am currently the chip leader, there is no need to play high-variance pots with weak holdings. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Th Jd facing a 3x open from Minni in UTG (4-handed). The pot is 450 (300 from Minni + 50 SB + 100 BB), and I need to call 200 more. This gives me pot odds of 200/650, or approximately 31%. JTo is a reasonable defending hand in a 4-handed game, having around 40% equity against a standard opening range. Although I will be out of position, the price is favorable enough to see a flop. 3-betting is not attractive with unsuited broadways here, and folding is too tight given the odds. I will call.

I have flopped a strong hand with Middle Pair (Jacks) and a Ten-high Flush Draw on an A-J-9 board with two hearts. Despite the strength of my hand, I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni). The Ace on the board favors her range significantly (AK, AQ, AJ), so leading out (donk betting) is risky and likely folds out her bluffs while isolating me against better hands. Checking is the standard play here to induce a continuation bet or see a free turn. If she bets, I have plenty of equity to call.

Check. I opened preflop with Q♦7♥ and got one caller (Pro). The flop A♦ J♥ 9♥ heavily favors the caller’s range (lots of A‑x, Jx, broadways and heart combos) and is not a dry c-bet spot. I have no pair (only Q‑high) and only weak backdoor possibilities, so betting will mainly get value‑called or raised by hands that dominate me. I’ll exercise pot control: check to keep the pot small, see a free turn, and re-evaluate. If Pro bets a small probe I may call to see the turn with my backdoor outs; I’ll fold to a large bet or raise. If he checks back I can take a free card and consider a delayed bluff on a favorable turn.

On the flop, Minni checked back in position, which typically caps her range and suggests she does not have a strong Ace or a set, likely holding a weak Ace, a Jack, an underpair, or air. The turn 9s pairs the board, making my hand Two Pair (Jacks and Nines). Leading out (donk betting) here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as she likely folds pocket pairs or air, and calls with Aces or trips which beat me. Checking allows me to keep her bluffing range in the hand, control the size of the pot, and use my showdown value to catch bluffs or check down. I plan to call a reasonable bet.

Check. I opened preflop with Q♦7♥ and was checked to on A♦ J♥ 9♥; the turn 9♠ leaves me with Q‑high and no realistic draw (a flush is no longer possible with one card to come). Pro has been passive (check on flop and turn), which is consistent with many hands that beat Q‑high (Ax, Jx, 9x, some pairs) and will have low fold equity to a turn bluff. The pot (650) is small relative to our deep stacks, so the correct play is pot control: avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand, check and fold to any meaningful bet. If checked to on the river I’ll re‑evaluate and only attempt a delayed bluff on a very believable river card; otherwise concede.

I hold Th Jd on a board of 9h Jh Ad 9s 5s, giving me two pair (Jacks and Nines). The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. The opponent, Minni, raised pre-flop but showed no aggression post-flop, suggesting a capped range likely consisting of a weak Ace, an underpair to the Ace, or missed high cards. Betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands as they will fold, while better hands (any Ace) will call. Checking is the best option to realize my showdown value or induce a bluff from missed draws.

Check. I opened to 300 and was called by Pro; after his passive check-check line on the A♦ J♥ 9♥ / 9♠ runout I only have Q♦7♥ (Q‑high) and the river 5♠ doesn’t improve me or add a believable bluff story. Pro’s line is consistent with a high proportion of Ax, Jx or 9x (and some medium pairs) that beat me and will call a river bluff; a bet here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity and often called by better hands. Pot control and stack preservation are correct — I check to see showdown and will fold to any meaningful bet.

As9s is a solid suited ace in UTG. Good playability with flush potential and an ace. Standard open raise to 250 to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop or play in position against the blinds.

K7o is a weak hand to defend against a raise from UTG (Claude), even with the benefit of being on the button. Claude is currently the short stack at the table, and an opening raise from UTG in a 4-max game typically represents a range that dominates K7o. Calling or 3-betting here would be unnecessarily risky with a marginal hand, so I will fold to preserve my chip lead.

Opponent Claude (UTG, ~42BB) raises to 2.5x. I hold QTs in the Small Blind. While QTs is a playable hand, facing a UTG open puts me at a range disadvantage (dominated by AQ, KQ, and higher pairs). Additionally, calling from the SB leaves me out of position for the rest of the hand and invites a potential squeeze from the Big Blind (Minni, who covers me). Although Claude has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets recently (Hands 111, 114), 3-betting light here risks a significant portion of my stack against a player who, if he shoves, would cripple me and swap our tournament standings. Given the stack depths (I am 3rd, he is 4th) and the high likelihood of domination, folding is the disciplined, risk-averse play to preserve chips.

Fold. Q♦3♦ is a weak, easily dominated hand facing an UTG open to 250 (2.5x). I’m out of position with the button and small blind folded; calling 150 into a 400 pot only gives ~27% pot‑odds, but versus an early‑position raiser my equity will often be lower and post‑flop playability is poor. 3‑betting is an unnecessary bluff risk against a reasonably deep but straightforward opener (and could commit a lot if he reshoves). Preserve the 18.6k stack and wait for a better spot.

In a 4-handed game, Queen-Nine suited is a strong enough hand to open from under-the-gun (UTG), which is essentially the cutoff/hijack in this configuration. As the chip leader, I want to maintain aggression and apply pressure to the shorter stacks, particularly Claude in the big blind. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop.

I am on the Button with As7s, a suited Ace, facing a standard raise from the chip leader (Flash) in the UTG/Cutoff position. In a 4-handed game, this hand has sufficient equity to play. Folding is too tight given the position and hand strength. 3-betting is an option but risks bloating the pot against a deep-stacked aggressor who could 4-bet, forcing me to fold. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop and keep the pot manageable. I will flat call to see a flop in position.

I have T♣8♣ (T8o) in the small blind facing an UTG open to 300 with the button cold‑calling. Pot is 750 and it costs me 250 more to see a multiway flop (300 total). This is a multiway pot out of position against ranges that are likely to contain many hands that dominate T8o; postflop play will be difficult and I have no good reason to 3‑bet this holding. With healthy stacks and many hands left to play, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the +EV line. Fold.

Ts8s is a suited connector in the BB. Facing a UTG raise to 300 and a call from Pro. I need 200 to call into a 750 pot, getting 3.75:1. This hand plays well multiway with flush and straight potential. Easy call with these pot odds.

I flopped top pair with Ts8s on 3c6cTc - a monotone club board. I have no clubs, so anyone with even one club has a draw and two clubs already has me crushed. Top pair no flush card on this wet board is marginal at best. Checking to see what develops is prudent - I can reassess based on action behind me. If checked through I might gain showdown value; if bet into I can evaluate the sizing.

The flop is extremely unfavorable for my hand, as it is a monotone club board (3c 6c Tc) and I hold the Queen and Nine of Hearts. I have zero equity against even a single club and no showdown value currently. In a three-way pot, the likelihood that either Claude or Pro has a club or a made hand is high. Betting here would be a pure bluff into two opponents on a very 'wet' board, which is generally a losing play. I will check to see the turn for free or potentially fold if a significant bet comes from Pro, as I have no piece of this board.

On a monotone flop of 3c 6c Tc, having missed completely with As 7s (no clubs), my hand has very little equity other than hoping to hit an Ace or Seven or winning at showdown with Ace-high. Bluffing into two opponents on a monotone board is risky without a club blocker or a draw, as they are likely to continue with any club or pair. Checking back allows me to realize equity for free, control the pot size, and re-evaluate on the turn in position.

Turn brought 9s, giving me a gutshot straight draw to go with top pair. The flop checked through on this monotone club board, which suggests no one has a made flush (they would have bet for value). With top pair and the additional equity from my gutshot, I should bet for value/protection. A small bet of ~1/3 pot can get value from worse tens, pairs, and club draws while keeping the pot manageable if someone wakes up with a flush.

On the turn, I have improved to a pair of Nines with a Queen kicker. The board is currently monotone with three clubs (3c, 6c, Tc) and I hold the Nine and Queen of Hearts, meaning I have no clubs. However, Claude's bet of 350 into a pot of 950 (bringing the total pot to 1300) is relatively small, offering me pot odds of 3.7 to 1 (or 4.7 to 1 relative to the final pot). Given that I have improved to second pair and Claude's bet could be a semi-bluff with a single club, a weaker Ten, or a pure bluff after the flop was checked around, folding would be too tight. Raising is also inappropriate as I don't want to be forced to fold to a shove from Claude or interest from Pro behind me. Calling is the most disciplined play to see a river and potentially reach showdown.

The board is monotone with three clubs [3c 6c Tc], and the turn brings a [9s]. Claude (BB) bets into two players, and Flash (UTG) calls. holding [As 7s], I have only Ace-high with no club and a weak gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) that is tainted by the flush potential. With two opponents showing interest—one betting and one calling—my hand has extremely low equity and effectively zero showdown value. I am getting good odds, but not enough to chase a dirty gutshot or hope Ace-high is good. Folding is the only prudent option.

River Qs is bad for me - it's an overcard to my tens and completes QJ straights. Flash called my turn bet, suggesting either a made hand or draw. My top pair is now second pair behind queens. Betting only gets called by better hands (flushes, queens, JK for straights). Checking allows me to potentially win at showdown against missed club draws or smaller pairs.

On the river, I have improved to top two pair (Queens and Nines) with a Queen kicker. The board is significantly coordinated with three clubs, and since I hold no clubs, any single club in Claude's hand makes a flush a possibility. Claude checked the flop, led for a small amount on the turn, and has now checked the river. This line suggests a marginal hand like a Ten or a Nine that is now wary of the board development, or potentially a trapped flush. While I have a very strong hand for a four-handed game, betting into a monotone board where I don't hold a blocker to the flush is risky. By checking back, I realize my high showdown value and secure the pot against Claude's weaker pairs or missed draws without exposing myself to a check-raise from a slow-played flush.

I am holding 9c 6h in the UTG position (first to act in a 4-handed game). This is a statistically weak hand (96o) with very poor playability and equity. Even in a short-handed table, this hand is well below the standard opening range. With a healthy stack of over 80 big blinds, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. Preserving chips for better spots is the optimal strategy here.

Button with A♠Q♣ — a strong, standard open-raise hand with position and deep effective stacks. Pot is 150 and calling would be passive; min-raising to 200 is small and invites lots of action. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to build the pot, apply pressure to the small blind, and take advantage of position postflop. If I face a small 3-bet I can call and play postflop; if faced with a big 3-bet or shove I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and implied odds.
5h3d is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a button raise from Minni who has been aggressive, I'm out of position with a hand that plays poorly postflop. Easy fold.

I am holding Ace-King suited, which is a premium hand. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and the action is on me in the Big Blind. I should 3-bet here to build the pot and take the initiative, especially given my strong chip lead. A raise to 1000 is a standard 3-bet size from out of position, putting pressure on Minni while keeping the pot manageable for my hand's value.

Call to 1000 (add 700). A♠Q♣ on the button is too strong to fold to a standard BB 3‑bet to 1000 and I have the positional advantage. Flash’s sizing is not enormous and his 3‑bet range will include broadway hands and some bluffs — calling keeps those hands in and lets me realize equity and control pot size. A tiny 4‑bet to 1700 would be awkwardly small/polarized and could induce a 5‑bet or isolate me out of position postflop; a big 4‑bet would commit too much out of position against a deeper-stacked opponent. Plan: call, see a flop, continue for value on A/Q‑heavy or dry boards and control/fold if I miss and face heavy action. Total committed: 1000 chips.

I have top pair, top kicker on a somewhat connected flop with a flush draw. I 3-bet pre-flop and was called by Minni, which narrows her range but still includes many hands I am beating, such as weaker Kings (KQ, KJ), flush draws, and straight draws (JT, QJ). I want to bet for value and to charge the various draws present on this board. A bet of around half the pot is appropriate here.

Flash's flop bet is small (950 into a 3,000 pot). I have A♠Q♣ — two overcards and a useful Ace blocker but no diamond. Calling 950 gives pot odds of about 950/(3950+950) ≈ 19.4% (i.e. 950 / 4,900), and my chance to make a pair (an A or Q by the river) is roughly 24% — so a call is +EV. In position I can realize equity, float against his c-bet range (which includes Kx, diamond draws and bluffs), and either extract value if I pair or fold to heavy turn aggression. Raising to the min (1900) is too committal with only Ace-high and would often isolate me vs hands that still dominate me. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — bet for value if I pair, check/fold to big aggression if I completely miss or a dangerous diamond completes.

The turn brings the Deuce of Diamonds, completing the potential flush draw from the flop. While I still have top pair, top kicker (Ah Kh), I do not hold a diamond in my hand, which makes this card quite unfavorable. Betting again into a completed flush board could lead to value-cutting myself if Minni has any diamonds in her range (like AdXd, QdJd, or JdTd) or a set. Checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot size and evaluate Minni's reaction. If she checks behind, I likely still have the best hand; if she bets, I will have to decide if my TPTK is worth a call based on her sizing and tendencies.

Check. I missed on the turn and the 2♦ just completed a three‑diamond board (K♦ 9♦ 8♥ 2♦). Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop c‑bet, turn check) is wide enough to include a lot of diamond combos, Kx hands and slow‑played made hands. I hold A♠Q♣ with no diamonds, so I have very poor blocker coverage vs flushes and only ~6 river outs to pair. Betting here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity and a good chance of running into a made flush or a protected K — unnecessarily bloating the pot out of position. I’ll check to control the pot, see the river for free, and re‑evaluate: if I pair the river I’ll bet for value; if it’s a safe blank I may fire a small probe; if Flash bets big on the river I’ll fold. Action: check (0).

On the river, the board pairs with the Deuce of Spades. My hand remains Top Pair, Top Kicker (Ah Kh). After I checked the turn when the flush draw completed, Minni checked behind, which strongly suggests she does not have a flush. She likely has a weaker King (like KQ or KJ) or a medium pocket pair and is looking to get to showdown. While I could bet for value, checking is a safer way to realize my equity and potentially induce a bluff from missed straight draws (like JT or QJ). Since I am the chip leader and want to protect my stack as the tournament nears its end, checking to see a showdown is the most prudent play.

River summary: board K♦ 9♦ 8♥ 2♦ 2♠, I hold A♠Q♣ — only Ace‑high, no diamonds and no pair. Flash 3‑bet preflop, made a small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn; that line is wide and includes Kx, pocket pairs, missed/turned diamond draws and some two‑diamond combos that now have a flush. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity — many Kx and mid pairs will simply call, and any two‑diamond hands or a 2 in the hole beat me. Pot is ~3950 and I have no good blockers to the likely made hands; bloating the pot out of position with ace‑high is unnecessary. I check to control the pot and take showdown — if Flash now fires a large river bet I will fold, but there’s no value to betting myself.

UTG with pocket eights (8♠8♥) — a playable, medium-strength pocket pair. I want to take the initiative preflop, minimize multiway action and either isolate the blinds or build a pot where my hand plays well (set-mining + postflop equity). Stacks are deep (my 16600, SB very deep, BB and Button mid/deep), so a standard 3x open is appropriate. Min-raising to 200 is too small and invites more callers; raising to 300 (3x the BB) gives a clear opener size, keeps weaker hands in, and sets up a plan to c-bet favorable flops or call small 3-bets and proceed cautiously vs large 3-bets or shoves. Action: raise to 300.
5s9h is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Even with button position, this hand lacks the strength and playability to profitably call 300 (7.6% of my stack). Easy fold.

I am holding 8c 3s in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand, and Minni has opened from UTG. Given my significant stack lead and the low quality of my cards, there is no strategic reason to call or raise here. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my chips.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o facing a raise from Minni in UTG. The pot odds are approximately 30.7% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). While K6o has raw equity close to this against a wide 4-handed opening range, it suffers significantly from reverse implied odds (domination by better Kings) and poor playability out of position. I will likely under-realize my equity. Given I still have a healthy stack (81.5 BB) and this is not a desperate spot, avoiding a marginal situation OOP is the better strategic choice.

4-handed with Js9h in UTG. This is a borderline hand but playable in a short-handed game. With ~39BB stack, I'll make a standard 2.5x open. Suited connectors/one-gappers have decent playability postflop if called.

I am currently the chip leader with over 31k chips. Claude, the UTG raiser, is the short stack with only 3,675 chips. T9o is a marginal hand that doesn't play particularly well against a UTG opening range, even in a 4-handed game. While I have successfully 3-bet Claude in previous hands to force folds, his stack is now small enough (under 40BB) that he may be looking for a spot to commit his remaining chips. Calling would also leave me vulnerable to a squeeze from the blinds (Pro or Minni). Given my dominant position and the stage of the tournament (hand 127 of 150), there is no reason to risk chips on a marginal hand against a short stack who might be desperate to double up.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o, a very weak hand. Claude raised from UTG, which typically indicates a stronger range. Being out of position with a hand that is likely dominated and has poor playability makes calling unprofitable, even with decent pot odds. The standard play is to fold.

Claude opened to 250 from early with a ~36bb stack. I’m in the big blind with K♥8♥ — suited, decent postflop playability and a useful K-blocker. The pot is 400 and calling 150 more (total 250) gives ~27% pot odds, which K8s clears heads-up against a typical UTG-open range. A tiny raise here (to 400) is weak and a larger 3-bet just bloats the pot out of position against a shorter-stacked opponent. I’ll flat call to see a flop and use position/postflop playability to realize equity and decide on future streets; fold only to extreme action that makes continuing incorrect.

Flop Kd Ad Ah vs my Kh8h: I only have second pair (Kings) with a weak kicker and only backdoor hearts — any Ace in Claude’s range crushes me. Claude was the UTG opener and his range here is Ace‑heavy (Ax, AKo, AQs, plus strong pairs) and he’s short‑stacked (~36bb), so he can easily shove/commit. I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand or turn myself into a caller of a large jam with very poor equity. Plan: exercise pot control — check and see how Claude reacts. I’ll call small C‑bets to see a turn occasionally, but will fold to large aggression/shoves since my hand is typically dominated.

Board is Kd-Ad-Ah - extremely dry and heavily favors my preflop raising range. Minni checked, which often indicates weakness. I have no made hand or draws, but this is an excellent board texture for a continuation bet. A small c-bet (~35-40% pot) represents strength credibly since this board smashes my opening range. If I get called or raised, I can easily give up on later streets.

Flop: K♦ A♦ A♥. I hold K♥8♥ — second pair with a weak kicker and only a runner‑runner heart possibility. Claude opened UTG and is short (~3.5k), so his range is very Ace‑heavy (Ax, AKo/AQo, occasional strong pairs) and he can easily shove later. His flop bet is very small (200 into a 750 pot) — not a clear sign of absolute strength and it’s cheap to continue. I want pot control out of position: raising just bloats the pot and risks getting jammed by an Ace, folding is too tight with a decent pair, so I’ll flat call to see a turn and re‑evaluate. Plan: call 200 now, keep the pot manageable, and fold to large turn/river shove or clear evidence I’m dominated; continue cautiously vs small turn bets.

Board K♦ A♦ A♥ J♣ vs my K♥8♥: I have second pair with a weak kicker and virtually no meaningful redraw. Claude opened UTG and is short (~3.5k) so his range is very Ace‑heavy — if he holds an Ace I’m crushed. He bet the flop very small and checked the turn, which shows some passivity but could also be a trap. Betting here only risks inducing a shove or a large call from an Ace‑holding range and bloating the pot out of position with a dominated hand. Pot is only ~950 and I have plenty of stack to wait for better spots. I’ll exercise pot control: check behind to keep the pot manageable, call very small river bets if they occur to see showdown, and fold to large turn/river shoves or clear strong aggression from Claude.

The turn Jc gave me a pair of jacks - now I have actual showdown value. On Kd-Ad-Ah-Jc, my jacks beat any pure floats, diamond draws, and small pairs. Minni called the flop c-bet and checked again. I should bet for value/protection against draws and weaker holdings like small pairs or gutshots. A ~40% pot bet (~375-400) extracts value while keeping the pot manageable with my short stack. If raised, I can fold - but I expect to often get called by worse or fold out hands with equity.

Claude opened UTG preflop and the board K♦ A♦ A♥ J♣ very strongly favors any A‑holding. He has shown continued aggression (small flop bet then a turn bet of 400 after my check), and with his short stack he has great incentive to shove later. Calling 400 into a 1350 pot gives only ~23% pot odds, but my actual equity vs a reasonable UTG‑continuing range (Ace‑heavy: AK/Ax plus some Kx and pairs) is well below that. Raising OOP with second pair and a weak kicker is high-variance and will often get me committed against Aces or a river shove I’m crushed by. Pot control and stack preservation are the right plays here — fold and wait for a better spot.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Queen-Eight offsuit. While I have a significant chip lead over the rest of the table, this hand is too weak to open from first position, especially with active players like Minni behind me and short stacks like Claude who might looking for an opportunity to shove. Folding is the standard professional play to maintain my stack advantage as we approach the end of the 150-hand session.

Playing 4-handed on the Button with 9h 8s (98o). While the Button is a stealing position, 98o is at the very bottom of a stealing range and often a fold, especially with active players in the blinds. Minni (SB) has a large stack (162 BB) and has shown aggression, making a steal attempt risky as she can comfortably 3-bet or call. Claude (BB) has ~43 BB, enough to defend or shove. Opening this hand invites high-variance spots post-flop often dominated. Conserving chips for better opportunities is the standard play here.

Heads-up to the big blind with K♥J♥ — a strong, suited Broadway hand. I’m out of position postflop, so I want to take the initiative preflop: raise to a standard ~3xBB to isolate Claude, gain fold equity, and build a pot when I’m likely ahead of his calling range. Size (300) is big enough to price out speculative hands but keeps worse hands in for value. If he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (KJs is playable vs a wide 3-bet range but behind premium pairs); if called I’ll c-bet favorable boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated textures.

Holding T8o in the BB facing a min-raise from Minni in the SB. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400) and I'll have position postflop. T8o is playable here - decent connected cards that can flop well. Minni has been aggressive throughout the tournament. Calling is standard.

I raised preflop to 300 and the BB called. Flop is A♦ T♣ 4♠ — I’ve missed (no pair), but I’m in position and the pot is only 600. This board favors the caller’s range (contains many Ax combos), so I don’t want to overcommit, but I do want to apply pressure and fold out weaker broadways and small pairs. A small continuation bet (≈1/3 pot) gives good fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: bet 200 to take it down now or call down on a blank turn; if Claude raises strongly I’ll give up (he’ll usually show a real Ace or a set). If called, I’ll fire a second barrel on safe turn cards (bricks) and check/plan to fold to heavy aggression when the board gets worse for me.

I flopped middle pair with Td on a 4s-Tc-Ad board. Minni's small 200 bet into 600 is a weak sizing. I'm beating bluffs and worse hands but losing to any Ace. With position and a reasonable showdown hand, calling to see the turn is standard. Raising risks getting blown off a decent hand if Minni has an Ace.

Board: A♦ T♣ 4♠ 5♥. I hold K♥J♥ — missed the flop and have no realistic draw after the turn (flush impossible from here). I c‑bet small on the flop (200) and was called; the turn 5♥ is a relatively blank card that doesn't improve most of Claude’s likely calling range (lots of A‑x, T‑x, small pairs and floats). I want to apply pressure to fold out Kx/Qx/floaters while keeping the pot controlled if I get called, so I’ll fire a modest second barrel of ~1/3 pot. If Claude raises strongly I’ll concede the hand (I have negligible showdown value). Betting 350 balances fold equity and pot control.

I have middle pair (tens) on 4s-Tc-Ad-5h. Minni continues with another small bet (350 into 1350). The 5h changes nothing. Getting ~3.9:1 pot odds (350 to win 1350), needing about 26% equity. My tens beat bluffs and worse pairs. The small sizing suggests either a weak Ace milking value or a bluff. Folding is too tight with this holding and these odds. Raising accomplishes little. Calling to see the river is standard.

The river K♣ pairs my K♥ and gives me top pair (KJ). Claude called my small c-bet on the A‑T‑4 flop and my modest turn barrel — his line is sticky and likely contains many A‑x, T‑x, and medium/weak pairs, plus some Kx floaters. Many of those hands (T, small pairs, some Kx with worse kickers, and floats) will pay a value bet; only A‑x and K with a better kicker (e.g. KQ) really beat me. With the action checked to me, I should extract thin value rather than check behind and give up value. I’ll size about half the pot to target worse pairs and floats while keeping the price reasonable for calls. If Claude raises big, his line will be polarized to strong A or sets and I’ll fold. Bet ~1/2 pot to get value from the hands I beat.

River brought the Kc, a terrible card for my middle pair of tens. Board is now 4s-Tc-Ad-5h-Kc with both an Ace and King above my pair. Minni bet 850 into 1700 (now 2550 total pot), giving me 3:1 odds needing ~25% equity. However, the King completing the board is devastating - now any A-x OR K-x beats me. Minni raised pre and has bet all three streets with increasing sizing (200→350→850), suggesting value. Their range heavily includes broadways, suited aces, and king-x hands that all now beat me. While the sizing has been small throughout, the river increase suggests confidence. My tens are now third pair on this board. Even at 3:1 odds, I don't think I have 25% equity against Minni's value-heavy river betting range. Folding is correct.

I am in UTG position (4-handed) with J6 offsuit. This is a very weak hand that falls well outside a standard opening range, even in a short-handed game. With 81 BBs, I have no reason to get involved with trash hands out of position. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I'm on the button with A♣7♦ and it's folded to me. This is a clear open-raise spot: good position, decent ace, deep stacks. A standard 3xBB (to 300 total) gives solid fold equity vs the blinds while not over-committing out of position if Flash defends wide or Claude 3-bets. Plan: if called I'll use position to c-bet dry flops and get value when I hit an Ace; if I face a sizable 3-bet from Flash or Claude I will strongly consider folding A7o unless the sizing is small and I can exploit. Raise to 300 to seize the initiative.

AKo is a premium hand. Facing a button open from Minni, I should 3-bet for value from the small blind. With 3525 chips (~35BB), a 3-bet to around 900-950 is standard. This builds the pot with a strong hand and puts pressure on Minni's likely wide button opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni (Button) raised to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) 3-bet to 950. Claude is very short-stacked with only about 2625 total, meaning he is likely committed to this hand. My hand is weak and does not perform well in multi-way 3-bet pots, especially when facing a potential shove from the Small Blind. I will fold and preserve my large stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♣7♦ and Claude (SB) 3‑bet to 950. Claude only has ~2,625 (≈26 BB) and the pot shows 1,350; calling costs me 650 more (950 total) and would make the pot ~2,000. That means I need roughly 650/2000 ≈ 32.5% equity to justify a call. A7o is marginal vs a tight value 3‑bet range (QQ+/AK/AQ) but has reasonable equity vs a somewhat wider SB 3‑bet range and I have the huge positional advantage and a very deep stack to play post‑flop. A 4‑bet bluff here (min to 1,600) is thin — I don’t have strong blockers and Claude can comfortably shove or call, putting me in an uncomfortable spot with a dominated ace. Folding surrenders too much equity and I’ll miss positive‑EV postflop spots. Plan: call 650 more (950 total), use position to see flops, pot‑control on dry boards, extract value when I hit an ace or pair, and fold to large turn/river aggression unless I improve or have clear equity.

I have AKo on a Th9c4c board - completely missed with just two overcards. This board texture favors Minni's calling range (pairs, suited connectors like T9, JT, flush draws). With no pair and no backdoor draws, c-betting into a connected/draw-heavy board out of position is risky. Better to check and control pot size, potentially calling a reasonable bet or folding to heavy aggression.

I called Claude’s 3‑bet preflop to use position and realize equity, but this flop (T♥ 9♣ 4♣) is relatively dry and doesn’t hit A7o — I have only Ace‑high with a backdoor club possibility. Claude 3‑betting narrows his range toward overpairs and strong broadways (and some club combos), so a continuation bet here would be thin: many of his hands will call or raise and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll exercise pot control: check and see a turn for free. Plan if Claude bets: call small probes (≈≤600) to use position and evaluate the turn, but fold to larger bets/raises (≈≥800–1000) since I have no pair and limited showdown equity. If he checks back I can take free cards and value bet if I pair my Ace or improve.

Turn brought a blank 3d. I still have just AK high on Th9c4c3d. Minni checked behind on the flop - either showing weakness, trapping, or pot controlling with a marginal hand. With just two overcards and no real draws, I should continue checking. Betting accomplishes nothing - I can't get better hands to fold and I'd be building a pot OOP with air. Checking lets me see a free river where I might improve to TPGK if an A or K comes, or I can re-evaluate based on Minni's action.

I check. With A♣7♦ on T♥9♣4♣3♦ I have only Ace‑high — no pair and no realistic draws (a flush is impossible with one card to come). Claude’s preflop 3‑bet narrows his range toward overpairs/strong broadways; his passive check‑call on the flop and check on the turn looks like medium/strong made hands rather than air. Betting the turn would bloat a pot I’m likely behind in and won’t fold out many of his calling hands. Pot control and position are my advantages here: see the river for free and re‑evaluate. If Claude fires a small probe (≈≤600) I’ll consider calling to use position and investigate the river; I will fold to larger turn bets/raises (≈≥800–1000) since I have no pair and limited showdown equity. If he checks back I’ll look to pair my Ace or potentially bluff on a much more favorable river texture.

River 8d completes a possible 76 straight but that's unlikely. I have AK high on Th9c4c3d8d - completely missed. Minni checked back both flop and turn, suggesting weakness (missed draw, marginal hand, or air). My AK high beats missed draws but loses to any pair. Betting as a bluff is risky since Minni's passive line could be a trap with Tx. Checking gives me showdown value against missed draws and busted flush draws while avoiding losing more chips to hands that beat me.

I called Claude’s preflop 3‑bet to realize equity in position, but the runout (T♥ 9♣ 4♣ 3♦ 8♦) leaves me with only A♣7♦ high — no pair, no backdoor flush/straight. Claude’s line (3‑bet pre then check‑check‑check) is consistent with medium‑to‑strong made hands (overpairs, big pairs, some 9x/8x combos or slow‑played strength) and not a lot of blown‑off air. Pot is 2,000 — a half‑pot bluff (~1,000) needs ~47% fold frequency to be +EV, a smaller probe still needs ~35–40% folds; given his range and shortish remaining stack I don’t expect that much fold equity and I’m a big favorite to lose if called. With limited showdown equity but good pot control and position earlier, the lowest‑variance +EV line here is to check and take the showdown rather than invest more chips on a thin bluff. Action: check.

UTG with J♥6♥ — a marginal, easily-dominated hand. In 4-handed play UTG opening should be reasonably strong; J6s is too weak to raise profitably from first position and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 100 cheaply invites multiway pots where I’ll often be crushed by higher jacks/broadways and get little fold equity. If I raise, Flash (very deep/aggressive) or Pro (BB) can 3‑bet and Claude (button, ~46BB) can shove or re‑raise — I don’t want to play a bloated pot OOP with this holding. Preserve stack for better spots — fold.

87o on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a standard steal spot - the hand has some playability if called and position advantage. With 46bb effective, I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to put pressure on the blinds.

I am the significant chip leader with over 310 BBs. Claude, the button, has raised to 250 (2.5x). While Ten-Nine offsuit is a semi-connected hand, playing it out of position against an active button opener and a capable Big Blind (Pro) is unnecessary given my current tournament standing. My priority is to maintain my stack and avoid giving the shorter stacks easy opportunities to double up through marginal spots. I will fold and wait for a better hand to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 2.5bb raise from the Button (Claude). My hand is T3o, which is extremely weak. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 400, requiring about 27% equity), this hand has very poor playability and realization potential out of position. T3o is well outside the standard defending range against a Button open. I should fold.

I have KK (pocket kings) in UTG - the second best starting hand in Hold'em. With 4775 chips (~48BB), I need to raise for value. This is a premium hand I should play aggressively. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB (250) to build the pot and get value from worse hands.

Claude is opening from UTG (effectively the Cutoff in this 4-handed game) with a relatively short stack of 4,525. While QUEEN OF HEARTS (Qh) SIX OF HEARTS (6h) is a suited hand with some post-flop potential, it is easily dominated by Claude's opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ, etc.) and is generally too weak to call a raise from early position, even 4-handed. 3-betting as a bluff is also risky given Claude's stack size, as he may be looking for an opportunity to commit his remaining chips. Folding is the most disciplined play to protect my significant chip lead and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 3s, facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. J3 offsuit is a very weak hand, well below the threshold for defending against an UTG open, especially from out of position. I need 33% equity to call but likely have far less against a reasonable opening range, plus the disadvantage of being out of position post-flop. The correct play is to fold and preserve chips.

Facing an UTG open to 250 in a 4-handed game with 5♦6♠ in the big blind. Calling 150 into a 400 pot gives ~27% pot odds, but 5-6 offsuit has significantly less equity against a typical UTG open (Claude’s range will be relatively strong with his ~45BB stack). There’s no value in a 3‑bet — he can call or shove and I’d be dominated. I’ll fold and preserve my 16,100 stack to pick a better spot against a wider range or in position.

I am the chip leader with over 310 BBs. In a 4-handed game, pocket eights (8c, 8s) are a very strong starting hand, significantly ahead of the average range of my opponents. I am in the UTG position and will open with a standard raise of 2.5x the big blind to 250 chips. This amount allows me to build a pot with a strong hand while maintaining the ability to respond to potential shoves from the shorter stacks, particularly Claude in the Big Blind who has about 48 BBs.

I hold Js 9d on the Button. The player Under The Gun (Flash), who is the chip leader, has raised to 250. J9 offsuit is a weak hand to play against an early position raise, as it is often dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, etc. Even with position, calling invites the blinds to enter, creating a multi-way pot where my hand has poor playability. My stack is healthy (approx 80 big blinds), and there is no need to bleed chips in marginal spots. The standard play is to fold.

Ah9h is a playable, suited Ace with good post‑flop playability. Facing an UTG open from Flash (a frequently aggressive player) I'm out of position in the small blind; a small 3‑bet (to 400) is too weak and a larger 3‑bet commits too much with a marginal hand that will often be OOP against a deep, aggressive opener. Calling keeps the pot manageable (pot is 400, I need to put in 200 more), lets me realize equity and play post‑flop (look to c‑bet or fold based on flop texture, press with A or heart draws, fold to heavy resistance when missed). I therefore call the raise and see a flop.
T4o is a weak hand. Getting 4:1 pot odds but out of position in a 3-way pot. This hand plays poorly multiway and has minimal potential. Easy fold.

Flop: 6h 2d 5h. I hold Ah9h — the nut heart draw plus an Ace overcard. Heads‑up vs Flash’s UTG open this is a very strong semi‑bluff: I have ~9 heart outs (≈35% to make a flush by the river) and ~12 combined outs to pair or make a flush (~45% to improve by river). The pot is 600 and it’s checked to me; betting gives fold equity vs small pairs/air and builds the pot when I have huge equity. I’ll size to about half the pot to balance fold equity and pot control — big enough to charge worse draws and get value when I hit, but small enough that I can comfortably call a raise to realize my equity. Plan: if Flash raises I’ll call the flop with the nut draw and re‑evaluate on the turn (if a heart or an Ace hits I’ll extract value; on a blank I’ll proceed cautiously).

I have an overpair with pocket eights on a low, somewhat coordinated flop of 6h-2d-5h. Minni's lead (donk bet) for half the pot could represent a variety of hands: a pair (6x, 5x), a draw (flush or straight), or occasionally a monster (sets). While calling is an option to keep her bluffs in, the board is quite 'wet' with many potential overcards and draw-completing cards possible on the turn. I will raise to 800 to protect my hand, take back the initiative, and extract value from draws and middle pairs.

Call. I c‑bet to ~300 with Ah9h (nut heart draw + A overcard); Flash’s aggressive 3‑bet to 800 is polarizing but common for him (strong pairs/overpairs/sets and some bluffs). Calling the extra 500 into a 1700 pot requires ~22.7% equity to break even (500/(1700+500)). I have ~35% to make the flush by the river and roughly ~54% to make a flush or pair an A/9 by the river, so a flat call is +EV. A raise to 1300 (or jamming) would bloat the pot out of position and get called by many better made hands — too committal given his range and stack depths. I’ll call to realize equity and keep the turn decision flexible: if a heart or Ace hits I’ll extract value; if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I can fold. Total to put in the pot on the flop after calling: 800 (I’ve already put 300, so I add 500).

I check. I’m heads‑up with Ah9h on 6h 2d 5h Qs — the nut heart draw plus an Ace overcard. Flash check‑raised my flop c‑bet to 800 (polarized/aggressive range) and then checked the queen on the turn, which is ambiguous (pot‑control top pair/overpair or a deceptive line). On the turn I only have ~9 hearts to make the flush (9/46 ≈ 19.6% on the river) and 3 A + 3 9 = 6 pairing outs; total unique outs = 15/46 ≈ 32.6% to improve to a flush or pair on the river, but many pairing outs are not clean vs his strong range. Pot is 2,200 and stacks are deep (I have 15,050, Flash ~29,975) — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal realization of equity. Betting now will rarely fold his value hands and will force difficult decisions if he raises. Checking keeps the pot manageable and gives me a free card to realize my draw. Plan: if the river is a heart or an Ace I will extract value; if it’s a blank and he barrels big I’ll fold; if it’s a blank and he checks I’ll check back to showdown.

The Queen of Spades on the turn is an overcard to my pocket eights. After raising the flop and being called by Minni, her range likely consists of mid-pairs, straight draws, or flush draws. While the Queen might not have hit her range directly (unless she floated with overcards), it is a scare card that reduces the value of my eights. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise, and give myself a chance to see a cheap river and reach showdown with what is still likely the best hand against her drawing range.

I have the nut ace‑high flush (Ah9h) on 6h 2d 5h Qs 8h. We checked the turn to control the pot and realize equity; the river heart completes my draw. It's heads‑up vs Flash (the aggressive UTG opener who check‑raised the flop), and many of his continuing hands — K‑high or other heart flushes, sets, two‑pair, or stubborn pairs — will call a value bet. Pot is 2200, so I size to ~2/3 pot to extract calls without overly polarizing (1400). If he raises, the only realistic hands that beat me are full houses/quads (unlikely given the runout), so I will strongly consider calling a reasonable raise with the nut flush.

On the river, I improve to a set of eights with the 8h. However, this card is a double-edged sword: it completes a possible heart flush and potential straights (specifically 4-7). Minni's line—donking the flop, calling my raise, checking the turn, and then leading the river for a significant amount (1400 into 2200)—strongly suggests a value hand. While my set beats her possible two-pairs (like Q-6 or 6-5) and lower sets (55, 22, 66), it loses to any flush or straight. Raising here likely only gets called by hands that beat me, whereas calling allows me to reach showdown and win against the part of her range I dominate while losing the minimum to a flush. Given the tournament stage and my chip lead, a cautious call is the most professional play.

I am in UTG position with J2s, which is a very weak hand. Even though the table is 4-handed, opening this hand from the first position is generally a losing strategy due to the lack of playability, domination issues, and positional disadvantage. With 80 BBs, I have plenty of chips to wait for better spots. Folding is the only reasonable option.

I’m on the button with T5h — a marginal but playable suited hand in position. Nobody has raised (only the blinds are left), so I want to take the initiative, apply pressure and either steal the blinds or play postflop in position. Limping gives up fold equity and invites multiway pots; a standard open-raise is preferable. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB): big enough to generate folds from the SB and to deny cheap equity to the BB, but not so large as to bloat the pot with a weak hand. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry textures.

A4o in the SB facing a button raise to 300. Minni has been active with opens. A4o has decent playability against a wide button range - the ace gives me showdown value and potential for top pair. Calling 250 more is reasonable to see a flop in position relative to the BB. 3-betting isn't great with this hand as it doesn't play well when called. Folding is too tight here vs a button open.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. Minni opened for 300 from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. With 200 more to call into a pot of 700, I am getting 3.5:1 odds. Ace-high has significant equity against a wide button-opening range, and since I am closing the action, calling is the most profitable and standard play here to see a flop.

Flop is Ts Qd Kc - a very coordinated Broadway board. I have A4o which gives me only a gutshot to Broadway (need a J). This board heavily favors Minni's button opening range (lots of KQ, KJ, QJ, KT, QT combos) and Flash's BB calling range. My hand has minimal showdown value with just ace-high and a 4-outer gutshot. Betting here as a bluff would be poor since this board smashes their ranges. Check and see what develops - if checked through I might improve, and if they bet I can fold cheaply.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw on a board that is very connected (T-Q-K). This texture likely hits the pre-flop raiser's range heavily. Since Claude checked, I will also check to see what Minni does. I'm looking to realize my equity cheaply or potentially fold if the action gets too heavy, as my current hand is quite weak.

Flop Kc Qd Ts — I hold Th5h: a vulnerable pair of tens with a very weak kicker. Both the SB and BB called my button raise, so their ranges include lots of Kx/Qx, broadways and straight combos (AJ, J9) that either already beat me or have equity vs me. Both opponents checked; on this dry but high-card, two-overcard texture my c-bet has limited fold equity and will often get value‑called by better hands. With deep stacks and position, I want pot control and information — check back to see a turn, preserve stack, and only invest more if I improve or get a clear read. Plan: check now, call small/medium turn bets with showdown value, fold to large aggression if unimproved, and bet/raise if the turn gives a meaningful improvement (second Ten, a 5, or a safe card that favors my range).

Turn 6h is a blank. Flop went check-check-check which suggests nobody has a strong hand on this KQT board. I still have just A-high with a gutshot to Broadway. Betting here as a semi-bluff is risky - I'll get called by any pair and fold out worse hands. Since the flop checked through, checking again to see a free river makes sense. I might win at showdown with ace-high if everyone is weak, or hit my J for the nuts.

On a very coordinated board of Ts-Qd-Kc-6h, everyone checked the flop. As the Big Blind with Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw, I have some equity and potential showdown value.
