Since the pre-flop raiser (Minni) checked the flop and the Small Blind (Claude) checked the turn, there is little incentive to bet into a board that connects so well with most ranges. I will check to see a free river and try to realize my equity or win at showdown if the action remains passive.

Board: T♠ Q♦ K♣ 6♥. I hold T♥5♥ — top pair with a very weak kicker. Both opponents checked flop and checked turn, showing no clear strength and leaving me with limited fold equity. Betting here would bloate the pot multi‑way and get value‑called by Kx/Qx (hands that currently beat me) or by worse hands I can’t extract much from. I’ll control the pot and preserve chips: check, see the river, and only invest if I improve or face a clear, exploitable line. If I face a small river bet I’ll call for showdown; I’ll fold to large aggression unless I improve.

River brought the Ace, giving me top pair with a weak kicker (A4). The board is now Ts Qd Kc 6h As - anyone with a Jack has Broadway. The pot has been checked through on flop and turn, suggesting nobody has a strong hand. However, my A4 is vulnerable - AK, AQ, AT all beat me, plus any Jx has a straight. With the passive action, I might have the best hand, but betting for value is risky since better hands call and worse hands fold. Checking to showdown is the prudent play - I might win against Kx or Qx that was afraid to bet, and I avoid being check-raised by the nuts.

On the river, the board is Ts-Qd-Kc-6h-As. I have improved to top pair with my Ace, but the board is extremely coordinated. Any Jack (J) makes a straight (T-J-Q-K-A). Since the action has been checked around on the flop and turn, and Claude has checked the river, it is likely that no one has a very strong hand, but my kicker (4) is extremely weak. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by worse hands given how many straights and two-pair combinations are possible. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free and potentially win against a missed draw or a weaker pair that was checking for control.

I check. I have top pair (T♥) but a very weak kicker on a high-card board T♠ Q♦ K♣ 6♥ A♠. Both opponents passively checked flop and turn, so betting into two players here has very little fold equity — most hands that will call a river bet (Kx, Qx, some worse broadways) either already beat me or are marginal and won’t fold. The pot is only 900; a river bet risks getting called by better hands or facing a raise/shove (Claude has ~4.5k) that I can’t profitably call with this kicker. Preserving stack and taking showdown is the highest EV line. If someone bets a small amount I’ll call for showdown; I’ll fold to large aggression.

I have A♦Q♠ in early position at a 4‑handed table with a deep stack (18,350 ≈ 183 BB). This is a standard, strong opening hand — I want the initiative and some fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. The min‑raise to 200 is too small and will invite too many callers/3‑bets; a 3x BB open to 300 is a standard sizing here to define ranges and isolate the blinds. Plan post‑flop: c‑bet favorable A/Q high or dry boards, pot‑control if multiple players stay or if board is highly coordinated. If I face a small 3‑bet (reasonable from Flash/Pro), I’ll usually call and play postflop for position/SPR; if I face a large 3‑bet or shove, I’ll re‑evaluate and fold to avoid marginal flip spots. Raise to 300 to open the pot and take control.
53 offsuit is a trash hand. Facing a UTG raise to 300, this is an easy fold. Even on the button with position, this hand has very poor playability and equity against a UTG opening range.

I am in the Small Blind with 92 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible hands. Minni, who raised from UTG, has been playing aggressively. Calling 250 more into a pot that will be played out of position is not profitable with this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten suited (Qc Tc), facing a 3x open raise from Minni (UTG) in a 4-handed game. With 4 players left, a UTG range is reasonably wide (comparable to a Cutoff range in 6-max). QTs is a strong defending hand that flops well (straights, flushes, top pair potential). The price to call is 200 chips into a pot of 450, offering excellent pot odds (~31%). 3-betting is an option but calling is the standard low-variance play to realize equity from out of position with a hand that plays well post-flop. I will call to close the action.

I hold QcTc on a Kh7h6d flop. I have missed the board completely, having no pair and no immediate draw (backdoor straight possibilities are very thin). The King-high texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's (UTG) range significantly more than my Big Blind defense range. Out of position with effectively air, my standard play is to check to the raiser. I do not have enough equity to lead or check-call a standard bet.

I opened preflop and have the initiative heads‑up vs Pro on a K♥‑7♥‑6♦ rainbow flop with A♦Q♠ — two overcards but no pair. Against his call/cold‑call range (broadways, middle pairs, Kx, small pairs and connectors) a continuation bet is the right play to fold out many worse hands and extract value from hands that will pay to see a turn. I’ll bet about half the pot to keep the pot manageable while preserving fold equity (325 into a 650 pot). Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll exercise pot control on the turn unless I improve to a pair (A or Q) or the turn changes the texture dramatically; if he raises large I will fold Ace‑high — it’s not strong enough to continue against significant aggression.

I hold QcTc on a flop of Kh7h6d. I have missed the board completely, holding only Queen-high with no flush draw and no realistic straight draw. The King-high texture favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Minni bets 325 into 650 (half pot). Facing a standard c-bet out of position with no equity and no backdoor potential, I cannot profitably continue. Preserving my stack of 76 BB is prioritized over making a low-probability float.

6s4d is a very weak hand - unsuited, disconnected low cards. From UTG position, I need to play tight and this hand is well below the threshold for opening. With 15 hands left and a short stack of ~50BB, I should preserve chips for better spots rather than open-raising trash from early position.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant margin as we approach the final 15 hands of the tournament. Holding Six-Trey of diamonds on the button, while suited, is a very weak hand. Claude has already folded. Although attacking the blinds from the button is standard, both Pro (SB) and Minni (BB) have shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet. Risking chips with a hand that has such low equity if called is unnecessary given my tournament position. I will fold and wait for a higher-equity situation to protect my stack.

Current hand is J5o in the Small Blind. Action has folded to me. The Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively. J5o is a weak hand, below the median strength for a blind-vs-blind stealing range. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressive big stack is likely -EV. Folding minimizes losses to just the small blind posted.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the best starting hand in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. I am in the UTG position with the largest stack at the table. My objective is to build a sizeable pot and gain value from my opponents, particularly targeting the shorter stacks of Pro and Claude. I will open with a standard raise of 3x the big blind to 300.

I am holding 8h 3h on the button facing an UTG raise to 300 from the chip leader. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, even in position. The risk of domination is high, and calling would be a losing play in the long run. With 76BB, I should preserve my stack for better opportunities. Folding is the only standard option here.

Facing an UTG open to 300 from a deep/aggressive opponent, K♠5♥ in the small blind is a weak, often-dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position. Calling (250 more, 300 total committed) will put me OOP against the raiser and the big blind and frequently face continuation bets or 3-bets; a min-raise to 500 is far too thin for a 3-bet bluff. With a healthy stack and lots of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the optimal line here.

I have 2h8d in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Flash raised from UTG which indicates a strong range. With 48 BBs and needing to call 200 into a 450 pot, 82o is a clear fold - it's not in my defending range against an UTG open.

I am in the UTG position (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 76 BBs. Ace-Three suited (A3s) is a standard opening hand from this position due to its flush and straight potential, as well as the blocker value of the Ace. Opening puts pressure on the short-stacked SB and allows me to play in position against the BB if they defend. I will open to 2.5x BB, consistent with standard tournament sizing.

I'm on the button with K♦ T♥ facing a standard UTG open to 250 (2.5bb). With 187bb I have deep, playable stack and position — KTo is a good hand to see flops with on the button. A 3-bet here would need to be polarized (bluffs or value) and against an UTG opener is often too thin; it also risks a shove or a cold 4-bet from the SB (48bb) or the opener (73bb), creating awkward spots. I prefer to flat and use position: call 250 to keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and then c-bet/float or fold to strong resistance based on texture and opponents' actions.

ATs is a strong hand. Pro opened to 250 and Minni called. I'm in the SB with a premium suited ace. While I'm out of position, 3-betting here makes sense to build the pot with a strong hand, take initiative, and potentially isolate. Calling is also reasonable but 3-betting to ~950 allows me to define my range and charge draws. If called, I'll have a strong hand to continue with on most boards.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. Currently, there is an UTG raise from Pro, a call from Minni on the Button, and a significant 3-bet from Claude in the Small Blind. Claude is playing a relatively short stack (around 39BBs) and his 3-bet from the Small Blind into an UTG opener and a caller indicates a very strong range, likely including pocket pairs 10s or better, and big aces like AK or AQ. King-Jack is frequently dominated in this scenario. Given that I have a massive chip lead and we are nearing the end of the tournament (hand 137 of 150), there is no reason to take a high-variance marginal spot against a short stack who is clearly looking to commit chips. Folding preserves my stack and avoids a potentially difficult post-flop situation out of position.

I opened A3s from UTG, which is a standard play, but facing a large 3-bet to 9.5 BB from the Small Blind (Claude) changes the situation significantly. Claude started the hand with about 39 BBs, so this 3-bet represents nearly a quarter of his stack, indicating a strong range or a squeeze attempt. Additionally, the Button (Minni) flat-called my initial raise and is still left to act behind me. Calling would put me in a difficult spot: potentially sandwiched between two players, out of position against the Button, and with a low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) against the Small Blind, which significantly reduces the implied odds I need for a speculative hand like A3s. My Ace is likely dominated by Claude's value 3-bet range. Preserving my healthy 73 BB stack is prioritized over playing a marginal hand in a bloated pot.

I’m on the button with K♦T♥ facing a short-stack SB 3-bet to 950 after an UTG open. I have a deep 184bb stack and position. KTo is not a 4-bet-for-value hand and a small 4-bet would be a thin, polarized play that risks an awkward shove from the 39bb SB. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me use position to realize equity, and lets me exploit a possibly wide SB 3-bet range postflop. Pot math: I need to call 700 into ~1550 (roughly 31% of the final pot), which is acceptable to see a flop in position. Plan: see the flop, c-bet or float favorable textures (top pair, good draws), and fold to heavy resistance on dry boards. Therefore I will call to keep the hand live and play postflop in position.

I 3-bet preflop with ATs and got called by Minni. Board is 2s 2d 9d - very dry paired board. I have the Ad for a backdoor diamond draw. As the preflop aggressor, I should c-bet here to continue representing strength. This board largely misses Minni's flatting range and I have two overcards. A small c-bet of about 1/3 pot is efficient on this dry texture.

Board is 2♠ 2♦ 9♦; Claude (SB) 3-bet preflop and now makes a small c‑bet of 750 into ~3000. Calling 750 to win a ~4500 pot requires only ~16.7% equity. With K♦T♥ I have position, two overcards and a tiny backdoor diamond runner-runner possibility, and vs a reasonably wide 3‑bet/c‑bet range my hand has enough equity to justify a cheap call. Raising is too thin here — I don't have a value hand and would be opening myself to calls or shoves from better holdings (pocket pairs, diamonds, strong broadways) unnecessarily. Plan: call the flop, use position to evaluate the turn (float and take the pot on timely turns or fold to clear strength).

Turn is 5h - a complete brick. I have A-high with the Ad for backdoor diamonds. Minni called my flop c-bet, showing some strength - likely a 9, pocket pair, or diamond draw. Firing a second barrel with just A-high is risky since Minni has shown willingness to continue. Checking to control pot size makes sense - I can potentially see a free river and improve to an ace or diamond, or make a decision if Minni bets.

Check. I have K♦T♥ with no pair and effectively no meaningful draw on the turn: K♦ + the two diamonds on the flop only gives 3 diamonds with one card to come, so I cannot make a flush and I only have ~6 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 Ts) → ~13% to improve on the river. Claude’s line (SB 3‑bet preflop to 950, a standard-sized flop c‑bet 750, then checking the turn) looks value/`pot‑control` heavy (pocket pairs, 9x, sets) or at least not pure air. He’s short‑stacked (≈3175) so any bet from me hands him a great shove/fold decision and I have very little fold equity — bluffing is unattractive. Pot math: the current pot is 3750; I only get correct pot odds to call a river bluff if his river bet is small (roughly ≤~650), otherwise my ~13% to hit isn’t enough. Therefore I’ll check the turn, keep the pot small, and fold to significant aggression on the river (call only small bets that offer correct odds or call if I actually pair up).

River brought Jc. I have A-high (ATs). Minni called my preflop 3-bet and flop c-bet, then checked back turn. Their range likely includes 9x, pocket pairs, or missed draws. The Jack could have improved hands like JT, QJ. With just A-high, I can't value bet - nothing worse calls. Bluffing is risky against a player who's shown willingness to call. Best to check and potentially show down against missed draws.

I check. On this river I hold K♦T♥ with no pair and no realistic river outs — the hand only wins if I can bluff Claude off a better holding. Claude’s line (SB 3‑bet preflop to 950, a small flop c‑bet 750 on 2♠2♦9♦, then checking turn and river) looks like value/pot‑control (pocket pairs, a 9x, or a set) or at least not pure air. With the pot at 3,750 and Claude short (~3,175), any bet from me hands him excellent shove/call leverage; my fold equity is limited and a small bet won’t credibly fold many of his medium‑strength hands. I preserve chips and avoid forcing marginal decisions by checking to showdown. If he now leads with a meaningful bet or jams, I will fold (only calling a very small bet that gives correct pot odds).

UTG with K7o is a marginal hand. There are three players yet to act (button, SB, BB) and both Flash and Claude have shown willingness to 3-bet/raise preflop; raising to 200 risks a cold 3-bet I don’t want to play out of position with a weak kicker, and calling 100 to see a flop will often leave me in multiway pots dominated by better Kx/Ax hands. My stack is deep (170 BB) so there’s no need to gamble — preserve chips and wait for a better, more profitable spot (stronger hand or position). Folding is the +EV play here.

I have Js9s on the button - a nice suited connector. With only the blinds left to act and good position, this is a standard open raise spot. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to put pressure on the blinds and play my positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. Claude, on the Button, has raised to 2.5 times the big blind. While the price to call is relatively small (200 more into a pot of 400), Queen-Six is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially with the Big Blind still to act behind me. Given my current chip lead and the stage of the tournament, I should avoid playing marginal hands out of position. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Q7o. The Button (Claude) raises to 2.5bb. This is a slightly larger sizing than a min-raise, which worsens my pot odds (requiring ~27% equity). While Q7o has raw equity against a button range, it plays poorly post-flop out of position, often dominated by better Queens or failing to connect. Claude is my direct competitor for 3rd place in the standings (I have 7250, he has 6675), so avoiding marginal spots where I am out of position and likely to bleed chips or face aggression is strategically sound. Q7o is typically a fold against a 2.5x open in standard charts due to reverse implied odds and poor playability. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket 7s from UTG is a solid opening hand. With ~70BB stack and 11 hands remaining, I should play standard poker. A 2.5x raise to 250 is a good open with this hand - sets up nicely for postflop play and builds the pot with a hand that can flop a set.

I am on the button with King-Queen of clubs, a premium drawing hand and a strong top-pair candidate. Claude, the UTG player, has opened for 250. In a 4-handed game, his range is wider than in a full-ring game. With a significant chip lead and 11 hands remaining in the tournament, I want to apply pressure while maintaining the advantage of my position. A 3-bet to 750 allows me to isolate Claude, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or play a larger pot in position where I can better utilize my stack advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with [Ad 4d] facing a UTG raise from Claude and a Button 3-bet from Flash. Although A4s is a playable hand, facing a 3-bet cold from the SB is a very difficult spot. I would be out of position against the chip leader (Flash) and the UTG opener. My hand is often dominated by their ranges (AK, AQ, pairs). Cold calling is losing strategy here, and 4-betting is too high risk given stack depths. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Claude opened UTG to 250 and Flash 3-bet to 750. I’m in the big blind with Q♦3♦ — a weak, often-dominated suited queen with very poor multiway playability. To call I must put in 650 more for a 750 total into a 1150 pot (need ~27% equity), but against an UTG open and a button 3‑bet my realized equity will be much lower and I’ll be out of position facing two ranges. Raising to the 1250 minimum would be a thin multiway bluff with bad blockers. With 169bb I can wait for a much better spot. Fold and preserve the stack.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash on the button with pocket 7s. Flash has been active with 3-bets throughout the tournament. Getting 2.3:1 pot odds, but set mining here is marginal. With 68BB effective and Flash's large stack (279BB), implied odds are decent if I hit a set. Folding 77 to a single 3-bet is too tight, and 4-betting is too aggressive. Calling to see a flop and folding if I don't improve is the standard play.

Flopped an underpair on A-Q-9 board. This is a terrible board for my 77 - I'm behind any Ax, Qx, or 9x that Flash could easily have in a 3-bet range. No set, no draws. Standard check here to control pot size and potentially fold to aggression.

I have flopped middle pair with a strong kicker (King) on an Ace-high board. Claude called my pre-flop 3-bet out of position, which often puts hands like AQ, AJ, AT, or even AK in his range. By checking behind on this flop, I am exercising pot control. My hand has significant showdown value but is unlikely to get three streets of value from worse hands, and betting might induce a check-raise from a stronger hand like an Ace or a set. Checking allows me to see a turn card for free and keeps the pot manageable, which is a prudent strategy as the chip leader near the end of the tournament.

Flash checked the flop after 3-betting preflop - could be pot controlling with a medium hand or missed entirely. The 3s turn doesn't change much. My 77 is still an underpair with no improvement. Betting as a bluff is risky since Flash could easily have Ax, Qx, or 9x. Better to check and see a free river - if Flash checks back again I might have showdown value, and if they bet I can fold cheaply.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. Claude has checked to me twice after calling my pre-flop 3-bet. While it's possible I am ahead of hands like JJ, TT, or straight draws like KJ/JT, betting here doesn't offer much value. If I bet, Claude is likely to fold his bluffs and only call or raise with an Ace or better. By checking back, I continue to control the size of the pot, protect my chip lead, and ensure I can reach a showdown or respond to a river action with more information. There is no need to bloat the pot with 12 hands remaining in the tournament.

River pairs the 9s. Flash checked back flop and turn after 3-betting pre - this line screams either a medium strength hand trying to get to showdown or a complete miss. My 77 is a pure bluff catcher now. Betting gains nothing - better hands call, worse hands fold. Checking gives me showdown value against missed draws like KJ, JT suited that Flash might have 3-bet. Free showdown is optimal.

On this Ace-high board that paired on the river, my middle pair (Queens) with a King kicker has significant showdown value but very little value-betting potential. Claude has checked three streets after calling my pre-flop 3-bet. If I bet, Claude is likely to fold hands that I beat (like JJ, TT, or missed straight draws) and only call or raise with hands that beat me (an Ace, a Nine, or a slow-played monster). By checking behind, I am guaranteed a showdown to realize my equity and maintain my substantial chip lead as the tournament nears its end.

With only 10 hands remaining and a significant chip lead, my priority is to maintain my stack and avoid unnecessary risks with marginal hands. K2o is a weak hand to open from under-the-gun even in a four-handed game, as it is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. Given the short stacks behind me (Pro and Claude), I should wait for stronger holdings to apply pressure.

I am on the Button with Ac8c, a strong starting hand for 4-handed play. The UTG player folded. I have a stack of 72 BBs. Standard strategy dictates opening with suited Aces from the button to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My standard open size has been around 2.5bb (250). This puts pressure on the blinds, particularly the Big Blind who has a similar stack size, while retaining the ability to navigate post-flop with a flush draw potential.

I’m in the small blind with T♦3♠ facing a button open to 250 (pot 400; I must call 200). This is a very weak, offsuit rag with poor playability and lots of domination against a button raiser. Calling requires ~33% equity (200 into a 600 pot) which T3o doesn’t have vs a typical BTN opening range, and a light 3‑bet/min‑raise here is too thin — it won’t fold enough better hands and leaves me OOP postflop. With a deep stack I can wait for a much better spot; folding preserves equity and avoids tough postflop decisions. Action: fold.
AKo is a premium hand in the BB facing a button open. Pro's button range is likely wide, and I should 3-bet for value. Standard sizing would be around 3x the raise, so 750-850. I'll 3-bet to 800.

I am opening from the Button in a 4-handed game with Ac8c, which is a standard raise. The Big Blind (Claude) has 3-bet to 800. In a Button vs Big Blind scenario, ranges are wider, and A8s is strong enough to defend, particularly given that I will have position post-flop. The pot odds require me to call 550 to win a pot that will be 1650, needing roughly 33% equity. A8s calls well against a polarized 3-bet range, having the potential to make nut flushes or top pair. Folding would be too tight in this spot, and 4-betting would overplay the hand. Therefore, calling is the optimal play.

I have AKo on Qc6c9s - missed the flop completely with just ace-high. As the preflop 3-bettor, I can c-bet small to represent the queen and take down some of Pro's missed hands. This board is somewhat neutral - he could have Qx, but I also rep strong. A small c-bet of ~35% pot keeps my range protected and lets me fold to resistance.

I hold Ac8c on a flop of Qc 6c 9s. I have the Nut Flush Draw and an Ace overcard. This is a very strong drawing hand. My opponent (Claude), who 3-bet pre-flop, has led out with a continuation bet of 575 into a pot of 1650 (approx. 1/3 pot). The pot odds are very favorable; I need to call 575 to play for a total pot of 2800, requiring only about 20.5% equity. With the nut flush draw alone, I have roughly 36% equity by the river, plus potential outs with the Ace. Raising might force him to fold weaker hands I dominate or commit me against a range that has me crushed or flipping. Calling keeps his bluffs in, utilizes my position, and gives me a great price to realize my equity. I will call.

Turn brings Tc completing flush draws and adding straight draws. Board is now very coordinated (Qc6c9sTc). I have AK with no pair, no clubs, just a gutshot to Broadway (need J). Pro called my flop c-bet showing interest. With this scary turn card and only ace-high, I should check and give up on the bluff. Barreling again would be burning chips.

I have the Nut Flush (Ac 8c) on a board of [Qc 6c 9s Tc]. The 9 is a spade, and I hold the 8c, making a straight flush impossible for the opponent. Therefore, I hold the absolute nuts. My opponent (Claude) 3-bet pre-flop, c-bet the flop, and checked the turn. His range includes sets (QQ, 99, 1010), high clubs (Kc), and two-pair hands. With a pot of 2800 and effective stacks of roughly 5000, betting now extracts value from draws and second-best hands and builds the pot for a river shove. I will bet 1200 (approx. 43% pot) to target calls from his value range and set up an easy jam on the river.

Facing a 1200 bet into 2800 pot. I have AK with no pair, no club, just a gutshot to Broadway (need J for 4 outs = ~8%). Board is very coordinated with three clubs and straight possibilities. Pro called my 3-bet and flop c-bet, now betting turn - likely has real equity (flush, pair, straight, or strong draw). Pot odds are 1200 to win 4000 (~30% needed), but I have ~8% equity. Clear fold.

I am in UTG position at a 4-handed table, which effectively makes me the Cutoff. My hand, 6d7d, is a suited connector with good post-flop playability and implied odds, suitable for opening in a short-handed game to balance my range of high cards. We are deep enough (86 BB) to play speculative hands. I will open with a standard raise to 250 to potentially steal the blinds or contest the pot in position against the blinds, while being prepared to fold to a 3-bet from the aggressive big stacks behind.

Fold. Pro opened UTG to 250 — an early-position raise that represents a fairly strong range. 8♥3♥ is a very weak, frequently dominated holding; while position is valuable, this hand has poor equity vs an UTG open and is a poor candidate to defend or to 3-bet as a bluff. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot (or a button steal opportunity) is the correct play.

As8s is a solid suited ace in the SB facing a UTG open. With ~49bb stack, this is a comfortable calling hand. I have decent playability postflop with the suited ace - potential for nut flushes and top pair. 3-betting is an option but calling to see a flop is cleaner against a UTG range. Folding would be too tight.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit. The UTG player (Pro) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Claude) called. It costs me only 150 chips to see the flop in a pot of 600, giving me 4:1 pot odds. While my hand is mediocre and I will be out of position, the price is too good to pass up given my chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament. I will call and see the flop.

I have As8s on 9s-Th-6c. I only have backdoor nut flush draw and a gutshot (7 makes a straight). This is a very marginal holding - just ace high with minimal draws. Checking is standard here. I don't want to build a pot with ace high when the board connects well with UTG's opening range (JT, T9, overpairs). Will check and see what develops.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on a coordinated flop of 9-10-6. The pre-flop action involved an UTG raise from Pro and a call from the Small Blind (Claude). Claude has checked to me. Since Pro, the original aggressor, is still to act behind me and my hand has virtually no equity on this board, there is no reason to bet. I will check and see how the action develops.

I hold 6d7d on a flop of 9s Th 6c. I have bottom pair and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). The board texture (9-T-6) connects well with the calling ranges of the Small Blind and Big Blind, who likely defend with hands like T9, J9, QJ, 78, and various pairs. Betting here is risky because I am likely to be called by better hands (better pairs, straight draws) or raised by strong made hands (two pair, sets, straights). By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity (seeing a free turn card to potentially hit a 6, 7, or 8), and keep my opponents' ranges wide. It also disguises my hand strength. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action.

The 7s is a massive card for me! I now have the nut straight (6-7-8-9-T) with my 8s. Additionally, I have the nut flush draw with As8s if another spade comes. I'm checked to with the current nuts. I need to bet for value - I want to extract chips from overpairs, two pairs, or draws. Betting around 60% pot should get calls from hands like JJ, QQ, T9, flush draws, while not scaring away too much.

On this highly coordinated turn card (7s), the board now presents multiple straight possibilities (any 8 makes a straight). Claude, who called from the Small Blind pre-flop and checked the flop, has decided to lead out for nearly two-thirds of the pot. My hand, King-high with a 5 of spades, has virtually no equity. I have no straight draw, no flush draw, and even if I hit a King, I could easily be behind a straight or two pair. Given that Pro is still left to act behind me and my significant chip lead near the end of the tournament, there is no reason to contest this pot with a weak hand.

On the turn, the board reads 9s-Th-6c-7s. I have made bottom two pair with my 6d7d. However, the board is extremely wet; specifically, any 8 now completes a straight (6-7-8-9-T). The Small Blind leading out into two opponents on this scary card strongly suggests he connects with this texture, likely holding an 8 (straights), a set, or a better two pair (T9). While he could have a semi-bluff flush draw, my hand is currently a bluff-catcher that draws nearly dead against his value range. Given the high likelihood of the opponent having an 8 in their defending range and the multi-way action considerations, folding bottom two pair is the disciplined play to avoid bleeding chips in a spot where I am often way behind.

Short-handed (4 players) I need to open a wider UTG range. A3o is a playable ace — I want the initiative, to pressure the blinds and possibly isolate Claude (shorter stack). Limping is too passive and invites multiway pots; folding is too tight given stack depth. I'll make a standard 3xBB open to 300 to steal and retain fold equity. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet from Flash/Pro, call a small 3-bet and play postflop for top pair or board control, c-bet favorable flops and give up when heavily resisted.

JTs on the button is a strong hand to play in position. Facing a min-raise from Minni, I have good implied odds with this suited connector. My position advantage allows me to play this profitably postflop. Calling 300 to see a flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni raised from UTG and Claude called on the button. There is no reason to put chips into the pot with such a poor hand out of position, especially considering the current tournament stage (hand 142 of 150). I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with KJo facing a standard open from UTG (Minni) and a flat call from the Button (Claude). The pot odds are very favorable: I need to call 200 to win a pot that will be 1150 (approx 5.75:1). My hand strength is well above the top 20% of hands and plays reasonably well, although being out of position against two players is a disadvantage. Squeezing is risky against a UTG open, and folding is too tight given the excellent price. Closing the action allows me to see a flop cheaply.

I hold JsKc on a dry board of 2d As 6h. I am out of position against two opponents, including the pre-flop raiser (UTG) and the Button. The Ace favors their ranges significantly more than my Big Blind defending range. With only King-high and no draws, I have very little equity. Leading out (donk betting) into two players on an Ace-high board is not a profitable strategy. The standard play is to check and fold to any significant aggression.

I opened to 3x preflop with Ad3h and the flop A♠ 2♦ 6♥ gives me top pair with a weak 3-kicker. The board is dry and not very coordinated, but two opponents called preflop so this isn’t a single-opponent spot — I still beat a large portion of their calling ranges (weaker Axs, small pairs, overcards) and want to get value and deny equity. A standard continuation bet around half the pot extracts value from worse aces/pairs and charges draws while keeping the pot controlled given my vulnerable kicker. I’ll bet ~50% pot (950 pot → 475). Plan: if I get a single call, re-evaluate on the turn and barrel blank cards; if I face a large, polarizing raise I’ll fold my weak kicker; against a small raise I may occasionally call to keep worse hands.

Flop came 2d As 6h - I completely missed with JTs. I have no pair, no flush draw, and only a backdoor gutshot at best. This is an Ace-high dry board that heavily favors Minni's raising range. With jack-high and no draws against a c-bet, this is a clear fold.

I hold King-Jack offsuit on an Ace-high dry board (2d As 6h). After checking to the pre-flop raiser (Minni), who bets half-pot into two opponents, and seeing the Button fold, I am facing a bet of 475. The UTG opener's range connects very well with this board (possessing many Ax hands), whereas I have no pair and no draw. My King-high has very little showdown value and virtually no equity against an Ace. Continuing out of position with no equity is not profitable. The correct play is to fold.
2c 7s is a very weak hand - unsuited, disconnected, no high cards. From UTG position, this is an easy fold. No reason to invest chips with this hand.

I am on the button with 9h 8c and a significant chip lead. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament, my primary goal is to maintain my stack while applying pressure in late-position spots. 9-8 offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button in a 4-handed game. I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot in position against the small or big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with Q9o facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). Q9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. The 3x sizing makes the price to call less attractive (requiring ~36% equity realization, which is hard to achieve OOP with this hand). 3-betting is risky against the chip leader who covers me significantly. With the tournament nearing its end (Hand 143/150), preserving my stack of ~80BB rather than bleeding chips in marginal spots is the correct strategy. Folding is standard here.

I’m in the big blind with JhTs (offsuit) facing a button open to 300. Pro has folded so it’s heads-up; the pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total) to call. JTo has reasonable equity vs a button opening range in a short-handed game and the pot odds (~200 into 450) make defending profitable. I don’t want to 3-bet with a marginal hand out of position and risk bloating the pot against a deeper stack — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity postflop. Plan: call, then play the flop cautiously (check-call vs standard c-bets on dry boards, raise/continue when I hit a pair or strong draw, fold to heavy action when I have little equity).

Check. On K♥7♣7♦ I have J♥T♠ — no pair, no strong draw and I’m out of position. Flash opened from the button, so his continuation range contains many hands that already beat me (Kx, 7x, overpairs) and he will c-bet this dry paired board frequently. Checking controls the pot and lets me see his action. Plan: if he checks back I get a free turn to realize equity; if he fires a small probe (≈<1/3 pot) I can call to see a turn (looking to pair J/T or pick up information), but I will fold to medium/large bets (≈≥1/2 pot) since I have poor equity and don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. I won’t attempt a check-raise bluff here given stack sizes and my weak holding.

As the pre-flop aggressor with a massive chip lead, the board of Kh-7c-7d is an excellent candidate for a continuation bet. This texture favors the raiser's range more than the Big Blind's, as I am more likely to hold a King or a pocket pair. Since Minni checked, I will fire a bet of 300 (roughly half the pot) to take the hand down immediately. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, maintaining pressure in these spots is optimal.

Call 300. On K♥7♣7♦ with J♥T♠ I have no pair or strong draw, but Flash’s 300 is a medium-sized continuation bet and the call is cheap relative to my stack. Calling 300 into a 950 pot (i.e. risking 300 to win 1250) requires only ~24% equity — JTo versus a fairly wide BTN c-bet range is in that neighborhood and the price to see a turn is attractive. I’ll keep the pot manageable and realize equity: check-call the flop, fold to sizable turn/river pressure if I miss, but continue (bet/call or raise) if I pair a J/T or pick up a legitimate draw. I won’t bluff-raise out of position with this holding.

I’m OOP on the turn with J♥T♠ on K♥–7♣–7♦–3♠. I have no pair and only a few clean outs on the river, while Flash (BTN) opened and c‑bet the flop so his range contains many hands that already beat me (Kx, 7x, overpairs and lots of broadways). Betting myself would often be called by better hands and needlessly bloats the pot with very little equity; check‑control keeps the pot manageable and lets me fold to real aggression or realize equity if he checks back. Plan: check and evaluate his action — call a very small probe to see the river only if the price is correct, fold to medium/large turn pressure, and look to extract value on the river only if I pair or pick up a clear read. No check‑raise bluff or building the pot with this marginal holding.

On a board of Kh-7c-7d-3s, I have 9-high with no draws. I raised pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, but Minni called. Her call on such a dry board suggests she has at least a piece of it (a King, a 7, or a pocket pair). The 3s on the turn is a complete brick that doesn't change the board state. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that I have zero showdown value and no equity to improve, I will check behind to see a free river and avoid inflating the pot with a hand that is unlikely to force a fold from Minni's current range.

I have J♥T♠ on K♥–7♣–7♦–3♠–Q♥. I missed — no pair and no meaningful draw — while Flash (BTN) opened and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn, which is consistent with a range that includes many hands that beat me (Kx, Qx, overpairs, some slowplayed strong hands). Betting as a bluff OOP has poor fold equity versus that range and would bloat the pot for little expected value. I’ll check to keep the pot small and plan to fold to any meaningful river bet; only consider calling a very tiny probe if the sizing/read makes a bluff likely.

I have 9-high with no showdown value. Minni called the pre-flop raise and the flop continuation bet, indicating she likely has a piece of the board (a King or a pair). The Queen on the river is a scare card, but given that she called the flop, she is unlikely to fold a King or a 7 to a single river bet. With my massive chip lead and only a few hands left in the tournament, there is no need to risk more chips on a low-probability bluff. I will check behind and concede the pot.

With only seven hands remaining in the tournament and holding a significant chip lead, my primary goal is to preserve my stack and avoid unnecessary risks. 2s3s is one of the weakest starting hands, even in a 4-handed game. Opening from UTG with this hand would likely lead to a difficult post-flop situation or force me to fold to a 3-bet from the shorter stacks (Claude or Pro). Folding is the most prudent play to maintain my dominant position.

I am on the Button with Tc 2c. The UTG player has folded. While being on the Button offers a positional advantage to steal the blinds, T2s is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop if called. The Small Blind has a large stack and may defend widely or 3-bet, and the Big Blind has 50 BBs. Given the late stage of the tournament (Hand 144/150) and my healthy stack of 80 BBs, there is no need to force a steal with such low equity. Folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better opportunities in the final few hands.

Heads-up vs the big blind (only Claude left). J♥4♥ is a marginal hand but playable in this spot — suited, decent blocker and good heads-up equity. Limping from the small blind surrenders initiative and leaves me OOP; I prefer to open and apply pressure. I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50, so add 250) — a standard 3x-ish open that maximizes fold equity without overcommitting against Claude’s ~50BB stack. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I can C-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously if he shows strength or 3-bets large.

J9o in the BB facing a min-raise from SB. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400). J9o is a reasonable defend against a SB min-raise, especially with position and good odds. Only 7 hands left in tournament with a medium stack - standard defend here.

I opened to 300 preflop and Claude called, so I’m the preflop aggressor but out of position. The flop (3d 9c 8c) is somewhat connected and has two clubs, so it hits many of Claude’s calling combos (9x/8x, suited clubs, connectors), but a large portion of his range (broadway hands, many Ax/Kx) still misses. With Jh4h I have no real showdown equity, but a continuation bet here can fold out unpaired overcards and charge marginal draws while gaining info. I’ll fire a size of ~2/3 pot (400) to apply pressure; if Claude raises I will fold (he’ll usually have a made hand or a strong draw), if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

Flopped middle pair (9s) with Jack kicker on 3d-9c-8c. Board is draw-heavy with flush and straight draws. Minni c-betting 400 into 600 is standard from SB. My pair of 9s beats most of their c-betting range (overcards, draws). Raising would bloat the pot unnecessarily; calling allows me to see the turn cheaply and re-evaluate. Folding middle pair here is too weak.

I’m out of position with Jh4h and the turn (2♦) is a blank — I still have no pair and no meaningful draw. I was the preflop/ flop aggressor and Claude’s call on the flop signals a range weighted toward made hands (9x/8x, pocket pairs) and some club draws. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity because he already showed willingness to continue; I’d often get called by hands that beat me. With a 1,400 pot and plenty of chips behind, the prudent play is pot control: check and allow Claude to either bluff or show strength. If he bets, I can fold; if he checks back I keep the pot small and can re-evaluate a river bluff or give up. Action: check.

Minni checked the turn after c-betting flop. This often indicates weakness or giving up. I have middle pair with jack kicker - decent showdown value. The board is draw-heavy with club flush draw still out there. Betting here for thin value risks getting check-raised by better hands or draws. Checking back protects my hand, keeps pot manageable, and lets me see a free river to realize my equity at showdown. With only 6 hands left, no need to bloat the pot unnecessarily.

Board: 3♦ 9♣ 8♣ 2♦ A♥. I hold J♥4♥ — no pair and no meaningful draw. I was the preflop and flop aggressor (bet 400), Claude called the flop and checked the turn. That line indicates a range weighted to made hands (9x/8x/pocket pairs) and some busted club draws. The river A♥ reduces my fold equity (it pairs the board and improves Ax combinations) and doesn’t complete any major draws that would make me look like a value hand. The pot (1,400) is small relative to my stack (17,450), so risking chips on a marginal bluff isn’t justified — many of Claude’s continuing hands will call a river bet. I’ll take the low-variance line: check and fold to any river bet. If he checks back we go to showdown for a free look; if he bets I’ll concede the pot.

River Ah completes no obvious draws but adds a scare card. Minni's line (raise preflop, c-bet flop, check turn, check river) typically indicates giving up with missed draws or weak holdings. My pair of 9s with Jack kicker has decent showdown value. Betting here for thin value is risky - worse hands fold, better hands call. If Minni had an Ace, they'd likely bet for value. Checking back secures the pot when I'm likely ahead.

I am in UTG position (4-handed) with 7h Td, which is a very weak hand (Seven-Ten offsuit). Even in a 4-handed game, this hand is below the standard opening range, as it lacks high card strength, connectivity, and suitedness. With 80 BBs, I have plenty of chips to wait for better spots, especially with the tournament nearing its end (Hand 145/150). There are no specific table dynamics that justify an ultra-loose open here. Therefore, the correct action is to fold.

On the button with K♠9♣ and deep stacks (17450). No one has raised; with only the small and big blinds to act this is a prime spot to take the initiative. I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300) — a standard size at this table that buys fold equity, keeps the pot manageable against potential 3-bets, and lets me play postflop in position. If I face a large 3-bet from Flash or Claude I’ll fold K9o; against a small 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and player tendencies.

QJs is a strong suited broadway hand. Facing a button raise from Minni (likely wide range), this is an easy call from the SB. With 58BB effective, I have good implied odds and the hand plays well postflop. 3-betting is also viable but calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity with position disadvantage.

I am the chip leader with 28,575 chips and only a few hands remaining in the tournament. In the Big Blind, I am holding Kd 6h. Minni raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude called from the Small Blind. I only need to commit 200 more chips to see the flop in a pot that will be 900. Given the favorable pot odds (3.5 to 1) and my large stack, defending the blind with a King-high hand is standard. I will play cautiously post-flop, especially since I will be out of position against the Button.

I have QJs on Kc9h3s. I missed this flop - I have a gutshot straight draw (need a T) and two overcards below the King. With two opponents in the pot and a King-high board that likely connects with Minni's button raising range and Flash's BB defending range, this is not a good spot to lead out. My hand has some showdown value against air but can't stand much heat. Checking to see a free turn is the best play - I can potentially hit my gutshot or catch running cards.

I have top pair with a King on a very dry board (Kc 9h 3s). Since I am in the Big Blind and the pre-flop aggressor was Minni on the Button, standard play is to check to the raiser to see how the action unfolds. My kicker (6) is weak, so I don't want to bloat the pot immediately by leading into two opponents. Checking allows me to maintain pot control and potentially trap weaker hands or bluffs from the Button.

I hold K♠9♣ on K♣ 9♥ 3♠ — that’s two pair (Kings and Nines), a very strong hand on a relatively dry flop. Both opponents checked, so betting for value and protection is the right line: extract from worse Kx, 9x and medium pairs while charging any backdoor draws. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (600) — big enough to get value from single-pair hands but not so large that I fold out most worse holdings or overcommit against a set. If I face a small/medium check-raise I’ll generally call and re-evaluate on the turn; a large shove likely represents a set and will force a tougher decision depending on sizing and villian.

I have QJs on Kc9h3s - just a gutshot (need a T) with no pair. Minni's 600 c-bet into 900 is standard. Getting 2100:600 (~28%) pot odds but gutshot is only ~8.5% to hit. No backdoor flush draw in spades since I have Js. With a thin draw, no showdown value against this K-high board that hits button's range, and Flash still to act behind me, folding is correct. Not worth committing more chips with just 4 outs.

I have top pair with King-six on a dry board of King-nine-trey. While my kicker is weak, folding top pair to a single continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor would be too tight, especially given the pot odds and my chip-leading position. Raising is risky as it primarily gets called by better Kings or sets, so calling is the most prudent play to keep the pot manageable and allow Minni to continue bluffing with weaker hands.

I currently hold top pair with a King, though my kicker (six) is quite weak. On the flop, I checked and called a bet from Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor. The turn card is a Ten of hearts, which introduces some straight draws (QJ, J8) and a heart flush draw. Since I am out of position against the primary aggressor and my hand is essentially a marginal made hand that functions well as a bluff catcher but poorly as a value bet, checking is the most strategic move. This allows me to keep the pot size manageable, maintain control of my chip lead with only five hands remaining in the tournament, and see how Minni chooses to proceed. If she checks behind, I am likely ahead; if she bets, I will have to re-evaluate based on the bet sizing and the board texture.

I have two pair (Ks9c) on Kc 9h 3s Th — a very strong hand on a fairly dry board. Flash check‑called my 600 flop bet, so his range likely contains a lot of single pair hands (Kx, 9x), some underpairs and occasional pocket pairs — fewer combos that currently beat me (sets or trips) and limited QJ/straight combos. The turn Ten does introduce straights/trips that can beat me (QJ, TT), but those combos are not dominating his whole calling range. Checking now gives him a free river card that can improve into these hands or allow cheap bluffs; betting extracts value from worse Kx/9x and underpairs and charges any equity hands. I’ll size for value and fold equity: betting ~2/3 pot is big enough to get value from worse hands while not folding them out. Action plan: bet 1,400. If Flash check‑raises large, that will usually polarize to sets/straights and I will strongly consider folding to extreme aggression; if he just calls I’ll reevaluate the river based on sizing and his tendencies.

I have top pair with King-six. While my kicker is weak, folding top pair to a 2/3 pot bet from the button in a heads-up situation is too conservative, especially given my significant chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament. Raising would likely only isolate me against better hands like stronger Kings, sets, or the newly completed straight (QJ). Calling allows me to control the pot size and see the river, keeping Minni's potential bluffs or weaker hands in the range.

I have top pair with a King, but my kicker (six) is very weak. The river card is a deuce of diamonds, which pairs the board's lowest card and is unlikely to have changed the strength of either hand significantly. Throughout the hand, Minni has been the aggressor. By checking, I control the size of the pot and allow Minni to potentially check behind with a weaker hand or a marginal King, or to bluff with missed draws. Given that I am the chip leader and we are very close to the end of the tournament (Hand 145 of 150), minimizing risk while still being in a position to win the pot is the most prudent strategy.

I have K♠9♣ on K♣ 9♥ 3♠ 10♥ 3♦ — two pair (Kings and Nines). Flash check‑called my 600 flop bet and my 1,400 turn wager, then checked the paired river. The river pairing (another 3) does create dangerous hands that beat me (any 3x, pocket sets full houses/quads, TT full houses, pocket 99/KK full houses), but Flash’s line (call/call/check) looks more like a medium, somewhat passive calling range (Kx, 9x, underpairs) than a heavy value/raise range. With the pot at 4,900 I want to extract value from those worse hands while keeping the price small so I’m not crushed by the rare full house/set. I’ll make a modest value bet (~30% pot) to get called by worse Kx/9x and some underpairs; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll fold to polarized strength. Bet 1,400 to extract value while preserving protection against the few hands that beat me.

I have top pair with King-six on a board of Kc-9h-3s-Th-3d. Minni has led for three streets, but her river bet is quite small (1,400 into 4,900), offering me excellent pot odds of approximately 4.5 to 1. Given these odds, I only need to be correct about 18% of the time to make a profitable call. While my kicker is weak and she could easily have a better King (KQ, KJ, KT) or a completed straight (QJ), she could also be value betting a weaker hand like a Ten, a Nine, or potentially turning a missed draw into a small bluff to buy the pot cheaply. Since I am the significant chip leader and the loss of 1,400 chips does not jeopardize my position with only five hands left in the tournament, calling is the most mathematically and strategically sound decision.

UTG with 4♠5♣ — a weak, low unsuited connector. I’m first to act and will be out of position to the Button, SB and BB; this hand has poor equity in multiway pots and is often dominated by the ranges I’ll face. Calling 100 (limping) simply invites raises and creates a bloated pot with little chance to win a big one; a min‑raise to 200 would be overplaying junk from UTG. I’ll preserve my healthy 21450 stack and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

Button with Q5s after UTG folds. This is a standard spot to raise for a steal attempt. I have position on both blinds and Q5 suited has some playability with flush potential. With 55BB effective, a standard 2.5x raise to 250 is appropriate.

I am currently the chip leader with 24,925 chips and there are only 5 hands remaining in the tournament. My goal is to maintain this lead. In this hand, I am in the Small Blind holding Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude, who is on the button and has a relatively short stack of 5,300, has raised to 250. While I am getting decent pot odds to call the extra 150, playing a junk hand out of position against a short stack who is likely looking to double up is unnecessary risk. I will fold and wait for better opportunities or simply protect my lead as the tournament concludes.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h Jh facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). The pot odds are 150 to call into a pot of 400 (roughly 27% equity required). While J3s is suited, it is a very disconnected hand (3-gapper) and is often dominated by the opener's range (e.g., KJ, QJ, JTs, A3s). Being out of position makes it difficult to realize equity. Although the price is cheap, J3s is typically at the bottom of or outside the standard defense range against a 2.5x open. Given the stage of the tournament (Hand 146/150) and the marginal nature of the hand, preserving chips by folding is the prudent decision.
86o is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. With 4 players remaining and being first to act, this is not a profitable opening hand. Standard fold.

I am on the button with an Ace. Since Claude folded, I only have the blinds to beat. Raising 2.5x is a standard play to exert pressure and potentially take the pot pre-flop or play in position. My stack size allows for this aggression as we approach the end of the tournament.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind. The Button (Flash) has raised to 250. ATo is a strong hand against a button opening range, but playing out of position in a large pot is difficult. A 3-bet might isolate the action but risks a 4-bet or playing a bloated pot OOP. Folding is too conservative given the hand strength and position of the raiser. Calling is the most prudent play to realize equity while keeping the pot manageable. I need to call 200 more chips.

I have T♣3♥ in the big blind facing a button open to 250 with the small blind already calling. This will be a multiway pot and T3o is very poor playability (unsuited, poorly connected, almost no backdoors) and is often dominated by button and SB ranges. Calling 150 into a 600 pot gives ~4:1 immediate odds, but multiway equity for this hand is well below the break‑even threshold and postflop play will be difficult out of position. 3‑betting as a bluff is too thin here — I have no useful blockers and would be risking chips unnecessarily. Fold and wait for a better spot or a hand with real postflop equity/position.

I am out of position on a dry, paired board [9s 9d Kc] holding Ace-high (AhTd). This texture generally favors the pre-flop raiser (Button) who has more Kx in their range. Leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose as it folds out bluffs I beat and isolates me against better hands. The standard play is to check to the aggressor and evaluate their action.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board is dry and paired (9s 9d Kc). This texture generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as it contains more Kings. Pro checked from the small blind. I will put in a small continuation bet of 200 (1/3 pot) to represent a King or a pocket pair and to force folds from hands that missed the flop. My Ace-high also has decent showdown value if checked down, but a bet here is standard to take control of the pot.

On a paired board of [9s 9d Kc], the opponent (Button) bets small (200 into 600, ~1/3 pot). This is a very standard continuation bet frequency and sizing on this texture with a wide range, including air, draws, and value. Holding Ace-high [Ah Td], I am ahead of their bluffs (like QJ, JTs, lower suited connectors) and getting excellent pot odds (5:1). I need very little equity to continue. Folding Ace-high to a single small bet on this dry board against a button range is too exploitable. I will call to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings the 9h, making the board [9s 9d Kc 9h]. This is a very significant card. With three nines on the board, any pocket pair in the opponent's range now makes a full house (e.g., 22 becomes 9-9-9-2-2). My hand is A-T, giving me Trip Nines with an Ace-King kicker (9-9-9-K-A). I am currently losing to any pocket pair, any King (who has 9-9-9-K-K), and obviously the case nine (Quads). I am only ahead of unpaired hands lower than Ace-high (like QJ) and splitting with other Ace-highs. Leading here is not profitable as better hands call and worse hands fold. I must check and see if the opponent continues aggression. If they check back, I have reasonable showdown value to chop or win against bluffs.

The turn brings a third nine to the board, giving everyone at least trip nines. My hand is effectively trip nines with an Ace and a King (from the board) as kickers, which is the best possible hand unless my opponent has a King for a full house or the case nine for quads. Pro called a bet on the flop (9-9-K), which suggests he either has a King, a nine, or a pocket pair. By checking the turn, I control the pot size and avoid over-committing against a potential full house while still being in a great position to win or chop at showdown with my Ace kicker. With only a few hands left in the tournament and a significant chip lead, minimizing unnecessary risk is a priority.

On a board of [9s 9d Kc 9h 8d], I hold [Ah Td] giving me Three of a Kind (Nines) with an Ace-King kicker (9-9-9-K-A). However, with three Nines on the board, any pocket pair in the opponent's range constitutes a Full House (e.g., 22 makes 9s full of 2s), and any King also makes a Full House (9s full of Kings). Since the opponent raised pre-flop and c-bet the flop, their range contains many Kings and pocket pairs. Checking the turn suggests they might be pot-controlling a marginal made hand (like a small pair) or giving up with air. Betting here has no value as better hands (Full Houses) will call and worse hands (Air) will fold. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply to win against air or chop with other Ace-high hands.

The board has paired three times with nines, giving me trip nines with an Ace kicker. On the river, Pro checked for a second time. My hand has significant showdown value, but it is vulnerable to any King or a pocket pair higher than eights, which would make a full house. Given my substantial chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament, checking back is the prudent play to see the showdown without risking more chips against a potential trap or a better hand, while still winning against lower kickers or chopping with other Aces.

I am currently the chip leader with only 3 hands remaining in the tournament. Jd 4s is a very weak hand to play from Under The Gun (UTG), even in a 4-handed game. Given the tournament stage and my position, there is no strategic value in entering the pot with this hand. I will fold and maintain my stack for the final two hands.

I am holding 5h 3s on the Button, which is a very weak hand (53 offsuit). With only 3 hands remaining in the tournament (Hand 148/150) and facing a large stack in the Small Blind and a short stack in the Big Blind, attempting to steal the blinds with such low equity is risky and unnecessary. The standard play here is to fold and wait for a better opportunity or simply preserve the stack for the final two hands.

7♠3♥ is essentially junk and plays very poorly out of position from the small blind. Calling 50 is cheap but will leave me in marginal post‑flop spots with little equity; raising to 200 with this hand is a pure bluff and easily punished by a defending big blind. I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot (especially on the button), so I fold and relinquish the small blind.

I am in UTG with King-Queen suited (KQs), which is a premium broadway hand. With 7975 chips (approx. 80 big blinds), I am deep enough to play this hand aggressively. It is strong enough to open-raise from early position to build the pot and define ranges. A standard open size of 2.5x or 3x is appropriate here. I will raise to 250 to pressure the blinds and Button while keeping the pot manageable if I face a 3-bet.

I’m on the button with 3♥9♥ facing an UTG open to 250. With deep stacks and position, this hand has enough playability for a cheap flop—suited, can make flushes/straights, and I can leverage position post‑flop. 3‑betting would be a thin, high-variance bluff that often gets me isolated against stronger ranges or squeezed by the big stack in the BB; folding is too nitty given the small price. Plan: call the 250 to see a flop, fold to a large 3‑bet, and play cautiously postflop (pot control/float and only commit with strong pairs or nut/near‑nut hearts).

K2o is a weak hand. Even getting decent pot odds (200 to win 650), this hand plays poorly postflop and is dominated by better kings. With only 2 hands left in the tournament, I should preserve my 5700 stack for a better spot rather than calling with a marginal hand out of position against two players.

I am holding Ace-Five offsuit in the Big Blind. Pro opened from UTG for 250, and Minni called on the button. With only two hands remaining in the tournament and being the current chip leader, I want to protect my position while taking mathematically sound spots. I am getting excellent pot odds of 150 to win a total pot of 650 (approximately 4.3:1). A5o is a standard defending hand in this situation. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially improve my lead without the high risk of a 3-bet squeeze against two opponents, one of whom (Minni) is my closest rival in stack size.

On a flop of Ac-Ts-5c, I have flopped top and bottom pair (Aces and Fives). This is a very strong hand, but given that I am out of position against both the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and the button (Minni), checking to the aggressor is the standard and most strategic play. Checking allows me to see how the action develops and potentially trap one of my opponents if they decide to bet. With only two hands left in the tournament and holding the chip lead, I want to play this hand carefully to avoid any massive swings, while still extracting value from weaker Aces or draws. If a bet occurs, I plan to call or raise depending on the sizing and the action behind me.

I raised pre-flop with KQs and received two callers, creating a multi-way pot. The flop falls Ac Ts 5c. While the Ace favors my UTG opening range, the board texture is wet (flush draw, straight connectivity) and connects well with the calling ranges of the Big Blind and the Button. Playing multi-way, my c-bet frequency should decrease. I hold a gutshot straight draw and backdoor flush potential, but currently only have King-high. Betting into two opponents is risky as I am unlikely to fold out an Ace, and I risk getting raised or called by strong draws. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially see a free turn to realize my equity (hitting a Jack or a Spade), and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal hand out of position to the Button.

Flop: A♣ T♠ 5♣. My 9♥3♥ has no pair and effectively no real draw — with two cards to come it’s impossible to make a heart flush (need three hearts on board) and I only have six outs to make a single pair (3s and 9s), ~24% to pair by the river. Both opponents checked the flop, showing weakness, but the preflop raiser’s range contains a lot of A‑x and broadway hands that will continue. Pot is only 800 and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to fire chips as a thin multiway bluff with virtually no equity. Best line is pot control and see a free turn: check and fold to meaningful aggression, call small probes only if the price is very cheap or if I improve. If checked through to the turn I can re-evaluate (look for a pair or a favorable card to bluff).

The turn card (Ten of Clubs) counterfeits my bottom pair (Fives) as the board now pairs Tens. My hand is now effectively Aces and Tens with a Five kicker. Since the flop was checked through, it is unlikely anyone is holding a monster (like Pocket Tens or Ace-Ten), but the Ten is a scare card that gives anyone holding a Ten trips and completes the club flush draw. With only two hands remaining in the tournament and currently holding the chip lead over Minni, my primary objective is pot control and risk mitigation. I will check to see how my opponents respond to this board pairing. If there is significant action, I can safely fold and maintain my lead for the final hand.

I hold KsQs (King-Queen suited) on a board of Ac Ts 5c Tc. I opened pre-flop from UTG and received callers from the Button and Big Blind. I checked the flop which had two clubs and an Ace. The Turn is the Ten of Clubs, which pairs the board (Tens) and completes a potential flush draw (three clubs on board). This is a very wet board texture that hits the callers' ranges hard (they could have flushes, trips with a Ten, or top pair with an Ace). Since I have no club and only King-high with a gutshot straight draw that is likely dead or drawing to a second-best hand (due to the flush potential), I have extremely low equity. Bluffing into two opponents on a board that likely improved at least one of them is not profitable. My best play is to check and fold to any aggression.

Check. I hold 3♥9♥ and have missed the board: flop A♣ T♠ 5♣ and the turn Tc pairs the Ten and brings a third club. I have no pair and no realistic draw on the turn (only 6 outs to pair on the river ≈13%), while opponents’ checking through is consistent with Ax, Tx, pocket tens, or club combos that beat me. With a multiway spot, deep stacks and a small pot (800), betting here would be a thin, high‑variance bluff likely to be called by better hands. Best line is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful bet. I’ll only consider calling a very tiny probe that offers >9:1 pot odds (which roughly matches my river equity) or re-evaluate on the river if I actually pair or the board gives a good bluffing opportunity.

With only one hand remaining after this and currently holding a 3,675 chip lead over my closest rival, Minni, my primary goal is risk management. On the river, the board is Ac-Ts-5c-Tc-4d. My hand is Aces and Tens with a Five kicker. While top pair is likely the best hand given that the flop and turn were checked through, the board is dangerous—a Ten makes trips and the club flush draw arrived on the turn. There is very little value in betting; I am unlikely to be called by worse, and a bet could provoke a bluff or expose me to a trap from a player slow-playing a Ten or a flush. By checking, I am guaranteed to see the showdown cheaply or for free, protecting my stack and ensuring I enter the final hand of the tournament with a significant lead.

I hold KsQs on a board of 5c Ts Ac Tc 4d. The board pairs Tens and has three clubs. Action has been checked around on the Flop and Turn. The River was checked to me by the Big Blind. My hand effectively plays as a Pair of Tens with Ace-King-Queen kickers. I beat any opponent who is 'playing the board' (e.g., missed straight draws like J9, 87, or lower pocket pairs like 22/33 which are counterfeited by the board T-T-A-5-4 vs my T-T-A-K-Q). However, I lose to any player holding a 4, 5, 66+, A, T, or a flush. Betting here yields little value as worse hands (playing the board) will fold, and better hands (any pair) will likely call given the checked-down action. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value against missed draws without risking chips against a trapped Ace or low pair.

I have 3♥9♥ and completely missed this board: A♣ T♠ 5♣ T♣ 4♦. The turn gave a paired Ten and the board has three clubs — opponents checking the river is consistent with Ax, Tx or a club flush. This is a multiway, checked‑through, small pot (800) spot where a bluff is very thin and likely to be called by better hands. With no pair and no realistic blocker to meaningful holdings, the correct, low‑variance line is to check and take it down if everyone shows; fold to any meaningful river bet.

I’m UTG with KcTs in a 50/100 game and deep stacks (I have 21,050, others ~5.7k / 25.5k / 7.6k). With three players to act behind, KTo is playable but vulnerable multi‑way; I want initiative and to define the pot rather than limp. Table has been seeing 250–300 opens from aggressors, so a standard 3x BB open to 300 keeps my range strong, gains fold equity, and uses the King blocker to reduce some premium AK/KK combos behind. If I get a simple call behind I’ll play postflop for position; if I face a large 3‑bet from the very deep stacks I can fold without committing too much. Goal is to build the pot with a reasonable hand and pressure the blinds/button while preserving stack maneuverability.

Final hand of the tournament. I have T8 suited on the button facing a standard UTG open from Minni. T8s is a decent suited connector with good playability in position. Getting ~2.5:1 pot odds to call. With position and implied odds potential, calling to see a flop is the best play here. Not strong enough to 3-bet, but too good to fold in position.

This is the final hand of the tournament (Hand 150). I am currently the chip leader with 25,475 chips, while Minni follows with 20,750. My primary goal is to preserve my lead to ensure the highest possible finish. My hand, 5h 3s, is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position against an UTG raiser and a caller. Calling would commit more chips to a pot where I have very little equity. Folding is the most strategic move to lock in my stack size and secure my position as the tournament leader without unnecessary risk.

This is the final hand (#150). I am in the Big Blind holding 4s 7h, a very weak offsuit hand. Minni opened from UTG indicating strength, and Claude flat-called on the Button. Although the price to call is cheap (200 chips into a pot of 750, offering nearly 5:1 odds), my equity with 74o against two ranges is likely around 20-25%, and I will be playing out of position against two opponents. The reverse implied odds are high, and this hand realizes equity very poorly. Maximizing my final stack requires avoiding negative EV spots, and folding this trash hand is the correct play.

I opened to 300 preflop and Claude (button) called, so I’m the preflop aggressor on a Qh‑8s‑6h flop holding KcTs. I have a clean overcard (King) and remote backdoor straight outs but no real draw. Claude’s calling range from the button is wide (Qx, small/medium pairs, broadways, suited connectors), so a continuation bet will fold out lots of junk and charge draws while also getting value when my King is best. I’ll use my initiative and put pressure on him with a standard c‑bet of roughly two‑thirds pot (~500 into 750). That size gives solid fold equity and keeps weaker Q/8/6 combos and floating hands honest; if Claude raises in a polarized way I’ll reassess (likely fold to a large, committed 3‑bet given I currently only have a single overcard).

Final hand - flopped middle pair (8s) with T8cc on 6h8sQh. Getting 2.5:1 pot odds to call Minni's 500 c-bet. My hand is decent but vulnerable. Board has two hearts creating flush draw potential, but I have no backdoor flush draw. Calling is standard here with middle pair in position - folding is too tight and raising only gets called by better. Will reevaluate turn.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop (500 into 750) and Claude called. On the turn the 4♥ comes, making a three‑heart board (Q♥,6♥,4♥). I hold K♣T♠ — just King‑high with no heart and no real draw. Claude already showed willingness to call a large flop bet, so his range likely contains Qx, a medium/mini pair, or heart combos — many of which now beat me (any heart = flush) or will simply call another barrel. Betting again here has very low fold equity and is thin value (I’m ahead of very little and behind lots). With pot control important out of position and a very weak hand, I’ll check and avoid committing more chips. If Claude fires a small probe on the turn I’ll fold most of the time; if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river for a possible bluff or a showdown attempt.

Turn brought 4h, putting 3 hearts on board. Minni checking after c-betting flop is interesting - could be giving up on a missed draw, checking with a weak made hand scared of the flush, or trapping with a completed flush. My middle pair of 8s has showdown value but is vulnerable. With no heart in my hand, betting for thin value risks getting check-raised by a flush. Checking behind to pot control and take a free card/showdown is the safer play.

I c-bet the flop and was called; turn brought the 4♥ and both of us checked, making the board Q♥‑8♠‑6♥‑4♥. I hold K♣T♠ — no heart, just King‑high. With three hearts on board any two‑heart combo already has a flush and single hearts have four‑flush only; Claude’s line (call flop, check turn) looks like Qx, a medium pair, or a cautious showdown hand. Betting the river would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity (top pair and medium pairs will usually call; a raise would put me to a tough decision with almost no equity). Pot control and avoiding a costly bluff is the higher‑EV line here, so I check to go to showdown.

River completed with 3c, keeping 3 hearts on board. Minni has checked turn and river after betting flop - this is a weakish line but could trap with a flush. My pair of 8s has showdown value. Betting thin value here is risky - I mostly get called by better (Qx, flushes) and fold out worse. Checking behind to realize my showdown value is optimal. This is the final hand so no need to take unnecessary risks.
